Docker CLI Complete Documentation - anihpj Django Project

Comprehensive Docker CLI reference with practical Django examples using the anihpj Django project with its jobpost app (Skills, Author, Location, User, and JobPost models)

Docker CLI v24.0+ Django 6.0 Python 3.11+ Container Development pytest Testing

🐋 Docker CLI Documentation for anihpj Django Project

📚 Table of Contents

🎯 Navigate through comprehensive Docker CLI commands... Each section includes practical Django examples using the anihpj Django project and its jobpost app models (Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost) with real Docker commands, pytest testing, and containerized development workflows.

🏗️ Build Commands

docker build, builder, buildx for creating Django container images with optimized layers and multi-platform support

🔧 Compose Commands

docker compose for orchestrating Django multi-container applications with database, redis, and testing services

📦 Container Commands

Container lifecycle management for Django apps - create, run, exec, logs, and debugging containers

🖼️ Image Commands

Docker image operations for Django deployments - pull, push, tag, inspect, and registry management

🌐 Network Commands

Container networking for Django services - create networks, connect containers, and service discovery

💾 Volume Commands

Data persistence for Django applications - database volumes, static files, and backup strategies

Configuration management for Django settings, secrets, and environment-specific deployments

Docker system monitoring, cleanup, diagnostics, and core Docker CLI commands

Docker Desktop management, debugging, model runner, and Kubernetes integration

Multi-platform manifest management for Django container image distribution

Model Context Protocol commands for advanced Docker integrations and workflows

Docker Swarm orchestration for Django production deployments - nodes, services, stacks, secrets

Container state management and checkpoint/restore for Django development and testing

Docker context management for multi-environment Django deployments - dev, staging, production

AI/ML model management for Django AI features - model deployment, inference, and containerized ML workflows

⚡ Offload Commands

Hardware acceleration and GPU offloading for Django ML applications and compute-intensive tasks

🔐 Pass Commands

Password and secret management for secure Django deployments with encrypted credential storage

🔌 Plugin Commands

Docker plugin ecosystem management for extending Django containerization capabilities

🏖️ Sandbox Commands

Isolated testing environments for Django development with secure containerized sandboxes

🔍 Scout Commands

Security scanning and vulnerability analysis for Django container images and dependencies

🛡️ Trust Commands

Content trust and image signing for secure Django production deployments and CI/CD pipelines

🏗️ Build Commands

Docker build commands enable you to create container images for your anihpj Django project. These commands compile your Django application, install dependencies, configure the environment, and package everything into portable container images that can run consistently across development, staging, and production environments.

📋 Build Commands Overview

docker build - Build images from Dockerfile
docker builder - Manage build cache and builders
docker buildx - Extended build capabilities
docker buildx bake - Build multiple images
docker buildx create - Create builder instances
docker buildx imagetools - Manage multi-arch images
🎯 Django Focus: This documentation shows practical examples of building containerized Django applications using the anihpj project with its jobpost app models (Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost). Learn how to create optimized Docker images, manage build contexts, and implement multi-stage builds for Django production deployments.

docker build

Official Docs: docker build | Docker CLI: docker build

Build Docker images from a Dockerfile and a "context" - a set of files at a specified location.

Full Description

The docker build command constructs Docker images by reading instructions from a Dockerfile. For your anihpj Django project, this command processes your Dockerfile to create container images that include your Django application code, Python dependencies from requirements.txt, static files, database migrations, and all necessary runtime configurations. The build process creates layered images where each Dockerfile instruction creates a new layer, enabling efficient caching and faster subsequent builds.

When building your anihpj Django application, Docker build reads the build context (typically your project directory), copies relevant files into the container, installs Python packages, configures Django settings, runs collectstatic for production deployments, and creates the final image. The resulting image can be used to run containers that serve your jobpost app APIs, handle user authentication, manage author profiles, process location-based queries, and execute JobPost operations.

Syntax

# Basic build from current directory
docker build .

# Build with custom tag
docker build -t {image-name}:{tag} .

# Build with Dockerfile in different location
docker build -f {dockerfile-path} {context-path}

# Build with build arguments
docker build --build-arg {ARG_NAME}={value} -t {image-name} .

# Build with target stage (multi-stage builds)
docker build --target {stage-name} -t {image-name} .

# Build with custom platform
docker build --platform {platform} -t {image-name} .

# Build with progress output
docker build --progress=plain -t {image-name} .

# Build without cache
docker build --no-cache -t {image-name} .

# Build with cache from another image
docker build --cache-from {image-name} -t {new-image} .

Parameters Description

  • context-path: The build context path (usually current directory .). Contains Dockerfile, source code, and files needed for the build. For Django projects, this includes manage.py, apps, requirements.txt, static files, and templates.
  • -t, --tag: Name and optionally tag for the image in format name:tag. Use descriptive tags like anihpj/jobpost-app:v1.0.0 or anihpj/django-dev:latest for different environments and versions.
  • -f, --file: Path to the Dockerfile (default is {context}/Dockerfile). Useful when you have multiple Dockerfiles like Dockerfile.dev, Dockerfile.prod, or Dockerfile.test for different Django deployment scenarios.
  • --build-arg: Set build-time variables that can be used in Dockerfile with ARG instruction. Common Django args include DJANGO_SETTINGS_MODULE, DEBUG, SECRET_KEY, DATABASE_URL for environment-specific builds.
  • --target: Set target build stage for multi-stage builds. Useful for Django apps with separate development, testing, and production stages in a single Dockerfile.
  • --platform: Set platform if server is multi-platform capable. Specify platforms like linux/amd64, linux/arm64 for Django deployments across different architectures.
  • --progress: Set type of progress output (auto, plain, tty). Use 'plain' for CI/CD pipelines building Django images to get detailed build logs.
  • --no-cache: Do not use cache when building the image. Forces complete rebuild of Django application, useful when you want to ensure latest dependencies or clean build environment.
  • --cache-from: Images to consider as cache sources. Speeds up Django image builds by reusing layers from previous builds or base images.
  • --pull: Always attempt to pull newer version of base image. Ensures your Django image uses latest security updates from base Python images.
  • --quiet, -q: Suppress build output and print image ID on success. Useful for automated Django deployment scripts.
  • --rm: Remove intermediate containers after successful build (default true). Keeps Docker environment clean during Django image builds.
  • --force-rm: Always remove intermediate containers even if build fails. Prevents accumulation of failed Django build containers.

Attributes

  • Image ID: Unique identifier for the built image (SHA256 hash). Used to reference the specific Django image version.
  • Build Context Size: Size of files sent to Docker daemon. Monitor this for Django projects to optimize build performance.
  • Total Build Time: Time taken for complete build process. Track this metric for Django application build optimization.
  • Layer Count: Number of layers in the final image. Each Dockerfile instruction creates a layer affecting Django image size and startup time.
  • Cache Status: Indicates which layers were cached vs rebuilt. Helps optimize Django build times through effective layer caching.

Methods

Build with Build Arguments

Syntax: docker build --build-arg {ARG}={VALUE} -t {image} {context}

Parameters: ARG (string) - Build argument name defined in Dockerfile with ARG instruction, VALUE (string) - Value to pass for the argument

Pass build-time variables to customize Django application builds for different environments, database configurations, or feature flags.

Multi-stage Build Target

Syntax: docker build --target {stage} -t {image} {context}

Parameters: stage (string) - Name of the build stage from Dockerfile FROM instruction with AS alias

Build specific stages of multi-stage Dockerfiles, useful for creating separate Django development, testing, and production images from single Dockerfile.

Cross-platform Build

Syntax: docker build --platform {platform} -t {image} {context}

Parameters: platform (string) - Target platform in format os/architecture like linux/amd64, linux/arm64

Build Django images for specific platforms, essential for deploying to different cloud providers or ARM-based servers.

Return Value

Returns the image ID of successfully built Docker image. On failure, returns non-zero exit code with error details. Build output includes layer information, cache status, and final image summary.

  • Success (0): Image built successfully, returns image ID
  • Error (1): Build failed due to Dockerfile syntax errors, missing files, or build context issues
  • Error (125): Docker daemon error or invalid command usage

5 Practical Django Examples

Example 1: Basic Django Application Build (Skills Model API)

Build a basic Django container image for the Skills model API service with production optimizations and proper layer caching.

Docker Context Setup:

# Create Dockerfile for Skills API service
cat > Dockerfile.skills << 'EOF'
FROM python:3.11-slim

# Set working directory
WORKDIR /app

# Install system dependencies for Django and PostgreSQL
RUN apt-get update && apt-get install -y \
    build-essential \
    libpq-dev \
    && rm -rf /var/lib/apt/lists/*

# Copy requirements first for better caching
COPY requirements.txt .
RUN pip install --no-cache-dir -r requirements.txt

# Copy Django project files
COPY . .

# Set Django environment variables
ENV DJANGO_SETTINGS_MODULE=anihpj.settings
ENV PYTHONPATH=/app
ENV DJANGO_APP=jobpost

# Run Django setup commands
RUN python manage.py collectstatic --noinput

# Expose port for Skills API
EXPOSE 8000

# Start Skills API server
CMD ["python", "manage.py", "runserver", "0.0.0.0:8000"]
EOF
# Build Skills API image with proper tagging
docker build -f Dockerfile.skills -t anihpj/skills-api:v1.0.0 .
Sending build context to Docker daemon 45.2MB Step 1/10 : FROM python:3.11-slim 3.11-slim: Pulling from library/python ... Status: Downloaded newer image for python:3.11-slim ---> 2b5b8b6f9b8c Step 2/10 : WORKDIR /app ---> Running in 4d5e6f7g8h9i Removing intermediate container 4d5e6f7g8h9i ---> 1a2b3c4d5e6f Step 3/10 : RUN apt-get update && apt-get install -y ... ---> Running in 7h8i9j0k1l2m ... ---> 9k8j7i6h5g4f Step 4/10 : COPY requirements.txt . ---> 3f2e1d0c9b8a Step 5/10 : RUN pip install --no-cache-dir -r requirements.txt ---> Running in 2m1l0k9j8i7h Collecting Django==6.0 Collecting psycopg2-binary==2.9.7 ... Successfully installed Django-6.0 psycopg2-binary-2.9.7 ... ---> 5a4b3c2d1e0f Step 6/10 : COPY . . ---> 7c6b5a4d3e2f Step 7/10 : ENV DJANGO_SETTINGS_MODULE=anihpj.settings ---> Running in 8d7c6b5a4f3e ---> 9e8d7c6b5a4f Step 8/10 : RUN python manage.py collectstatic --noinput ---> Running in 0f9e8d7c6b5a 128 static files copied to '/app/static'. ---> 1g0f9e8d7c6b Step 9/10 : EXPOSE 8000 ---> Running in 2h1g0f9e8d7c ---> 3i2h1g0f9e8d Step 10/10 : CMD ["python", "manage.py", "runserver", "0.0.0.0:8000"] ---> Running in 4j3i2h1g0f9e ---> 5k4j3i2h1g0f Successfully built 5k4j3i2h1g0f Successfully tagged anihpj/skills-api:v1.0.0

Test the built image:

# Run container to test Skills API functionality
docker run -d -p 8000:8000 --name skills-api-test anihpj/skills-api:v1.0.0

# Test Skills model API endpoints
curl http://localhost:8000/jobpost/skills/
[ { "id": 1, "name": "Python", "description": "Python programming language", "category": "Programming", "level": "Advanced", "created_at": "2026-01-11T10:30:00Z" }, { "id": 2, "name": "Django", "description": "Django web framework", "category": "Framework", "level": "Expert", "created_at": "2026-01-11T10:31:00Z" }, { "id": 3, "name": "PostgreSQL", "description": "PostgreSQL database management", "category": "Database", "level": "Intermediate", "created_at": "2026-01-11T10:32:00Z" } ]

Django Model Query Output:

# Execute in Django shell within container
docker exec -it skills-api-test python manage.py shell

# Skills model operations
from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost

# Query Skills objects
skills_queryset = Skills.objects.all()
print("Skills QuerySet:", skills_queryset)
print("Skills Count:", skills_queryset.count())

# Show Skills objects with details
for skill in skills_queryset:
    print(f"Skill: {skill.name} - {skill.description} ({skill.level})")
Skills QuerySet: , , , , ]> Skills Count: 5 Skill: Python - Python programming language (Advanced) Skill: Django - Django web framework (Expert) Skill: PostgreSQL - PostgreSQL database management (Intermediate) Skill: Docker - Container platform (Intermediate) Skill: Kubernetes - Container orchestration (Beginner)
Example 2: Multi-stage Build for User Authentication Service

Create a multi-stage Docker build that separates development dependencies from production deployment for the User authentication service, optimizing final image size.

Multi-stage Dockerfile Setup:

# Create multi-stage Dockerfile for User auth service
cat > Dockerfile.user-auth << 'EOF'
# Development stage with all tools
FROM python:3.11 as development
WORKDIR /app
COPY requirements-dev.txt .
RUN pip install -r requirements-dev.txt
COPY . .
RUN python manage.py test

# Production stage with minimal dependencies
FROM python:3.11-slim as production
WORKDIR /app

# Install only runtime system dependencies
RUN apt-get update && apt-get install -y \
    libpq-dev \
    && rm -rf /var/lib/apt/lists/* \
    && apt-get clean

# Create non-root user for security
RUN groupadd -r django && useradd -r -g django django

# Copy only production requirements
COPY requirements.txt .
RUN pip install --no-cache-dir -r requirements.txt

# Copy application code
COPY --chown=django:django . .

# Run Django production setup
RUN python manage.py collectstatic --noinput
RUN python manage.py compilemessages

# Switch to non-root user
USER django

EXPOSE 8001

CMD ["gunicorn", "--bind", "0.0.0.0:8001", "anihpj.wsgi:application"]
EOF
# Build production target for User authentication service
docker build -f Dockerfile.user-auth --target production -t anihpj/user-auth:production .
Sending build context to Docker daemon 52.8MB Step 1/15 : FROM python:3.11 as development ---> 2a3b4c5d6e7f Step 2/15 : WORKDIR /app ---> Using cache ---> 8g7f6e5d4c3b Step 3/15 : COPY requirements-dev.txt . ---> 9h8g7f6e5d4c Step 4/15 : RUN pip install -r requirements-dev.txt ---> Running in 0i9h8g7f6e5d Collecting Django==6.0 Collecting pytest==7.4.2 Collecting coverage==7.3.1 ... ---> 1j0i9h8g7f6e Step 5/15 : COPY . . ---> 2k1j0i9h8g7f Step 6/15 : RUN python manage.py test ---> Running in 3l2k1j0i9h8g System check identified no issues (0 silenced). .................... Ran 20 tests in 2.456s OK ---> 4m3l2k1j0i9h Step 7/15 : FROM python:3.11-slim as production 3.11-slim: Pulling from library/python ... ---> 5n4m3l2k1j0i Step 8/15 : WORKDIR /app ---> Running in 6o5n4m3l2k1j ---> 7p6o5n4m3l2k Step 9/15 : RUN apt-get update && apt-get install -y ... ---> Running in 8q7p6o5n4m3l ---> 9r8q7p6o5n4m Step 10/15 : RUN groupadd -r django && useradd -r -g django django ---> Running in 0s9r8q7p6o5n ---> 1t0s9r8q7p6o Step 11/15 : COPY requirements.txt . ---> 2u1t0s9r8q7p Step 12/15 : RUN pip install --no-cache-dir -r requirements.txt ---> Running in 3v2u1t0s9r8q ---> 4w3v2u1t0s9r Step 13/15 : COPY --chown=django:django . . ---> 5x4w3v2u1t0s Step 14/15 : RUN python manage.py collectstatic --noinput ---> Running in 6y5x4w3v2u1t 128 static files copied to '/app/static'. ---> 7z6y5x4w3v2u Step 15/15 : USER django ---> Running in 8a7z6y5x4w3v ---> 9b8a7z6y5x4w Successfully built 9b8a7z6y5x4w Successfully tagged anihpj/user-auth:production

Test User authentication functionality:

# Run User authentication service
docker run -d -p 8001:8001 --name user-auth-prod anihpj/user-auth:production

# Test User authentication endpoints
curl -X POST http://localhost:8001/api/auth/login/ \
     -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
     -d '{"username": "testuser", "password": "testpass123"}'
{ "access_token": "eyJ0eXAiOiJKV1QiLCJhbGciOiJIUzI1NiJ9...", "refresh_token": "eyJ0eXAiOiJKV1QiLCJhbGciOiJIUzI1NiJ9...", "user": { "id": 1, "username": "testuser", "email": "test@example.com", "first_name": "Test", "last_name": "User", "date_joined": "2026-01-11T10:35:00Z" } }

User Model Query Operations:

# Execute User model queries in container
docker exec -it user-auth-prod python manage.py shell

# User and Author model operations  
from django.contrib.auth.models import User
from jobpost.models import Author, Skills, Location, JobPost

# Query User objects with related Author profiles
users_with_authors = User.objects.select_related('author').all()
print("Users with Authors:", users_with_authors)

# Create relationships between models
for user in users_with_authors[:3]:
    print(f"User: {user.username} - Author: {getattr(user, 'author', None)}")
Users with Authors: , , , , ]> User: admin - Author: AdminAuthor User: testuser - Author: TestAuthor User: author1 - Author: TechAuthor User: author2 - Author: DesignAuthor User: john_doe - Author: None
Example 3: Build with Environment-specific Arguments (Author Content Management)

Build Author content management service with build arguments for different deployment environments, enabling flexible configuration for development, staging, and production.

Parameterized Dockerfile:

# Create Dockerfile with build arguments for Author service
cat > Dockerfile.author << 'EOF'
FROM python:3.11-slim

# Define build arguments with defaults
ARG DJANGO_ENV=production
ARG DEBUG=False
ARG MEDIA_ROOT=/app/media
ARG ALLOWED_HOSTS=localhost
ARG DATABASE_URL=postgresql://localhost/anihpj

WORKDIR /app

# Install system dependencies based on environment
RUN apt-get update && apt-get install -y \
    libpq-dev \
    $(if [ "$DJANGO_ENV" = "development" ]; then echo "vim curl"; fi) \
    && rm -rf /var/lib/apt/lists/*

# Copy and install Python dependencies
COPY requirements.txt .
RUN pip install --no-cache-dir -r requirements.txt

# Install dev dependencies in development environment
RUN if [ "$DJANGO_ENV" = "development" ]; then \
        pip install --no-cache-dir -r requirements-dev.txt; \
    fi

# Copy application code
COPY . .

# Set environment variables from build args
ENV DJANGO_ENV=$DJANGO_ENV
ENV DEBUG=$DEBUG
ENV MEDIA_ROOT=$MEDIA_ROOT
ENV ALLOWED_HOSTS=$ALLOWED_HOSTS
ENV DATABASE_URL=$DATABASE_URL

# Create media directory for Author uploads
RUN mkdir -p $MEDIA_ROOT && chmod 755 $MEDIA_ROOT

# Collect static files
RUN python manage.py collectstatic --noinput

EXPOSE 8002

# Use different commands based on environment
CMD if [ "$DJANGO_ENV" = "development" ]; then \
        python manage.py runserver 0.0.0.0:8002; \
    else \
        gunicorn --bind 0.0.0.0:8002 anihpj.wsgi:application; \
    fi
EOF
# Build Author service for development environment
docker build -f Dockerfile.author \
    --build-arg DJANGO_ENV=development \
    --build-arg DEBUG=True \
    --build-arg ALLOWED_HOSTS="localhost,127.0.0.1,0.0.0.0" \
    -t anihpj/author-service:dev .
Sending build context to Docker daemon 48.3MB Step 1/12 : FROM python:3.11-slim ---> 1a2b3c4d5e6f Step 2/12 : ARG DJANGO_ENV=production ---> Running in 2b3c4d5e6f7g ---> 3c4d5e6f7g8h Step 3/12 : ARG DEBUG=False ---> Running in 4d5e6f7g8h9i ---> 5e6f7g8h9i0j [Build args processed: DJANGO_ENV=development, DEBUG=True] Step 4/12 : WORKDIR /app ---> Running in 6f7g8h9i0j1k ---> 7g8h9i0j1k2l Step 5/12 : RUN apt-get update && apt-get install -y ... ---> Running in 8h9i0j1k2l3m [Installing development tools: vim, curl] ---> 9i0j1k2l3m4n Step 6/12 : COPY requirements.txt . ---> 0j1k2l3m4n5o Step 7/12 : RUN pip install --no-cache-dir -r requirements.txt ---> Running in 1k2l3m4n5o6p ---> 2l3m4n5o6p7q Step 8/12 : RUN if [ "$DJANGO_ENV" = "development" ]; then ... ---> Running in 3m4n5o6p7q8r [Installing development dependencies] ---> 4n5o6p7q8r9s Step 9/12 : COPY . . ---> 5o6p7q8r9s0t Step 10/12 : ENV DJANGO_ENV=development ---> Running in 6p7q8r9s0t1u ---> 7q8r9s0t1u2v [Environment configured for development] Successfully built 8r9s0t1u2v3w Successfully tagged anihpj/author-service:dev

Test Author content management:

# Run Author service in development mode
docker run -d -p 8002:8002 -v $(pwd)/media:/app/media --name author-dev anihpj/author-service:dev

# Test Author profile creation
curl -X POST http://localhost:8002/api/authors/ \
     -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
     -d '{"username": "content_creator", "email": "creator@example.com", "bio": "Content specialist"}'
{ "id": 1, "username": "content_creator", "email": "creator@example.com", "bio": "Content specialist", "profile_picture": null, "website": null, "location": null, "skills": [], "created_at": "2026-01-11T10:45:00Z", "updated_at": "2026-01-11T10:45:00Z" }

Author and Skills Model Relationships:

# Query Author model with related Skills
docker exec -it author-dev python manage.py shell

from jobpost.models import Author, Skills, User, Location, JobPost

# Create Authors with Skills relationships
authors = Author.objects.prefetch_related('skills').all()
print("Authors QuerySet:", authors)

# Show Author-Skills relationships
for author in authors:
    skills_list = list(author.skills.all())
    print(f"Author: {author.username} - Skills: {[s.name for s in skills_list]}")
    
# Count objects across models
print("Total Authors:", Author.objects.count())
print("Total Skills:", Skills.objects.count())
Authors QuerySet: , , , ]> Author: content_creator - Skills: ['Writing', 'Content Strategy', 'SEO'] Author: tech_writer - Skills: ['Python', 'Django', 'Technical Writing'] Author: design_expert - Skills: ['UI/UX Design', 'Figma', 'Design Systems'] Author: data_scientist - Skills: ['Python', 'Machine Learning', 'Statistics'] Total Authors: 4 Total Skills: 8
Example 4: Cross-platform Build for Location Service

Build Location service Docker image for multiple architectures (AMD64 and ARM64) to support deployment across different cloud providers and development environments.

Platform-aware Dockerfile:

# Create Dockerfile with platform-specific optimizations
cat > Dockerfile.location << 'EOF'
FROM python:3.11-slim

# Use build platform for architecture-specific optimizations
ARG TARGETPLATFORM
ARG BUILDPLATFORM

WORKDIR /app

# Install platform-specific dependencies
RUN echo "Building for $TARGETPLATFORM on $BUILDPLATFORM" && \
    apt-get update && apt-get install -y \
    libpq-dev \
    $(if [ "$TARGETPLATFORM" = "linux/arm64" ]; then echo "gcc-aarch64-linux-gnu"; fi) \
    && rm -rf /var/lib/apt/lists/*

# Copy requirements and install Python packages
COPY requirements-geo.txt .
RUN pip install --no-cache-dir -r requirements-geo.txt

# Install platform-specific geo libraries
RUN if [ "$TARGETPLATFORM" = "linux/amd64" ]; then \
        pip install --no-cache-dir geopy[speedups]; \
    else \
        pip install --no-cache-dir geopy; \
    fi

# Copy application code
COPY . .

# Set environment for Location service
ENV DJANGO_APP=location
ENV GEO_SERVICE=enabled
ENV CACHE_BACKEND=redis

# Setup static files and geo data
RUN python manage.py collectstatic --noinput
RUN python manage.py setup_geo_data

EXPOSE 8003

CMD ["gunicorn", "--bind", "0.0.0.0:8003", "--workers", "4", "anihpj.wsgi:application"]
EOF
# Build Location service for multiple platforms
docker build -f Dockerfile.location --platform linux/amd64 -t anihpj/location-service:amd64 .
docker build -f Dockerfile.location --platform linux/arm64 -t anihpj/location-service:arm64 .
Building for linux/amd64: Sending build context to Docker daemon 55.1MB Step 1/11 : FROM python:3.11-slim ---> 1a2b3c4d5e6f (linux/amd64) Step 2/11 : ARG TARGETPLATFORM ---> Running in 2b3c4d5e6f7g ---> 3c4d5e6f7g8h Step 3/11 : WORKDIR /app ---> Running in 4d5e6f7g8h9i ---> 5e6f7g8h9i0j Step 4/11 : RUN echo "Building for linux/amd64 on linux/amd64" Building for linux/amd64 on linux/amd64 ---> 6f7g8h9i0j1k [Installing AMD64 optimized geo libraries] Successfully built 7g8h9i0j1k2l Successfully tagged anihpj/location-service:amd64 Building for linux/arm64: Step 1/11 : FROM python:3.11-slim ---> 8h9i0j1k2l3m (linux/arm64) [Cross-compilation for ARM64 architecture] Building for linux/arm64 on linux/amd64 Successfully built 9i0j1k2l3m4n Successfully tagged anihpj/location-service:arm64

Test Location service APIs:

# Run Location service (platform automatically detected)
docker run -d -p 8003:8003 --name location-api anihpj/location-service:amd64

# Test location-based queries
curl "http://localhost:8003/api/locations/search/?city=San Francisco"
{ "results": [ { "id": 1, "name": "San Francisco", "state": "California", "country": "United States", "latitude": 37.7749, "longitude": -122.4194, "timezone": "America/Los_Angeles", "job_count": 245, "created_at": "2026-01-11T10:50:00Z" }, { "id": 2, "name": "San Francisco Bay Area", "state": "California", "country": "United States", "latitude": 37.8272, "longitude": -122.2913, "timezone": "America/Los_Angeles", "job_count": 892, "created_at": "2026-01-11T10:51:00Z" } ], "count": 2, "total_jobs": 1137 }

Location Model Geo Queries:

# Test Location model geographic capabilities
docker exec -it location-api python manage.py shell

from jobpost.models import Location, JobPost, Author, Skills, User
from django.contrib.gis.measure import Distance
from django.contrib.gis.geos import Point

# Query locations near coordinates
sf_point = Point(-122.4194, 37.7749, srid=4326)
nearby_locations = Location.objects.filter(
    point__distance_lte=(sf_point, Distance(mi=50))
)
print("Nearby Locations:", nearby_locations)

# Query JobPosts by location
for location in nearby_locations:
    job_count = JobPost.objects.filter(location=location).count()
    print(f"Location: {location.name} - Jobs: {job_count}")
Nearby Locations: , , , ]> Location: San Francisco - Jobs: 245 Location: Oakland - Jobs: 156 Location: San Jose - Jobs: 189 Location: Palo Alto - Jobs: 78
Example 5: Optimized Build with Cache Layers (JobPost Processing Service)

Build JobPost processing service with advanced caching strategies, layer optimization, and build context minimization for fast iterative development and CI/CD pipelines.

Optimized Dockerfile with cache layers:

# Create .dockerignore for optimal build context
cat > .dockerignore << 'EOF'
.git
.gitignore
README.md
.pytest_cache
.coverage
*.pyc
__pycache__
.venv
node_modules
.env.local
.env.development
.env.test
media/temp
logs/*.log
EOF

# Create optimized Dockerfile for JobPost service
cat > Dockerfile.jobpost << 'EOF'
FROM python:3.11-slim as base

# Create app user for security
RUN groupadd -r jobpost && useradd -r -g jobpost jobpost

# Install system dependencies (cached layer)
RUN apt-get update && apt-get install -y \
    libpq-dev \
    && rm -rf /var/lib/apt/lists/* \
    && apt-get clean

FROM base as dependencies

WORKDIR /app

# Copy only requirements files for caching
COPY requirements.txt requirements-celery.txt ./

# Install Python dependencies (cached layer)
RUN pip install --no-cache-dir --upgrade pip && \
    pip install --no-cache-dir -r requirements.txt && \
    pip install --no-cache-dir -r requirements-celery.txt

FROM dependencies as application

# Copy application code (frequently changing layer)
COPY --chown=jobpost:jobpost . .

# Django setup commands
RUN python manage.py collectstatic --noinput
RUN python manage.py check --deploy

# Create necessary directories
RUN mkdir -p /app/logs /app/media/jobpost && \
    chown -R jobpost:jobpost /app/logs /app/media

USER jobpost

EXPOSE 8004

# Health check
HEALTHCHECK --interval=30s --timeout=10s --start-period=5s --retries=3 \
    CMD curl -f http://localhost:8004/health/ || exit 1

CMD ["celery", "worker", "-A", "anihpj", "--loglevel=info"]
EOF
# Build with cache optimization and progress output
docker build -f Dockerfile.jobpost \
    --cache-from anihpj/jobpost-base:latest \
    --progress=plain \
    -t anihpj/jobpost-processor:v2.0.0 .
BUILDKIT_PROGRESS=plain #1 [internal] load build definition from Dockerfile.jobpost #1 sha256:8f7e9d2a4b1c... 0.1s done #1 transferring dockerfile: 1.24kB 0.1s done #2 [internal] load .dockerignore #2 sha256:2b5c8e7f1a9d... 0.1s done #2 transferring context: 157B 0.1s done #3 [internal] load build context #3 sha256:4e1a7c9b8f2d... 2.3s done #3 transferring context: 12.5MB 2.3s done #4 [base 1/2] FROM python:3.11-slim #4 CACHED #5 [base 2/2] RUN groupadd -r jobpost && useradd -r -g jobpost jobpost #5 CACHED #6 [dependencies 1/3] WORKDIR /app #6 CACHED #7 [dependencies 2/3] COPY requirements.txt requirements-celery.txt ./ #7 sha256:7f8e9c4b2a1d... 0.2s done #8 [dependencies 3/3] RUN pip install --no-cache-dir --upgrade pip #8 sha256:3c7f2e8a9b4d... 45.3s done #9 [application 1/5] COPY --chown=jobpost:jobpost . . #9 sha256:9e2f7c1a8b4d... 1.8s done #10 [application 2/5] RUN python manage.py collectstatic --noinput #10 sha256:5a9e2c7f1b4d... 8.2s done #10 128 static files copied to '/app/static'. #11 [application 3/5] RUN python manage.py check --deploy #11 sha256:8c4f7e2a9b1d... 2.1s done #11 System check identified no issues (0 silenced). #12 [application 4/5] RUN mkdir -p /app/logs /app/media/jobpost #12 sha256:2e8c7f1a4b9d... 0.3s done #13 [application 5/5] USER jobpost #13 sha256:7a4e8c2f9b1d... 0.1s done #14 exporting to image #14 sha256:1f8e7c4a9b2d... 2.1s done Successfully built and tagged anihpj/jobpost-processor:v2.0.0

Run JobPost processing service:

# Start JobPost processor with Redis backend
docker network create anihpj-network
docker run -d --name redis --network anihpj-network redis:alpine
docker run -d --name jobpost-processor --network anihpj-network \
    -e CELERY_BROKER_URL=redis://redis:6379/0 \
    anihpj/jobpost-processor:v2.0.0

# Monitor JobPost processing
docker logs -f jobpost-processor
[2026-01-11 11:00:00,000: INFO/MainProcess] Connected to redis://redis:6379/0 [2026-01-11 11:00:00,010: INFO/MainProcess] mingle: searching for neighbor nodes... [2026-01-11 11:00:01,020: INFO/MainProcess] mingle: all alone [2026-01-11 11:00:01,035: INFO/MainProcess] celery@jobpost-processor ready. [2026-01-11 11:00:05,123: INFO/MainProcess] Received task: jobpost.tasks.process_job_posting[abc123] [2026-01-11 11:00:05,456: INFO/ForkPoolWorker-1] Job posting processed: Senior Python Developer [2026-01-11 11:00:06,789: INFO/ForkPoolWorker-1] Task jobpost.tasks.process_job_posting[abc123] succeeded

JobPost Model Complex Queries:

# Execute complex JobPost queries in container
docker exec -it jobpost-processor python manage.py shell

from jobpost.models import JobPost, Author, Skills, Location, User
from django.db.models import Count, Q, Prefetch

# Complex JobPost queries with relationships
complex_queryset = JobPost.objects.select_related('author', 'location').prefetch_related(
    Prefetch('skills', queryset=Skills.objects.filter(level__in=['Advanced', 'Expert']))
).filter(
    Q(location__name__icontains='San Francisco') | Q(remote_friendly=True)
).annotate(
    skills_count=Count('skills')
).order_by('-created_at')

print("Complex JobPost QuerySet:", complex_queryset)

# Analyze JobPost distribution
for job in complex_queryset[:3]:
    print(f"Job: {job.title}")
    print(f"  Author: {job.author.username}")
    print(f"  Location: {job.location.name}")
    print(f"  Skills: {[s.name for s in job.skills.all()]}")
    print(f"  Skills Count: {job.skills_count}")
    print("---")
Complex JobPost QuerySet: , , , ]> Job: Senior Python Developer Author: tech_recruiter Location: San Francisco Skills: ['Python', 'Django', 'PostgreSQL', 'Docker'] Skills Count: 4 --- Job: Django Full-Stack Engineer Author: startup_founder Location: San Francisco Bay Area Skills: ['Python', 'Django', 'React', 'AWS'] Skills Count: 4 --- Job: Remote DevOps Engineer Author: remote_company Location: Remote Skills: ['Docker', 'Kubernetes', 'AWS', 'Terraform'] Skills Count: 4 ---

docker builder

Official Docs: docker builder | Docker CLI: docker builder

Manage the builder environment for Docker builds, including cache management and builder instance configuration.

Full Description

The docker builder command manages Docker build environments, providing advanced build cache management and builder instance configuration. For your anihpj Django project, this command helps optimize build performance by managing build cache, configuring build environments, and cleaning up unnecessary build artifacts. The builder system enables faster iterative development by reusing cached layers from previous Django application builds.

Builder management is crucial for Django development workflows where you frequently rebuild images during development cycles. Proper cache management ensures that your jobpost app builds leverage cached layers for unchanged dependencies, static files, and Django setup commands, significantly reducing build times for Skills, User, Author, Location, and JobPost service containers.

Syntax

# Show builder disk usage
docker builder prune

# Remove build cache (interactive)
docker builder prune

# Remove all unused build cache without prompt
docker builder prune -f

# Remove build cache with specific filters
docker builder prune --filter until=24h

# Show builder storage usage
docker system df

# Remove build cache older than specified time
docker builder prune --filter until=72h --force

Parameters Description

  • prune: Remove unused build cache to free up disk space. Essential for Django development environments where frequent builds can accumulate significant cache data from dependency installations, static file collection, and application layer builds.
  • -f, --force: Do not prompt for confirmation before removing cache. Useful in automated Django CI/CD pipelines where interactive prompts would halt the build process.
  • --filter: Provide filter values to selectively remove cache entries. Common filters include 'until' for time-based cleanup, 'label' for labeled cache entries, and 'type' for specific cache types in Django build workflows.
  • --all, -a: Remove all unused cache, not just dangling cache. More aggressive cleanup that removes all build cache including partially used cache from Django application layers.
  • --keep-storage: Amount of disk space to keep for cache. Specify in bytes, KB, MB, or GB to maintain optimal cache size for Django project builds without exhausting disk space.

Attributes

  • Cache Size: Total disk space consumed by build cache. Monitor this to optimize Django build performance while managing storage resources.
  • Reclaimable Space: Amount of space that can be freed by pruning unused cache. Important metric for maintaining efficient Django development environments.
  • Cache Entries: Number of cached build layers stored. Each Django build instruction creates cache entries that can be reused in subsequent builds.
  • Last Used: Timestamp when cache entries were last accessed. Helps identify stale cache from Django builds that can be safely removed.

Methods

Prune with Time Filter

Syntax: docker builder prune --filter until={time}

Parameters: time (duration) - Time duration like 24h, 7d, 1w for removing cache older than specified time

Remove Django build cache older than specified duration to maintain recent cache while freeing space from outdated dependency and application builds.

Forced Cache Cleanup

Syntax: docker builder prune --all --force

Parameters: --all (boolean) - Remove all cache including active cache, --force (boolean) - Skip confirmation prompt

Aggressively clean all build cache without prompts, useful for Django development environment reset or CI/CD pipeline cleanup stages.

Return Value

Returns information about cache cleanup operations including total space reclaimed, number of cache entries removed, and remaining cache statistics. Success operations return zero exit code, while errors return non-zero codes with descriptive messages.

  • Success (0): Cache cleanup completed successfully
  • Error (1): General error in cache operations
  • Error (125): Docker daemon error or invalid command usage

5 Practical Django Examples

Example 1: Development Cache Management for Skills API

Manage build cache during active Skills API development, balancing performance and disk space usage for optimal Django development workflow.

# Check current builder disk usage before cleanup
docker system df
TYPE TOTAL ACTIVE SIZE RECLAIMABLE Images 12 5 2.1GB 1.2GB (57%) Containers 3 2 45MB 22MB (48%) Local Volumes 4 2 890MB 445MB (50%) Build Cache 25 0 1.5GB 1.5GB (100%)
# Clean up unused build cache from Skills API development
docker builder prune
WARNING! This will remove all unused build cache. Are you sure? [y/N] y Deleted build cache objects: - cache mount sha256:8a9b7c6d5e4f3a2b1c0d9e8f7a6b5c4d - cache mount sha256:3f2e1d0c9b8a7f6e5d4c3b2a1f0e9d8c - cache mount sha256:7e6d5c4b3a2f1e0d9c8b7a6f5e4d3c2b Total reclaimed: 1.5GB

Verify Skills API build performance after cache cleanup:

# Rebuild Skills API to test cache effectiveness
docker build -t anihpj/skills-api:clean-cache .

# Test Skills API functionality after rebuild
docker run -d -p 8000:8000 --name skills-clean anihpj/skills-api:clean-cache
Sending build context to Docker daemon 45.2MB Step 1/10 : FROM python:3.11-slim 3.11-slim: Pulling from library/python (cache miss - expected after cleanup) Step 2/10 : WORKDIR /app ---> Running in abc123def456 Step 3/10 : COPY requirements.txt . ---> def456ghi789 Step 4/10 : RUN pip install --no-cache-dir -r requirements.txt ---> Running in ghi789jkl012 (rebuilding Python dependencies) Successfully built mno345pqr678 Successfully tagged anihpj/skills-api:clean-cache
# Test Skills model after cache cleanup
curl http://localhost:8000/jobpost/skills/
[ { "id": 1, "name": "Python", "description": "Python programming expertise", "category": "Programming", "level": "Expert", "demand_score": 95 }, { "id": 2, "name": "Django", "description": "Django web framework mastery", "category": "Framework", "level": "Advanced", "demand_score": 88 } ]

Skills Model Cache Impact Analysis:

# Analyze Skills model performance in clean environment
docker exec -it skills-clean python manage.py shell

from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost
from django.core.cache import cache
import time

# Test Skills query performance
start_time = time.time()
skills_qs = Skills.objects.all().order_by('name')
skills_list = list(skills_qs)
query_time = time.time() - start_time

print("Skills Query Performance:")
print(f"Total Skills: {len(skills_list)}")
print(f"Query Time: {query_time:.4f} seconds")
print("Skills Cache Status:", cache.get('skills_list_cache') is not None)
Skills Query Performance: Total Skills: 15 Query Time: 0.0234 seconds Skills Cache Status: False Skills QuerySet: , , , , , , , , , ]>
Example 2: Automated Cache Cleanup for User Authentication Service

Implement automated build cache management for User authentication service in CI/CD pipeline with time-based cleanup strategies.

# Create automated cleanup script for User auth service
cat > cleanup-user-auth-cache.sh << 'EOF'
#!/bin/bash
echo "=== User Auth Service Cache Cleanup ==="

# Show current cache status
echo "Current build cache usage:"
docker system df --format "table {{.Type}}\t{{.TotalCount}}\t{{.Size}}\t{{.Reclaimable}}"

# Clean cache older than 48 hours (development cycle)
echo "Cleaning cache older than 48 hours..."
docker builder prune --filter until=48h --force

# Clean all cache if usage exceeds 2GB
CACHE_SIZE=$(docker system df --format "{{.Size}}" | grep "Build Cache" | cut -d' ' -f1)
if [[ "$CACHE_SIZE" > "2GB" ]]; then
    echo "Cache size ($CACHE_SIZE) exceeds limit, performing full cleanup..."
    docker builder prune --all --force
fi

echo "Cache cleanup completed"
EOF

chmod +x cleanup-user-auth-cache.sh
# Execute automated cache cleanup
./cleanup-user-auth-cache.sh
=== User Auth Service Cache Cleanup === Current build cache usage: TYPE TOTALCOUNT SIZE RECLAIMABLE Images 8 1.8GB 890MB Containers 2 23MB 12MB Local Volumes 3 445MB 220MB Build Cache 18 2.3GB 2.1GB Cleaning cache older than 48 hours... Deleted build cache objects: - expired cache mount (48h+): sha256:abc123... - expired cache mount (48h+): sha256:def456... Total reclaimed: 850MB Cache size (2.3GB) exceeds limit, performing full cleanup... Total reclaimed: 2.1GB Cache cleanup completed
# Build User auth service after automated cleanup
docker build -f Dockerfile.user-auth -t anihpj/user-auth:post-cleanup .

# Test User authentication after cache reset
docker run -d -p 8001:8001 --name user-auth-clean anihpj/user-auth:post-cleanup
Sending build context to Docker daemon 52.8MB Step 1/12 : FROM python:3.11-slim ---> Using cache (base layer still cached) Step 2/12 : WORKDIR /app ---> Using cache Step 3/12 : RUN apt-get update && apt-get install -y... ---> Running in stu901vwx234 (system dependencies rebuilding) Step 4/12 : COPY requirements.txt . ---> yza567bcd890 Successfully built efg123hij456 Successfully tagged anihpj/user-auth:post-cleanup

User Authentication Test:

# Test User model functionality after cleanup
curl -X POST http://localhost:8001/api/auth/register/ \
     -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
     -d '{"username": "cleanuser", "email": "clean@example.com", "password": "securepass123"}'
{ "success": true, "user": { "id": 5, "username": "cleanuser", "email": "clean@example.com", "date_joined": "2026-01-11T11:30:00Z", "is_active": true }, "message": "User registered successfully" }

User Model Cache Recovery:

# Test User model operations after cache cleanup
docker exec -it user-auth-clean python manage.py shell

from django.contrib.auth.models import User
from jobpost.models import Author, Skills, Location, JobPost
from django.core.cache import cache

# User model performance after cache reset
users = User.objects.select_related().all()
print("Users after cache cleanup:", users.count())

# Cache warming for User-related queries
for user in users:
    if hasattr(user, 'author'):
        cache.set(f'user_profile_{user.id}', user.author, 3600)
        
print("Cache status after warming:", cache.get('user_profile_1') is not None)
Users after cache cleanup: 5 Cache status after warming: True User QuerySet: , , , , ]> Cache warming completed for User profiles
Example 3: Selective Cache Management for Author Content Service

Implement selective build cache management for Author content service using labels and filters to preserve critical cache while cleaning development artifacts.

# Create labeled Dockerfile for Author service cache management
cat > Dockerfile.author-labeled << 'EOF'
FROM python:3.11-slim

# Add cache labels for selective cleanup
LABEL cache.type="author-service"
LABEL cache.env="development" 
LABEL cache.version="v2.1"

WORKDIR /app

# Labeled requirements layer for cache retention
COPY requirements.txt .
RUN --mount=type=cache,target=/root/.cache/pip,sharing=shared \
    pip install --no-cache-dir -r requirements.txt

# Copy application with cache labels
COPY . .

# Author service specific setup with cache labels
RUN --mount=type=cache,target=/tmp/author-cache \
    python manage.py collectstatic --noinput && \
    python manage.py setup_author_data

EXPOSE 8002
CMD ["gunicorn", "--bind", "0.0.0.0:8002", "anihpj.wsgi:application"]
EOF
# Build Author service with cache labels
DOCKER_BUILDKIT=1 docker build -f Dockerfile.author-labeled -t anihpj/author-service:labeled .

# Show detailed cache usage with labels
docker builder prune --filter "label=cache.env=development" --dry-run
Sending build context to Docker daemon 48.3MB Step 1/8 : FROM python:3.11-slim ---> Using cache Step 2/8 : LABEL cache.type="author-service" ---> klm789nop012 Step 3/8 : WORKDIR /app ---> Using cache Step 4/8 : COPY requirements.txt . ---> qrs345tuv678 Step 5/8 : RUN --mount=type=cache... ---> Running in wxy901zab234 (using pip cache mount) Step 6/8 : COPY . . ---> cde567fgh890 Successfully built ijk123lmn456 WARNING: dry-run mode, would delete: - cache mount sha256:abc... (labeled cache.env=development) - cache mount sha256:def... (labeled cache.env=development) Total: 380MB would be reclaimed
# Selectively clean only development cache, preserve production
docker builder prune --filter "label=cache.env=development" --force

# Verify Author service functionality
docker run -d -p 8002:8002 --name author-selective anihpj/author-service:labeled
Deleted build cache objects: - cache mount sha256:abc123... (label: cache.env=development) - cache mount sha256:def456... (label: cache.env=development) Total reclaimed: 380MB (development cache only)

Author Content Management Test:

# Test Author content APIs after selective cleanup
curl -X GET http://localhost:8002/api/authors/profile/content_creator/
{ "id": 2, "username": "content_creator", "email": "creator@anihpj.com", "bio": "Specialized in technical content creation", "profile_picture": "/media/authors/creator_profile.jpg", "website": "https://techcontent.blog", "location": "Remote", "skills": ["Technical Writing", "Content Strategy", "SEO"], "published_posts": 47, "total_views": 125000, "created_at": "2026-01-11T09:15:00Z" }

Author Model Selective Cache Impact:

# Analyze Author model with selective cache management
docker exec -it author-selective python manage.py shell

from jobpost.models import Author, Skills, User, Location, JobPost
from django.core.cache import cache
from django.db.models import Prefetch

# Test Author queries with cache-aware operations
author_queryset = Author.objects.prefetch_related(
    Prefetch('skills', queryset=Skills.objects.filter(category='Content'))
).select_related('user')

print("Authors with Content Skills:")
for author in author_queryset:
    content_skills = [skill.name for skill in author.skills.all()]
    print(f"Author: {author.username} - Content Skills: {content_skills}")
    
# Cache status for Author-related data
print("Author cache preserved:", cache.get('author_skills_cache') is not None)
Authors with Content Skills: Author: content_creator - Content Skills: ['Technical Writing', 'Content Strategy', 'SEO'] Author: blog_author - Content Skills: ['Blogging', 'Social Media', 'Copywriting'] Author: tech_writer - Content Skills: ['Documentation', 'API Writing', 'Tutorial Creation'] Author cache preserved: True Author QuerySet: , , , ]>
Example 4: Cache Storage Limits for Location Service

Manage build cache storage limits for Location service to maintain optimal cache size while preventing disk space exhaustion in geographic data processing workflows.

# Monitor current cache usage before Location service builds
docker system df --format "table {{.Type}}\t{{.Size}}\t{{.Reclaimable}}"
TYPE SIZE RECLAIMABLE Images 2.1GB 1.2GB (57%) Containers 45MB 22MB (48%) Local Volumes 890MB 445MB (50%) Build Cache 3.2GB 2.8GB (87%)
# Set cache storage limit to 1GB and clean excess
docker builder prune --keep-storage 1GB --force

# Check cache reduction results
docker system df --format "{{.Type}}: {{.Size}} ({{.Reclaimable}} reclaimable)"
Deleted build cache objects to maintain 1GB limit: - cache mount sha256:ghi789... (size: 450MB) - cache mount sha256:jkl012... (size: 380MB) - cache mount sha256:mno345... (size: 290MB) - cache mount sha256:pqr678... (size: 220MB) Total reclaimed: 2.2GB (keeping 1GB as requested) Images: 2.1GB (1.2GB reclaimable) Containers: 45MB (22MB reclaimable) Local Volumes: 890MB (445MB reclaimable) Build Cache: 1.0GB (200MB reclaimable)
# Build Location service with managed cache limits
docker build -f Dockerfile.location -t anihpj/location-service:cached .

# Test Location service geographic capabilities
docker run -d -p 8003:8003 -v location_data:/app/geodata --name location-cached anihpj/location-service:cached
Sending build context to Docker daemon 55.1MB Step 1/10 : FROM python:3.11-slim ---> Using cache (within 1GB limit) Step 2/10 : WORKDIR /app ---> Using cache Step 3/10 : RUN apt-get update... ---> Running in abc123... (rebuilding due to cache limit) Step 4/10 : COPY requirements-geo.txt . ---> Using cache Successfully built xyz789abc123 Successfully tagged anihpj/location-service:cached

Location Service Geographic Queries:

# Test Location service after cache optimization
curl "http://localhost:8003/api/locations/nearby/?lat=37.7749&lng=-122.4194&radius=25"
{ "nearby_locations": [ { "id": 1, "name": "San Francisco", "latitude": 37.7749, "longitude": -122.4194, "distance_km": 0.0, "job_count": 245 }, { "id": 3, "name": "Oakland", "latitude": 37.8044, "longitude": -122.2711, "distance_km": 13.8, "job_count": 156 }, { "id": 5, "name": "Berkeley", "latitude": 37.8715, "longitude": -122.2730, "distance_km": 19.2, "job_count": 89 } ], "total_results": 3, "search_radius_km": 25 }

Location Model Cache-Optimized Queries:

# Test Location model with optimized cache usage
docker exec -it location-cached python manage.py shell

from jobpost.models import Location, JobPost, Author, Skills, User
from django.contrib.gis.geos import Point
from django.contrib.gis.measure import Distance

# Location queries optimized for cache-limited environment
sf_point = Point(-122.4194, 37.7749, srid=4326)
nearby_locs = Location.objects.filter(
    point__distance_lte=(sf_point, Distance(mi=25))
).prefetch_related('jobpost_set')

print("Cache-optimized Location queries:")
for location in nearby_locs:
    job_count = location.jobpost_set.count()
    print(f"Location: {location.name} - Jobs: {job_count}")
    
print("Total cached locations:", Location.objects.count())
Cache-optimized Location queries: Location: San Francisco - Jobs: 245 Location: Oakland - Jobs: 156 Location: Berkeley - Jobs: 89 Location: Palo Alto - Jobs: 78 Total cached locations: 25 Location QuerySet: , , , , ]>
Example 5: Advanced Cache Analytics for JobPost Processing

Implement comprehensive cache analytics and monitoring for JobPost processing service to optimize build performance and resource utilization across development lifecycle.

# Create cache analytics script for JobPost service
cat > analyze-jobpost-cache.sh << 'EOF'
#!/bin/bash
echo "=== JobPost Service Cache Analytics ==="

# Get detailed cache statistics
echo "Detailed Cache Analysis:"
docker system df --format "{{.Type}}: {{.TotalCount}} items, {{.Size}} total, {{.Reclaimable}} reclaimable"

# Show cache age distribution
echo -e "\nCache Age Analysis:"
docker builder prune --filter until=1h --dry-run 2>/dev/null | grep "Total:" || echo "No cache older than 1h"
docker builder prune --filter until=24h --dry-run 2>/dev/null | grep "Total:" || echo "No cache older than 24h"
docker builder prune --filter until=7d --dry-run 2>/dev/null | grep "Total:" || echo "No cache older than 7d"

# Cache efficiency metrics
echo -e "\nCache Efficiency Metrics:"
TOTAL_CACHE=$(docker system df --format "{{.Size}}" | grep -E "Build Cache" | head -1)
RECLAIMABLE=$(docker system df --format "{{.Reclaimable}}" | grep -E "Build Cache" | head -1)
echo "Total Cache: $TOTAL_CACHE"
echo "Reclaimable: $RECLAIMABLE"
echo "Cache Efficiency: $(echo "$TOTAL_CACHE $RECLAIMABLE" | awk '{if($1 > 0) print 100-(($2/$1)*100) "% active"}' || echo "N/A")"
EOF

chmod +x analyze-jobpost-cache.sh
# Run comprehensive cache analysis
./analyze-jobpost-cache.sh
=== JobPost Service Cache Analytics === Detailed Cache Analysis: Images: 15 items, 3.2GB total, 1.8GB reclaimable Containers: 8 items, 125MB total, 67MB reclaimable Local Volumes: 6 items, 1.2GB total, 580MB reclaimable Build Cache: 32 items, 2.8GB total, 2.1GB reclaimable Cache Age Analysis: Total: 450MB (cache older than 1h) Total: 1.2GB (cache older than 24h) Total: 2.1GB (cache older than 7d) Cache Efficiency Metrics: Total Cache: 2.8GB Reclaimable: 2.1GB Cache Efficiency: 25% active
# Implement smart cache cleanup based on analytics
docker builder prune --filter until=24h --force

# Build JobPost processor with optimized cache
docker build -f Dockerfile.jobpost --target production -t anihpj/jobpost-processor:analytics .

# Start JobPost service with monitoring
docker run -d -p 8004:8004 --name jobpost-analytics anihpj/jobpost-processor:analytics

(size: 320MB) Total reclaimed: 1.2GB (keeping recent cache) Sending build context to Docker daemon 62.4MB Step 1/15 : FROM python:3.11-slim as base ---> Using cache (recent build) Step 2/15 : WORKDIR /app ---> Using cache Successfully built opq456rst789 Successfully tagged anihpj/jobpost-processor:analytics

JobPost Processing Performance Test:

# Test JobPost processing after cache optimization
curl -X POST http://localhost:8004/api/jobposts/ \
     -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
     -d '{
       "title": "Senior Django Developer",
       "description": "Lead Django development team",
       "location_id": 1,
       "author_id": 2,
       "skills": [1, 2, 3],
       "salary_range": "120000-150000",
       "remote_friendly": true
     }'
{ "id": 15, "title": "Senior Django Developer", "description": "Lead Django development team", "slug": "senior-django-developer-15", "location": { "id": 1, "name": "San Francisco", "state": "California" }, "author": { "id": 2, "username": "tech_recruiter" }, "skills": [ {"id": 1, "name": "Python"}, {"id": 2, "name": "Django"}, {"id": 3, "name": "PostgreSQL"} ], "salary_range": "120000-150000", "remote_friendly": true, "status": "published", "created_at": "2026-01-11T12:15:00Z" }

JobPost Model Cache-Optimized Analytics:

# Comprehensive JobPost model analysis with cache optimization
docker exec -it jobpost-analytics python manage.py shell

from jobpost.models import JobPost, Author, Skills, Location, User
from django.db.models import Count, Q, Avg, Max
from django.core.cache import cache
import time

# Cache-aware complex JobPost analytics
start_time = time.time()
jobpost_analytics = JobPost.objects.select_related('author', 'location').prefetch_related('skills').aggregate(
    total_posts=Count('id'),
    avg_skills_per_job=Avg('skills__id'),
    max_salary=Max('salary_max'),
    remote_percentage=Count('id', filter=Q(remote_friendly=True)) * 100.0 / Count('id')
)
query_time = time.time() - start_time

print("JobPost Analytics (Cache-Optimized):")
print(f"Query Time: {query_time:.4f}s")
for key, value in jobpost_analytics.items():
    print(f"{key}: {value}")

# Model relationships analysis
print("\nModel Relationship Summary:")
print(f"Total JobPosts: {JobPost.objects.count()}")
print(f"Total Authors: {Author.objects.count()}")
print(f"Total Skills: {Skills.objects.count()}")
print(f"Total Locations: {Location.objects.count()}")
JobPost Analytics (Cache-Optimized): Query Time: 0.0156s total_posts: 15 avg_skills_per_job: 3.2 max_salary: 180000 remote_percentage: 73.33 Model Relationship Summary: Total JobPosts: 15 Total Authors: 8 Total Skills: 12 Total Locations: 6 JobPost QuerySet: , , , , ]>

docker buildx

Official Docs: docker buildx | Docker CLI: docker buildx

Extended build capabilities with support for multi-platform builds, advanced caching, and BuildKit features for modern container workflows.

Full Description

The docker buildx command provides extended build capabilities beyond the standard docker build functionality. For your anihpj Django project, buildx enables multi-platform container builds, advanced caching strategies, concurrent builds, and modern BuildKit features. This is essential when deploying Django applications across different architectures (AMD64, ARM64) or when building for multiple environments simultaneously.

Buildx is particularly valuable for Django development teams that need to build container images for different deployment targets - from local development on Apple Silicon Macs to production deployment on AMD64 cloud infrastructure. The command manages multiple builder instances, supports BuildKit's advanced features like cache mounts and secrets, and enables efficient CI/CD pipelines for your jobpost application across various platforms.

Syntax

# List available builders
docker buildx ls

# Create new builder instance
docker buildx create --name {builder-name} --driver {driver}

# Use specific builder
docker buildx use {builder-name}

# Build for multiple platforms
docker buildx build --platform linux/amd64,linux/arm64 -t {image-name} .

# Build with advanced features
docker buildx build --cache-from type=registry,ref={cache-image} --cache-to type=registry,ref={cache-image} .

# Inspect builder capabilities
docker buildx inspect {builder-name}

# Remove builder instance
docker buildx rm {builder-name}

# Bootstrap builder (ensure it's ready)
docker buildx inspect --bootstrap {builder-name}

Parameters Description

  • --platform: Target platforms for multi-architecture builds in format os/architecture. Specify multiple platforms comma-separated like linux/amd64,linux/arm64 for Django applications that need to run on different infrastructures.
  • --builder: Name of builder instance to use for the build. Different builders can have different configurations, drivers, and capabilities optimized for specific Django deployment scenarios.
  • --driver: Driver for the builder instance (docker, docker-container, kubernetes). Each driver provides different capabilities for Django build workflows and deployment environments.
  • --cache-from: External cache sources to import during build. Supports registry, local, gha (GitHub Actions), s3, and other cache backends to accelerate Django application builds.
  • --cache-to: External cache destinations to export after build. Enables cache sharing between Django development team members and CI/CD systems.
  • --push: Push the built image to registry immediately after successful build. Streamlines Django application deployment by combining build and publish steps.
  • --load: Load build result to local Docker images after build. Useful for testing Django applications locally after multi-platform builds.
  • --output: Output destination and format for build results. Can export to local filesystem, registry, or other formats beyond standard Docker images.

Attributes

  • Builder Name: Unique identifier for builder instances. Each builder can have different configurations for various Django deployment scenarios.
  • Driver Type: Builder driver that determines available features and performance characteristics for Django builds.
  • Platform Support: List of architectures and operating systems supported by the builder for Django multi-platform deployments.
  • Node Endpoints: Network endpoints where builder nodes are available, important for distributed Django build workflows.
  • Builder Status: Current state of builder instance (running, stopped, error) affecting Django build availability.

Methods

Create Multi-Platform Builder

Syntax: docker buildx create --name {name} --driver docker-container --platform {platforms}

Parameters: name (string) - Builder instance name, driver (string) - Builder driver type, platform (string) - Supported platforms

Create builder instances optimized for Django multi-platform builds supporting different deployment architectures.

Build with Registry Cache

Syntax: docker buildx build --cache-from type=registry,ref={ref} --cache-to type=registry,ref={ref}

Parameters: ref (string) - Registry reference for cache storage and retrieval

Enable distributed cache sharing for Django builds across development team and CI/CD systems using registry-based cache.

Concurrent Platform Build

Syntax: docker buildx build --platform {platform1},{platform2} --push -t {image}

Parameters: platform (string) - Comma-separated list of target platforms

Build Django applications for multiple architectures simultaneously and push to registry for cross-platform deployment.

Return Value

Returns build results including image digests, manifest information, and platform-specific details. For multi-platform builds, returns manifest list with references to platform-specific images. Build failures return detailed error information including platform-specific failure details.

  • Success (0): Build completed successfully for all requested platforms
  • Error (1): Build failed on one or more platforms with detailed error messages
  • Error (125): BuildKit daemon error or invalid buildx configuration

5 Practical Django Examples

Example 1: Multi-Platform Skills API Build

Build Skills API service for multiple architectures to support deployment across different cloud providers and development environments including Apple Silicon and Intel-based systems.

# Create multi-platform builder for Skills API
docker buildx create --name skills-builder --driver docker-container --platform linux/amd64,linux/arm64
skills-builder
# Use the new builder and inspect capabilities
docker buildx use skills-builder
docker buildx inspect skills-builder --bootstrap
Name: skills-builder Driver: docker-container Nodes: Name: skills-builder0 Endpoint: unix:///var/run/docker.sock Status: running Platforms: linux/amd64*, linux/arm64* BuildKit version: v0.12.4 Runtimes: runc Features: cache_export, cache_import, multi_platform, container_image_export
# Build Skills API for multiple platforms with push
docker buildx build --platform linux/amd64,linux/arm64 \
    -t anihpj/skills-api:multiarch \
    --push \
    -f Dockerfile.skills .
[+] Building 45.2s (28/28) FINISHED => [internal] load build definition from Dockerfile.skills 0.1s => [internal] load .dockerignore 0.1s => [internal] load build context 2.3s => [linux/amd64 internal] load metadata for docker.io/library/python:3.11-slim 1.2s => [linux/arm64 internal] load metadata for docker.io/library/python:3.11-slim 1.2s => [linux/amd64 1/8] FROM docker.io/library/python:3.11-slim@sha256:abc123... 0.0s => [linux/arm64 1/8] FROM docker.io/library/python:3.11-slim@sha256:def456... 0.0s => [linux/amd64 2/8] WORKDIR /app 0.3s => [linux/arm64 2/8] WORKDIR /app 0.4s => [linux/amd64 3/8] RUN apt-get update && apt-get install -y... 8.2s => [linux/arm64 3/8] RUN apt-get update && apt-get install -y... 12.1s => [linux/amd64 4/8] COPY requirements.txt . 0.1s => [linux/arm64 4/8] COPY requirements.txt . 0.1s => [linux/amd64 5/8] RUN pip install --no-cache-dir -r requirements.txt 15.3s => [linux/arm64 5/8] RUN pip install --no-cache-dir -r requirements.txt 18.7s => [linux/amd64 6/8] COPY . . 0.8s => [linux/arm64 6/8] COPY . . 0.8s => [linux/amd64 7/8] RUN python manage.py collectstatic --noinput 2.1s => [linux/arm64 7/8] RUN python manage.py collectstatic --noinput 2.3s => [linux/amd64 8/8] EXPOSE 8000 0.1s => [linux/arm64 8/8] EXPOSE 8000 0.1s => exporting to image 3.2s => => exporting layers 2.8s => => exporting manifest sha256:ghi789... 0.1s => => exporting config sha256:jkl012... 0.1s => => exporting manifest list 0.1s => => pushing layers 2.1s => => pushing manifest for docker.io/anihpj/skills-api:multiarch 0.2s

Verify multi-platform image manifest:

# Inspect multi-platform manifest
docker buildx imagetools inspect anihpj/skills-api:multiarch
Name: docker.io/anihpj/skills-api:multiarch MediaType: application/vnd.docker.distribution.manifest.list.v2+json Digest: sha256:abcdef123456... Manifests: Name: docker.io/anihpj/skills-api:multiarch@sha256:111222... MediaType: application/vnd.docker.distribution.manifest.v2+json Platform: linux/amd64 Name: docker.io/anihpj/skills-api:multiarch@sha256:333444... MediaType: application/vnd.docker.distribution.manifest.v2+json Platform: linux/arm64

Test Skills API on different platforms:

# Run on current platform (will use appropriate architecture)
docker run -d -p 8000:8000 --name skills-multiarch anihpj/skills-api:multiarch

# Test Skills API functionality
curl http://localhost:8000/jobpost/skills/
[ { "id": 1, "name": "Python", "description": "Python programming language", "category": "Programming", "level": "Expert", "demand_score": 95, "platform_built": "linux/arm64" }, { "id": 2, "name": "Django", "description": "Django web framework", "category": "Framework", "level": "Advanced", "demand_score": 88, "platform_built": "linux/arm64" } ]

Skills Model Multi-Platform Query:

# Test Skills model on multi-platform build
docker exec -it skills-multiarch python manage.py shell

from jobpost.models import Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost
import platform
import os

# Check runtime platform and Skills data
print(f"Container Platform: {platform.platform()}")
print(f"Architecture: {platform.machine()}")
print(f"Python Version: {platform.python_version()}")

# Skills model operations across platforms
skills_qs = Skills.objects.all().order_by('demand_score')
print(f"\nSkills QuerySet (Platform: {platform.machine()}):")
for skill in skills_qs:
    print(f"  {skill.name} - {skill.level} (Score: {skill.demand_score})")
Container Platform: Linux-6.2.0-39-generic-aarch64-with-glibc2.36 Architecture: aarch64 Python Version: 3.11.0 Skills QuerySet (Platform: aarch64): Kubernetes - Beginner (Score: 72) Docker - Intermediate (Score: 78) PostgreSQL - Advanced (Score: 82) Django - Advanced (Score: 88) Python - Expert (Score: 95) Skills QuerySet: , , , , ]>
Example 2: Registry Cache Build for User Authentication

Build User authentication service with registry-based cache sharing to accelerate builds across team members and CI/CD pipelines while maintaining security.

# Create builder with registry cache support
docker buildx create --name auth-cache-builder --driver docker-container

# Use cache builder and build with registry cache
docker buildx use auth-cache-builder
auth-cache-builder
# Build User auth with registry cache import and export
docker buildx build \
    --cache-from type=registry,ref=anihpj/build-cache:user-auth \
    --cache-to type=registry,ref=anihpj/build-cache:user-auth,mode=max \
    --platform linux/amd64,linux/arm64 \
    -t anihpj/user-auth:cached \
    --push \
    -f Dockerfile.user-auth .
[+] Building 32.1s (35/35) FINISHED => [internal] load build definition from Dockerfile.user-auth 0.1s => [internal] load .dockerignore 0.1s => [internal] importing cache manifest from anihpj/build-cache:user-auth 2.3s => [linux/amd64 base 1/4] FROM docker.io/library/python:3.11-slim 0.0s => [linux/arm64 base 1/4] FROM docker.io/library/python:3.11-slim 0.0s => CACHED [linux/amd64 base 2/4] RUN apt-get update && apt-get install -y libpq-dev 0.0s => CACHED [linux/arm64 base 2/4] RUN apt-get update && apt-get install -y libpq-dev 0.0s => CACHED [linux/amd64 base 3/4] RUN groupadd -r django && useradd -r -g django django 0.0s => CACHED [linux/arm64 base 3/4] RUN groupadd -r django && useradd -r -g django django 0.0s => [linux/amd64 deps 1/3] WORKDIR /app 0.2s => [linux/arm64 deps 1/3] WORKDIR /app 0.2s => CACHED [linux/amd64 deps 2/3] COPY requirements.txt . 0.0s => CACHED [linux/arm64 deps 2/3] COPY requirements.txt . 0.0s => CACHED [linux/amd64 deps 3/3] RUN pip install --no-cache-dir -r requirements.txt 0.0s => CACHED [linux/arm64 deps 3/3] RUN pip install --no-cache-dir -r requirements.txt 0.0s => [linux/amd64 app 1/6] COPY --chown=django:django . . 1.2s => [linux/arm64 app 1/6] COPY --chown=django:django . . 1.2s => [linux/amd64 app 2/6] RUN python manage.py collectstatic --noinput 3.1s => [linux/arm64 app 2/6] RUN python manage.py collectstatic --noinput 3.3s => [linux/amd64 app 3/6] USER django 0.1s => [linux/arm64 app 3/6] USER django 0.1s => exporting to image 4.2s => => exporting layers 3.8s => => exporting manifest list 0.1s => => pushing layers 2.8s => => pushing manifest for docker.io/anihpj/user-auth:cached 0.2s => exporting cache 5.1s => => preparing build cache for export 4.9s => => writing layer sha256:abc123... to anihpj/build-cache:user-auth 0.1s => => writing config sha256:def456... to anihpj/build-cache:user-auth 0.1s

Verify cache effectiveness:

# Rebuild to test cache effectiveness
time docker buildx build \
    --cache-from type=registry,ref=anihpj/build-cache:user-auth \
    --platform linux/amd64 \
    -t anihpj/user-auth:cached-test \
    --load \
    -f Dockerfile.user-auth .
[+] Building 8.7s (18/18) FINISHED => [internal] load build definition from Dockerfile.user-auth 0.1s => [internal] load .dockerignore 0.1s => [internal] importing cache manifest from anihpj/build-cache:user-auth 1.2s => CACHED [base 1/4] FROM docker.io/library/python:3.11-slim 0.0s => CACHED [base 2/4] RUN apt-get update && apt-get install -y libpq-dev 0.0s => CACHED [deps 1/3] WORKDIR /app 0.0s => CACHED [deps 2/3] COPY requirements.txt . 0.0s => CACHED [deps 3/3] RUN pip install --no-cache-dir -r requirements.txt 0.0s => [app 1/6] COPY --chown=django:django . . 1.1s => CACHED [app 2/6] RUN python manage.py collectstatic --noinput 0.0s => => exporting to docker image format 6.2s => => exporting layers 5.8s real 0m8.7s user 0m0.2s sys 0m0.1s

Test cached User authentication:

# Run cached User auth service
docker run -d -p 8001:8001 --name user-auth-cached anihpj/user-auth:cached-test

# Test authentication endpoint
curl -X POST http://localhost:8001/api/auth/register/ \
     -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
     -d '{"username": "cached_user", "email": "cached@example.com", "password": "secure123"}'
{ "success": true, "user": { "id": 8, "username": "cached_user", "email": "cached@example.com", "date_joined": "2026-01-11T13:45:00Z", "is_active": true, "cache_build": true, "build_time_saved": "75% faster build" }, "message": "User registered with cached build" }

User Model Cache Performance Analysis:

# Analyze User model performance with registry cache
docker exec -it user-auth-cached python manage.py shell

from django.contrib.auth.models import User
from jobpost.models import Author, Skills, Location, JobPost
from django.core.cache import cache
import time

# User authentication performance with cached build
start = time.time()
users = User.objects.select_related('author').prefetch_related('author__skills')
user_data = [(u.username, getattr(u, 'author', None)) for u in users]
query_time = time.time() - start

print(f"User Query Performance (Cached Build): {query_time:.4f}s")
print(f"Total Users: {len(user_data)}")

for username, author in user_data[:5]:
    author_name = author.username if author else "No Author"
    print(f"User: {username} -> Author: {author_name}")
User Query Performance (Cached Build): 0.0189s Total Users: 8 User: admin -> Author: AdminAuthor User: testuser -> Author: TestAuthor User: cached_user -> Author: No Author User: author1 -> Author: TechAuthor User: content_creator -> Author: ContentAuthor User QuerySet: , , , , ]>
Example 3: Concurrent Build for Author Content Management

Build Author content management service with concurrent multi-stage builds and output to multiple destinations for different deployment scenarios.

# Create concurrent builder for Author service
docker buildx create --name author-concurrent --driver docker-container --config ./buildx-config.toml
author-concurrent
# Build Author service with multiple outputs concurrently
docker buildx use author-concurrent

docker buildx build \
    --platform linux/amd64,linux/arm64 \
    --target production \
    -t anihpj/author-service:concurrent \
    --output type=image,push=true \
    --output type=docker,dest=./author-service.tar \
    --cache-from type=registry,ref=anihpj/cache:author \
    --cache-to type=registry,ref=anihpj/cache:author \
    -f Dockerfile.author .

Author Model Concurrent Build Analysis:

# Analyze Author model with concurrent build
docker exec -it author-concurrent python manage.py shell

from jobpost.models import Author, Skills, User, Location, JobPost
from django.db.models import Prefetch
import platform

# Author model analysis with concurrent build
print(f"Build Platform: {platform.machine()}")
authors = Author.objects.prefetch_related(
    Prefetch('skills', queryset=Skills.objects.filter(category='Content'))
).select_related('user')

print("Authors from Concurrent Build:")
for author in authors:
    skills_names = [s.name for s in author.skills.all()]
    print(f"Author: {author.username}")
    print(f"  Skills: {skills_names}")
    print(f"  Email: {author.email}")
    
print(f"\nTotal Authors: {Author.objects.count()}")
print(f"Content Skills: {Skills.objects.filter(category='Content').count()}")
Build Platform: aarch64 Authors from Concurrent Build: Author: concurrent_author Skills: ['Content Creation', 'Technical Writing'] Email: concurrent@example.com Author: content_creator Skills: ['Content Strategy', 'SEO'] Email: creator@anihpj.com Author: blog_author Skills: ['Blogging', 'Social Media'] Email: blog@anihpj.com Total Authors: 6 Content Skills: 8 Author QuerySet: , , , ]>
Example 4: Advanced Location Service with Secret Mounts

Build Location service with BuildKit advanced features including secret mounts for API keys, SSH agent forwarding, and security-enhanced builds for geographic data processing.

# Create secrets for Location service build
echo "geo_api_key=sk_geo_abcd1234" > geo-secrets.txt
echo "maps_token=pk.eyJ1234" >> geo-secrets.txt

# Create Dockerfile with secret mounts
cat > Dockerfile.location-secure << 'EOF'
# syntax=docker/dockerfile:1
FROM python:3.11-slim

WORKDIR /app

# Use secret mount for API keys during build
RUN --mount=type=secret,id=geo-secrets \
    apt-get update && apt-get install -y \
    libpq-dev curl && \
    rm -rf /var/lib/apt/lists/*

# Copy requirements and install with secret access
COPY requirements-geo.txt .
RUN --mount=type=secret,id=geo-secrets \
    pip install --no-cache-dir -r requirements-geo.txt && \
    pip install --no-cache-dir geopy requests

# Copy application code
COPY . .

# Setup geo data with secure API access
RUN --mount=type=secret,id=geo-secrets \
    export $(cat /run/secrets/geo-secrets | xargs) && \
    python manage.py setup_geo_apis && \
    python manage.py collectstatic --noinput

EXPOSE 8003
CMD ["gunicorn", "--bind", "0.0.0.0:8003", "anihpj.wsgi:application"]
EOF
# Build Location service with secrets
docker buildx build \
    --secret id=geo-secrets,src=./geo-secrets.txt \
    --platform linux/amd64,linux/arm64 \
    -t anihpj/location-service:secure \
    --push \
    -f Dockerfile.location-secure .
[+] Building 38.7s (45/45) FINISHED => [internal] load build definition from Dockerfile.location-secure 0.1s => [internal] load .dockerignore 0.1s => [internal] load build context 2.1s => [linux/amd64 1/9] FROM docker.io/library/python:3.11-slim 0.0s => [linux/arm64 1/9] FROM docker.io/library/python:3.11-slim 0.0s => [linux/amd64 2/9] WORKDIR /app 0.2s => [linux/arm64 2/9] WORKDIR /app 0.3s => [linux/amd64 3/9] RUN --mount=type=secret,id=geo-secrets apt-get update... 6.8s => [linux/arm64 3/9] RUN --mount=type=secret,id=geo-secrets apt-get update... 8.2s => [linux/amd64 4/9] COPY requirements-geo.txt . 0.1s => [linux/arm64 4/9] COPY requirements-geo.txt . 0.1s => [linux/amd64 5/9] RUN --mount=type=secret,id=geo-secrets pip install... 12.3s => [linux/arm64 5/9] RUN --mount=type=secret,id=geo-secrets pip install... 15.1s => [linux/amd64 6/9] COPY . . 1.2s => [linux/arm64 6/9] COPY . . 1.2s => [linux/amd64 7/9] RUN --mount=type=secret,id=geo-secrets export... 4.3s => [linux/arm64 7/9] RUN --mount=type=secret,id=geo-secrets export... 4.7s => exporting to image 5.8s => => exporting layers 5.4s => => exporting manifest list 0.1s => => pushing layers 4.2s => => pushing manifest for docker.io/anihpj/location-service:secure 0.3s
