docker buildx history --filter "tag=anihpj*" --limit 20

# Export project-specific history
docker buildx history --filter "context=." --format json > anihpj-build-history.json

# Generate build report
cat > build-report.sh << 'EOF'
#!/bin/bash
echo "AnihPJ Build History Report - $(date)"
echo "======================================"

TOTAL_BUILDS=$(docker buildx history --format json | jq 'length')
SUCCESS_BUILDS=$(docker buildx history --filter "status=success" --format json | jq 'length')
FAILED_BUILDS=$(docker buildx history --filter "status=failed" --format json | jq 'length')

echo "Total builds: $TOTAL_BUILDS"
echo "Successful: $SUCCESS_BUILDS"
echo "Failed: $FAILED_BUILDS"
echo "Success rate: $(($SUCCESS_BUILDS * 100 / $TOTAL_BUILDS))%"
EOF

chmod +x build-report.sh
./build-report.sh
AnihPJ build report: 18 total builds, 16 successful, 2 failed (89% success rate)
Example 5: Advanced History Management
# Comprehensive history management workflow
echo "Starting comprehensive build history analysis..."

# Export complete history
docker buildx history --format json > complete-history.json

# Analyze build patterns
echo "Build frequency analysis:"
docker buildx history --format json | jq 'group_by(.created[:10]) | map({date: .[0].created[:10], count: length}) | sort_by(.date)'

# Performance analysis
echo "Performance metrics:"
docker buildx history --format json | jq '{
  total_builds: length,
  avg_duration: ([.[] | .duration] | add / length),
  max_duration: ([.[] | .duration] | max),
  min_duration: ([.[] | .duration] | min)
}'

# Cache effectiveness
echo "Cache analysis:"
docker buildx history --format json | jq '[.[] | select(.cache_hit)] | length' cache_hits
echo "Cache hit rate: $(echo "scale=2; $cache_hits * 100 / $TOTAL_BUILDS" | bc)%"

# Generate comprehensive report
docker buildx history export --format detailed > anihpj-detailed-history.txt
Advanced analysis complete: detailed patterns identified, optimization recommendations generated

docker buildx history export

Export build history data to external formats. Enables backup, analysis, and sharing of build history information across teams and systems.

Syntax
docker buildx history export [OPTIONS] [DESTINATION]
Parameters
  • --format - Export format (json, csv, yaml)
  • --filter - Filter history entries to export
  • --compress - Compress exported data
  • --include-traces - Include detailed trace data
Examples
Example: Export Build History
# Export anihpj build history to JSON
docker buildx history export --format json anihpj-history.json

# Export with compression
docker buildx history export --format json --compress anihpj-history.gz

# Export filtered data
docker buildx history export --filter "since=7d" --format csv weekly-builds.csv
History exported: 156 build records, 2.3MB data saved

docker buildx history import

Import build history data from external sources. Allows restoration of build history from backups or integration from other systems.

Syntax
docker buildx history import [OPTIONS] SOURCE
Parameters
  • --format - Source data format (json, csv, yaml)
  • --merge - Merge with existing history
  • --validate - Validate imported data
Examples
Example: Import Build History
# Import from JSON backup
docker buildx history import --format json anihpj-history.json

# Import and merge with current history
docker buildx history import --merge --format json backup-history.json

# Import with validation
docker buildx history import --validate --format csv builds.csv
History imported: 156 records processed, 142 new entries added

docker buildx history inspect

Inspect detailed information about specific build history entries. Provides comprehensive analysis of individual builds.

Syntax
docker buildx history inspect [OPTIONS] BUILD_ID
Parameters
  • BUILD_ID - Specific build to inspect
  • --verbose - Show detailed information
  • --format - Output format
Examples
Example: Inspect Build Details
# Inspect specific build
docker buildx history inspect build-abc123

# Detailed inspection
docker buildx history inspect --verbose build-abc123

# JSON format output
docker buildx history inspect --format json build-abc123
Build abc123: Django app, 2m 34s duration, 15 layers, cache hit 78%

docker buildx history logs

View build logs from historical builds. Access complete build output and debugging information from past executions.

Syntax
docker buildx history logs [OPTIONS] BUILD_ID
Parameters
  • BUILD_ID - Build to show logs for
  • --follow - Follow log output (for running builds)
  • --tail - Show last N lines
Examples
Example: View Build Logs
# View full build logs
docker buildx history logs build-abc123

# Show last 50 lines
docker buildx history logs --tail 50 build-abc123

# Save logs to file
docker buildx history logs build-abc123 > anihpj-build.log
Build logs displayed: 247 lines, showing pip install and Django setup

docker buildx history ls

List build history entries in a tabular format. Provides overview of all historical builds with key metadata.

Syntax
docker buildx history ls [OPTIONS]
Parameters
  • --format - Output format (table, json, yaml)
  • --filter - Filter build entries
  • --limit - Limit number of results
Examples
Example: List Build History
# List all builds
docker buildx history ls

# List recent 10 builds
docker buildx history ls --limit 10

# List failed builds only
docker buildx history ls --filter "status=failed"
BUILD ID TAG CREATED STATUS DURATION abc123 anihpj:v1.0 2 hours ago Success 2m 34s def456 anihpj:dev 5 hours ago Failed 1m 12s

docker buildx history open

Open build history in external viewer or browser. Provides interactive analysis interface for build data.

Syntax
docker buildx history open [OPTIONS] [BUILD_ID]
Parameters
  • BUILD_ID - Specific build to open (optional)
  • --viewer - Specify viewer application
  • --port - Web viewer port
Examples
Example: Open History Viewer
# Open history in browser
docker buildx history open

# Open specific build
docker buildx history open build-abc123

# Open on custom port
docker buildx history open --port 8080
History viewer opened at http://localhost:3000

docker buildx history rm

Remove build history entries. Clean up historical build data to save storage space.

Syntax
docker buildx history rm [OPTIONS] BUILD_ID...
Parameters
  • BUILD_ID - Build history entries to remove
  • --force - Force removal without confirmation
  • --all - Remove all history entries
Examples
Example: Remove History Entries
# Remove specific build
docker buildx history rm build-abc123

# Remove multiple builds
docker buildx history rm build-abc123 build-def456

# Remove all failed builds
docker buildx history ls --filter "status=failed" -q | xargs docker buildx history rm
Removed 2 build history entries, freed 45MB storage

docker buildx history trace

Show detailed execution trace for historical builds. Provides step-by-step analysis of build execution flow.

Syntax
docker buildx history trace [OPTIONS] BUILD_ID
Parameters
  • BUILD_ID - Build to trace
  • --format - Trace output format
  • --verbose - Detailed trace information
Examples
Example: Trace Build Execution
# Show build trace
docker buildx history trace build-abc123

# Verbose trace with timing
docker buildx history trace --verbose build-abc123

# Export trace to JSON
docker buildx history trace --format json build-abc123 > trace.json
Build trace: 15 stages, total 2m 34s, slowest: pip install (1m 12s)

docker buildx imagetools create

Official Docs: docker buildx imagetools create | Docker CLI: docker buildx imagetools create

Create multi-platform manifest and push to registry. Combines platform-specific images into unified multi-architecture manifests.

Full Description

The docker buildx imagetools create command creates multi-platform manifests by combining platform-specific images into unified registry entries. For your anihpj Django project, this command enables deployment to diverse architectures (x86_64, ARM64) from a single manifest, allowing your jobpost application to run efficiently across cloud providers, edge devices, and various computing environments.

When deploying your anihpj Django application across multiple platforms, this command consolidates individually built images (linux/amd64, linux/arm64) into a single manifest that automatically selects the appropriate architecture. This approach optimizes deployment flexibility, reduces registry storage, and simplifies container orchestration for your Django app across heterogeneous infrastructure.

Syntax

# Create multi-platform manifest
docker buildx imagetools create -t anihpj:latest anihpj:amd64 anihpj:arm64

# Append to existing manifest
docker buildx imagetools create --append -t anihpj:v2.0 anihpj:new-arch

# Dry run to preview changes
docker buildx imagetools create --dry-run -t anihpj:test anihpj:amd64 anihpj:arm64

Parameters Description

  • --tag, -t: Target manifest name and tag. Use descriptive tags like anihpj:v1.0 or anihpj:production to clearly identify different versions of your Django application's multi-platform manifest.
  • --append: Add to existing manifest instead of replacing. Useful for incrementally adding platform support to your anihpj Django app without recreating the entire manifest structure.
  • --dry-run: Preview changes without execution. Verify manifest composition before pushing to production registries, essential for validating your Django app's multi-platform deployment strategy.
  • --progress: Control progress output during manifest creation. Use 'plain' for CI/CD pipelines deploying your anihpj Django application to get detailed operation logs.

Attributes

  • Platform Unification: Combines multiple architecture images into single manifest
  • Registry Integration: Seamless push to container registries with manifest support
  • Architecture Detection: Automatic platform selection based on deployment target
  • Incremental Updates: Append functionality for adding new platforms
  • Validation Support: Dry-run capability for manifest verification

Methods

  • combine_platforms() - Merge platform-specific images into unified manifest
  • validate_images() - Verify source image compatibility and availability
  • create_manifest() - Generate multi-platform manifest structure
  • push_registry() - Upload manifest to container registry
  • verify_result() - Confirm successful manifest creation and accessibility

Return Values

  • Manifest Creation: Successful multi-platform manifest with platform list
  • Registry Location: Complete manifest reference and accessibility details
  • Platform Summary: List of included architectures and their image details
  • Operation Status: Creation success confirmation and any warnings
  • Verification Data: Manifest integrity and registry synchronization status

Examples

Example: Create Multi-platform Manifest
# Create multi-arch manifest for anihpj
docker buildx imagetools create \
  --tag anihpj:multiarch \
  anihpj:amd64 \
  anihpj:arm64

# Append to existing manifest
docker buildx imagetools create \
  --append \
  --tag anihpj:latest \
  anihpj:new-platform
Multi-platform manifest created: anihpj:multiarch (amd64, arm64)

docker buildx imagetools inspect

Official Docs: docker buildx imagetools inspect | Docker CLI: docker buildx imagetools inspect

Inspect multi-platform image manifests and metadata. Shows detailed information about image architectures, layers, and manifest structure.

Full Description

The docker buildx imagetools inspect command provides detailed analysis of multi-platform image manifests and their constituent platform-specific images. For your anihpj Django project, this command helps verify multi-architecture deployments, troubleshoot platform-specific issues, analyze image composition, and ensure proper manifest structure for optimal deployment across diverse computing environments.

When managing your anihpj Django application across multiple platforms, use this command to verify manifest integrity, check platform availability, analyze layer composition differences between architectures, and troubleshoot deployment issues. The detailed manifest inspection ensures your jobpost app deploys correctly across x86_64 and ARM64 environments with proper architecture selection.

Syntax

# Inspect multi-platform manifest
docker buildx imagetools inspect anihpj:latest

# Show raw manifest data
docker buildx imagetools inspect --raw anihpj:latest

# JSON format for automation
docker buildx imagetools inspect --format json anihpj:latest

Parameters Description

  • NAME: Image name or manifest reference to inspect. Can include registry URLs, tags, or digest references for your anihpj Django application's multi-platform images stored in various registries.
  • --format: Output format control (json, yaml). JSON format enables automated analysis and integration with deployment pipelines managing your Django application's multi-architecture distribution.
  • --raw: Display raw manifest JSON without processing. Essential for low-level manifest debugging and verification of your anihpj Django app's multi-platform image structure.

Attributes

  • Platform Detection: Automatic identification of supported architectures
  • Manifest Analysis: Detailed breakdown of manifest structure and components
  • Layer Inspection: Individual platform image layer composition
  • Metadata Extraction: Image labels, annotations, and configuration details
  • Registry Integration: Direct registry manifest querying and verification

Methods

  • parse_manifest() - Analyze manifest structure and platform list
  • inspect_platforms() - Examine individual platform image details
  • extract_metadata() - Retrieve image labels, annotations, and config
  • verify_integrity() - Validate manifest and platform image consistency
  • format_output() - Structure inspection results for display or processing

Return Values

  • Platform List: Complete inventory of supported architectures and OS variants
  • Manifest Details: Structural information about multi-platform manifest
  • Image Metadata: Labels, environment variables, and configuration data
  • Layer Information: Platform-specific layer composition and sizes
  • Registry Status: Availability and accessibility of manifest components

Examples

Example: Inspect Multi-platform Image
# Inspect anihpj multi-platform image
docker buildx imagetools inspect anihpj:latest

# Show raw manifest
docker buildx imagetools inspect --raw anihpj:latest

# JSON format output
docker buildx imagetools inspect --format json anihpj:latest
anihpj:latest - Platforms: linux/amd64, linux/arm64, Total size: 245MB

docker buildx inspect

Official Docs: docker buildx inspect | Docker CLI: docker buildx inspect

Inspect builder instances and their configurations. Shows detailed information about builder capabilities, drivers, and status for comprehensive build environment analysis.

Full Description

The docker buildx inspect command provides detailed information about builder instances and their configurations. For your anihpj Django project, this command reveals builder capabilities, supported platforms, driver configurations, and current status, essential for troubleshooting build issues and planning multi-platform deployments of your jobpost app.

When managing builds for your anihpj Django application, use this command to verify builder configurations, check platform support for cross-architecture deployments, diagnose build failures, and ensure your builder has the necessary capabilities for complex build scenarios including multi-stage builds, cache optimization, and platform-specific optimizations.

Syntax

# Inspect current builder
docker buildx inspect

# Inspect specific builder
docker buildx inspect anihpj-builder

# Bootstrap and inspect
docker buildx inspect --bootstrap mybuilder

Parameters Description

  • BUILDER: Builder instance name to inspect. If omitted, inspects the currently active builder used for your anihpj Django project builds.
  • --bootstrap: Ensure builder is running before inspection. Starts the builder if it's stopped, useful for getting complete configuration details including runtime capabilities.
  • --format: Output format for structured data processing. Use json format for integration with CI/CD pipelines managing your Django deployment infrastructure.

Attributes

  • Builder Identity: Name, driver type, and unique identifier
  • Platform Capabilities: Supported architectures and operating systems
  • Driver Configuration: Implementation details and connection settings
  • Feature Support: Available BuildKit features and extensions
  • Status Information: Current operational state and health

Methods

  • inspect_builder() - Examine builder configuration and status
  • verify_capabilities() - Check supported features and platforms
  • bootstrap_instance() - Initialize and start builder if needed
  • format_details() - Structure output for processing
  • validate_config() - Confirm builder configuration integrity

Return Values

  • Builder Details: Complete configuration and capability information
  • Platform Support: List of supported architectures and platforms
  • Driver Status: Current state and connection details
  • Feature Matrix: Available BuildKit features and limitations
  • Health Status: Operational state and any issues detected

Examples

Example: Inspect Builder
# Inspect current builder
docker buildx inspect

# Inspect specific builder
docker buildx inspect anihpj-builder

# Bootstrap and inspect
docker buildx inspect --bootstrap mybuilder
Builder: anihpj-builder, Driver: docker-container, Platforms: linux/amd64, linux/arm64

docker buildx ls

Official Docs: docker buildx ls | Docker CLI: docker buildx ls

List all available builder instances. Shows builder names, drivers, status, and supported platforms for your Docker build environment.

Full Description

The docker buildx ls command displays all configured builder instances in your Docker environment. For your anihpj Django project, this command helps you identify available builders, their current status, supported platforms (like linux/amd64, linux/arm64), and driver types (docker, docker-container, kubernetes). This is essential for managing multi-platform builds and understanding your build infrastructure capabilities.

When developing your anihpj Django application, use this command to verify builder configurations before executing complex builds, ensure cross-platform support is available, and troubleshoot build environment issues. The output shows which builder is currently active (marked with *), helping you understand where your builds will execute and what platforms are supported for your jobpost app deployment.

Syntax

# List all builders
docker buildx ls

# JSON format output
docker buildx ls --format json

# Filter active builders
docker buildx ls --filter "status=running"

Parameters Description

  • --format: Output format (table, json). Use json for programmatic processing of builder information in CI/CD pipelines or scripts managing your anihpj Django deployment infrastructure.
  • --filter: Filter builders by criteria such as status, driver type, or platform support. Useful for identifying specific builders for particular build requirements in your Django project workflow.

Attributes

  • Name Display: Shows builder instance names with active indicator (*)
  • Driver Information: Lists driver type (docker, docker-container, kubernetes)
  • Status Tracking: Indicates running, stopped, or error states
  • Platform Support: Shows supported architectures for multi-platform builds
  • Endpoint Details: Displays connection endpoints for remote builders

Methods

  • list_builders() - Enumerate all available builder instances
  • show_status() - Display current operational status
  • format_output() - Control output formatting and structure
  • filter_builders() - Apply selection criteria to builder list
  • identify_active() - Highlight currently selected builder

Return Values

  • Builder List: Complete inventory of configured builders
  • Status Information: Current operational state of each builder
  • Platform Support: Available architectures for each builder
  • Driver Details: Builder implementation and capabilities
  • Active Indicator: Currently selected builder marked with asterisk

Examples

Example: List Builders
# List all builders
docker buildx ls

# JSON format
docker buildx ls --format json

# Filter active builders
docker buildx ls --filter "status=running"
NAME DRIVER STATUS PLATFORMS anihpj-builder * docker-container running linux/amd64, linux/arm64 default docker running linux/amd64

docker buildx prune

Official Docs: docker buildx prune | Docker CLI: docker buildx prune

Remove build cache and unused builder resources. Cleans up BuildKit cache to free disk space and maintain optimal build performance for your Django development workflow.

Full Description

The docker buildx prune command removes BuildKit build cache and unused builder resources to reclaim disk space. For your anihpj Django project, this command is essential for maintaining clean development environments by removing cached layers from previous builds, outdated dependencies, and intermediate build artifacts that accumulate during iterative Django development cycles.

Regular cache pruning optimizes your anihpj Django development workflow by preventing disk space exhaustion, improving build performance, and ensuring clean builds when needed. The command intelligently preserves recent and frequently-used cache while removing stale entries, balancing storage efficiency with build speed for your jobpost app and related Django components.

Syntax

# Remove unused cache
docker buildx prune

# Remove all cache
docker buildx prune --all --force

# Keep specific amount of cache
docker buildx prune --keep-storage 5GB --force

Parameters Description

  • --all, -a: Remove all cache, not just dangling entries. Use when you need complete cache cleanup for your anihpj Django project, such as after major dependency updates or when troubleshooting build issues.
  • --filter: Filter cache entries by criteria such as age, size, or labels. Useful for targeted cleanup of specific build cache related to particular anihpj Django app components or development branches.
  • --force, -f: Don't prompt for confirmation. Essential for automated CI/CD pipelines managing your Django project builds and deployments.
  • --keep-storage: Keep specified amount of cache storage. Maintains performance balance by preserving recent cache for your Django builds while cleaning older entries.

Attributes

  • Cache Intelligence: Distinguishes between active and stale cache
  • Storage Optimization: Reclaims disk space efficiently
  • Performance Balance: Maintains useful cache while cleaning unused
  • Selective Removal: Supports filtering and conditional cleanup
  • Automation Support: Force mode for unattended operations

Methods

  • analyze_cache() - Identify unused and stale cache entries
  • selective_prune() - Remove cache based on criteria
  • preserve_active() - Maintain frequently-used cache
  • estimate_savings() - Calculate potential space reclamation
  • execute_cleanup() - Perform cache removal operations

Return Values

  • Space Reclaimed: Amount of disk space freed by pruning
  • Cache Statistics: Before and after cache usage summary
  • Cleanup Report: Details of removed and preserved cache
  • Performance Impact: Expected effect on subsequent builds
  • Storage Status: Current cache utilization after cleanup

Examples

Example: Prune Build Cache
# Remove unused cache
docker buildx prune

# Remove all cache
docker buildx prune --all --force

# Keep 5GB of cache
docker buildx prune --keep-storage 5GB --force
Pruned build cache: 3.2GB reclaimed, 1.8GB remaining

docker buildx rm

Official Docs: docker buildx rm | Docker CLI: docker buildx rm

Remove builder instances. Deletes builder configurations and associated resources to clean up build infrastructure.

Syntax

docker buildx rm [OPTIONS] BUILDER...

Parameters Description

  • BUILDER: Builder instance name(s) to remove from your anihpj Django project infrastructure
  • --all-inactive: Remove all inactive builders to clean up unused build resources
  • --force: Force removal without confirmation, essential for automated cleanup scripts
  • --keep-daemon: Preserve daemon while removing builder configuration
  • --keep-state: Maintain builder state files for potential restoration
Parameters
  • BUILDER - Builder instance(s) to remove
  • --all-inactive - Remove all inactive builders
  • --force - Force removal
  • --keep-daemon - Keep daemon running
  • --keep-state - Keep builder state
Examples
Example: Remove Builder
# Remove specific builder
docker buildx rm anihpj-builder

# Remove all inactive builders
docker buildx rm --all-inactive

# Force remove with state cleanup
docker buildx rm --force old-builder
Removed builder: anihpj-builder, freed 120MB storage

docker buildx stop

Official Docs: docker buildx stop | Docker CLI: docker buildx stop

Stop running builder instances. Gracefully stops builder containers without removing them, preserving configurations for later use.

Syntax

docker buildx stop [OPTIONS] [BUILDER...]

Parameters Description

  • BUILDER: Builder instance name(s) to stop. If omitted, stops current active builder used for your anihpj Django project builds
Parameters
  • BUILDER - Builder instance(s) to stop
Examples
Example: Stop Builder
# Stop specific builder
docker buildx stop anihpj-builder

# Stop all builders
docker buildx stop

# Stop multiple builders
docker buildx stop builder1 builder2
Stopped builder: anihpj-builder

docker buildx use

Official Docs: docker buildx use | Docker CLI: docker buildx use

Set the default builder instance. Changes the active builder for subsequent build operations, essential for managing build environments and multi-platform deployments.

Full Description

The docker buildx use command sets the default builder instance for subsequent build operations. For your anihpj Django project, this command allows you to switch between different build environments, such as local development builders, CI/CD builders, or multi-platform builders optimized for cross-architecture deployments of your jobpost application.

When developing your anihpj Django application across different environments, use this command to switch to builders with specific capabilities - development builders for rapid iteration, production builders with multi-platform support for deployment, or specialized builders with custom configurations for your Django app's unique requirements including ARM64 support for cost-effective cloud deployments.

Syntax

# Use specific builder
docker buildx use anihpj-builder

# Set as default globally  
docker buildx use --default --global anihpj-builder

# Switch back to default
docker buildx use default

Parameters Description

  • BUILDER: Builder instance name to activate. Specify builders configured for your anihpj Django project needs, such as development, staging, or production environments with different platform support.
  • --default: Set as default builder for the current Docker context. Makes the builder persistent across Docker CLI sessions for consistent build behavior in your Django development workflow.
  • --global: Set globally for all Docker contexts. Ensures the builder selection persists across different Docker contexts and environments in your anihpj project infrastructure.

Attributes

  • Context Scope: Builder selection applies to current or global context
  • Session Persistence: Selection persists across Docker CLI sessions
  • Environment Isolation: Different builders for different development stages
  • Platform Switching: Access to different architecture support
  • Configuration Inheritance: Adopts builder's capabilities and settings

Methods

  • switch_builder() - Change active builder instance
  • set_default() - Configure persistent builder selection
  • apply_global() - Set builder across all contexts
  • validate_builder() - Verify builder availability and status
  • inherit_config() - Adopt builder capabilities and settings

Return Values

  • Selection Confirmation: Verification of active builder change
  • Builder Information: Details about newly active builder
  • Context Status: Scope of builder selection (local/global)
  • Capability Summary: Available features and platform support
  • Configuration State: Persistence and inheritance details

Examples

Example: Switch Builder
# Use specific builder
docker buildx use anihpj-builder

# Set as default globally
docker buildx use --default --global anihpj-builder

# Switch back to default
docker buildx use default
Switched to builder: anihpj-builder (docker-container)

docker buildx version

Official Docs: docker buildx version | Docker CLI: docker buildx version

Show BuildX version information. Displays version details, build info, and supported features for troubleshooting and compatibility verification.

Full Description

The docker buildx version command displays comprehensive version information about your BuildX installation. For your anihpj Django project, this command helps verify BuildX capabilities, troubleshoot build issues, ensure compatibility with build requirements, and document the build environment for reproducible Django application deployments across development and production environments.

Syntax

# Show BuildX version
docker buildx version

# JSON format for automation
docker buildx version --format json

# Combined with Docker version
docker version && docker buildx version

Parameters Description

  • --format: Output format control (text, json). JSON format enables automated version checking and compatibility validation in CI/CD pipelines deploying your anihpj Django application.

Attributes

  • Version Identity: BuildX version number and build information
  • Feature Support: Available capabilities and extensions
  • Compatibility Info: Docker Engine and BuildKit version requirements
  • Build Details: Compilation information and source references

Methods

  • display_version() - Show version and build information
  • check_features() - Verify available capabilities
  • format_output() - Structure version data for processing
  • validate_compatibility() - Check version compatibility requirements

Return Values

  • Version Information: BuildX version number and release details
  • Build Metadata: Compilation details and source information
  • Feature Matrix: Available capabilities and supported features
  • Compatibility Status: Version compatibility with current Docker installation

Syntax

docker buildx version [OPTIONS]
Parameters
  • --format - Output format (text, json)
Examples
Example: Show Version
# Show buildx version
docker buildx version

# JSON format
docker buildx version --format json

# Show full Docker and BuildX versions
docker version && docker buildx version
docker-buildx v0.12.1 (github.com/docker/buildx.git@abc123)

📸 Checkpoint Commands

Docker checkpoint commands provide container state management capabilities for creating, managing, and restoring container checkpoints. For your anihpj Django project, these commands enable you to save running container states during development, testing, or debugging phases, allowing you to restart containers from specific execution points with preserved application state, database connections, and in-memory data. Note that containers are restarted from checkpoints using standard docker start commands with the checkpoint reference.

📋 Checkpoint Commands Overview

docker checkpoint - Container checkpoint management and operations
docker checkpoint create - Create container checkpoints
docker checkpoint ls - List container checkpoints
docker checkpoint rm - Remove container checkpoints
🎯 Django Focus: This documentation shows practical examples of managing container checkpoints for Django development workflows using the anihpj project. Learn how to preserve container states during debugging, create development snapshots, and manage checkpoint storage for Django applications with jobpost app models (Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost).

docker checkpoint

Official Docs: docker checkpoint | Docker CLI: docker checkpoint

Container state management and checkpoint operations for running containers.

Full Description

The docker checkpoint command provides container state management capabilities for creating, listing, and removing container checkpoints. For your anihpj Django project, this command group enables you to save and restore container states during development, debugging, and testing workflows, preserving application memory, process states, and runtime configurations.

Syntax

# View available checkpoint subcommands
docker checkpoint --help

# Common checkpoint operations for Django containers
docker checkpoint create anihpj_web debug_snapshot
docker checkpoint ls anihpj_web
docker checkpoint rm anihpj_web old_snapshot

Available Subcommands for anihpj Django Development

  • create: Create new checkpoints of running Django containers, preserving application state and memory contents.
  • ls: List all existing checkpoints for Django containers, showing creation timestamps and checkpoint names.
  • rm: Remove unused checkpoints to free disk space and maintain clean checkpoint history.

Django-Specific Use Cases

Development Workflow Integration
# Checkpoint before major refactoring
docker checkpoint create --leave anihpj_web pre_refactor_state

# List all development checkpoints
docker checkpoint ls anihpj_web

# Clean up completed debugging checkpoints
docker checkpoint rm anihpj_web debug_session_1 debug_session_2

Integrate checkpoint management into your Django development workflow for safer experimentation and debugging.

Real-world anihpj Examples

Complete Checkpoint Workflow
# Create checkpoint before testing JobPost feature
docker checkpoint create --leave anihpj_web jobpost_feature_test

# Verify checkpoint was created
docker checkpoint ls anihpj_web

# After testing, remove temporary checkpoint
docker checkpoint rm anihpj_web jobpost_feature_test
checkpoint created: anihpj_web:jobpost_feature_test CHECKPOINT NAME CREATED jobpost_feature_test just now checkpoint removed: anihpj_web:jobpost_feature_test

docker checkpoint create

Official Docs: docker checkpoint create | Docker CLI: docker checkpoint create

Create a checkpoint of a running container, preserving its current state.

Full Description

The docker checkpoint create command creates a checkpoint of a running container, preserving its current state including memory contents, process states, and open file descriptors. For your anihpj Django project, this command enables you to capture snapshots of running Django development servers, database containers, or background task processors, allowing you to restore these exact states later for debugging, testing, or development purposes.

When creating checkpoints for your anihpj Django application, the command saves the complete runtime state including Python interpreter memory, Django application cache, active database connections, session data, and any in-progress background tasks. This enables precise restoration of development environments, debugging scenarios, and testing states for your jobpost app components including Skills models, User authentication states, Author profiles, Location data processing, and JobPost operations.

Syntax

# Basic checkpoint creation
docker checkpoint create {container} {checkpoint-name}

# Create checkpoint and leave container running
docker checkpoint create --leave {container} {checkpoint-name}

# Create checkpoint for Django web server
docker checkpoint create anihpj_web development_snapshot

# Create checkpoint with descriptive name
docker checkpoint create --leave anihpj_web debug_jobpost_feature

# Create database checkpoint during testing
docker checkpoint create anihpj_db test_data_snapshot

Parameters Description

  • container: Name or ID of the running Django container to checkpoint. Must be in running state to capture live application memory and process states.
  • checkpoint-name: Unique identifier for the checkpoint. Use descriptive names like 'debug_session', 'pre_migration', or 'feature_test_state' for Django development workflows.
  • --leave: Keep the container running after creating checkpoint. Essential for Django development when you want to continue working while preserving a restore point.

Attributes

  • Checkpoint ID: Unique identifier for the created checkpoint state. Used to reference specific checkpoint for restoration operations.
  • Creation Timestamp: Time when checkpoint was created. Helps track checkpoint history and identify most recent development states.
  • Container State Size: Disk space used by the checkpoint data. Monitor this for Django applications to manage storage efficiently.
  • Process Count: Number of processes captured in checkpoint. Includes Django server processes, database connections, and background tasks.
  • Memory Footprint: Amount of memory state preserved. Important for Django applications with large dataset caches or active user sessions.

Methods

Create with Continue Operation

Syntax: docker checkpoint create --leave {container} {checkpoint-name}

Parameters: container (string) - Running Django container name, checkpoint-name (string) - Checkpoint identifier

Create checkpoint while keeping Django development server running, enabling continuous development workflow with safety restore points.

Create for Debugging State

Syntax: docker checkpoint create {container} debug_{feature-name}

Parameters: container (string) - Django container with debug setup, feature-name (string) - Specific feature being debugged

Capture Django application state when encountering bugs, preserving exact conditions for later analysis and reproduction.

Return Value

Returns confirmation message with checkpoint name and container reference. On failure, returns error details about checkpoint creation issues.

  • Success (0): Checkpoint created successfully with confirmation message
  • Error (1): Container not running or checkpoint name already exists
  • Error (125): Invalid command usage or Docker daemon error

5 Practical Django Examples

Example 1: Development State Preservation (Skills Model Testing)

Create checkpoint before testing new Skills model functionality to preserve stable development state for safe experimentation.

# Create checkpoint of Django development environment
docker checkpoint create --leave anihpj_web skills_model_testing

# Print confirmation and container state
echo "Skills model testing checkpoint created"
docker checkpoint ls anihpj_web

Django Models Context:

# Skills model being tested
class Skills(models.Model):
    name = models.CharField(max_length=100)
    category = models.CharField(max_length=50)
    level = models.CharField(max_length=20)
    created_at = models.DateTimeField(auto_now_add=True)
    
    def __str__(self):
        return f"{self.name} - {self.level}"

# Test data in development database
skills_data = [
    Skills(name="Python", category="Programming", level="Expert"),
    Skills(name="Django", category="Framework", level="Advanced"),
    Skills(name="PostgreSQL", category="Database", level="Intermediate")
]

# Print current skills count before testing
print(f"Current Skills count: {Skills.objects.count()}")
for skill in Skills.objects.all():
    print(f"Skill: {skill.name}, Category: {skill.category}, Level: {skill.level}")
checkpoint created: anihpj_web:skills_model_testing CHECKPOINT NAME CREATED skills_model_testing just now Current Skills count: 3 Skill: Python, Category: Programming, Level: Expert Skill: Django, Category: Framework, Level: Advanced Skill: PostgreSQL, Category: Database, Level: Intermediate
Example 2: Database Migration Checkpoint (User Authentication)

Create checkpoint before running User model migration to preserve database state for rollback if needed.

# Create checkpoint before database migration
docker checkpoint create anihpj_db pre_user_migration

# Verify checkpoint creation
docker checkpoint ls anihpj_db

Django Models Context:

# User model before migration
class User(models.Model):
    username = models.CharField(max_length=150, unique=True)
    email = models.EmailField()
    first_name = models.CharField(max_length=50)
    last_name = models.CharField(max_length=50)
    is_active = models.BooleanField(default=True)
    date_joined = models.DateTimeField(auto_now_add=True)
    
    def __str__(self):
        return self.username

# Current user data preserved in checkpoint
users_data = [
    User(username="john_doe", email="john@anihpj.com", first_name="John", last_name="Doe"),
    User(username="jane_smith", email="jane@anihpj.com", first_name="Jane", last_name="Smith"),
    User(username="admin_user", email="admin@anihpj.com", first_name="Admin", last_name="User")
]

# Print user data being preserved
print(f"Users being preserved in checkpoint: {User.objects.count()}")
for user in User.objects.all():
    print(f"User: {user.username}, Email: {user.email}, Active: {user.is_active}")
checkpoint created: anihpj_db:pre_user_migration CHECKPOINT NAME CREATED pre_user_migration just now Users being preserved in checkpoint: 3 User: john_doe, Email: john@anihpj.com, Active: True User: jane_smith, Email: jane@anihpj.com, Active: True User: admin_user, Email: admin@anihpj.com, Active: True
Example 3: Feature Development Checkpoint (Author Profile System)

Create checkpoint while developing Author model features to preserve working state during experimental changes.

# Create development checkpoint for Author feature
docker checkpoint create --leave anihpj_web author_feature_development

# Continue development while preserving restore point
docker checkpoint ls anihpj_web

Django Models Context:

# Author model under development
class Author(models.Model):
    user = models.OneToOneField(User, on_delete=models.CASCADE)
    bio = models.TextField(max_length=500)
    profile_image = models.ImageField(upload_to='author_profiles/', null=True, blank=True)
    website = models.URLField(null=True, blank=True)
    social_media = models.JSONField(default=dict)
    specializations = models.ManyToManyField(Skills)
    is_verified = models.BooleanField(default=False)
    created_at = models.DateTimeField(auto_now_add=True)
    
    def __str__(self):
        return f"Author: {self.user.username}"

# Development data being preserved
authors_data = [
    Author(user_id=1, bio="Experienced Django developer", website="https://johndoe.dev", is_verified=True),
    Author(user_id=2, bio="Full-stack Python developer", website="https://janesmith.com", is_verified=False)
]

# Print current development state
print(f"Authors in development: {Author.objects.count()}")
for author in Author.objects.all():
    print(f"Author: {author.user.username}, Bio: {author.bio[:50]}..., Verified: {author.is_verified}")
    print(f"Specializations: {[skill.name for skill in author.specializations.all()]}")
checkpoint created: anihpj_web:author_feature_development CHECKPOINT NAME CREATED author_feature_development just now Authors in development: 2 Author: john_doe, Bio: Experienced Django developer..., Verified: True Specializations: ['Python', 'Django'] Author: jane_smith, Bio: Full-stack Python developer..., Verified: False Specializations: ['Python', 'PostgreSQL']
Example 4: Debug Session Checkpoint (Location-based Queries)

Create checkpoint when debugging Location model query performance issues to preserve exact problematic state.

# Create checkpoint during debug session
docker checkpoint create anihpj_web location_query_debug

# Debug state preserved for analysis
docker checkpoint ls anihpj_web

Django Models Context:

# Location model with performance issues
class Location(models.Model):
    name = models.CharField(max_length=100)
    country = models.CharField(max_length=50)
    region = models.CharField(max_length=50)
    city = models.CharField(max_length=50)
    postal_code = models.CharField(max_length=10)
    latitude = models.DecimalField(max_digits=9, decimal_places=6)
    longitude = models.DecimalField(max_digits=9, decimal_places=6)
    is_remote_friendly = models.BooleanField(default=False)
    created_at = models.DateTimeField(auto_now_add=True)
    
    def __str__(self):
        return f"{self.city}, {self.country}"

# Debug data showing performance issue
locations_data = [
    Location(name="San Francisco Bay Area", country="USA", region="California", 
             city="San Francisco", postal_code="94105", latitude=37.7749, longitude=-122.4194),
    Location(name="New York Metro", country="USA", region="New York", 
             city="New York", postal_code="10001", latitude=40.7128, longitude=-74.0060),
    Location(name="Remote Position", country="Global", region="Remote", 
             city="Remote", postal_code="00000", latitude=0.0000, longitude=0.0000, is_remote_friendly=True)
]

# Print location data causing performance issues
print(f"Locations causing debug session: {Location.objects.count()}")
for location in Location.objects.all():
    print(f"Location: {location.name}, City: {location.city}, Remote: {location.is_remote_friendly}")
    print(f"Coordinates: {location.latitude}, {location.longitude}")
checkpoint created: anihpj_web:location_query_debug CHECKPOINT NAME CREATED location_query_debug just now Locations causing debug session: 3 Location: San Francisco Bay Area, City: San Francisco, Remote: False Coordinates: 37.774900, -122.419400 Location: New York Metro, City: New York, Remote: False Coordinates: 40.712800, -74.006000 Location: Remote Position, City: Remote, Remote: True Coordinates: 0.000000, 0.000000
Example 5: JobPost Processing State Checkpoint (Background Tasks)

Create checkpoint of Celery worker processing JobPost tasks to preserve queue state during heavy processing load.

# Create checkpoint of worker during JobPost processing
docker checkpoint create anihpj_worker jobpost_processing_state

# Preserve background task state
docker checkpoint ls anihpj_worker

Django Models Context:

# JobPost model with active processing
class JobPost(models.Model):
    title = models.CharField(max_length=200)
    description = models.TextField()
    author = models.ForeignKey(Author, on_delete=models.CASCADE)
    location = models.ForeignKey(Location, on_delete=models.CASCADE)
    required_skills = models.ManyToManyField(Skills)
    salary_min = models.DecimalField(max_digits=10, decimal_places=2, null=True, blank=True)
    salary_max = models.DecimalField(max_digits=10, decimal_places=2, null=True, blank=True)
    is_remote = models.BooleanField(default=False)
    is_published = models.BooleanField(default=False)
    created_at = models.DateTimeField(auto_now_add=True)
    updated_at = models.DateTimeField(auto_now=True)
    
    def __str__(self):
        return self.title

# JobPost data being processed in background
jobposts_data = [
    JobPost(title="Senior Django Developer", description="Looking for experienced Django developer...", 
            author_id=1, location_id=1, salary_min=120000, salary_max=150000, is_remote=True, is_published=True),
    JobPost(title="Python Data Scientist", description="Data scientist role with Python focus...", 
            author_id=2, location_id=2, salary_min=100000, salary_max=130000, is_remote=False, is_published=True),
    JobPost(title="Full Stack Developer", description="Full stack position with Django and React...", 
            author_id=1, location_id=3, salary_min=90000, salary_max=120000, is_remote=True, is_published=False)
]

# Print JobPost processing state
print(f"JobPosts being processed: {JobPost.objects.count()}")
for jobpost in JobPost.objects.all():
    print(f"Job: {jobpost.title}, Author: {jobpost.author.user.username}")
    print(f"Location: {jobpost.location.city}, Remote: {jobpost.is_remote}, Published: {jobpost.is_published}")
    print(f"Skills: {[skill.name for skill in jobpost.required_skills.all()]}")
    print(f"Salary: ${jobpost.salary_min} - ${jobpost.salary_max}")
checkpoint created: anihpj_worker:jobpost_processing_state CHECKPOINT NAME CREATED jobpost_processing_state just now JobPosts being processed: 3 Job: Senior Django Developer, Author: john_doe Location: San Francisco, Remote: True, Published: True Skills: ['Python', 'Django', 'PostgreSQL'] Salary: $120000 - $150000 Job: Python Data Scientist, Author: jane_smith Location: New York, Remote: False, Published: True Skills: ['Python'] Salary: $100000 - $130000 Job: Full Stack Developer, Author: john_doe Location: Remote, Remote: True, Published: False Skills: ['Python', 'Django'] Salary: $90000 - $120000

docker checkpoint ls

Official Docs: docker checkpoint ls | Docker CLI: docker checkpoint ls

List all checkpoints created for a specific container.

Full Description

The docker checkpoint ls command lists all checkpoints created for a specific container. For your anihpj Django project, this command helps you view all saved states for your Django development containers, track checkpoint history, and identify available restore points for debugging or development rollback scenarios.

When listing checkpoints for your anihpj Django application containers, the command displays checkpoint names, creation timestamps, and associated metadata. This enables effective management of development snapshots, debugging states, and testing restore points for your jobpost app components including Skills model states, User authentication checkpoints, Author profile snapshots, Location data backups, and JobPost processing states.

Syntax

# Basic checkpoint listing
docker checkpoint ls {container}

# List with custom format
docker checkpoint ls --format {template} {container}

# List checkpoints for Django web container
docker checkpoint ls anihpj_web

# List checkpoints with formatted output
docker checkpoint ls --format "table {{.Name}}\t{{.Created}}" anihpj_web

# List database container checkpoints
docker checkpoint ls anihpj_db

Parameters Description

  • container: Name or ID of the container to list checkpoints for. Must be existing container that has had checkpoints created (anihpj_web, anihpj_db, anihpj_worker).
  • --format: Format the output using a Go template. Supports table, json, and custom formats with fields like Name, Created, and Size for checkpoint information.

Attributes

  • Checkpoint Name: Unique identifier for each checkpoint. Shows descriptive names like 'debug_session', 'pre_migration', or 'feature_test_state'.
  • Creation Date: Timestamp when checkpoint was created. Helps identify most recent or relevant checkpoints for Django development workflows.
  • Checkpoint Size: Disk space consumed by checkpoint data. Important for monitoring storage usage in Django development environments.
  • Container State: Status of container when checkpoint was created. Indicates if checkpoint captured running or stopped container state.
  • Checkpoint Count: Total number of checkpoints for the container. Helps track checkpoint accumulation and storage management needs.

Methods

List with Custom Format

Syntax: docker checkpoint ls --format {template} {container}

Parameters: template (string) - Go template format string, container (string) - Django container name to list checkpoints for

Display checkpoint information in custom format for scripting, monitoring, or integration with Django development tools and CI/CD pipelines.

List for Development History

Syntax: docker checkpoint ls {django-container}

Parameters: django-container (string) - Django application container with development checkpoints

View complete checkpoint history for Django development containers, enabling tracking of development states and debugging sessions.

Return Value

Returns formatted list of checkpoints with names, creation dates, and metadata. On failure, returns error details about container access or listing issues.

  • Success (0): Checkpoint list displayed with specified format
  • Error (1): Container not found or no checkpoints exist
  • Error (125): Invalid command usage or Docker daemon error

5 Practical Django Examples

Example 1: Skills Model Development History (Checkpoint Tracking)

List all checkpoints created during Skills model feature development to track development progress and state management.

# List all checkpoints for Django web container
docker checkpoint ls anihpj_web

# List with detailed format
docker checkpoint ls --format "table {{.Name}}\t{{.Created}}\t{{.Size}}" anihpj_web

Django Models Context:

# Skills model development states preserved in checkpoints
class Skills(models.Model):
    name = models.CharField(max_length=100)
    category = models.CharField(max_length=50)
    level = models.CharField(max_length=20)
    description = models.TextField(blank=True)
    created_at = models.DateTimeField(auto_now_add=True)
    updated_at = models.DateTimeField(auto_now=True)
    
    def __str__(self):
        return f"{self.name} - {self.level}"

# Skills data evolution tracked through checkpoints
checkpoint_skills_data = {
    'initial_state': [
        Skills(name="Python", category="Programming", level="Expert"),
        Skills(name="Django", category="Framework", level="Advanced")
    ],
    'feature_development': [
        Skills(name="PostgreSQL", category="Database", level="Intermediate"),
        Skills(name="Redis", category="Caching", level="Beginner")
    ],
    'final_state': [
        Skills(name="Docker", category="DevOps", level="Advanced"),
        Skills(name="Kubernetes", category="Orchestration", level="Intermediate")
    ]
}

# Print Skills development history from checkpoints
print("Skills Model Development History:")
print(f"Total Skills across all checkpoints: {sum(len(skills) for skills in checkpoint_skills_data.values())}")
for checkpoint_name, skills_list in checkpoint_skills_data.items():
    print(f"\nCheckpoint: {checkpoint_name}")
    for skill in skills_list:
        print(f"  - {skill.name}: {skill.category} ({skill.level})")
CHECKPOINT NAME CREATED SIZE skills_initial_state 3 days ago 2.1MB skills_feature_development 2 days ago 2.3MB skills_final_state 1 day ago 2.5MB Skills Model Development History: Total Skills across all checkpoints: 6 Checkpoint: initial_state - Python: Programming (Expert) - Django: Framework (Advanced) Checkpoint: feature_development - PostgreSQL: Database (Intermediate) - Redis: Caching (Beginner) Checkpoint: final_state - Docker: DevOps (Advanced) - Kubernetes: Orchestration (Intermediate)
Example 2: User Authentication Checkpoint Analysis (Migration History)

Examine checkpoint history for User model migrations to understand database evolution and rollback points.

# List User model migration checkpoints
docker checkpoint ls anihpj_db

# Format output for migration analysis
docker checkpoint ls --format "{{.Name}} - {{.Created}}" anihpj_db

Django Models Context:

# User model evolution captured in database checkpoints
class User(models.Model):
    username = models.CharField(max_length=150, unique=True)
    email = models.EmailField()
    first_name = models.CharField(max_length=50)
    last_name = models.CharField(max_length=50)
    password_hash = models.CharField(max_length=128)
    is_active = models.BooleanField(default=True)
    is_staff = models.BooleanField(default=False)
    is_superuser = models.BooleanField(default=False)
    last_login = models.DateTimeField(null=True, blank=True)
    date_joined = models.DateTimeField(auto_now_add=True)
    
    def __str__(self):
        return self.username

# User data states preserved in migration checkpoints
migration_user_data = {
    'pre_email_migration': [
        User(username="john_doe", first_name="John", last_name="Doe", is_active=True),
        User(username="jane_smith", first_name="Jane", last_name="Smith", is_active=True)
    ],
    'post_email_migration': [
        User(username="john_doe", email="john@anihpj.com", first_name="John", last_name="Doe"),
        User(username="jane_smith", email="jane@anihpj.com", first_name="Jane", last_name="Smith")
    ],
    'authentication_update': [
        User(username="admin_user", email="admin@anihpj.com", is_staff=True, is_superuser=True),
        User(username="test_user", email="test@anihpj.com", is_active=False)
    ]
}

# Print User migration checkpoint analysis
print("User Authentication Migration History:")
print(f"Total migration checkpoints: {len(migration_user_data)}")
for checkpoint_name, users_list in migration_user_data.items():
    print(f"\nMigration Checkpoint: {checkpoint_name}")
    print(f"Users count: {len(users_list)}")
    for user in users_list:
        print(f"  - {user.username}: {user.email or 'No email'} (Active: {user.is_active})")
pre_email_migration - 4 days ago post_email_migration - 3 days ago authentication_update - 2 days ago User Authentication Migration History: Total migration checkpoints: 3 Migration Checkpoint: pre_email_migration Users count: 2 - john_doe: No email (Active: True) - jane_smith: No email (Active: True) Migration Checkpoint: post_email_migration Users count: 2 - john_doe: john@anihpj.com (Active: True) - jane_smith: jane@anihpj.com (Active: True) Migration Checkpoint: authentication_update Users count: 2 - admin_user: admin@anihpj.com (Active: True) - test_user: test@anihpj.com (Active: False)
Example 3: Author Profile Development States (Feature Branch Checkpoints)

List Author model feature development checkpoints to track profile system implementation across different development branches.

# List Author feature development checkpoints
docker checkpoint ls anihpj_web

# Custom format for feature tracking
docker checkpoint ls --format "table {{.Name}}\t{{.Created}}" anihpj_web | grep author

Django Models Context:

# Author model development tracked through feature checkpoints
class Author(models.Model):
    user = models.OneToOneField(User, on_delete=models.CASCADE)
    bio = models.TextField(max_length=500, blank=True)
    profile_image = models.ImageField(upload_to='author_profiles/', null=True, blank=True)
    website = models.URLField(blank=True)
    github_username = models.CharField(max_length=39, blank=True)
    linkedin_profile = models.URLField(blank=True)
    specializations = models.ManyToManyField(Skills, blank=True)
    years_experience = models.PositiveIntegerField(default=0)
    is_verified = models.BooleanField(default=False)
    reputation_score = models.IntegerField(default=0)
    created_at = models.DateTimeField(auto_now_add=True)
    
    def __str__(self):
        return f"Author: {self.user.username}"

# Author development states in feature checkpoints
feature_author_data = {
    'author_basic_profile': [
        Author(user_id=1, bio="Django developer with 5 years experience", years_experience=5),
        Author(user_id=2, bio="Full-stack developer specializing in Python", years_experience=3)
    ],
    'author_social_features': [
        Author(user_id=1, website="https://johndoe.dev", github_username="john_doe_dev", 
               linkedin_profile="https://linkedin.com/in/johndoe", is_verified=True),
        Author(user_id=2, github_username="jane_smith_dev", is_verified=False)
    ],
    'author_reputation_system': [
        Author(user_id=1, reputation_score=150, is_verified=True),
        Author(user_id=2, reputation_score=85, is_verified=True),
        Author(user_id=3, reputation_score=45, is_verified=False)
    ]
}

# Print Author feature development checkpoint analysis
print("Author Profile Feature Development:")
print(f"Feature development checkpoints: {len(feature_author_data)}")
for checkpoint_name, authors_list in feature_author_data.items():
    print(f"\nFeature Checkpoint: {checkpoint_name}")
    print(f"Authors count: {len(authors_list)}")
    for author in authors_list:
        print(f"  - User ID {author.user_id}: Experience: {author.years_experience} years")
        print(f"    Bio: {author.bio[:50]}..." if author.bio else "    No bio")
        print(f"    Verified: {author.is_verified}, Reputation: {author.reputation_score}")
        if hasattr(author, 'website') and author.website:
            print(f"    Website: {author.website}")
author_basic_profile 2 days ago author_social_features 1 day ago author_reputation_system 6 hours ago Author Profile Feature Development: Feature development checkpoints: 3 Feature Checkpoint: author_basic_profile Authors count: 2 - User ID 1: Experience: 5 years Bio: Django developer with 5 years experience Verified: False, Reputation: 0 - User ID 2: Experience: 3 years Bio: Full-stack developer specializing in Python... Verified: False, Reputation: 0 Feature Checkpoint: author_social_features Authors count: 2 - User ID 1: Experience: 0 years No bio Verified: True, Reputation: 0 Website: https://johndoe.dev - User ID 2: Experience: 0 years No bio Verified: False, Reputation: 0 Feature Checkpoint: author_reputation_system Authors count: 3 - User ID 1: Experience: 0 years No bio Verified: True, Reputation: 150 - User ID 2: Experience: 0 years No bio Verified: True, Reputation: 85 - User ID 3: Experience: 0 years No bio Verified: False, Reputation: 45
Example 4: Location Data Processing Checkpoints (Geographic Feature Testing)

Examine Location model testing checkpoints to analyze geographic data processing and query optimization states.

# List Location processing checkpoints
docker checkpoint ls anihpj_web

# Filter location-specific checkpoints
docker checkpoint ls --format "{{.Name}} ({{.Created}})" anihpj_web | grep location

Django Models Context:

# Location model testing states preserved in checkpoints
class Location(models.Model):
    name = models.CharField(max_length=100)
    country = models.CharField(max_length=50)
    region = models.CharField(max_length=50)
    city = models.CharField(max_length=50)
    postal_code = models.CharField(max_length=10)
    latitude = models.DecimalField(max_digits=9, decimal_places=6)
    longitude = models.DecimalField(max_digits=9, decimal_places=6)
    timezone = models.CharField(max_length=50)
    is_remote_friendly = models.BooleanField(default=False)
    cost_of_living_index = models.IntegerField(null=True, blank=True)
    created_at = models.DateTimeField(auto_now_add=True)
    
    def __str__(self):
        return f"{self.city}, {self.country}"

# Location testing data across checkpoint states
location_test_data = {
    'location_basic_data': [
        Location(name="San Francisco Bay Area", country="USA", region="California", 
                city="San Francisco", postal_code="94105", latitude=37.7749, longitude=-122.4194,
                timezone="America/Los_Angeles", cost_of_living_index=95),
        Location(name="New York Metro", country="USA", region="New York", 
                city="New York", postal_code="10001", latitude=40.7128, longitude=-74.0060,
                timezone="America/New_York", cost_of_living_index=88)
    ],
    'location_geographic_features': [
        Location(name="London Tech Hub", country="UK", region="England", 
                city="London", postal_code="EC1A", latitude=51.5074, longitude=-0.1278,
                timezone="Europe/London", cost_of_living_index=85),
        Location(name="Berlin Startup Scene", country="Germany", region="Brandenburg", 
                city="Berlin", postal_code="10115", latitude=52.5200, longitude=13.4050,
                timezone="Europe/Berlin", cost_of_living_index=75)
    ],
    'location_remote_optimization': [
        Location(name="Remote Position Global", country="Global", region="Remote", 
                city="Remote", postal_code="00000", latitude=0.0000, longitude=0.0000,
                timezone="UTC", is_remote_friendly=True, cost_of_living_index=50),
        Location(name="Hybrid Work Hub", country="Canada", region="Ontario", 
                city="Toronto", postal_code="M5H", latitude=43.6532, longitude=-79.3832,
                timezone="America/Toronto", is_remote_friendly=True, cost_of_living_index=78)
    ]
}

# Print Location testing checkpoint analysis
print("Location Geographic Processing History:")
print(f"Testing checkpoint states: {len(location_test_data)}")
total_locations = sum(len(locations) for locations in location_test_data.values())
print(f"Total locations tested: {total_locations}")

for checkpoint_name, locations_list in location_test_data.items():
    print(f"\nCheckpoint State: {checkpoint_name}")
    print(f"Locations tested: {len(locations_list)}")
    for location in locations_list:
        print(f"  - {location.name}")
        print(f"    City: {location.city}, Country: {location.country}")
        print(f"    Coordinates: ({location.latitude}, {location.longitude})")
        print(f"    Remote Friendly: {location.is_remote_friendly}")
        print(f"    Cost Index: {location.cost_of_living_index}")
location_basic_data (3 days ago) location_geographic_features (2 days ago) location_remote_optimization (1 day ago) Location Geographic Processing History: Testing checkpoint states: 3 Total locations tested: 6 Checkpoint State: location_basic_data Locations tested: 2 - San Francisco Bay Area City: San Francisco, Country: USA Coordinates: (37.774900, -122.419400) Remote Friendly: False Cost Index: 95 - New York Metro City: New York, Country: USA Coordinates: (40.712800, -74.006000) Remote Friendly: False Cost Index: 88 Checkpoint State: location_geographic_features Locations tested: 2 - London Tech Hub City: London, Country: UK Coordinates: (51.507400, -0.127800) Remote Friendly: False Cost Index: 85 - Berlin Startup Scene City: Berlin, Country: Germany Coordinates: (52.520000, 13.405000) Remote Friendly: False Cost Index: 75 Checkpoint State: location_remote_optimization Locations tested: 2 - Remote Position Global City: Remote, Country: Global Coordinates: (0.000000, 0.000000) Remote Friendly: True Cost Index: 50 - Hybrid Work Hub City: Toronto, Country: Canada Coordinates: (43.653200, -79.383200) Remote Friendly: True Cost Index: 78
Example 5: JobPost Processing Queue Checkpoints (Background Task Analysis)

List Celery worker checkpoints to analyze JobPost processing states and background task queue management during high-load periods.

# List JobPost processing checkpoints
docker checkpoint ls anihpj_worker

# Detailed format for queue analysis
docker checkpoint ls --format "table {{.Name}}\t{{.Created}}\t{{.Size}}" anihpj_worker

Django Models Context:

# JobPost model processing states captured in worker checkpoints
class JobPost(models.Model):
    title = models.CharField(max_length=200)
    description = models.TextField()
    author = models.ForeignKey(Author, on_delete=models.CASCADE)
    location = models.ForeignKey(Location, on_delete=models.CASCADE)
    required_skills = models.ManyToManyField(Skills)
    salary_min = models.DecimalField(max_digits=10, decimal_places=2, null=True, blank=True)
    salary_max = models.DecimalField(max_digits=10, decimal_places=2, null=True, blank=True)
    employment_type = models.CharField(max_length=20, default='FULL_TIME')
    experience_level = models.CharField(max_length=20, default='MID_LEVEL')
    is_remote = models.BooleanField(default=False)
    is_published = models.BooleanField(default=False)
    is_featured = models.BooleanField(default=False)
    application_deadline = models.DateTimeField(null=True, blank=True)
    created_at = models.DateTimeField(auto_now_add=True)
    updated_at = models.DateTimeField(auto_now=True)
    
    def __str__(self):
        return self.title

# JobPost processing queue states in worker checkpoints
queue_processing_data = {
    'jobpost_queue_initial': [
        JobPost(title="Senior Django Developer", description="Looking for experienced Django developer...",
                author_id=1, location_id=1, salary_min=120000, salary_max=150000,
                employment_type='FULL_TIME', experience_level='SENIOR', is_remote=True),
        JobPost(title="Python Data Scientist", description="Data scientist role with Python focus...",
                author_id=2, location_id=2, salary_min=100000, salary_max=130000,
                employment_type='FULL_TIME', experience_level='MID_LEVEL', is_remote=False)
    ],
    'jobpost_processing_heavy_load': [
        JobPost(title="Full Stack Developer", description="Full stack position with Django and React...",
                author_id=1, location_id=3, salary_min=90000, salary_max=120000,
                employment_type='FULL_TIME', experience_level='MID_LEVEL', is_remote=True, is_published=True),
        JobPost(title="DevOps Engineer", description="DevOps role with Docker and Kubernetes...",
                author_id=2, location_id=4, salary_min=110000, salary_max=140000,
                employment_type='FULL_TIME', experience_level='SENIOR', is_remote=True, is_featured=True),
        JobPost(title="Junior Python Developer", description="Entry-level Python development position...",
                author_id=1, location_id=5, salary_min=60000, salary_max=80000,
                employment_type='FULL_TIME', experience_level='JUNIOR', is_remote=False)
    ],
    'jobpost_queue_optimized': [
        JobPost(title="Machine Learning Engineer", description="ML engineer for AI-driven job matching...",
                author_id=2, location_id=6, salary_min=140000, salary_max=180000,
                employment_type='FULL_TIME', experience_level='SENIOR', is_remote=True, is_published=True, is_featured=True),
        JobPost(title="Contract Frontend Developer", description="Short-term React development contract...",
                author_id=1, location_id=1, salary_min=80000, salary_max=100000,
                employment_type='CONTRACT', experience_level='MID_LEVEL', is_remote=True, is_published=True)
    ]
}

# Print JobPost queue processing analysis
print("JobPost Processing Queue Analysis:")
print(f"Worker checkpoint states: {len(queue_processing_data)}")
total_jobs = sum(len(jobs) for jobs in queue_processing_data.values())
print(f"Total jobs processed: {total_jobs}")

for checkpoint_name, jobs_list in queue_processing_data.items():
    print(f"\nQueue State: {checkpoint_name}")
    print(f"Jobs in queue: {len(jobs_list)}")
    published_count = sum(1 for job in jobs_list if hasattr(job, 'is_published') and job.is_published)
    featured_count = sum(1 for job in jobs_list if hasattr(job, 'is_featured') and job.is_featured)
    remote_count = sum(1 for job in jobs_list if job.is_remote)
    
    print(f"Published: {published_count}, Featured: {featured_count}, Remote: {remote_count}")
    
    for job in jobs_list:
        print(f"  - {job.title}")
        print(f"    Salary: ${job.salary_min:,} - ${job.salary_max:,}")
        print(f"    Type: {job.employment_type}, Level: {job.experience_level}")
        print(f"    Remote: {job.is_remote}, Published: {getattr(job, 'is_published', False)}")
CHECKPOINT NAME CREATED SIZE jobpost_queue_initial 4 hours ago 1.8MB jobpost_processing_heavy_load 2 hours ago 3.2MB jobpost_queue_optimized 30 min ago 2.1MB JobPost Processing Queue Analysis: Worker checkpoint states: 3 Total jobs processed: 7 Queue State: jobpost_queue_initial Jobs in queue: 2 Published: 0, Featured: 0, Remote: 1 - Senior Django Developer Salary: $120,000 - $150,000 Type: FULL_TIME, Level: SENIOR Remote: True, Published: False - Python Data Scientist Salary: $100,000 - $130,000 Type: FULL_TIME, Level: MID_LEVEL Remote: False, Published: False Queue State: jobpost_processing_heavy_load Jobs in queue: 3 Published: 1, Featured: 1, Remote: 2 - Full Stack Developer Salary: $90,000 - $120,000 Type: FULL_TIME, Level: MID_LEVEL Remote: True, Published: True - DevOps Engineer Salary: $110,000 - $140,000 Type: FULL_TIME, Level: SENIOR Remote: True, Published: False - Junior Python Developer Salary: $60,000 - $80,000 Type: FULL_TIME, Level: JUNIOR Remote: False, Published: False Queue State: jobpost_queue_optimized Jobs in queue: 2 Published: 2, Featured: 1, Remote: 2 - Machine Learning Engineer Salary: $140,000 - $180,000 Type: FULL_TIME, Level: SENIOR Remote: True, Published: True - Contract Frontend Developer Salary: $80,000 - $100,000 Type: CONTRACT, Level: MID_LEVEL Remote: True, Published: True
# List all checkpoints for Django web container
docker checkpoint ls anihpj_web

# List checkpoints for database container
docker checkpoint ls anihpj_db

# List checkpoints for Celery worker with formatted output
docker checkpoint ls --format "table {{.Name}}\t{{.Created}}" anihpj_worker

Key Options for anihpj Django Development

  • --format: Format the output using a Go template. Useful for scripting and automation in Django development workflows.
  • CONTAINER: Name or ID of the container to list checkpoints for (anihpj_web, anihpj_db, anihpj_worker).
Syntax
docker checkpoint ls [OPTIONS] CONTAINER

Django-Specific Use Cases

Development History Tracking
# View all Django server checkpoints
docker checkpoint ls anihpj_web

# Check database checkpoint history with timestamps
docker checkpoint ls --format "{{.Name}} - {{.Created}}" anihpj_db

# List worker checkpoints for task debugging
docker checkpoint ls anihpj_worker

Track development checkpoint history to understand available restore points for your Django application components.

Real-world anihpj Examples

Checkpoint Inventory
# List all web server checkpoints
docker checkpoint ls anihpj_web
CHECKPOINT NAME CREATED debug_state 2 hours ago before_refactor 1 day ago jobpost_bug_reproduction 3 days ago

docker checkpoint rm

Official Docs: docker checkpoint rm | Docker CLI: docker checkpoint rm

Remove one or more checkpoints from a container.

Full Description

The docker checkpoint rm command removes one or more checkpoints from a container. For your anihpj Django project, this command helps you clean up unused checkpoint states, manage disk space used by saved container states, and maintain a clean checkpoint history for your Django development and testing environments.

When removing checkpoints from your anihpj Django application containers, the command permanently deletes checkpoint data including preserved memory states, process information, and application snapshots. This enables efficient storage management and cleanup of obsolete development states from Skills model testing, User authentication debugging, Author profile experiments, Location processing snapshots, and JobPost queue management operations.

Syntax

# Remove single checkpoint
docker checkpoint rm {container} {checkpoint-name}

# Remove multiple checkpoints
docker checkpoint rm {container} {checkpoint1} {checkpoint2} {checkpoint3}

# Remove specific Django development checkpoint
docker checkpoint rm anihpj_web debug_state

# Remove multiple old checkpoints
docker checkpoint rm anihpj_web old_checkpoint1 old_checkpoint2

# Remove database checkpoint after testing
docker checkpoint rm anihpj_db test_migration_checkpoint

Parameters Description

  • container: Name or ID of the container containing the checkpoints to remove. Must be existing container with stored checkpoint data.
  • checkpoint-name: One or more checkpoint names to remove from the container. Checkpoint names must exist or command will return error.

Attributes

  • Removal Status: Confirmation of successful checkpoint deletion. Shows which checkpoints were removed and any failures.
  • Storage Freed: Amount of disk space recovered by removing checkpoint data. Important for managing Django development environment storage.
  • Checkpoint Count: Remaining number of checkpoints after removal operation. Helps track checkpoint storage management.
  • Container State: Current state of container after checkpoint removal. Confirms container remains functional after cleanup.
  • Operation Time: Duration required to remove checkpoint data. Varies based on checkpoint size and storage performance.

Methods

Remove Single Checkpoint

Syntax: docker checkpoint rm {container} {checkpoint-name}

Parameters: container (string) - Django container name, checkpoint-name (string) - Specific checkpoint to remove

Remove individual checkpoint from Django development container, useful for cleaning up completed debugging sessions or obsolete development states.

Bulk Checkpoint Removal

Syntax: docker checkpoint rm {container} {checkpoint1} {checkpoint2} {checkpointN}

Parameters: container (string) - Django container name, checkpointN (string) - Multiple checkpoint names to remove

Remove multiple checkpoints simultaneously for efficient storage cleanup and checkpoint history management in Django development workflows.

Return Value

Returns confirmation message for each successfully removed checkpoint. On failure, returns error details about missing checkpoints or removal issues.

  • Success (0): Checkpoint(s) removed successfully with confirmation
  • Error (1): Checkpoint not found or container access denied
  • Error (125): Invalid command usage or Docker daemon error

5 Practical Django Examples

Example 1: Skills Model Testing Cleanup (Development State Management)

Remove obsolete Skills model testing checkpoints after completing feature development to free storage space.

# Remove completed Skills testing checkpoints
docker checkpoint rm anihpj_web skills_test_session1 skills_test_session2

# Verify checkpoints removed
docker checkpoint ls anihpj_web

Django Models Context:

# Skills model testing states being cleaned up
class Skills(models.Model):
    name = models.CharField(max_length=100)
    category = models.CharField(max_length=50)
    level = models.CharField(max_length=20)
    certification_url = models.URLField(blank=True)
    created_at = models.DateTimeField(auto_now_add=True)
    
    def __str__(self):
        return f"{self.name} - {self.level}"

# Skills data from removed testing checkpoints
removed_checkpoint_skills = {
    'skills_test_session1': [
        Skills(name="Python", category="Programming", level="Expert"),
        Skills(name="Django", category="Framework", level="Advanced"),
        Skills(name="PostgreSQL", category="Database", level="Intermediate")
    ],
    'skills_test_session2': [
        Skills(name="Docker", category="DevOps", level="Advanced"),
        Skills(name="Redis", category="Caching", level="Beginner"),
        Skills(name="Celery", category="Task Queue", level="Intermediate")
    ]
}

# Print cleanup results for Skills testing
print("Skills Model Testing Checkpoint Cleanup:")
total_removed = sum(len(skills) for skills in removed_checkpoint_skills.values())
print(f"Checkpoints removed: {len(removed_checkpoint_skills)}")
print(f"Total Skills test data cleaned: {total_removed}")

for checkpoint_name, skills_list in removed_checkpoint_skills.items():
    print(f"\nRemoved Checkpoint: {checkpoint_name}")
    print(f"Skills test data cleared: {len(skills_list)}")
    for skill in skills_list:
        print(f"  - {skill.name}: {skill.category} ({skill.level})")

# Current remaining Skills after cleanup
remaining_skills = [
    Skills(name="Kubernetes", category="Orchestration", level="Intermediate"),
    Skills(name="AWS", category="Cloud", level="Advanced")
]
print(f"\nRemaining Skills in development: {len(remaining_skills)}")
for skill in remaining_skills:
    print(f"  - {skill.name}: {skill.category} ({skill.level})")
