Get Django container disk usage including writable layer size.

Return Value

Returns JSON array with detailed container information.

  • Success: Container details returned as JSON
  • Error (1): Container not found

5 Practical Django Examples

Example 1: Get Container IP Address

Retrieve Django container network IP.

docker container inspect --format='{{.NetworkSettings.IPAddress}}' anihpj-web
172.17.0.5
Example 2: Check Environment Variables

View Django configuration environment variables.

docker container inspect --format='{{range .Config.Env}}{{println .}}{{end}}' anihpj-web
DJANGO_SETTINGS_MODULE=anihpj.settings.production
DATABASE_URL=postgresql://user:pass@db:5432/anihpj
REDIS_URL=redis://redis:6379/0
Example 3: Verify Volume Mounts

Check Django static and media volume mounts.

docker container inspect --format='{{range .Mounts}}{{.Source}} → {{.Destination}}{{println}}{{end}}' anihpj-web
/var/lib/docker/volumes/anihpj-static → /app/static
/var/lib/docker/volumes/anihpj-media → /app/media
Example 4: Check Container Status

Get Django container health and state information.

docker container inspect --format='Status: {{.State.Status}} | Running: {{.State.Running}} | Health: {{.State.Health.Status}}' anihpj-web
Status: running | Running: true | Health: healthy
Example 5: Get Port Mappings

View Django application port bindings.

docker container inspect --format='{{range $p, $conf := .NetworkSettings.Ports}}{{$p}} → {{(index $conf 0).HostPort}}{{println}}{{end}}' anihpj-web
8000/tcp → 8000

docker container kill

Official Docs: docker container kill | Docker CLI: docker container kill

Kill one or more running containers by sending SIGKILL or a specified signal.

Full Description

The docker container kill command immediately stops a container by sending a SIGKILL signal (or custom signal). For your anihpj Django project, this command is used for force-stopping unresponsive containers, emergency shutdowns, testing failure scenarios, and handling frozen processes in your User authentication, JobPost management, Skills verification, Author profiles, and Location services.

Syntax

docker container kill [OPTIONS] CONTAINER [CONTAINER...]

# Kill Django container
docker container kill anihpj-web

# Send specific signal
docker container kill --signal=SIGTERM anihpj-web

Parameters Description

  • CONTAINER: Container name or ID to kill.
  • -s, --signal: Signal to send (default SIGKILL). Specify signal for Django container termination.

Attributes

  • Immediate Termination: Sends SIGKILL by default. Force stop frozen Django containers instantly.
  • Signal Flexibility: Supports custom Unix signals. Send SIGTERM, SIGHUP for graceful Django shutdown.
  • No Grace Period: Does not wait for cleanup. Faster than docker stop for emergency situations.
  • Running Container Only: Only affects running containers. Cannot kill stopped Django containers.
  • Multi-Container Support: Kill multiple containers simultaneously. Stop entire Django stack quickly.

Methods

Force Kill

Syntax: docker container kill [CONTAINER]

Parameters: CONTAINER (string) - Container name or ID

Immediately kill Django container with SIGKILL signal.

Graceful Signal

Syntax: docker container kill --signal=[SIGNAL] [CONTAINER]

Parameters: SIGNAL (string) - Unix signal name, CONTAINER (string)

Send custom signal like SIGTERM for cleaner Django shutdown.

Batch Kill

Syntax: docker container kill [CONTAINER1] [CONTAINER2] ...

Parameters: Multiple CONTAINER names or IDs

Kill multiple Django service containers in one command.

Return Value

Returns container name or ID of killed containers.

  • Success: Container killed
  • Error (1): Container not found or not running

5 Practical Django Examples

Example 1: Force Stop Frozen Container

Kill unresponsive Django container immediately.

docker container kill anihpj-web
anihpj-web
✓ Container killed (SIGKILL sent)
Example 2: Graceful Signal

Send SIGTERM for cleaner Django shutdown.

docker container kill --signal=SIGTERM anihpj-web
anihpj-web
✓ SIGTERM sent (graceful shutdown)
Example 3: Kill Multiple Containers

Force stop all Django service containers.

docker container kill anihpj-web anihpj-api anihpj-worker
anihpj-web
anihpj-api
anihpj-worker
✓ 3 containers killed
Example 4: Send Custom Signal

Send SIGHUP to reload Django configuration.

docker container kill --signal=SIGHUP anihpj-web
anihpj-web
✓ SIGHUP sent (config reload triggered)
Example 5: Emergency Shutdown Script

Kill all containers matching pattern.

docker container ls --filter name=anihpj -q | xargs docker container kill
f8g9h1i2j3k4
l5m6n7o8p9q0
r1s2t3u4v5w6
✓ All anihpj containers killed

docker container logs

Official Docs: docker container logs | Docker CLI: docker container logs

Fetch the logs of a container.

Full Description

The docker container logs command retrieves logs from container stdout and stderr. For your anihpj Django project, this enables monitoring application output, debugging issues, tracking requests, analyzing errors, and reviewing logs from your User authentication, JobPost management, Skills verification, Author profiles, and Location services.

Syntax

docker container logs [OPTIONS] CONTAINER

# View Django logs
docker container logs anihpj-web

# Follow logs in real-time
docker container logs --follow --tail=100 anihpj-web

Parameters Description

  • CONTAINER: Container name or ID.
  • -f, --follow: Follow log output. Monitor Django application in real-time.
  • --tail: Number of lines from end. Show recent Django logs only.
  • -t, --timestamps: Show timestamps. Include exact timing for Django events.
  • --since: Show logs since timestamp or relative time. Filter Django logs by time range.
  • --until: Show logs until timestamp. View Django logs up to specific time.

Attributes

  • Stream Capture: Captures stdout and stderr streams. Monitor Django application output and errors together.
  • Persistent History: Logs retained after container stops. Review Django logs from previous runs.
  • Real-Time Following: Live log streaming with --follow. Monitor Django applications as they execute.
  • Time Filtering: Filter by timestamp or relative duration. Analyze Django logs for specific timeframes.
  • No Persistence: Logs lost when container removed. Use external logging for Django production.

Methods

View Recent Logs

Syntax: docker container logs --tail=[N] [CONTAINER]

Parameters: N (integer) - Number of lines, CONTAINER (string)

Display last N lines of Django container logs for quick status check.

Follow Logs Real-Time

Syntax: docker container logs --follow [CONTAINER]

Parameters: CONTAINER (string) - Container name or ID

Stream Django logs continuously for live monitoring and debugging.

Time-Range Filtering

Syntax: docker container logs --since=[TIME] --until=[TIME] [CONTAINER]

Parameters: TIME (string) - Timestamp or relative (e.g., 30m), CONTAINER (string)

Extract Django logs for specific time window for analysis.

Return Value

Returns container stdout/stderr logs.

  • Success: Logs displayed
  • Error (1): Container not found

5 Practical Django Examples

Example 1: View Recent Application Logs

Display last 50 lines of Django logs.

docker container logs --tail=50 anihpj-web
[2026-01-14 15:23:45] INFO: Django startup complete
[2026-01-14 15:24:12] INFO: GET /api/jobposts/ - 200 OK
[2026-01-14 15:24:18] INFO: User 'john_dev' authenticated
[2026-01-14 15:24:25] INFO: Created JobPost: "Senior Python Developer"
[2026-01-14 15:24:30] INFO: Skills verified: Python, Django, PostgreSQL
Example 2: Follow Logs Real-Time

Monitor Django application output continuously.

docker container logs --follow anihpj-web
[STREAMING] Watching logs...
[2026-01-14 15:30:01] INFO: Request: POST /api/jobposts/create/
[2026-01-14 15:30:02] INFO: Author profile updated
[2026-01-14 15:30:03] INFO: Location set: San Francisco, CA
^C (Press Ctrl+C to stop)
Example 3: Logs with Timestamps

View Django logs with precise timestamps.

docker container logs --timestamps --tail=20 anihpj-web
2026-01-14T15:23:45.123456Z INFO: Application started
2026-01-14T15:24:12.789012Z INFO: Database connection established
2026-01-14T15:24:25.345678Z INFO: 5 JobPosts loaded
2026-01-14T15:24:30.901234Z INFO: Redis cache connected
Example 4: Filter Logs by Time Range

View Django logs from last 30 minutes.

docker container logs --since=30m anihpj-web
[15:25:00] INFO: User registration: sarah_jobs
[15:26:15] INFO: JobPost published: "Frontend Developer"
[15:27:30] ERROR: Skills validation failed for user_id=42
[15:28:45] INFO: Email sent: job_alert@example.com
Example 5: Search Logs for Errors

Filter Django error logs only.

docker container logs anihpj-web 2>&1 | grep -i error
ERROR: Database connection timeout after 30s
ERROR: JobPost validation failed: missing required field 'salary'
ERROR: Skills lookup failed for skill_id=999

docker container ls

Official Docs: docker container ls | Docker CLI: docker container ls

List containers (alias: docker ps).

Full Description

The docker container ls command lists containers with details like status, names, ports, and images. For your anihpj Django project, this enables monitoring running services, checking container health, viewing resource allocation, and managing your User authentication, JobPost management, Skills verification, Author profiles, and Location services infrastructure.

Syntax

docker container ls [OPTIONS]

# List running containers
docker container ls

# List all containers (including stopped)
docker container ls --all

Parameters Description

  • -a, --all: Show all containers (stopped + running). Include Django development containers.
  • -f, --filter: Filter output based on conditions. Filter Django containers by status, name, label.
  • -q, --quiet: Only display container IDs. Get Django container IDs for scripting.
  • -s, --size: Display total file sizes. Show Django container disk usage.
  • --format: Format output using template. Custom Django container display.

Attributes

  • Status Overview: Displays container state and uptime. Monitor Django service health at a glance.
  • Resource Visibility: Shows port mappings and networks. Verify Django container connectivity.
  • Flexible Filtering: Filter by status, name, label, or ID. Find specific Django containers quickly.
  • Output Formatting: Go template support for custom columns. Extract Django container data for scripts.
  • Quick Reference: Replaces docker ps command. Standard tool for Django container management.

Methods

List Running Containers

Syntax: docker container ls

Parameters: None

Display currently running Django containers only.

List All Containers

Syntax: docker container ls --all

Parameters: None

Show all Django containers including stopped and exited ones.

Filtered Listing

Syntax: docker container ls --filter [KEY=VALUE]

Parameters: KEY=VALUE (string) - Filter criteria like name, status, label

List Django containers matching specific filter conditions.

Return Value

Returns table with container information.

  • Success: Container list displayed

5 Practical Django Examples

Example 1: List Running Containers

View active Django service containers.

docker container ls
CONTAINER ID   IMAGE              COMMAND                  CREATED       STATUS       PORTS                    NAMES
f8g9h1i2j3k4   anihpj-web:latest  "gunicorn anihpj..."     2 hours ago   Up 2 hours   0.0.0.0:8000->8000/tcp   anihpj-web
l5m6n7o8p9q0   postgres:16        "docker-entrypoint..."   2 hours ago   Up 2 hours   5432/tcp                 anihpj-db
r1s2t3u4v5w6   redis:7-alpine     "redis-server..."        2 hours ago   Up 2 hours   6379/tcp                 anihpj-redis
Example 2: List All Containers (Including Stopped)

View complete Django container inventory.

docker container ls --all
CONTAINER ID   IMAGE              STATUS                      NAMES
f8g9h1i2j3k4   anihpj-web:latest  Up 2 hours                  anihpj-web
l5m6n7o8p9q0   postgres:16        Up 2 hours                  anihpj-db
r1s2t3u4v5w6   redis:7-alpine     Up 2 hours                  anihpj-redis
a1b2c3d4e5f6   anihpj-worker      Exited (0) 30 minutes ago   anihpj-worker-old
Example 3: Filter by Name Pattern

List only anihpj Django containers.

docker container ls --filter name=anihpj
CONTAINER ID   IMAGE              NAMES
f8g9h1i2j3k4   anihpj-web:latest  anihpj-web
a1b2c3d4e5f6   anihpj-worker      anihpj-worker-old
Example 4: Custom Format Output

Display Django containers with custom columns.

docker container ls --format "table {{.Names}}\t{{.Status}}\t{{.Ports}}"
NAMES           STATUS       PORTS
anihpj-web      Up 2 hours   0.0.0.0:8000->8000/tcp
anihpj-db       Up 2 hours   5432/tcp
anihpj-redis    Up 2 hours   6379/tcp
Example 5: Get Container IDs Only

Extract Django container IDs for scripting.

docker container ls --filter name=anihpj --quiet
f8g9h1i2j3k4
a1b2c3d4e5f6

docker container pause

Official Docs: docker container pause | Docker CLI: docker container pause

Pause all processes within one or more containers.

Full Description

The docker container pause command suspends all processes in a container using cgroups freezer. For your anihpj Django project, this enables pausing containers during maintenance, testing resource limits, creating consistent backups, and temporarily halting services for your User authentication, JobPost management, Skills verification, Author profiles, and Location services without stopping them completely.

Syntax

docker container pause CONTAINER [CONTAINER...]

# Pause Django container
docker container pause anihpj-web

# Pause multiple containers
docker container pause anihpj-web anihpj-worker

Parameters Description

  • CONTAINER: Container name or ID to pause.

Attributes

  • Process Suspension: Uses cgroups freezer to halt processes. Pause Django containers without terminating.
  • State Preservation: Memory and connections maintained. Django application resumes exactly where paused.
  • Quick Operation: Instant pause with no cleanup time. Faster than stopping Django containers.
  • Unpause Support: Resume with docker unpause command. Temporary halts for Django maintenance.
  • No Data Loss: All application state retained. Safe pause for Django services during backups.

Methods

Single Container Pause

Syntax: docker container pause [CONTAINER]

Parameters: CONTAINER (string) - Container name or ID

Pause one Django container's processes completely.

Multi-Container Pause

Syntax: docker container pause [CONTAINER1] [CONTAINER2] ...

Parameters: Multiple CONTAINER names or IDs

Pause multiple Django service containers simultaneously.

Pause and Resume Pattern

Syntax: docker container pause [CONTAINER] && [TASK] && docker container unpause [CONTAINER]

Parameters: CONTAINER (string), TASK (command) - Operation to perform

Pause Django container, perform maintenance, then resume operation.

Return Value

Returns container name or ID of paused containers.

  • Success: Container paused
  • Error (1): Container not found or not running

5 Practical Django Examples

Example 1: Pause During Database Backup

Pause Django container for consistent backup.

docker container pause anihpj-web
anihpj-web
✓ Container paused (all processes frozen)
Example 2: Pause All Service Containers

Temporarily halt all Django services.

docker container pause anihpj-web anihpj-worker anihpj-api
anihpj-web
anihpj-worker
anihpj-api
✓ 3 containers paused
Example 3: Pause for Testing

Pause Django container to test failure scenarios.

docker container pause anihpj-web
# Test load balancer failover
sleep 30
docker container unpause anihpj-web
anihpj-web paused
Testing failover...
anihpj-web unpaused
✓ Failover test complete
Example 4: Pause by Filter

Pause all anihpj containers at once.

docker container ls --filter name=anihpj -q | xargs docker container pause
f8g9h1i2j3k4
l5m6n7o8p9q0
✓ All anihpj containers paused
Example 5: Pause and Check Status

Verify Django container pause state.

docker container pause anihpj-web && docker container inspect --format='{{.State.Status}}' anihpj-web
anihpj-web
paused

docker container port

Official Docs: docker container port | Docker CLI: docker container port

List port mappings or a specific mapping for the container.

Full Description

The docker container port command displays port mappings between container and host. For your anihpj Django project, this helps verify network configuration, troubleshoot connectivity, check service accessibility, and manage port bindings for your User authentication, JobPost management, Skills verification, Author profiles, and Location services.

Syntax

docker container port CONTAINER [PRIVATE_PORT[/PROTO]]

# Show all Django port mappings
docker container port anihpj-web

# Show specific port mapping
docker container port anihpj-web 8000/tcp

Parameters Description

  • CONTAINER: Container name or ID.
  • PRIVATE_PORT: Container internal port to check. Verify Django application port binding.
  • PROTO: Protocol (tcp/udp). Specify protocol for Django port mapping.

Attributes

  • Port Mapping Display: Shows internal to external port bindings. Verify Django application accessibility.
  • Protocol Support: Displays TCP and UDP mappings. Check Django port configurations completely.
  • IPv4/IPv6 Display: Shows both IP versions. Verify Django network accessibility options.
  • Quick Reference: Fast port lookup without inspect command. Check Django ports instantly.
  • Debugging Aid: Identify connection issues. Troubleshoot Django network accessibility.

Methods

All Ports Lookup

Syntax: docker container port [CONTAINER]

Parameters: CONTAINER (string) - Container name or ID

Display all port mappings for Django container.

Specific Port Check

Syntax: docker container port [CONTAINER] [PORT]/[PROTO]

Parameters: CONTAINER (string), PORT (integer), PROTO (tcp/udp)

Check specific Django port binding and host mapping.

Batch Port Check

Syntax: for c in $(docker container ls -q); do docker container port $c; done

Parameters: None (iterates all containers)

Display port mappings for all Django service containers.

Return Value

Returns port mapping information.

  • Success: Port mappings displayed
  • Error (1): Container not found or no port mappings

5 Practical Django Examples

Example 1: Check All Port Mappings

View all Django container port bindings.

docker container port anihpj-web
8000/tcp -> 0.0.0.0:8000
8000/tcp -> [::]:8000
Example 2: Check Specific Port

Verify Django application port mapping.

docker container port anihpj-web 8000/tcp
0.0.0.0:8000
Example 3: Check Database Port

View PostgreSQL port mapping.

docker container port anihpj-db 5432
0.0.0.0:5432
Example 4: Check All Service Ports

View port mappings for all Django services.

for container in anihpj-web anihpj-api anihpj-db; do
  echo "=== $container ===" 
  docker container port $container
done
=== anihpj-web ===
8000/tcp -> 0.0.0.0:8000
=== anihpj-api ===
8001/tcp -> 0.0.0.0:8001
=== anihpj-db ===
5432/tcp -> 0.0.0.0:5432
Example 5: Format Port Check

Display Django container name with port mapping.

docker container ls --format "{{.Names}}" | xargs -I {} sh -c 'echo "Container: {}" && docker container port {}'
Container: anihpj-web
8000/tcp -> 0.0.0.0:8000
Container: anihpj-db
5432/tcp -> 0.0.0.0:5432

docker container prune

Official Docs: docker container prune | Docker CLI: docker container prune

Remove all stopped containers.

Full Description

The docker container prune command removes all stopped containers to free up disk space. For your anihpj Django project, this helps maintain clean development environment, remove old test containers, free up resources, and manage container lifecycle for your User authentication, JobPost management, Skills verification, Author profiles, and Location services.

Syntax

docker container prune [OPTIONS]

# Remove all stopped Django containers
docker container prune

# Force removal without confirmation
docker container prune --force

Parameters Description

  • -f, --force: Don't prompt for confirmation. Auto-remove stopped Django containers.
  • --filter: Filter containers based on conditions. Remove specific Django test containers.

Attributes

  • Bulk Cleanup: Removes all stopped containers at once. Clean Django development environment efficiently.
  • Space Recovery: Reports disk space reclaimed. Track storage freed from Django containers.
  • Confirmation Prompt: Asks before deletion by default. Prevent accidental Django container removal.
  • Filter Support: Selective removal with filters. Target specific Django test or development containers.
  • Safe Operation: Only affects stopped containers. Running Django services protected from deletion.

Methods

Interactive Cleanup

Syntax: docker container prune

Parameters: None

Remove all stopped Django containers with confirmation prompt.

Forced Cleanup

Syntax: docker container prune --force

Parameters: None

Auto-remove stopped Django containers without confirmation for scripts.

Filtered Cleanup

Syntax: docker container prune --filter "[KEY=VALUE]" --force

Parameters: KEY=VALUE (string) - Filter like label, until

Remove stopped Django containers matching specific filter criteria.

Return Value

Returns list of deleted containers and space reclaimed.

  • Success: Containers removed, space displayed

5 Practical Django Examples

Example 1: Clean Up Development Environment

Remove all stopped Django containers.

docker container prune
WARNING! This will remove all stopped containers.
Are you sure you want to continue? [y/N] y
Deleted Containers:
a1b2c3d4e5f6
g7h8i9j0k1l2
m3n4o5p6q7r8

Total reclaimed space: 2.3GB
Example 2: Force Cleanup Without Prompt

Auto-remove stopped Django containers.

docker container prune --force
Deleted Containers:
s9t0u1v2w3x4
y5z6a7b8c9d0

Total reclaimed space: 875MB
Example 3: Prune with Label Filter

Remove Django test containers only.

docker container prune --filter "label=env=test" --force
Deleted Containers:
test-container-1
test-container-2
test-container-3

Total reclaimed space: 1.5GB
Example 4: Prune Old Containers

Remove Django containers stopped more than 24 hours ago.

docker container prune --filter "until=24h" --force
Deleted Containers:
old-dev-container
staging-test-1
migration-test-2

Total reclaimed space: 3.2GB
Example 5: Scheduled Cleanup Script

Weekly cleanup of stopped Django containers.

# cleanup-containers.sh
docker container prune --force
docker image prune --force
docker volume prune --force
Containers: 2.3GB reclaimed
Images: 4.1GB reclaimed
Volumes: 1.8GB reclaimed
✓ Total: 8.2GB freed

docker container rename

Official Docs: docker container rename | Docker CLI: docker container rename

Rename a container.

Full Description

The docker container rename command changes the name of an existing container. For your anihpj Django project, this helps reorganize containers, follow naming conventions, update environment designations, and maintain clear identification for your User authentication, JobPost management, Skills verification, Author profiles, and Location services.

Syntax

docker container rename CONTAINER NEW_NAME

# Rename Django container
docker container rename old-name anihpj-web-prod

# Rename with descriptive name
docker container rename f8g9h1i2j3k4 anihpj-worker-celery

Parameters Description

  • CONTAINER: Current container name or ID.
  • NEW_NAME: New name for container. Use descriptive Django service names.

Attributes

  • Non-Disruptive: Rename without stopping container. Update Django container names while running.
  • Unique Names Required: New name must not exist. Prevent Django container name conflicts.
  • Instant Update: Name change takes effect immediately. Reference Django containers by new name right away.
  • Silent Success: No output on successful rename. Check with docker ps to verify Django container name.
  • ID Preservation: Container ID unchanged. Links and references using ID remain valid.

Methods

Basic Rename

Syntax: docker container rename [OLD_NAME] [NEW_NAME]

Parameters: OLD_NAME (string), NEW_NAME (string)

Rename Django container to new descriptive name.

ID-Based Rename

Syntax: docker container rename [CONTAINER_ID] [NEW_NAME]

Parameters: CONTAINER_ID (string), NEW_NAME (string)

Assign meaningful name to Django container created with auto-generated ID.

Batch Rename Pattern

Syntax: docker container ls -q | xargs -I {} docker container rename {} new-{}

Parameters: Pattern-based naming

Rename multiple Django containers following consistent naming convention.

Return Value

Container renamed successfully (no output on success).

  • Success: No output (silent success)
  • Error (1): Container not found or name conflict

5 Practical Django Examples

Example 1: Rename to Production Convention

Update Django container to production naming.

docker container rename anihpj-web anihpj-web-prod
✓ Container renamed: anihpj-web → anihpj-web-prod
Example 2: Rename by Container ID

Give descriptive name to container created with ID.

docker container rename f8g9h1i2j3k4 anihpj-worker-celery-beat
✓ Container f8g9h1i2j3k4 renamed to anihpj-worker-celery-beat
Example 3: Rename for Environment

Update Django container to staging environment.

docker container rename anihpj-web-dev anihpj-web-staging
✓ Environment updated: dev → staging
Example 4: Batch Rename Containers

Update naming convention for all Django containers.

docker container rename web-container anihpj-web
docker container rename db-container anihpj-db
docker container rename cache-container anihpj-redis
✓ web-container → anihpj-web
✓ db-container → anihpj-db
✓ cache-container → anihpj-redis
Example 5: Rename with Version

Add version identifier to Django container name.

docker container rename anihpj-web anihpj-web-v2.5.3
✓ Version added: anihpj-web-v2.5.3

docker container restart

Official Docs: docker container restart | Docker CLI: docker container restart

Restart one or more containers.

Full Description

The docker container restart command stops and starts containers with configurable timeout. For your anihpj Django project, this enables applying configuration changes, recovering from errors, performing maintenance, and managing service availability for your User authentication, JobPost management, Skills verification, Author profiles, and Location services.

Syntax

docker container restart [OPTIONS] CONTAINER [CONTAINER...]

# Restart Django container
docker container restart anihpj-web

# Restart with custom timeout
docker container restart --time=30 anihpj-web

Parameters Description

  • CONTAINER: Container name or ID to restart.
  • -t, --time: Seconds to wait before killing container (default 10). Custom Django shutdown timeout.

Attributes

  • Graceful Restart: Stops then starts container with timeout. Allow Django cleanup before restart.
  • Configurable Timeout: Custom wait time before force kill. Adjust for Django application shutdown needs.
  • Multi-Container Support: Restart multiple containers sequentially. Update entire Django stack with one command.
  • State Preservation: Maintains configuration and networks. Django container settings persist through restart.
  • Quick Recovery: Faster than stop + start. Apply Django configuration changes efficiently.

Methods

Basic Restart

Syntax: docker container restart [CONTAINER]

Parameters: CONTAINER (string) - Container name or ID

Restart Django container with default 10-second timeout.

Custom Timeout Restart

Syntax: docker container restart --time=[SECONDS] [CONTAINER]

Parameters: SECONDS (integer), CONTAINER (string)

Restart Django container with custom graceful shutdown duration.

Stack Restart

Syntax: docker container restart [CONTAINER1] [CONTAINER2] ...

Parameters: Multiple CONTAINER names or IDs

Restart all Django service containers to apply stack-wide changes.

Return Value

Returns container name or ID of restarted containers.

  • Success: Container restarted
  • Error (1): Container not found

5 Practical Django Examples

Example 1: Restart Django Application

Restart web container to apply configuration changes.

docker container restart anihpj-web
anihpj-web
✓ Container restarted successfully
Example 2: Restart with Extended Timeout

Allow Django graceful shutdown with 30s timeout.

docker container restart --time=30 anihpj-web
Stopping anihpj-web (30s timeout)...
Starting anihpj-web...
✓ Restart complete
Example 3: Restart All Services

Restart complete Django stack.

docker container restart anihpj-web anihpj-worker anihpj-api
anihpj-web
anihpj-worker
anihpj-api
✓ 3 containers restarted
Example 4: Restart After Code Deployment

Deploy Django code and restart services.

git pull origin main
docker container restart anihpj-web
docker container logs --tail=20 anihpj-web
Pulled latest code
anihpj-web
[2026-01-14 16:30:00] INFO: Django restarted
[2026-01-14 16:30:01] INFO: Loaded new configuration
✓ Deployment complete
Example 5: Scheduled Restart Script

Daily restart for Django containers (cleanup).

docker container ls --filter name=anihpj -q | xargs docker container restart
f8g9h1i2j3k4
l5m6n7o8p9q0
r1s2t3u4v5w6
✓ All anihpj containers restarted

docker container rm

Official Docs: docker container rm | Docker CLI: docker container rm

Remove one or more containers.

Full Description

The docker container rm command deletes containers and their writable layers. For your anihpj Django project, this enables cleaning up stopped containers, removing test environments, freeing disk space, and managing container lifecycle for your User authentication, JobPost management, Skills verification, Author profiles, and Location services.

Syntax

docker container rm [OPTIONS] CONTAINER [CONTAINER...]

# Remove stopped Django container
docker container rm anihpj-web-old

# Force remove running container
docker container rm --force anihpj-web

Parameters Description

  • CONTAINER: Container name or ID to remove.
  • -f, --force: Force remove running container. Stop and remove Django container.
  • -v, --volumes: Remove anonymous volumes. Clean up Django data volumes.
  • -l, --link: Remove specified link. Delete Django container links.

Attributes

  • Stopped Containers Only: Requires container to be stopped unless --force used. Prevent accidental Django service deletion.
  • Volume Cleanup: --volumes flag removes anonymous volumes. Clean up Django temporary data automatically.
  • Multi-Container Delete: Remove multiple containers in one command. Batch delete Django test containers.
  • Force Option: Stop and remove running containers. Quickly clean up Django containers regardless of state.
  • Permanent Action: Container and writable layer deleted. Django container configuration must be recreated.

Methods

Standard Removal

Syntax: docker container rm [CONTAINER]

Parameters: CONTAINER (string) - Container name or ID

Remove stopped Django container and its writable layer.

Force Removal

Syntax: docker container rm --force [CONTAINER]

Parameters: CONTAINER (string)

Stop running Django container and remove it immediately.

Removal with Volumes

Syntax: docker container rm --volumes [CONTAINER]

Parameters: CONTAINER (string)

Remove Django container and its anonymous volumes for complete cleanup.

Return Value

Returns container name or ID of removed containers.

  • Success: Container removed
  • Error (1): Container not found or still running (without --force)

5 Practical Django Examples

Example 1: Remove Stopped Container

Delete old Django development container.

docker container rm anihpj-web-old
anihpj-web-old
✓ Container removed
Example 2: Force Remove Running Container

Stop and remove Django container immediately.

docker container rm --force anihpj-web
anihpj-web
✓ Container force removed
Example 3: Remove Multiple Containers

Clean up Django test containers.

docker container rm test-container-1 test-container-2 test-container-3
test-container-1
test-container-2
test-container-3
✓ 3 containers removed
Example 4: Remove with Volumes

Delete Django container and anonymous volumes.

docker container rm --volumes anihpj-web-test
anihpj-web-test
✓ Container and volumes removed
Example 5: Remove All Stopped Containers

Bulk remove all stopped Django containers.

docker container ls --all --quiet --filter status=exited | xargs docker container rm
a1b2c3d4e5f6
g7h8i9j0k1l2
m3n4o5p6q7r8
✓ All stopped containers removed

docker container run

Official Docs: docker container run | Docker CLI: docker container run

Create and run a new container from an image.

Full Description

The docker container run command creates and starts a container in one command. For your anihpj Django project, this is the most commonly used command for launching containers with environment variables, volumes, networks, and ports for your User authentication, JobPost management, Skills verification, Author profiles, and Location services.

Syntax

docker container run [OPTIONS] IMAGE [COMMAND] [ARG...]

# Run Django development server
docker container run -p 8000:8000 anihpj-web:latest

# Run with environment and volumes
docker container run -d --name anihpj-web -e DJANGO_SETTINGS_MODULE=anihpj.settings.production \
  -v anihpj-static:/app/static -p 8000:8000 anihpj-web:latest

Parameters Description

  • IMAGE: Docker image to use. Specify Django application image.
  • -d, --detach: Run in background. Daemonize Django container.
  • --name: Assign container name. Name Django service container.
  • -p, --publish: Publish ports. Expose Django application ports.
  • -e, --env: Set environment variables. Configure Django settings.
  • -v, --volume: Mount volumes. Persist Django data.
  • --network: Connect to network. Link Django services.
  • --rm: Auto-remove on stop. Clean up Django test containers.
  • -it: Interactive terminal. Run Django shell interactively.

Attributes

  • One-Step Creation: Creates and starts container in single command. Launch Django applications quickly.
  • Rich Configuration: Supports extensive options for networking, volumes, environment. Configure Django completely on launch.
  • Detached Mode: Run containers in background with -d flag. Django services run as daemons.
  • Interactive Mode: -it flags enable shell access. Run Django management commands interactively.
  • Auto-Cleanup: --rm removes container on stop. Perfect for Django test runs and one-off commands.

Methods

Production Deployment

Syntax: docker container run -d --name [NAME] -e [ENV] -v [VOLUME] -p [PORT] --network [NET] [IMAGE]

Parameters: NAME, ENV, VOLUME, PORT, NET, IMAGE (strings)

Launch Django application with full production configuration.

Interactive Command

Syntax: docker container run -it --rm --name [NAME] [IMAGE] [COMMAND]

Parameters: NAME, IMAGE, COMMAND (strings)

Run Django management command or shell interactively with auto-cleanup.

Development Mode

Syntax: docker container run -it -p [PORT] -v [LOCAL]:[CONTAINER] [IMAGE]

Parameters: PORT (port mapping), LOCAL, CONTAINER (volume paths), IMAGE (string)

Run Django with live code reloading for development.

Return Value

Returns container ID or logs (if not detached).

  • Success: Container created and started
  • Error (125): Docker daemon error
  • Error (126): Command cannot be invoked
  • Error (127): Command not found

5 Practical Django Examples

Example 1: Run Production Django Container

Launch Django application with full configuration.

docker container run -d --name anihpj-web-prod \
  -e DJANGO_SETTINGS_MODULE=anihpj.settings.production \
  -e DATABASE_URL=postgresql://user:pass@db:5432/anihpj \
  -e REDIS_URL=redis://redis:6379/0 \
  -v anihpj-static:/app/static \
  -v anihpj-media:/app/media \
  -p 8000:8000 \
  --network anihpj-network \
  anihpj-web:v2.5.3
f8g9h1i2j3k4l5m6n7o8p9q0r1s2t3u4v5w6x7y8z9
✓ Production container started
✓ Django accessible at http://localhost:8000
Example 2: Run Interactive Django Shell

Start Django shell for database queries.

docker container run -it --rm --name django-shell \
  --network anihpj-network \
  anihpj-web:latest python manage.py shell
Python 3.11.4 (main, Jan 14 2026, 15:30:00)
Django shell loaded
>>> from jobpost.models import JobPost
>>> JobPost.objects.count()
157
Example 3: Run Celery Worker

Launch Celery worker for async tasks.

docker container run -d --name anihpj-worker \
  -e DJANGO_SETTINGS_MODULE=anihpj.settings.production \
  -e REDIS_URL=redis://redis:6379/0 \
  --network anihpj-network \
  anihpj-web:latest celery -A anihpj worker -l info
l5m6n7o8p9q0r1s2t3u4v5w6x7y8z9a0b1c2d3e4f5
✓ Celery worker started
✓ Ready to process tasks
Example 4: Run One-Off Command

Execute Django migrations with auto-cleanup.

docker container run --rm --name django-migrate \
  -e DATABASE_URL=postgresql://user:pass@db:5432/anihpj \
  --network anihpj-network \
  anihpj-web:latest python manage.py migrate
Operations to perform:
  Apply all migrations: jobpost, auth, contenttypes, sessions
Running migrations:
  Applying jobpost.0015_add_skills_verification... OK
  Applying jobpost.0016_add_location_fields... OK
✓ 2 migrations applied
Example 5: Run with Resource Limits

Launch Django container with memory and CPU constraints.

docker container run -d --name anihpj-web \
  --memory="2g" \
  --cpus="1.5" \
  -e DJANGO_SETTINGS_MODULE=anihpj.settings.production \
  -p 8000:8000 \
  anihpj-web:latest
r1s2t3u4v5w6x7y8z9a0b1c2d3e4f5g6h7i8j9k0l1
✓ Container started with limits:
  Memory: 2GB
  CPUs: 1.5

docker container start

Official Docs: docker container start | Docker CLI: docker container start

Start one or more stopped containers.

Full Description

The docker container start command starts existing stopped containers. For your anihpj Django project, this enables resuming services after maintenance, starting containers after system reboot, managing service availability, and controlling your User authentication, JobPost management, Skills verification, Author profiles, and Location services without recreating containers.

Syntax

docker container start [OPTIONS] CONTAINER [CONTAINER...]

# Start Django container
docker container start anihpj-web

# Start and attach to container
docker container start --attach anihpj-web

Parameters Description

  • CONTAINER: Container name or ID to start.
  • -a, --attach: Attach stdout/stderr. View Django startup logs.
  • -i, --interactive: Attach stdin. Enable Django shell interaction.

Attributes

  • Resume Operation: Starts stopped containers without recreation. Resume Django services quickly.
  • Preserves State: Maintains container configuration and data. Django settings and volumes persist.
  • Multi-Container Support: Start multiple containers simultaneously. Launch entire Django stack together.
  • Attach Option: View logs during startup with --attach. Monitor Django initialization process.
  • Fast Startup: No image pull or creation needed. Start existing Django containers instantly.

Methods

Basic Start

Syntax: docker container start [CONTAINER]

Parameters: CONTAINER (string) - Container name or ID

Start stopped Django container in background.

Start with Logs

Syntax: docker container start --attach [CONTAINER]

Parameters: CONTAINER (string)

Start Django container and stream startup logs to console.

Batch Start

Syntax: docker container start [CONTAINER1] [CONTAINER2] ...

Parameters: Multiple CONTAINER names or IDs

Start multiple Django service containers in one command.

Return Value

Returns container name or ID of started containers.

  • Success: Container started
  • Error (1): Container not found or already running

5 Practical Django Examples

Example 1: Start Django Container

Resume stopped Django web service.

docker container start anihpj-web
anihpj-web
✓ Container started
Example 2: Start and View Logs

Start Django container and monitor startup.

docker container start --attach anihpj-web
[2026-01-14 16:45:00] INFO: Django starting...
[2026-01-14 16:45:01] INFO: Database connected
[2026-01-14 16:45:02] INFO: Redis cache initialized
[2026-01-14 16:45:03] INFO: Application ready on port 8000
Example 3: Start Multiple Services

Start all Django stack containers.

docker container start anihpj-web anihpj-worker anihpj-db
anihpj-web
anihpj-worker
anihpj-db
✓ 3 containers started
Example 4: Start All Stopped Containers

Bulk start all stopped anihpj containers.

docker container ls --all --filter status=exited --filter name=anihpj -q | xargs docker container start
f8g9h1i2j3k4
l5m6n7o8p9q0
✓ All stopped anihpj containers started
Example 5: Start with Interactive Session

Start Django shell container interactively.

docker container start -ai django-shell
Python 3.11.4 Django shell
>>> from django.contrib.auth.models import User
>>> User.objects.count()
342

docker container stats

Official Docs: docker container stats | Docker CLI: docker container stats

Display a live stream of container(s) resource usage statistics.

Full Description

The docker container stats command shows real-time resource usage (CPU, memory, network, disk I/O) for containers. For your anihpj Django project, this enables monitoring performance, detecting resource issues, optimizing allocations, and tracking resource consumption for your User authentication, JobPost management, Skills verification, Author profiles, and Location services.

Syntax

docker container stats [OPTIONS] [CONTAINER...]

# Monitor all containers
docker container stats

# Monitor specific Django container
docker container stats anihpj-web

Parameters Description

  • CONTAINER: Container name or ID to monitor. Track Django service resources.
  • -a, --all: Show all containers (stopped + running). Include Django development containers.
  • --no-stream: Display single snapshot. Get Django resource snapshot.
  • --format: Format output using template. Custom Django stats display.

Attributes

  • Real-Time Monitoring: Updates statistics continuously. Track Django container performance live.
  • Multi-Metric Display: Shows CPU, memory, network, and disk I/O. Comprehensive Django resource visibility.
  • Snapshot Mode: --no-stream provides single reading. Quick Django resource check for scripts.
  • Formatted Output: Go template support for custom columns. Extract specific Django metrics programmatically.
  • Multi-Container View: Monitor multiple containers simultaneously. Track entire Django stack resources.

Methods

Live Monitoring

Syntax: docker container stats [CONTAINER]

Parameters: CONTAINER (string, optional) - Specific container or all containers

Stream live Django container resource statistics continuously.

Snapshot Mode

Syntax: docker container stats --no-stream [CONTAINER]

Parameters: CONTAINER (string, optional)

Get single resource reading for Django containers for scripting.

Custom Metrics

Syntax: docker container stats --format "{{.[FIELD]}}" [CONTAINER]

Parameters: FIELD (string) - Metric name like .MemUsage, CONTAINER (string)

Extract specific Django container metrics in custom format.

Return Value

Returns live table with container resource statistics.

  • Success: Stats displayed

5 Practical Django Examples

Example 1: Monitor Django Container

Real-time resource monitoring for web service.

docker container stats anihpj-web
CONTAINER ID   NAME          CPU %     MEM USAGE / LIMIT     MEM %     NET I/O           BLOCK I/O
f8g9h1i2j3k4   anihpj-web    12.5%     485MiB / 2GiB        23.7%     1.2MB / 850KB     12MB / 4.5MB
Example 2: Monitor All Services

Track resources for entire Django stack.

docker container stats
CONTAINER      CPU %     MEM USAGE / LIMIT     MEM %
anihpj-web     12.5%     485MiB / 2GiB        23.7%
anihpj-worker  8.3%      320MiB / 1GiB        31.2%
anihpj-db      15.2%     680MiB / 4GiB        16.5%
anihpj-redis   2.1%      45MiB / 512MiB       8.8%
Example 3: Single Stats Snapshot

Get Django container resource snapshot.

docker container stats --no-stream anihpj-web
CONTAINER ID   NAME         CPU %     MEM USAGE / LIMIT
f8g9h1i2j3k4   anihpj-web   11.8%     492MiB / 2GiB
Example 4: Custom Format Output

Display Django container name and memory only.

docker container stats --no-stream --format "table {{.Name}}\t{{.MemUsage}}\t{{.MemPerc}}"
NAME            MEM USAGE          MEM %
anihpj-web      485MiB / 2GiB      23.7%
anihpj-worker   320MiB / 1GiB      31.2%
anihpj-db       680MiB / 4GiB      16.5%
Example 5: Monitor High CPU Usage

Track Django container during load test.

docker container stats anihpj-web --no-stream
CONTAINER ID   NAME         CPU %     MEM USAGE / LIMIT     NET I/O
f8g9h1i2j3k4   anihpj-web   78.4%     1.2GiB / 2GiB        8.5MB / 12MB
⚠ High CPU usage detected during stress test

docker container stop

Official Docs: docker container stop | Docker CLI: docker container stop

Stop one or more running containers gracefully.

Full Description

The docker container stop command sends SIGTERM signal to gracefully stop containers, waiting for configured timeout before sending SIGKILL. For your anihpj Django project, this enables safe shutdowns, completing active requests, closing database connections, and managing service availability for your User authentication, JobPost management, Skills verification, Author profiles, and Location services.

Syntax

docker container stop [OPTIONS] CONTAINER [CONTAINER...]

# Stop Django container
docker container stop anihpj-web

# Stop with custom timeout
docker container stop --time=30 anihpj-web

Parameters Description

  • CONTAINER: Container name or ID to stop.
  • -t, --time: Seconds to wait before killing (default 10). Django graceful shutdown timeout.

Attributes

  • Graceful Shutdown: Sends SIGTERM first for cleanup. Allow Django to finish active requests and connections.
  • Configurable Timeout: Default 10 seconds before SIGKILL. Adjust for Django application shutdown requirements.
  • Multi-Container Stop: Stop multiple containers in one command. Shutdown entire Django stack efficiently.
  • Connection Cleanup: Time for Django to close database connections. Prevent data corruption with proper shutdown.
  • Non-Destructive: Container can be restarted. Django configuration and data preserved for restart.

Methods

Graceful Stop

Syntax: docker container stop [CONTAINER]

Parameters: CONTAINER (string) - Container name or ID

Stop Django container with default 10-second graceful timeout.

Extended Timeout Stop

Syntax: docker container stop --time=[SECONDS] [CONTAINER]

Parameters: SECONDS (integer), CONTAINER (string)

Stop Django container with custom shutdown duration for complex cleanup.

Stack Shutdown

Syntax: docker container stop [CONTAINER1] [CONTAINER2] ...

Parameters: Multiple CONTAINER names or IDs

Stop all Django service containers for maintenance or deployment.

Return Value

Returns container name or ID of stopped containers.

  • Success: Container stopped
  • Error (1): Container not found or not running

5 Practical Django Examples

Example 1: Stop Django Container

Gracefully stop Django web service.

docker container stop anihpj-web
anihpj-web
✓ Container stopped gracefully (SIGTERM sent)
Example 2: Stop with Extended Timeout

Allow Django 30 seconds for graceful shutdown.

docker container stop --time=30 anihpj-web
Stopping anihpj-web (30s timeout)...
✓ Container stopped after 12s
Example 3: Stop All Services

Shutdown entire Django stack.

docker container stop anihpj-web anihpj-worker anihpj-api
anihpj-web
anihpj-worker
anihpj-api
✓ 3 containers stopped
Example 4: Stop Before Maintenance

Stop Django containers for database maintenance.

docker container stop --time=60 anihpj-web anihpj-worker
# Perform database backup
docker container start anihpj-web anihpj-worker
Stopping services (60s timeout)...
✓ anihpj-web stopped
✓ anihpj-worker stopped
[Maintenance completed]
✓ Services restarted
Example 5: Stop All Running Containers

Emergency shutdown of all anihpj containers.

docker container ls --filter name=anihpj -q | xargs docker container stop
f8g9h1i2j3k4
l5m6n7o8p9q0
r1s2t3u4v5w6
✓ All anihpj containers stopped

docker container top

Official Docs: docker container top | Docker CLI: docker container top

Display the running processes of a container.

Full Description

The docker container top command shows running processes inside a container. For your anihpj Django project, this enables monitoring active processes, debugging performance, identifying resource-intensive tasks, and managing process health for your User authentication, JobPost management, Skills verification, Author profiles, and Location services.

Syntax

docker container top CONTAINER [ps OPTIONS]

# View Django processes
docker container top anihpj-web

# Custom process listing
docker container top anihpj-web aux

Parameters Description

  • CONTAINER: Container name or ID.
  • ps OPTIONS: Options passed to ps command. Customize Django process display.

Attributes

  • Process Visibility: Shows running processes inside container. Monitor Django worker processes and threads.
  • ps Command Integration: Accepts standard ps options. Customize Django process information display.
  • Real-Time Snapshot: Shows current process state. Monitor Django application process health.
  • Host PID Display: Shows PIDs from host perspective. Correlate Django container processes with host system.
  • Debug Tool: Identify process issues. Troubleshoot Django performance and resource problems.

Methods

Basic Process List

Syntax: docker container top [CONTAINER]

Parameters: CONTAINER (string) - Container name or ID

Display default process information for Django container.

Detailed Process View

Syntax: docker container top [CONTAINER] aux

Parameters: CONTAINER (string)

Show Django processes with CPU, memory, and resource usage details.

Custom Format

Syntax: docker container top [CONTAINER] -eo pid,comm,%mem,%cpu

Parameters: CONTAINER (string), format options

Display Django processes with custom columns for specific metrics.

Return Value

Returns table with process information.

  • Success: Process list displayed
  • Error (1): Container not found or not running

5 Practical Django Examples

Example 1: View Django Processes

List running processes in Django container.

docker container top anihpj-web
UID      PID    PPID   C   STIME   TTY   TIME       CMD
root     1234   1200   0   15:30   ?     00:00:05   gunicorn: master [anihpj]
django   1235   1234   2   15:30   ?     00:02:15   gunicorn: worker [anihpj]
django   1236   1234   2   15:30   ?     00:02:18   gunicorn: worker [anihpj]
django   1237   1234   2   15:30   ?     00:02:12   gunicorn: worker [anihpj]
Example 2: Detailed Process Info

View Django processes with resource usage.

docker container top anihpj-web aux
USER     PID   %CPU   %MEM    VSZ     RSS   STAT  START   TIME    COMMAND
root     1234  0.5    2.3     185MB   47MB  Ss    15:30   0:05    gunicorn master
django   1235  12.3   8.5     245MB   170MB Sl    15:30   2:15    gunicorn worker
django   1236  11.8   8.2     242MB   165MB Sl    15:30   2:18    gunicorn worker
Example 3: Check Celery Workers

Monitor Celery worker processes.

docker container top anihpj-worker
UID      PID    CMD
root     5678   celery worker -A anihpj -l info
django   5679   celery worker: MainProcess
django   5680   celery worker: Worker-1
django   5681   celery worker: Worker-2
Example 4: Compare Process Counts

Check process counts across Django services.

for container in anihpj-web anihpj-worker anihpj-api; do
  echo "=== $container ==="
  docker container top $container | wc -l
done
=== anihpj-web ===
5 processes
=== anihpj-worker ===
4 processes
=== anihpj-api ===
3 processes
Example 5: Monitor Database Processes

View PostgreSQL processes in database container.

docker container top anihpj-db
UID      PID    CMD
postgres 8901   postgres -D /var/lib/postgresql/data
postgres 8902   postgres: checkpointer
postgres 8903   postgres: background writer
postgres 8904   postgres: walwriter
postgres 8905   postgres: autovacuum launcher
postgres 8906   postgres: stats collector

docker container unpause

Official Docs: docker container unpause | Docker CLI: docker container unpause

Unpause all processes within one or more containers.

Full Description

The docker container unpause command resumes all processes in paused containers. For your anihpj Django project, this enables resuming services after maintenance, completing backup operations, ending test scenarios, and restoring normal operations for your User authentication, JobPost management, Skills verification, Author profiles, and Location services.

Syntax

docker container unpause CONTAINER [CONTAINER...]

# Unpause Django container
docker container unpause anihpj-web

# Unpause multiple containers
docker container unpause anihpj-web anihpj-worker

Parameters Description

  • CONTAINER: Container name or ID to unpause.

Attributes

  • Process Resumption: Resumes all paused processes instantly. Django application continues exactly where paused.
  • State Restoration: All memory and connections preserved. Django resumes with no data loss or interruption.
  • Quick Operation: Instant resume with no startup time. Faster than restarting Django containers.
  • Pair with Pause: Reverses docker container pause operation. Complete Django maintenance workflow support.
  • Multi-Container Resume: Unpause multiple containers simultaneously. Resume entire Django stack after maintenance.

Methods

Single Container Unpause

Syntax: docker container unpause [CONTAINER]

Parameters: CONTAINER (string) - Container name or ID

Resume paused Django container's processes immediately.

Multi-Container Unpause

Syntax: docker container unpause [CONTAINER1] [CONTAINER2] ...

Parameters: Multiple CONTAINER names or IDs

Resume multiple Django service containers after maintenance.

Unpause After Operation

Syntax: [MAINTENANCE] && docker container unpause [CONTAINER]

Parameters: MAINTENANCE (command), CONTAINER (string)

Execute maintenance task then automatically resume Django container.

Return Value

Returns container name or ID of unpaused containers.

  • Success: Container unpaused
  • Error (1): Container not found or not paused

5 Practical Django Examples

Example 1: Resume Django Service

Unpause Django container after backup.

docker container unpause anihpj-web
anihpj-web
✓ Container unpaused (processes resumed)
Example 2: Unpause All Services

Resume all Django stack containers.

docker container unpause anihpj-web anihpj-worker anihpj-api
anihpj-web
anihpj-worker
anihpj-api
✓ 3 containers unpaused
Example 3: Unpause After Testing

Resume Django after failover test.

docker container unpause anihpj-web
docker container logs --tail=10 anihpj-web
anihpj-web
[2026-01-14 17:00:00] INFO: Container resumed
[2026-01-14 17:00:01] INFO: Processing queued requests
✓ Services operational
Example 4: Bulk Unpause by Filter

Unpause all anihpj containers at once.

docker container ls --filter status=paused --filter name=anihpj -q | xargs docker container unpause
f8g9h1i2j3k4
l5m6n7o8p9q0
✓ All paused anihpj containers resumed
Example 5: Unpause and Verify Status

Resume Django and confirm running state.

docker container unpause anihpj-web && docker container inspect --format='{{.State.Status}}' anihpj-web
anihpj-web
running

docker container update

Official Docs: docker container update | Docker CLI: docker container update

Update configuration of one or more containers.

Full Description

The docker container update command updates resource constraints of running containers without restart. For your anihpj Django project, this enables adjusting memory limits, CPU shares, restart policies, and resource allocations dynamically for your User authentication, JobPost management, Skills verification, Author profiles, and Location services.

Syntax

docker container update [OPTIONS] CONTAINER [CONTAINER...]

# Update Django memory limit
docker container update --memory="4g" anihpj-web

# Update CPU shares
docker container update --cpus="2.0" anihpj-web

Parameters Description

  • CONTAINER: Container name or ID to update.
  • --memory: Memory limit. Adjust Django container memory allocation.
  • --cpus: CPU shares. Update Django CPU allocation.
  • --restart: Restart policy. Change Django restart behavior.
  • --memory-reservation: Memory soft limit. Set Django memory reservation.
  • --cpu-shares: CPU shares (relative weight). Adjust Django CPU priority.

Attributes

  • Runtime Updates: Change resource limits without restart. Adjust Django container resources on the fly.
  • Multiple Resources: Update memory, CPU, and restart policies. Configure Django resource allocation comprehensively.
  • Multi-Container Update: Update multiple containers simultaneously. Apply Django stack-wide resource changes.
  • No Downtime: Changes applied without stopping container. Django continues serving during updates.
  • Persistent Changes: Updates maintained across container restarts. Django resource configuration persists.

Methods

Memory Update

Syntax: docker container update --memory=[SIZE] [CONTAINER]

Parameters: SIZE (string like "4g"), CONTAINER (string)

Update Django container memory limit dynamically.

CPU Update

Syntax: docker container update --cpus=[NUMBER] [CONTAINER]

Parameters: NUMBER (string like "2.0"), CONTAINER (string)

Adjust Django container CPU allocation for performance tuning.

Restart Policy Update

Syntax: docker container update --restart=[POLICY] [CONTAINER]

Parameters: POLICY (string: always, unless-stopped, on-failure), CONTAINER (string)

Change Django container restart behavior for production requirements.

Return Value

Returns container name or ID of updated containers.

  • Success: Container updated
  • Error (1): Container not found or invalid configuration

5 Practical Django Examples

Example 1: Increase Memory Limit

Allocate more memory to Django container under load.

docker container update --memory="4g" anihpj-web
anihpj-web
✓ Memory limit updated: 2GB → 4GB
Example 2: Update CPU Allocation

Increase Django container CPU shares.

docker container update --cpus="2.5" anihpj-web
anihpj-web
✓ CPU allocation updated: 1.5 → 2.5 CPUs
Example 3: Change Restart Policy

Update Django container to always restart.

docker container update --restart=always anihpj-web
anihpj-web
✓ Restart policy updated: no → always
Example 4: Update Multiple Containers

Apply resource limits to all Django services.

docker container update --memory="2g" --cpus="1.5" anihpj-web anihpj-worker anihpj-api
anihpj-web
anihpj-worker
anihpj-api
✓ 3 containers updated with new resource limits
Example 5: Set Memory Reservation

Configure Django container soft memory limit.

docker container update --memory="4g" --memory-reservation="2g" anihpj-web
anihpj-web
✓ Memory hard limit: 4GB
✓ Memory reservation: 2GB

docker container wait

Official Docs: docker container wait | Docker CLI: docker container wait

Block until one or more containers stop, then print their exit codes.

Full Description

The docker container wait command blocks execution until containers stop and returns exit codes. For your anihpj Django project, this enables sequential task execution, migration orchestration, testing workflows, and managing dependencies between your User authentication, JobPost management, Skills verification, Author profiles, and Location services.

Syntax

docker container wait CONTAINER [CONTAINER...]

# Wait for Django migration
docker container wait django-migrate

# Wait for multiple tasks
docker container wait task-1 task-2 task-3

Parameters Description

  • CONTAINER: Container name or ID to wait for.

Attributes

  • Blocking Operation: Waits until container stops. Synchronize Django task execution in scripts.
  • Exit Code Return: Returns container exit code. Detect Django task success or failure.
  • Multi-Container Wait: Wait for multiple containers simultaneously. Coordinate Django parallel tasks.
  • Script Integration: Perfect for shell scripts and CI/CD. Orchestrate Django deployment workflows.
  • No Timeout: Waits indefinitely until stop. Ensure Django tasks complete before proceeding.

Methods

Single Container Wait

Syntax: docker container wait [CONTAINER]

Parameters: CONTAINER (string) - Container name or ID

Wait for Django task container to complete and return exit code.

Multi-Container Wait

Syntax: docker container wait [CONTAINER1] [CONTAINER2] ...

Parameters: Multiple CONTAINER names or IDs

Wait for multiple Django tasks to complete in parallel.

Error Detection Pattern

Syntax: EXIT_CODE=$(docker container wait [CONTAINER]) && [SUCCESS] || [FAILURE]

Parameters: CONTAINER (string), SUCCESS/FAILURE (commands)

Execute Django operations conditionally based on task exit code.

Return Value

Returns exit code of stopped container(s).

  • 0: Container exited successfully
  • Non-zero: Container exited with error

5 Practical Django Examples

Example 1: Wait for Migration Completion

Block until Django migrations finish.

docker container run --rm --name django-migrate anihpj-web:latest python manage.py migrate &
docker container wait django-migrate
echo "Exit code: $?"
Running migrations in background...
Waiting for completion...
0
✓ Migrations completed successfully
Example 2: Sequential Task Execution

Run Django tasks in sequence.

docker container run --rm --name task-migrate anihpj-web migrate &
docker container wait task-migrate
docker container run --rm --name task-collectstatic anihpj-web collectstatic --noinput &
docker container wait task-collectstatic
Task 1: Migrations
0 (Success)
Task 2: Static files
0 (Success)
✓ Sequential tasks completed
Example 3: Wait for Multiple Containers

Wait for parallel Django tasks.

docker container run --rm --name job1 anihpj-web task1 &
docker container run --rm --name job2 anihpj-web task2 &
docker container wait job1 job2
0
0
✓ Both tasks completed successfully
Example 4: Error Detection

Detect Django migration failures.

docker container run --rm --name django-migrate anihpj-web migrate &
EXIT_CODE=$(docker container wait django-migrate)
if [ $EXIT_CODE -eq 0 ]; then
  echo "Migration successful"
else
  echo "Migration failed with code $EXIT_CODE"
fi
1
Migration failed with code 1
✗ Check migration logs for errors
Example 5: Deployment Orchestration

Wait for Django setup tasks before starting service.

docker container run --rm --name setup-db anihpj-web migrate &
docker container run --rm --name setup-static anihpj-web collectstatic --noinput &
docker container wait setup-db setup-static
docker container start anihpj-web
0
0
✓ Setup complete, starting Django web service
anihpj-web

🎯 Context Commands

Docker context management enables switching between different Docker environments (local, staging, production) seamlessly. Essential for multi-environment Django deployments.

docker context

Official Docs: docker context | Docker CLI: docker context

Manage Docker contexts for multi-environment deployments.

Full Description

The docker context command provides a suite of subcommands for managing Docker contexts, which represent connections to different Docker daemons. For your anihpj Django project, contexts enable seamless switching between local development, staging, and production environments for User authentication, JobPost management, Skills verification, Author profiles, and Location services.

Syntax

docker context COMMAND

# Available subcommands
docker context create    # Create new context
docker context export    # Export context
docker context import    # Import context
docker context inspect   # Display context details
docker context ls        # List contexts
docker context rm        # Remove context
docker context show      # Show current context
docker context update    # Update context
docker context use       # Set active context

Parameters Description

  • COMMAND: Subcommand for context operations.

Attributes

  • Environment Switching: Switch between dev, staging, prod Docker daemons. Deploy Django to different environments easily.
  • Connection Management: Store daemon endpoints and credentials. Manage Django deployment targets securely.
  • Context Isolation: Separate configurations per environment. Prevent Django deployment mistakes across environments.
  • Portable Contexts: Export/import contexts for team sharing. Standardize Django deployment configurations.
  • Default Context: Automatically use active context. Streamline Django deployment workflows.

Methods

Create and Switch Context

Syntax: docker context create [NAME] --docker host=[HOST] && docker context use [NAME]

Parameters: NAME (string), HOST (string)

Create new Django deployment context and activate it.

List and Inspect

Syntax: docker context ls && docker context inspect [NAME]

Parameters: NAME (string)

View available Django contexts and examine configuration details.

Export and Share

Syntax: docker context export [NAME] [FILE] && docker context import [NAME] [FILE]

Parameters: NAME, FILE (strings)

Share Django deployment contexts across team members.

Return Value

Returns usage information and available subcommands.

5 Practical Django Examples

Example 1: Multi-Environment Setup

Configure contexts for all Django deployment environments.

# Create local development context
docker context create anihpj-dev --docker host=unix:///var/run/docker.sock

# Create staging context
docker context create anihpj-staging --docker host=ssh://user@staging.anihpj.com

# Create production context
docker context create anihpj-prod --docker host=ssh://user@prod.anihpj.com

# List all contexts
docker context ls
anihpj-dev
anihpj-staging
anihpj-prod

NAME              DESCRIPTION    DOCKER ENDPOINT                    
anihpj-dev        Local dev      unix:///var/run/docker.sock        
anihpj-staging    Staging        ssh://user@staging.anihpj.com      
anihpj-prod       Production     ssh://user@prod.anihpj.com         
default *         Current        unix:///var/run/docker.sock
Example 2: Switch Between Environments

Deploy Django to different environments.

# Deploy to staging
docker context use anihpj-staging
docker container run -d --name anihpj-web anihpj/web:staging

# Switch to production
docker context use anihpj-prod
docker container run -d --name anihpj-web anihpj/web:prod

# Return to local dev
docker context use anihpj-dev
anihpj-staging
✓ Deployed to staging environment

anihpj-prod
✓ Deployed to production environment

anihpj-dev
✓ Switched back to local development
Example 3: Context Information

View active context and details.

# Show current context
docker context show

# Inspect production context
docker context inspect anihpj-prod
anihpj-prod

[
  {
    "Name": "anihpj-prod",
    "Metadata": {},
    "Endpoints": {
      "docker": {
        "Host": "ssh://user@prod.anihpj.com",
        "SkipTLSVerify": false
      }
    }
  }
]
Example 4: Share Team Configuration

Export and distribute Django deployment contexts.

# Export production context
docker context export anihpj-prod anihpj-prod.tar

# Team member imports
docker context import anihpj-prod anihpj-prod.tar
Written file anihpj-prod.tar

anihpj-prod
✓ Production context imported
✓ Team member ready to deploy
Example 5: Clean Up Old Contexts

Remove unused Django deployment contexts.

# List contexts
docker context ls

# Remove old staging context
docker context rm anihpj-old-staging

# Verify removal
docker context ls
NAME                 DOCKER ENDPOINT
anihpj-dev           unix:///var/run/docker.sock
anihpj-staging       ssh://user@staging.anihpj.com
anihpj-old-staging   ssh://user@old-staging.anihpj.com
anihpj-prod          ssh://user@prod.anihpj.com

anihpj-old-staging
✓ Context removed

NAME             DOCKER ENDPOINT
anihpj-dev       unix:///var/run/docker.sock
anihpj-staging   ssh://user@staging.anihpj.com
anihpj-prod      ssh://user@prod.anihpj.com

docker context create

Official Docs: docker context create | Docker CLI: docker context create

Create a new Docker context.

Full Description

The docker context create command creates a new context with specified Docker daemon connection details. For your anihpj Django project, this enables setting up deployment targets for different environments running User authentication, JobPost management, Skills verification, Author profiles, and Location services.

Syntax

docker context create [OPTIONS] CONTEXT

# Create local context
docker context create anihpj-dev

# Create remote context
docker context create anihpj-prod --docker host=ssh://user@prod.anihpj.com

Parameters Description

  • CONTEXT: Name for new context.
  • --description: Context description. Document Django environment purpose.
  • --docker: Docker daemon endpoint. Specify Django deployment host.
  • --from: Create from existing context. Clone Django context configuration.

Attributes

  • Environment Configuration: Define Docker daemon connections. Set up Django deployment environments.
  • Named References: Use descriptive context names. Identify Django environments clearly.
  • Connection Types: Support unix socket, TCP, SSH. Connect to Django hosts flexibly.
  • Metadata Storage: Store descriptions and settings. Document Django deployment details.
  • Inheritance: Clone existing contexts with --from. Replicate Django configurations quickly.

Methods

Local Context

Syntax: docker context create [NAME] --docker host=unix:///var/run/docker.sock

Parameters: NAME (string)

Create local development context for Django.

Remote SSH Context

Syntax: docker context create [NAME] --docker host=ssh://[USER]@[HOST]

Parameters: NAME, USER, HOST (strings)

Create remote context for Django staging/production deployment.

Clone Context

Syntax: docker context create [NEW_NAME] --from [EXISTING_NAME]

Parameters: NEW_NAME, EXISTING_NAME (strings)

Duplicate Django context configuration for similar environment.

Return Value

Returns context name on successful creation.

5 Practical Django Examples

Example 1: Create Development Context

Set up local Django development environment.

docker context create anihpj-dev \
  --description "Local development environment" \
  --docker host=unix:///var/run/docker.sock
anihpj-dev
✓ Successfully created context "anihpj-dev"
Example 2: Create Production Context

Configure Django production deployment target.

docker context create anihpj-prod \
  --description "Production environment" \
  --docker host=ssh://deploy@prod.anihpj.com
anihpj-prod
✓ Production context created
✓ Connected to prod.anihpj.com
Example 3: Create Staging from Production

Clone production context for Django staging.

docker context create anihpj-staging \
  --from anihpj-prod \
  --docker host=ssh://deploy@staging.anihpj.com
anihpj-staging
✓ Created from anihpj-prod configuration
✓ Updated host to staging.anihpj.com
Example 4: Create TCP Context

Set up Django deployment via TCP connection.

docker context create anihpj-remote \
  --docker "host=tcp://192.168.1.100:2375"
anihpj-remote
✓ TCP context created
✓ Connected to 192.168.1.100:2375
Example 5: Create Multi-Region Contexts

Set up Django deployment across regions.

docker context create anihpj-us-east \
  --description "US East region" \
  --docker host=ssh://deploy@us-east.anihpj.com

docker context create anihpj-eu-west \
  --description "EU West region" \
  --docker host=ssh://deploy@eu-west.anihpj.com
anihpj-us-east
anihpj-eu-west
✓ Multi-region deployment configured

docker context export

Official Docs: docker context export | Docker CLI: docker context export

Export a Docker context to a tar archive.

Full Description

The docker context export command exports context configuration to a tar file for sharing. For your anihpj Django project, this enables distributing deployment configurations across team members for User authentication, JobPost management, Skills verification, Author profiles, and Location services.

Syntax

docker context export [OPTIONS] CONTEXT [FILE]

# Export to file
docker context export anihpj-prod anihpj-prod.tar

# Export to stdout
docker context export anihpj-prod -

Parameters Description

  • CONTEXT: Name of context to export.
  • FILE: Output file path (optional, defaults to CONTEXT.tar).

Attributes

  • Portable Configuration: Export Django context as tar archive. Share deployment settings easily.
  • Team Sharing: Distribute contexts to team members. Standardize Django deployments across team.
  • Backup: Archive context configurations. Preserve Django environment settings.
  • Version Control: Store contexts in git. Track Django deployment configuration changes.
  • Stdout Support: Pipe to other commands. Automate Django context distribution.

Methods

Export to File

Syntax: docker context export [CONTEXT] [FILE].tar

Parameters: CONTEXT, FILE (strings)

Export Django context configuration to tar archive.

Export to Stdout

Syntax: docker context export [CONTEXT] -

Parameters: CONTEXT (string)

Stream Django context to stdout for piping.

Backup All Contexts

Syntax: for c in $(docker context ls -q); do docker context export $c; done

Parameters: None

Backup all Django deployment contexts systematically.

Return Value

Returns file path or exports to stdout.

5 Practical Django Examples

Example 1: Share Production Context

Export Django production configuration for team.

docker context export anihpj-prod anihpj-prod.tar
Written file anihpj-prod.tar
✓ Context exported successfully
Example 2: Backup All Contexts

Archive all Django environment configurations.

mkdir -p context-backups
for context in $(docker context ls -q); do
  docker context export $context context-backups/${context}.tar
done
Written file context-backups/anihpj-dev.tar
Written file context-backups/anihpj-staging.tar
Written file context-backups/anihpj-prod.tar
✓ All contexts backed up
Example 3: Version Control Storage

Store Django contexts in git repository.

docker context export anihpj-prod contexts/production.tar
git add contexts/production.tar
git commit -m "Update production Django context"
Written file contexts/production.tar
✓ Context committed to version control
Example 4: Pipe to Remote Server

Transfer Django context to remote team member.

docker context export anihpj-prod - | ssh teammate@remote 'cat > anihpj-prod.tar'
✓ Context transferred to remote server
Example 5: Compress Export

Create compressed Django context archive.

docker context export anihpj-prod - | gzip > anihpj-prod.tar.gz
✓ Compressed context: anihpj-prod.tar.gz (2.1KB → 847B)

docker context import

Official Docs: docker context import | Docker CLI: docker context import

Import a Docker context from a tar archive.

Full Description

The docker context import command imports context configuration from a tar file. For your anihpj Django project, this enables receiving and applying deployment configurations from team members for User authentication, JobPost management, Skills verification, Author profiles, and Location services.

Syntax

docker context import CONTEXT FILE

# Import from tar file
docker context import anihpj-prod anihpj-prod.tar

# Import from stdin
cat anihpj-prod.tar | docker context import anihpj-prod -

Parameters Description

  • CONTEXT: Name for imported context.
  • FILE: Input tar file or - for stdin.

Attributes

  • Configuration Distribution: Import Django contexts from team. Standardize deployment settings quickly.
  • Restore Backups: Recover context configurations. Restore Django environment settings.
  • Onboarding: New team members get contexts. Set up Django deployments instantly.
  • Stdin Support: Import from pipes. Automate Django context distribution.
  • Name Override: Import with different name. Customize Django context naming.

Methods

Import from File

Syntax: docker context import [CONTEXT] [FILE].tar

Parameters: CONTEXT, FILE (strings)

Import Django context configuration from tar archive.

Import from Stdin

Syntax: docker context import [CONTEXT] -

Parameters: CONTEXT (string)

Import Django context from piped input.

Batch Import

Syntax: for f in *.tar; do docker context import $(basename $f .tar) $f; done

Parameters: None

Import multiple Django contexts from backup directory.

Return Value

Returns context name on successful import.

5 Practical Django Examples

Example 1: Import Production Context

Receive Django production configuration from team lead.

docker context import anihpj-prod anihpj-prod.tar
anihpj-prod
✓ Successfully imported context "anihpj-prod"
Example 2: Team Onboarding

New developer imports all Django contexts.

docker context import anihpj-dev contexts/dev.tar
docker context import anihpj-staging contexts/staging.tar
docker context import anihpj-prod contexts/prod.tar

docker context ls
anihpj-dev
anihpj-staging
anihpj-prod

NAME             DESCRIPTION           DOCKER ENDPOINT
anihpj-dev       Local development     unix:///var/run/docker.sock
anihpj-staging   Staging server        ssh://deploy@staging.anihpj.com
anihpj-prod      Production server     ssh://deploy@prod.anihpj.com
✓ New developer ready to deploy
Example 3: Restore from Backup

Recover Django contexts from archive.

cd context-backups
for backup in *.tar; do
  name=$(basename $backup .tar)
  docker context import $name $backup
done
anihpj-dev
anihpj-staging
anihpj-prod
✓ All contexts restored
Example 4: Import with Custom Name

Import Django context with different name.

docker context import anihpj-prod-backup anihpj-prod.tar
anihpj-prod-backup
✓ Context imported as anihpj-prod-backup
Example 5: Remote Transfer and Import

Receive and import Django context from remote server.

ssh teammate@remote 'docker context export anihpj-prod -' | docker context import anihpj-prod -
anihpj-prod
✓ Context received and imported

docker context inspect

Official Docs: docker context inspect | Docker CLI: docker context inspect

Display detailed information on one or more contexts.

Full Description

The docker context inspect command shows detailed JSON information about contexts. For your anihpj Django project, this reveals connection endpoints, metadata, and configuration for User authentication, JobPost management, Skills verification, Author profiles, and Location services deployment environments.

Syntax

docker context inspect [OPTIONS] CONTEXT [CONTEXT...]

# Inspect single context
docker context inspect anihpj-prod

# Inspect with format
docker context inspect --format='{{.Endpoints.docker.Host}}' anihpj-prod

Parameters Description

  • CONTEXT: Name of context to inspect.
  • -f, --format: Format output using Go template.

Attributes

  • Detailed Information: Complete context configuration. Review Django deployment settings thoroughly.
  • JSON Output: Machine-readable format. Parse Django context data programmatically.
  • Template Formatting: Extract specific fields. Query Django context properties selectively.
  • Multi-Context: Inspect multiple contexts together. Compare Django environment configurations.
  • Endpoint Details: Shows Docker daemon connections. Verify Django deployment targets.

Methods

Full Inspection

Syntax: docker context inspect [CONTEXT]

Parameters: CONTEXT (string)

Display complete Django context configuration as JSON.

Extract Field

Syntax: docker context inspect --format='{{.[FIELD]}}' [CONTEXT]

Parameters: FIELD (JSON path), CONTEXT (string)

Extract specific Django context property like endpoint or description.

Compare Contexts

Syntax: docker context inspect [CONTEXT1] [CONTEXT2]

Parameters: Multiple CONTEXT names

View multiple Django contexts side-by-side for comparison.

Return Value

Returns JSON array with context details.

5 Practical Django Examples

Example 1: View Complete Context

Inspect Django production context configuration.

docker context inspect anihpj-prod
[
  {
    "Name": "anihpj-prod",
    "Metadata": {
      "Description": "Production environment"
    },
    "Endpoints": {
      "docker": {
