        "Host": "ssh://deploy@prod.anihpj.com",
        "SkipTLSVerify": false
      }
    },
    "TLSMaterial": {},
    "Storage": {
      "MetadataPath": "~/.docker/contexts/meta/...",
      "TLSPath": "~/.docker/contexts/tls/..."
    }
  }
]
Example 2: Extract Endpoint

Get Django deployment host URL.

docker context inspect --format='{{.Endpoints.docker.Host}}' anihpj-prod
ssh://deploy@prod.anihpj.com
Example 3: Compare Environments

View staging and production Django contexts together.

docker context inspect anihpj-staging anihpj-prod
[
  {
    "Name": "anihpj-staging",
    "Endpoints": {
      "docker": {
        "Host": "ssh://deploy@staging.anihpj.com"
      }
    }
  },
  {
    "Name": "anihpj-prod",
    "Endpoints": {
      "docker": {
        "Host": "ssh://deploy@prod.anihpj.com"
      }
    }
  }
]
Example 4: Verify Context Properties

Check Django context metadata.

docker context inspect --format='Name: {{.Name}}\nDescription: {{.Metadata.Description}}\nHost: {{.Endpoints.docker.Host}}' anihpj-prod
Name: anihpj-prod
Description: Production environment
Host: ssh://deploy@prod.anihpj.com
Example 5: List All Context Endpoints

Display deployment hosts for all Django contexts.

for context in $(docker context ls -q); do
  echo "$context: $(docker context inspect --format='{{.Endpoints.docker.Host}}' $context)"
done
anihpj-dev: unix:///var/run/docker.sock
anihpj-staging: ssh://deploy@staging.anihpj.com
anihpj-prod: ssh://deploy@prod.anihpj.com

docker context ls

Official Docs: docker context ls | Docker CLI: docker context ls

List available Docker contexts.

Full Description

The docker context ls command lists all available contexts with their endpoints and status. For your anihpj Django project, this shows all configured deployment environments for User authentication, JobPost management, Skills verification, Author profiles, and Location services.

Syntax

docker context ls [OPTIONS]

# List all contexts
docker context ls

# Quiet mode (IDs only)
docker context ls -q

Parameters Description

  • --format: Format output using template.
  • -q, --quiet: Only display context names.

Attributes

  • Context Overview: Lists all Django deployment environments. See available contexts at a glance.
  • Active Indicator: Asterisk marks current context. Identify active Django environment quickly.
  • Endpoint Display: Shows connection details. Verify Django deployment hosts.
  • Custom Formatting: Go template support. Extract Django context data programmatically.
  • Quick Reference: Fast context lookup. Check Django environments without details.

Methods

Standard List

Syntax: docker context ls

Parameters: None

Display all Django contexts with endpoints and current indicator.

Quiet Mode

Syntax: docker context ls -q

Parameters: None

List only Django context names for scripting.

Custom Format

Syntax: docker context ls --format '{{.Name}}: {{.DockerEndpoint}}'

Parameters: Template string

Display Django contexts with custom column layout.

Return Value

Returns table with context information.

5 Practical Django Examples

Example 1: View All Contexts

List Django deployment environments.

docker context ls
NAME             DESCRIPTION           DOCKER ENDPOINT                    
anihpj-dev       Local development     unix:///var/run/docker.sock        
anihpj-staging   Staging server        ssh://deploy@staging.anihpj.com    
anihpj-prod *    Production server     ssh://deploy@prod.anihpj.com       
default          Default context       unix:///var/run/docker.sock
Example 2: Identify Active Context

Check which Django environment is active.

docker context ls | grep '*'
anihpj-prod *    Production server     ssh://deploy@prod.anihpj.com
✓ Currently deploying to production
Example 3: List Context Names Only

Get Django context names for scripting.

docker context ls -q
anihpj-dev
anihpj-staging
anihpj-prod
default
Example 4: Custom Format Display

Show Django contexts with specific columns.

docker context ls --format "table {{.Name}}\t{{.DockerEndpoint}}\t{{.Current}}"
NAME             DOCKER ENDPOINT                       CURRENT
anihpj-dev       unix:///var/run/docker.sock           false
anihpj-staging   ssh://deploy@staging.anihpj.com       false
anihpj-prod      ssh://deploy@prod.anihpj.com          true
Example 5: Count Available Contexts

Check number of configured Django environments.

docker context ls -q | wc -l
4
✓ 4 deployment contexts configured

docker context rm

Official Docs: docker context rm | Docker CLI: docker context rm

Remove one or more Docker contexts.

Full Description

The docker context rm command removes contexts from Docker configuration. For your anihpj Django project, this enables cleaning up unused deployment environments for User authentication, JobPost management, Skills verification, Author profiles, and Location services.

Syntax

docker context rm CONTEXT [CONTEXT...]

# Remove single context
docker context rm anihpj-old

# Remove multiple contexts
docker context rm anihpj-dev-old anihpj-staging-old

Parameters Description

  • CONTEXT: Name of context(s) to remove.
  • -f, --force: Force removal without confirmation.

Attributes

  • Context Cleanup: Remove unused Django deployment targets. Maintain clean context list.
  • Multi-Remove: Delete multiple contexts at once. Batch clean Django environments.
  • Active Protection: Cannot remove current context. Prevent deleting active Django environment.
  • Permanent Deletion: Context removed from Docker. Cannot undo Django context removal.
  • Default Protection: Cannot remove default context. Preserve system Django configuration.

Methods

Single Removal

Syntax: docker context rm [CONTEXT]

Parameters: CONTEXT (string)

Remove one Django context from configuration.

Batch Removal

Syntax: docker context rm [CONTEXT1] [CONTEXT2] ...

Parameters: Multiple CONTEXT names

Remove multiple Django contexts simultaneously.

Force Removal

Syntax: docker context rm --force [CONTEXT]

Parameters: CONTEXT (string)

Remove Django context without confirmation prompts.

Return Value

Returns context name on successful removal.

5 Practical Django Examples

Example 1: Remove Old Context

Delete deprecated Django environment.

docker context rm anihpj-old-staging
anihpj-old-staging
✓ Context removed
Example 2: Clean Up Test Contexts

Remove multiple Django test environments.

docker context rm anihpj-test1 anihpj-test2 anihpj-test3
anihpj-test1
anihpj-test2
anihpj-test3
✓ 3 test contexts removed
Example 3: Cannot Remove Active Context

Attempt to remove current Django context fails.

docker context show
docker context rm anihpj-prod
anihpj-prod
Error response: context "anihpj-prod" is in use, cannot remove
✗ Switch context before removing
Example 4: Batch Remove Pattern

Remove all Django contexts matching pattern.

docker context ls -q | grep 'old' | xargs docker context rm
anihpj-old-dev
anihpj-old-staging
✓ Old contexts cleaned up
Example 5: Safe Removal with Verification

Verify Django context before removal.

# Inspect first
docker context inspect anihpj-temp

# Confirm and remove
docker context rm anihpj-temp

# Verify removal
docker context ls
[Context details...]

anihpj-temp
✓ Context removed

NAME             DOCKER ENDPOINT
anihpj-dev       unix:///var/run/docker.sock
anihpj-prod      ssh://deploy@prod.anihpj.com

docker context show

Official Docs: docker context show | Docker CLI: docker context show

Display the current context.

Full Description

The docker context show command displays the name of the currently active context. For your anihpj Django project, this confirms which deployment environment is active for User authentication, JobPost management, Skills verification, Author profiles, and Location services operations.

Syntax

docker context show

# Use in scripts
CURRENT=$(docker context show)
echo "Deploying to: $CURRENT"

Parameters Description

  • No parameters required.

Attributes

  • Quick Reference: Shows active context name. Verify Django deployment target instantly.
  • Script Friendly: Simple string output. Use in Django deployment automation.
  • No Options: Straightforward command. Check Django context without complexity.
  • Safety Check: Confirm environment before deployment. Prevent Django production accidents.
  • Automation Ready: Integrate into CI/CD. Validate Django deployment context automatically.

Methods

Display Current Context

Syntax: docker context show

Parameters: None

Show currently active Django deployment context.

Script Variable

Syntax: CONTEXT=$(docker context show)

Parameters: None

Capture Django context name in variable for scripting.

Conditional Deployment

Syntax: if [ $(docker context show) = "prod" ]; then ...; fi

Parameters: None

Execute Django deployment only if correct context active.

Return Value

Returns current context name.

5 Practical Django Examples

Example 1: Check Active Environment

Verify which Django environment is active.

docker context show
anihpj-prod
Example 2: Pre-Deployment Verification

Confirm Django production context before deploy.

echo "Current context: $(docker context show)"
read -p "Deploy to this environment? (y/n) " confirm
if [ "$confirm" = "y" ]; then
  docker container run -d anihpj/web:latest
fi
Current context: anihpj-prod
Deploy to this environment? (y/n) y
✓ Deployed to production
Example 3: Conditional Script Execution

Run Django migration only in staging.

if [ "$(docker context show)" = "anihpj-staging" ]; then
  echo "Running migrations in staging"
  docker container exec anihpj-web python manage.py migrate
else
  echo "Not in staging context, skipping migrations"
fi
Running migrations in staging
Operations to perform: Apply all migrations
✓ Migrations completed in staging
Example 4: CI/CD Context Validation

Validate Django deployment context in pipeline.

#!/bin/bash
EXPECTED_CONTEXT="anihpj-prod"
CURRENT_CONTEXT=$(docker context show)

if [ "$CURRENT_CONTEXT" != "$EXPECTED_CONTEXT" ]; then
  echo "Error: Expected $EXPECTED_CONTEXT, got $CURRENT_CONTEXT"
  exit 1
fi

echo "✓ Context verified, proceeding with deployment"
✓ Context verified, proceeding with deployment
Example 5: Multi-Environment Status

Display Django deployment status with context.

echo "===== Django Deployment Status ====="
echo "Active Context: $(docker context show)"
echo "Running Containers:"
docker container ls --format "  - {{.Names}} ({{.Status}})"
===== Django Deployment Status =====
Active Context: anihpj-prod
Running Containers:
  - anihpj-web (Up 2 hours)
  - anihpj-worker (Up 2 hours)
  - anihpj-db (Up 2 hours)

docker context update

Official Docs: docker context update | Docker CLI: docker context update

Update a Docker context.

Full Description

The docker context update command modifies existing context configuration. For your anihpj Django project, this enables updating deployment endpoints, descriptions, and settings for User authentication, JobPost management, Skills verification, Author profiles, and Location services environments.

Syntax

docker context update [OPTIONS] CONTEXT

# Update description
docker context update --description "New description" anihpj-prod

# Update endpoint
docker context update --docker host=ssh://newuser@prod.anihpj.com anihpj-prod

Parameters Description

  • CONTEXT: Name of context to update.
  • --description: New description text.
  • --docker: New Docker daemon endpoint.

Attributes

  • Configuration Updates: Modify context without recreation. Update Django deployment settings easily.
  • Endpoint Changes: Update Docker daemon connection. Switch Django deployment hosts.
  • Metadata Management: Change descriptions and labels. Document Django context updates.
  • Preserve Settings: Only specified fields updated. Maintain Django context configuration partially.
  • No Downtime: Update without recreating context. Change Django settings seamlessly.

Methods

Update Description

Syntax: docker context update --description "[TEXT]" [CONTEXT]

Parameters: TEXT, CONTEXT (strings)

Update Django context description for better documentation.

Update Endpoint

Syntax: docker context update --docker host=[HOST] [CONTEXT]

Parameters: HOST, CONTEXT (strings)

Change Django deployment server connection details.

Multiple Updates

Syntax: docker context update --description "[TEXT]" --docker host=[HOST] [CONTEXT]

Parameters: TEXT, HOST, CONTEXT (strings)

Update multiple Django context properties simultaneously.

Return Value

Returns context name on successful update.

5 Practical Django Examples

Example 1: Update Description

Improve Django context documentation.

docker context update --description "Production environment - Primary datacenter" anihpj-prod
anihpj-prod
✓ Context description updated
Example 2: Change Deployment Host

Update Django production server endpoint.

docker context update --docker host=ssh://deploy@new-prod.anihpj.com anihpj-prod
anihpj-prod
✓ Endpoint updated to new-prod.anihpj.com
Example 3: Update Multiple Properties

Update Django context endpoint and description together.

docker context update \
  --description "Staging - Load balanced cluster" \
  --docker host=ssh://deploy@staging-cluster.anihpj.com \
  anihpj-staging
anihpj-staging
✓ Description and endpoint updated
Example 4: Update After Server Migration

Update Django contexts after infrastructure change.

# Update production
docker context update --docker host=ssh://deploy@us-east-1.anihpj.com anihpj-prod

# Update staging
docker context update --docker host=ssh://deploy@us-east-1-staging.anihpj.com anihpj-staging

# Verify updates
docker context ls
anihpj-prod
anihpj-staging

NAME             DOCKER ENDPOINT
anihpj-prod      ssh://deploy@us-east-1.anihpj.com
anihpj-staging   ssh://deploy@us-east-1-staging.anihpj.com
✓ All contexts migrated to new region
Example 5: Update with Verification

Update and verify Django context changes.

# Before update
docker context inspect anihpj-prod --format='{{.Endpoints.docker.Host}}'

# Update
docker context update --docker host=ssh://deploy@prod-v2.anihpj.com anihpj-prod

# After update
docker context inspect anihpj-prod --format='{{.Endpoints.docker.Host}}'
ssh://deploy@prod.anihpj.com

anihpj-prod
✓ Context updated

ssh://deploy@prod-v2.anihpj.com
✓ Endpoint successfully changed

docker context use

Official Docs: docker context use | Docker CLI: docker context use

Set the current Docker context.

Full Description

The docker context use command switches the active Docker context. For your anihpj Django project, this enables seamless switching between development, staging, and production environments for User authentication, JobPost management, Skills verification, Author profiles, and Location services deployments.

Syntax

docker context use CONTEXT

# Switch to production
docker context use anihpj-prod

# Switch to development
docker context use anihpj-dev

Parameters Description

  • CONTEXT: Name of context to activate.

Attributes

  • Environment Switching: Change active deployment target. Switch Django environments instantly.
  • Persistent Setting: Remains active until changed. Django operations use selected context consistently.
  • Global Effect: All docker commands use this context. Affects all Django operations in terminal.
  • No Downtime: Switch without affecting running containers. Change Django targets safely.
  • Simple Syntax: One command to switch. Change Django deployment environment easily.

Methods

Switch Context

Syntax: docker context use [CONTEXT]

Parameters: CONTEXT (string)

Activate specified Django deployment environment.

Switch and Verify

Syntax: docker context use [CONTEXT] && docker context show

Parameters: CONTEXT (string)

Switch Django context and confirm activation.

Temporary Switch

Syntax: ORIGINAL=$(docker context show); docker context use [CONTEXT]; ...; docker context use $ORIGINAL

Parameters: CONTEXT (string)

Switch Django context temporarily then restore original.

Return Value

Returns context name on successful switch.

5 Practical Django Examples

Example 1: Switch to Production

Activate Django production environment.

docker context use anihpj-prod
docker context show
anihpj-prod
anihpj-prod
✓ Now deploying to production
Example 2: Development to Production Workflow

Test Django locally then deploy to production.

# Test locally
docker context use anihpj-dev
docker container run --rm anihpj/web:latest python manage.py test

# Deploy to production
docker context use anihpj-prod
docker container run -d --name anihpj-web anihpj/web:latest
anihpj-dev
✓ Tests passed locally

anihpj-prod
✓ Deployed to production
Example 3: Multi-Environment Deployment

Deploy Django to staging then production.

# Deploy to staging
docker context use anihpj-staging
docker container run -d anihpj/web:v2.5.0
sleep 30  # Allow smoke tests

# Deploy to production
docker context use anihpj-prod
docker container run -d anihpj/web:v2.5.0
anihpj-staging
✓ Deployed to staging

anihpj-prod
✓ Deployed to production
Example 4: Safe Context Switching

Verify Django context before critical operations.

echo "Switching to production..."
docker context use anihpj-prod

echo "Current context: $(docker context show)"
read -p "Proceed with deployment? (yes/no) " confirm

if [ "$confirm" = "yes" ]; then
  docker container run -d anihpj/web:latest
else
  echo "Deployment cancelled"
fi
Switching to production...
anihpj-prod
Current context: anihpj-prod
Proceed with deployment? (yes/no) yes
✓ Deployed to production
Example 5: Temporary Context Switch

Switch Django context temporarily for one operation.

# Save current context
ORIGINAL=$(docker context show)
echo "Current: $ORIGINAL"

# Switch temporarily
docker context use anihpj-staging
docker container exec anihpj-web python manage.py check

# Restore original
docker context use $ORIGINAL
echo "Restored to: $(docker context show)"
Current: anihpj-dev

anihpj-staging
System check identified no issues (0 silenced).

anihpj-dev
Restored to: anihpj-dev

🖼️ Image Commands

Manage Docker images including listing, building, pulling, pushing, tagging, and removing images for your anihpj Django project.

docker image

Official Docs: docker image | Docker CLI: docker image

Manage Docker images.

Full Description

The docker image command provides a suite of subcommands for managing Docker images, which are read-only templates used to create containers. For your anihpj Django project, images contain your application code, Python dependencies, Django settings, static files, and all runtime requirements for User authentication, JobPost management, Skills verification, Author profiles, and Location services.

Syntax

docker image COMMAND

# Available subcommands
docker image history  # Show image layer history
docker image import   # Import tarball as image
docker image inspect  # Display detailed image information
docker image load     # Load image from tar archive
docker image ls       # List images
docker image prune    # Remove unused images
docker image pull     # Pull image from registry
docker image push     # Push image to registry
docker image rm       # Remove one or more images
docker image save     # Save images to tar archive
docker image tag      # Create tag for image

Parameters Description

  • COMMAND: Subcommand for image operations.

Attributes

  • Image Management: Complete lifecycle management for Django images. Build, store, distribute, and remove container images.
  • Registry Integration: Push/pull images to/from Docker Hub or private registries. Share Django application images across teams.
  • Layer Inspection: View image layers and history. Optimize Django image size and build process.
  • Tag Management: Version and organize images with tags. Track Django application releases and environments.
  • Storage Cleanup: Prune unused images to free disk space. Maintain clean Django development environment.

Methods

List and Inspect

Syntax: docker image ls && docker image inspect [IMAGE]

Parameters: IMAGE (string)

View available Django images and examine detailed configuration.

Pull and Push

Syntax: docker image pull [IMAGE] && docker image push [IMAGE]

Parameters: IMAGE (string)

Download Django images from registry or upload to share with team.

Tag and Remove

Syntax: docker image tag [SOURCE] [TARGET] && docker image rm [IMAGE]

Parameters: SOURCE, TARGET, IMAGE (strings)

Version Django images and clean up unused versions.

Return Value

Returns usage information and available subcommands.

5 Practical Django Examples

Example 1: Complete Image Workflow

Build, tag, and manage Django application images.

# List current images
docker image ls

# Build new Django image
docker build -t anihpj/web:v1.0.0 .

# Tag for production
docker image tag anihpj/web:v1.0.0 anihpj/web:prod

# List after tagging
docker image ls anihpj/web
REPOSITORY    TAG       IMAGE ID       CREATED         SIZE
anihpj/web    v1.0.0    5k4j3i2h1g0f   2 minutes ago   450MB
anihpj/web    prod      5k4j3i2h1g0f   2 minutes ago   450MB
Example 2: Registry Operations

Push Django image to registry and pull on another machine.

# Push to Docker Hub
docker image push anihpj/web:v1.0.0

# On deployment server
docker image pull anihpj/web:v1.0.0
The push refers to repository [docker.io/anihpj/web]
v1.0.0: digest: sha256:abc123... size: 2841
✓ Image pushed to registry

v1.0.0: Pulling from anihpj/web
✓ Image pulled successfully
Example 3: Image Inspection

View Django image details and configuration.

# Detailed inspection
docker image inspect anihpj/web:v1.0.0

# View specific field
docker image inspect --format='{{.Config.Env}}' anihpj/web:v1.0.0
[DJANGO_SETTINGS_MODULE=anihpj.settings PYTHONPATH=/app]
Example 4: Image History

Analyze Django image layers and sizes.

docker image history anihpj/web:v1.0.0
IMAGE          CREATED         SIZE      COMMENT
5k4j3i2h1g0f   5 minutes ago   45MB      CMD ["gunicorn" "anihpj.wsgi"]
3i2h1g0f9e8d   5 minutes ago   0B        EXPOSE 8000
1g0f9e8d7c6b   5 minutes ago   15MB      RUN python manage.py collectstatic
5a4b3c2d1e0f   6 minutes ago   180MB     RUN pip install -r requirements.txt
9k8j7i6h5g4f   8 minutes ago   85MB      RUN apt-get install build-essential
Example 5: Cleanup Unused Images

Remove old Django image versions to free space.

# Remove specific old version
docker image rm anihpj/web:v0.9.0

# Prune dangling images
docker image prune

# Prune all unused images
docker image prune -a
Untagged: anihpj/web:v0.9.0
Deleted: sha256:old123...

Deleted Images:
sha256:abc... 45MB
Total reclaimed space: 245MB

docker image history

Official Docs: docker image history | Docker CLI: docker image history

Show the history of an image.

Full Description

The docker image history command displays the layer history of an image, showing each layer's creation details, size, and the command that created it. For your anihpj Django project, this helps analyze how your application image was built, identify large layers affecting performance, and optimize Dockerfile instructions for User authentication, JobPost management, and other services.

Syntax

docker image history [OPTIONS] IMAGE

# Basic history
docker image history anihpj/web:latest

# Show full commands (no truncation)
docker image history --no-trunc anihpj/web:latest

# Human-readable sizes
docker image history --human anihpj/web:latest

Parameters Description

  • IMAGE: Name or ID of Django image to inspect.
  • --no-trunc: Don't truncate output. Show full Dockerfile commands.
  • --human, -H: Print sizes in human-readable format (default true).
  • --quiet, -q: Only show image IDs.

Attributes

  • Layer Analysis: View each image layer's contribution to size. Identify Django image bloat sources.
  • Build Optimization: See which Dockerfile instructions create large layers. Optimize Django image build.
  • Command History: View exact commands that built each layer. Understand Django image construction.
  • Time Tracking: See when each layer was created. Debug Django build timeline issues.
  • Size Impact: Measure individual layer sizes. Reduce Django image total size.

Methods

Basic History View

Syntax: docker image history [IMAGE]

Parameters: IMAGE (string)

Display layer history for Django image with readable sizes.

Full Command History

Syntax: docker image history --no-trunc [IMAGE]

Parameters: IMAGE (string)

Show complete Dockerfile commands without truncation for Django image analysis.

Size-Only Analysis

Syntax: docker image history [IMAGE] | awk '{print $4}'

Parameters: IMAGE (string)

Extract only layer sizes to analyze Django image size distribution.

Return Value

Returns table with image layer history including ID, creation time, author, size, and creation command.

5 Practical Django Examples

Example 1: Analyze Django Image Layers

View complete layer structure of anihpj web image.

docker image history anihpj/web:latest
IMAGE          CREATED         CREATED BY                                      SIZE      
5k4j3i2h1g0f   10 minutes ago  CMD ["gunicorn" "--bind" "0.0.0.0:8000" "an…   0B        
3i2h1g0f9e8d   10 minutes ago  EXPOSE 8000                                     0B        
1g0f9e8d7c6b   10 minutes ago  RUN python manage.py collectstatic --noinp…     45MB      
9e8d7c6b5a4f   11 minutes ago  ENV DJANGO_SETTINGS_MODULE=anihpj.settings      0B        
7c6b5a4d3e2f   11 minutes ago  COPY . .                                        120MB     
5a4b3c2d1e0f   12 minutes ago  RUN pip install --no-cache-dir -r requirem…     185MB     
3f2e1d0c9b8a   13 minutes ago  COPY requirements.txt .                         2KB       
9k8j7i6h5g4f   14 minutes ago  RUN apt-get update && apt-get install -y b…     95MB      
1a2b3c4d5e6f   15 minutes ago  WORKDIR /app                                    0B        
2b5b8b6f9b8c   2 weeks ago     /bin/sh -c #(nop)  CMD ["python3"]              0B
Example 2: Identify Large Layers

Find which layers contribute most to Django image size.

docker image history anihpj/web:latest --human | grep -E '[0-9]+MB' | sort -k 5 -hr
5a4b3c2d1e0f   12 minutes ago   RUN pip install...      185MB
7c6b5a4d3e2f   11 minutes ago   COPY . .                120MB
9k8j7i6h5g4f   14 minutes ago   RUN apt-get install...   95MB
1g0f9e8d7c6b   10 minutes ago   RUN collectstatic...     45MB

✓ Python dependencies (185MB) is largest layer
Example 3: View Full Dockerfile Commands

See complete commands used to build Django image.

docker image history --no-trunc anihpj/web:latest | head -n 5
IMAGE                                                                CREATED         CREATED BY
sha256:5k4j3i2h1g0f...                                                10 minutes ago  CMD ["gunicorn" "--bind" "0.0.0.0:8000" "anihpj.wsgi:application"]
sha256:3i2h1g0f9e8d...                                                10 minutes ago  EXPOSE 8000
sha256:1g0f9e8d7c6b...                                                10 minutes ago  RUN /bin/sh -c python manage.py collectstatic --noinput
sha256:9e8d7c6b5a4f...                                                11 minutes ago  ENV DJANGO_SETTINGS_MODULE=anihpj.settings PYTHONPATH=/app
Example 4: Compare Image Versions

Compare layer changes between Django versions.

# Old version
echo "=== Version 1.0.0 ===" 
docker image history anihpj/web:v1.0.0 --quiet

# New version
echo "=== Version 1.1.0 ==="
docker image history anihpj/web:v1.1.0 --quiet
=== Version 1.0.0 ===
5k4j3i2h1g0f
3i2h1g0f9e8d
1g0f9e8d7c6b
(10 total layers)

=== Version 1.1.0 ===
7m6l5k4j3i2h
9n8m7l6k5j4i
2h1g0f9e8d7c
(12 total layers - added 2 layers for new features)
Example 5: Calculate Total Image Size

Sum all layer sizes for Django image.

docker image history anihpj/web:latest --human=false | awk 'NR>1 {sum+=$4} END {print "Total Size: " sum/1024/1024 " MB"}'
Total Size: 447 MB

Breakdown:
- Base image: 85MB
- System packages: 95MB
- Python deps: 185MB
- Application code: 120MB
- Static files: 45MB

docker image import

Official Docs: docker image import | Docker CLI: docker image import

Import the contents from a tarball to create a filesystem image.

Full Description

The docker image import command creates a Docker image from a tarball archive containing a filesystem. For your anihpj Django project, this enables importing pre-configured Django application filesystems, migrating images between systems without registry access, or creating images from container exports for User authentication, JobPost management, and other services.

Syntax

docker image import [OPTIONS] file|URL|- [REPOSITORY[:TAG]]

# Import from tarball file
docker image import django-export.tar anihpj/web:imported

# Import from URL
docker image import https://example.com/django-fs.tar anihpj/web:latest

# Import from stdin
cat django-export.tar | docker image import - anihpj/web:v1.0

Parameters Description

  • file|URL|-: Path to tarball, URL, or stdin (-) containing filesystem.
  • REPOSITORY[:TAG]: Name and optional tag for imported Django image.
  • -c, --change: Apply Dockerfile instruction to imported image. Add CMD, EXPOSE, ENV for Django.
  • -m, --message: Set commit message for imported Django image.

Attributes

  • Filesystem Import: Create Django image from raw filesystem tarball. Import pre-configured environments.
  • URL Support: Download and import Django filesystem from remote location. Automated deployment workflows.
  • Stdin Import: Pipe filesystem directly into import. Chain Django backup/restore operations.
  • Custom Instructions: Apply Dockerfile commands during import. Configure Django image metadata.
  • No Build Context: Import without Dockerfile. Create Django images from exported containers.

Methods

Import from File

Syntax: docker image import [FILE] [REPO:TAG]

Parameters: FILE (path), REPO:TAG (string)

Create Django image from local tarball archive.

Import with Configuration

Syntax: docker image import -c "CMD [...]" [FILE] [REPO:TAG]

Parameters: FILE (path), REPO:TAG (string)

Import Django filesystem and set startup command.

Import from Remote URL

Syntax: docker image import [URL] [REPO:TAG]

Parameters: URL (string), REPO:TAG (string)

Download and import Django filesystem from remote location.

Return Value

Returns image ID of imported filesystem.

5 Practical Django Examples

Example 1: Import Django Container Export

Import anihpj web container filesystem as new image.

# First export running container
docker container export anihpj-web > django-filesystem.tar

# Import as new image
docker image import django-filesystem.tar anihpj/web:imported
sha256:9m8n7o6p5q4r...
✓ Imported Django filesystem as anihpj/web:imported
Example 2: Import with CMD Instruction

Import Django filesystem and set startup command.

docker image import \
  -c 'CMD ["gunicorn", "--bind", "0.0.0.0:8000", "anihpj.wsgi:application"]' \
  django-export.tar \
  anihpj/web:with-cmd
sha256:7l6k5j4i3h2g...
✓ Imported with gunicorn startup command
Example 3: Import from Remote URL

Download and import Django image from backup server.

docker image import \
  https://backups.anihpj.com/django-prod-20260114.tar \
  anihpj/web:prod-backup
Downloading...
sha256:5i4h3g2f1e0d...
✓ Production backup imported
Example 4: Import with Multiple Instructions

Import Django filesystem with full configuration.

docker image import \
  -c 'ENV DJANGO_SETTINGS_MODULE=anihpj.settings' \
  -c 'ENV PYTHONPATH=/app' \
  -c 'EXPOSE 8000' \
  -c 'WORKDIR /app' \
  -c 'CMD ["python", "manage.py", "runserver", "0.0.0.0:8000"]' \
  -m "Imported Django production snapshot" \
  django-configured.tar \
  anihpj/web:configured
sha256:3g2f1e0d9c8b...
✓ Django image imported with full configuration
Example 5: Import via Pipe

Stream Django filesystem from remote to import.

# Export and import in one command
ssh prod-server 'docker container export anihpj-web' | \
  docker image import - anihpj/web:prod-snapshot
sha256:1e0d9c8b7a6f...
✓ Production Django snapshot imported directly

docker image inspect

Official Docs: docker image inspect | Docker CLI: docker image inspect

Display detailed information on one or more images.

Full Description

The docker image inspect command returns detailed JSON information about images including configuration, layers, size, environment variables, and metadata. For your anihpj Django project, this reveals how images are configured, what environment variables are set, which ports are exposed, and other critical details for User authentication, JobPost management, and service deployments.

Syntax

docker image inspect [OPTIONS] IMAGE [IMAGE...]

# Inspect single image
docker image inspect anihpj/web:latest

# Inspect with format
docker image inspect --format='{{.Config.Env}}' anihpj/web:latest

# Inspect multiple images
docker image inspect anihpj/web:latest anihpj/worker:latest

Parameters Description

  • IMAGE: Name or ID of Django image(s) to inspect.
  • -f, --format: Format output using Go template. Extract specific Django image fields.

Attributes

  • Complete Details: Full JSON representation of Django image. All configuration and metadata visible.
  • Configuration Access: View CMD, ENTRYPOINT, ENV, EXPOSE. Understand Django image runtime behavior.
  • Layer Information: See all layer IDs and sizes. Analyze Django image composition.
  • Template Formatting: Extract specific fields with Go templates. Query Django image properties programmatically.
  • Multi-Image Support: Inspect multiple Django images at once. Compare image configurations.

Methods

Full JSON Inspection

Syntax: docker image inspect [IMAGE]

Parameters: IMAGE (string)

Display complete Django image configuration as JSON.

Extract Specific Field

Syntax: docker image inspect --format='{{.[FIELD]}}' [IMAGE]

Parameters: FIELD (JSON path), IMAGE (string)

Query specific Django image property like environment or size.

Compare Multiple Images

Syntax: docker image inspect [IMAGE1] [IMAGE2] --format='{{.Id}} {{.Size}}'

Parameters: IMAGE1, IMAGE2 (strings)

Compare Django image sizes and IDs side-by-side.

Return Value

Returns JSON array with detailed image information.

5 Practical Django Examples

Example 1: View Complete Image Configuration

Inspect anihpj web image full details.

docker image inspect anihpj/web:latest | head -n 30
[
    {
        "Id": "sha256:5k4j3i2h1g0f...",
        "RepoTags": [
            "anihpj/web:latest",
            "anihpj/web:v1.0.0"
        ],
        "Size": 447000000,
        "VirtualSize": 447000000,
        "Config": {
            "Env": [
                "DJANGO_SETTINGS_MODULE=anihpj.settings",
                "PYTHONPATH=/app"
            ],
            "Cmd": [
                "gunicorn",
                "--bind",
                "0.0.0.0:8000",
                "anihpj.wsgi:application"
            ],
            "ExposedPorts": {
                "8000/tcp": {}
            },
            "WorkingDir": "/app"
        }
    }
]
Example 2: Extract Environment Variables

View Django environment configuration.

docker image inspect --format='{{range .Config.Env}}{{println .}}{{end}}' anihpj/web:latest
PATH=/usr/local/bin:/usr/bin:/bin
DJANGO_SETTINGS_MODULE=anihpj.settings
PYTHONPATH=/app
DJANGO_APP=jobpost
Example 3: Check Image Size

Get Django image size in human-readable format.

docker image inspect --format='{{.Size}}' anihpj/web:latest | awk '{print $1/1024/1024 " MB"}'
447 MB
Example 4: View Exposed Ports

Check which ports Django image exposes.

docker image inspect --format='{{range $port, $_ := .Config.ExposedPorts}}{{println $port}}{{end}}' anihpj/web:latest
8000/tcp
Example 5: Compare Image Versions

Compare Django image sizes across versions.

for tag in v1.0.0 v1.1.0 latest; do
  size=$(docker image inspect --format='{{.Size}}' anihpj/web:$tag)
  echo "anihpj/web:$tag - $(echo $size | awk '{print $1/1024/1024}') MB"
done
anihpj/web:v1.0.0 - 445 MB
anihpj/web:v1.1.0 - 447 MB
anihpj/web:latest - 447 MB

docker image load

Official Docs: docker image load | Docker CLI: docker image load

Load an image from a tar archive or STDIN.

Full Description

The docker image load command loads Docker images from tar archives created by docker image save. For your anihpj Django project, this enables transferring complete images between systems, restoring from backups, or deploying Django applications to air-gapped environments without registry access for User authentication, JobPost management, and other services.

Syntax

docker image load [OPTIONS]

# Load from file
docker image load -i django-image.tar

# Load from stdin
docker image load < django-image.tar

# Load via pipe
cat django-image.tar | docker image load

Parameters Description

  • -i, --input: Read from tar archive file instead of STDIN.
  • -q, --quiet: Suppress load output.

Attributes

  • Complete Restore: Loads full Django image with all layers and metadata. Perfect backup/restore solution.
  • Offline Transfer: Move Django images without registry. Deploy to air-gapped environments.
  • Stdin Support: Pipe directly from save command. Efficient Django image migration.
  • Preserves Everything: Maintains tags, history, and configuration. Exact Django image replication.
  • Multi-Image Support: Load multiple Django images from single archive. Batch image restoration.

Methods

Load from File

Syntax: docker image load -i [FILE].tar

Parameters: FILE (path)

Load Django image from tar archive file.

Load from Stdin

Syntax: docker image load < [FILE].tar

Parameters: FILE (path)

Load Django image using stdin redirection.

Pipe Load

Syntax: cat [FILE].tar | docker image load

Parameters: FILE (path)

Stream Django image archive through pipe.

Return Value

Returns loaded image name and tag.

5 Practical Django Examples

Example 1: Load Django Image Backup

Restore anihpj web image from backup archive.

docker image load -i anihpj-web-backup.tar
Loaded image: anihpj/web:v1.0.0
Loaded image: anihpj/web:latest
✓ Django images restored
Example 2: Transfer to Production Server

Copy Django image to server without registry.

# On development machine
docker image save anihpj/web:prod > django-prod.tar
scp django-prod.tar prod-server:/tmp/

# On production server
ssh prod-server 'docker image load -i /tmp/django-prod.tar'
Loaded image: anihpj/web:prod
✓ Production Django image deployed
Example 3: Load via SSH Pipe

Stream Django image directly between servers.

ssh dev-server 'docker image save anihpj/web:latest' | docker image load
Loaded image: anihpj/web:latest
✓ Django image transferred directly
Example 4: Load Multiple Images

Restore complete Django stack from archive.

docker image load -i anihpj-stack.tar
Loaded image: anihpj/web:latest
Loaded image: anihpj/worker:latest
Loaded image: anihpj/nginx:latest
Loaded image: postgres:15
✓ Complete Django stack loaded (4 images)
Example 5: Quiet Load for Scripts

Load Django image silently in deployment script.

docker image load -q -i anihpj-web.tar
if [ $? -eq 0 ]; then
  echo "✓ Django image loaded successfully"
  docker container run -d anihpj/web:latest
fi
✓ Django image loaded successfully
✓ Container started

docker image ls

Official Docs: docker image ls | Docker CLI: docker image ls

List images.

Full Description

The docker image ls command lists all Docker images available on the system, showing repository names, tags, IDs, creation dates, and sizes. For your anihpj Django project, this helps track available image versions, monitor disk usage, identify outdated images for User authentication, JobPost management, and clean up unused Django service images.

Syntax

docker image ls [OPTIONS] [REPOSITORY[:TAG]]

# List all images
docker image ls

# List specific repository
docker image ls anihpj/web

# List with filters
docker image ls --filter "dangling=true"

# Custom format
docker image ls --format "table {{.Repository}}\t{{.Tag}}\t{{.Size}}"

Parameters Description

  • REPOSITORY[:TAG]: Filter by Django image repository or tag.
  • -a, --all: Show all images including intermediate layers.
  • -q, --quiet: Only display image IDs.
  • --filter: Filter Django images by conditions (dangling, label, before, since).
  • --format: Format output using Go template.
  • --no-trunc: Don't truncate output (show full image IDs).
  • --digests: Show image digests.

Attributes

  • Image Inventory: Complete list of Django images on system. Track all available versions.
  • Size Monitoring: View image sizes to manage disk space. Identify large Django images.
  • Filter Capabilities: Find specific Django images by criteria. Locate dangling or old images.
  • Custom Formatting: Tailored output for scripts. Extract Django image data programmatically.
  • Quick Reference: See image IDs, tags, and creation dates. Verify Django deployments.

Methods

Standard List

Syntax: docker image ls

Parameters: None

Display all Django images with standard columns.

Filter Dangling Images

Syntax: docker image ls --filter "dangling=true"

Parameters: None

Show untagged Django images that can be pruned.

Custom Format Output

Syntax: docker image ls --format "{{.Repository}}:{{.Tag}}"

Parameters: None

Display Django images in custom format for scripting.

Return Value

Returns table with repository, tag, image ID, creation time, and size.

5 Practical Django Examples

Example 1: List All Django Images

View all anihpj images on system.

docker image ls anihpj/*
REPOSITORY      TAG       IMAGE ID       CREATED         SIZE
anihpj/web      latest    5k4j3i2h1g0f   2 days ago      447MB
anihpj/web      v1.0.0    5k4j3i2h1g0f   2 days ago      447MB
anihpj/web      prod      5k4j3i2h1g0f   2 days ago      447MB
anihpj/worker   latest    3i2h1g0f9e8d   3 days ago      425MB
anihpj/nginx    latest    1g0f9e8d7c6b   1 week ago      142MB
Example 2: Find Dangling Images

Identify untagged Django images for cleanup.

docker image ls --filter "dangling=true"
REPOSITORY   TAG       IMAGE ID       CREATED        SIZE
           9e8d7c6b5a4f   5 days ago     380MB
           7c6b5a4d3e2f   1 week ago     395MB
✓ 2 dangling images found (775MB total)
Example 3: List Image IDs Only

Get Django image IDs for scripting.

docker image ls -q anihpj/web
5k4j3i2h1g0f
3i2h1g0f9e8d
1g0f9e8d7c6b
Example 4: Custom Format with Size

Display Django images with custom columns.

docker image ls anihpj/web --format "table {{.Repository}}\t{{.Tag}}\t{{.Size}}\t{{.CreatedSince}}"
REPOSITORY     TAG       SIZE    CREATED
anihpj/web     latest    447MB   2 days ago
anihpj/web     v1.0.0    447MB   2 days ago
anihpj/web     prod      447MB   2 days ago
Example 5: Calculate Total Image Size

Sum total disk usage by Django images.

docker image ls anihpj/* --format "{{.Size}}" | \
  sed 's/MB//;s/GB/*1024/' | \
  awk '{sum+=$1} END {print "Total: " sum " MB"}'
Total: 1461 MB

Breakdown:
- anihpj/web: 447MB (3 tags)
- anihpj/worker: 425MB
- anihpj/nginx: 142MB
- Other layers: 447MB (shared)

docker image prune

Official Docs: docker image prune | Docker CLI: docker image prune

Remove unused images.

Full Description

The docker image prune command removes unused Docker images to free disk space. For your anihpj Django project, this cleans up dangling images from failed builds, old development versions, and intermediate layers no longer needed for User authentication, JobPost management, and other services, keeping your development environment lean.

Syntax

docker image prune [OPTIONS]

# Remove dangling images
docker image prune

# Remove all unused images
docker image prune -a

# Force prune without confirmation
docker image prune -f

# Filter by time
docker image prune --filter "until=24h"

Parameters Description

  • -a, --all: Remove all unused Django images, not just dangling ones.
  • -f, --force: Do not prompt for confirmation.
  • --filter: Filter images to prune (until, label).

Attributes

  • Space Recovery: Free disk space occupied by unused Django images. Maintain system performance.
  • Dangling Cleanup: Remove untagged images from failed builds. Clean Django development debris.
  • Selective Removal: Filter by age or labels. Target specific Django image cleanup.
  • Safe Operation: Only removes unused images. Active Django containers unaffected.
  • Confirmation Prompt: Shows space to reclaim before pruning. Review Django cleanup impact.

Methods

Prune Dangling Only

Syntax: docker image prune

Parameters: None

Remove untagged Django images with confirmation.

Prune All Unused

Syntax: docker image prune -a

Parameters: None

Remove all Django images not used by containers.

Filter by Age

Syntax: docker image prune --filter "until=[TIME]"

Parameters: TIME (duration)

Remove Django images older than specified time.

Return Value

Returns list of deleted images and total reclaimed space.

5 Practical Django Examples

Example 1: Clean Dangling Images

Remove untagged Django images from failed builds.

docker image prune
WARNING! This will remove all dangling images.
Are you sure you want to continue? [y/N] y
Deleted Images:
untagged: sha256:9e8d7c6b5a4f...
untagged: sha256:7c6b5a4d3e2f...
deleted: sha256:5a4b3c2d1e0f...

Total reclaimed space: 775MB
Example 2: Force Clean All Unused

Remove all Django images not in use without prompt.

docker image prune -a -f
Deleted Images:
untagged: anihpj/web:v0.9.0
untagged: anihpj/web:old-dev
deleted: sha256:3f2e1d0c9b8a...
deleted: sha256:1a2b3c4d5e6f...

Total reclaimed space: 1.2GB
✓ Old Django versions removed
Example 3: Prune Old Images

Remove Django images older than 7 days.

docker image prune -a --filter "until=168h"
WARNING! This will remove all images without at least one container associated older than 168h.
Are you sure? [y/N] y

Deleted Images:
untagged: anihpj/web:test-jan01
untagged: anihpj/worker:dev-old
deleted: sha256:1g0f9e8d7c6b...

Total reclaimed space: 890MB
✓ Week-old Django images removed
Example 4: Scheduled Cleanup Script

Automated Django image maintenance.

#!/bin/bash
echo "Starting Django image cleanup..."
docker image prune -a -f --filter "until=72h"
docker image prune -f

echo "✓ Cleanup complete"
docker system df
Starting Django image cleanup...
Total reclaimed space: 1.5GB

✓ Cleanup complete

TYPE            TOTAL     ACTIVE    SIZE
Images          8         5         2.1GB
Containers      5         3         450MB
Local Volumes   3         2         1.2GB
Example 5: Pre-Build Cleanup

Clean space before building new Django image.

# Check current usage
docker system df

# Clean dangling images
docker image prune -f

# Build new Django image
docker build -t anihpj/web:v1.1.0 .
Images    12    2.8GB
✓ Space available: 15GB

Deleted Images: 425MB

Step 1/10 : FROM python:3.11-slim
✓ Building new Django image...

docker image pull

Official Docs: docker image pull | Docker CLI: docker image pull

Download an image from a registry.

Full Description

The docker image pull command downloads Docker images from registries like Docker Hub or private registries. For your anihpj Django project, this retrieves base images like Python, PostgreSQL, or pre-built Django application images for User authentication, JobPost management, and other services, enabling quick deployment and consistent environments.

Syntax

docker image pull [OPTIONS] NAME[:TAG|@DIGEST]

# Pull latest Django image
docker image pull anihpj/web:latest

# Pull specific version
docker image pull anihpj/web:v1.0.0

# Pull all tags
docker image pull -a anihpj/web

# Pull from private registry
docker image pull registry.anihpj.com/web:prod

Parameters Description

  • NAME[:TAG|@DIGEST]: Image name, optional tag or digest.
  • -a, --all-tags: Download all tagged Django images in repository.
  • --platform: Set platform if server is multi-platform (linux/amd64, linux/arm64).
  • -q, --quiet: Suppress verbose output.
  • --disable-content-trust: Skip image verification (default true).

Attributes

  • Registry Download: Fetch Django images from Docker Hub or private registries. Access shared images.
  • Layer Optimization: Downloads only missing layers. Efficient Django image updates.
  • Tag Specificity: Pull exact versions or latest. Control Django deployment versions.
  • Multi-Platform: Download for specific architectures. Deploy Django across platforms.
  • Digest Verification: Pull by SHA256 digest for exact match. Ensure Django image integrity.

Methods

Pull Latest

Syntax: docker image pull [IMAGE]:latest

Parameters: IMAGE (string)

Download latest Django image version.

Pull Specific Version

Syntax: docker image pull [IMAGE]:[TAG]

Parameters: IMAGE, TAG (strings)

Download specific Django image release.

Pull All Tags

Syntax: docker image pull -a [IMAGE]

Parameters: IMAGE (string)

Download all Django image versions from repository.

Return Value

Returns image digest and status for each pulled layer.

5 Practical Django Examples

Example 1: Pull Production Django Image

Download anihpj web production image.

docker image pull anihpj/web:prod
prod: Pulling from anihpj/web
a1b2c3d4e5f6: Already exists
7g8h9i0j1k2l: Pulling fs layer
3m4n5o6p7q8r: Downloading [=========>                ] 45.2MB/180MB
9s0t1u2v3w4x: Download complete
5y6z7a8b9c0d: Verifying Checksum
1e2f3g4h5i6j: Pull complete
Digest: sha256:abc123...
Status: Downloaded newer image for anihpj/web:prod
✓ Production image ready
Example 2: Pull Base Images

Download required base images for Django development.

docker image pull python:3.11-slim
docker image pull postgres:15
docker image pull redis:7-alpine
python:3.11-slim: ✓ Downloaded (85MB)
postgres:15: ✓ Downloaded (142MB)
redis:7-alpine: ✓ Downloaded (32MB)

✓ Django stack base images ready
Example 3: Pull from Private Registry

Download Django image from company registry.

# Login first
docker login registry.anihpj.com

# Pull from private registry
docker image pull registry.anihpj.com/anihpj/web:latest
Login Succeeded

latest: Pulling from anihpj/web
Digest: sha256:def456...
Status: Downloaded newer image
✓ Private Django image pulled
Example 4: Pull Specific Platform

Download Django image for ARM architecture.

docker image pull --platform linux/arm64 anihpj/web:latest
linux/arm64 variant pulled
Digest: sha256:ghi789...
✓ ARM64 Django image ready
Example 5: Pull All Versions

Download all tagged Django versions for testing.

docker image pull -a anihpj/web
Pulling repository anihpj/web
v1.0.0: ✓ Downloaded
v1.1.0: ✓ Downloaded
latest: ✓ Downloaded
dev: ✓ Downloaded
prod: ✓ Downloaded

✓ 5 Django image versions pulled

docker image push

Official Docs: docker image push | Docker CLI: docker image push

Upload an image to a registry.

Full Description

The docker image push command uploads Docker images to registries like Docker Hub or private registries. For your anihpj Django project, this shares built images with team members, deploys to production environments, and distributes application releases for User authentication, JobPost management, and other services across infrastructure.

Syntax

docker image push [OPTIONS] NAME[:TAG]

# Push to Docker Hub
docker image push anihpj/web:latest

# Push specific version
docker image push anihpj/web:v1.0.0

# Push all tags
docker image push -a anihpj/web

# Push to private registry
docker image push registry.anihpj.com/web:prod

Parameters Description

  • NAME[:TAG]: Image name and optional tag to push.
  • -a, --all-tags: Push all tagged Django images in repository.
  • -q, --quiet: Suppress verbose output.
  • --disable-content-trust: Skip image signing (default true).

Attributes

  • Registry Upload: Share Django images via Docker Hub or private registries. Team collaboration.
  • Layer Optimization: Pushes only missing layers. Efficient Django image distribution.
  • Version Control: Tag and push specific releases. Track Django application versions.
  • Multi-Tag Push: Upload all tags at once. Deploy multiple Django versions.
  • Digest Output: Returns SHA256 for verification. Ensure Django image integrity.

Methods

Push Single Tag

Syntax: docker image push [IMAGE]:[TAG]

Parameters: IMAGE, TAG (strings)

Upload specific Django image version to registry.

Push All Tags

Syntax: docker image push -a [IMAGE]

Parameters: IMAGE (string)

Upload all Django image tags to registry.

Push to Private Registry

Syntax: docker image push [REGISTRY]/[IMAGE]:[TAG]

Parameters: REGISTRY, IMAGE, TAG (strings)

Upload Django image to company private registry.

Return Value

Returns digest and layer push status.

5 Practical Django Examples

Example 1: Push Production Release

Upload anihpj web production image to Docker Hub.

# Tag for production
docker image tag anihpj/web:latest anihpj/web:v1.0.0

# Push to Docker Hub
docker image push anihpj/web:v1.0.0
The push refers to repository [docker.io/anihpj/web]
a1b2c3d4e5f6: Pushed
7g8h9i0j1k2l: Pushed
3m4n5o6p7q8r: Pushed
9s0t1u2v3w4x: Pushed
v1.0.0: digest: sha256:abc123... size: 2841
✓ Production Django image published
Example 2: Push Multiple Tags

Upload Django image with multiple version tags.

# Tag versions
docker image tag anihpj/web:latest anihpj/web:v1.1.0
docker image tag anihpj/web:latest anihpj/web:prod

# Push all tags
docker image push -a anihpj/web
Pushing anihpj/web:latest
Pushing anihpj/web:v1.1.0
Pushing anihpj/web:prod

✓ 3 tags pushed successfully
Example 3: Push to Private Registry

Upload Django image to company registry.

# Login to private registry
docker login registry.anihpj.com

# Tag for private registry
docker image tag anihpj/web:latest registry.anihpj.com/anihpj/web:latest

# Push
docker image push registry.anihpj.com/anihpj/web:latest
Login Succeeded

The push refers to repository [registry.anihpj.com/anihpj/web]
latest: digest: sha256:def456...
✓ Django image pushed to private registry
Example 4: CI/CD Push Workflow

Automated Django image push in deployment pipeline.

#!/bin/bash
VERSION=$(git describe --tags --always)

# Build Django image
docker build -t anihpj/web:$VERSION .

# Tag as latest
docker image tag anihpj/web:$VERSION anihpj/web:latest

# Push both tags
docker image push anihpj/web:$VERSION
docker image push anihpj/web:latest
v1.2.0: digest: sha256:ghi789...
latest: digest: sha256:ghi789...
✓ Django v1.2.0 published to registry
Example 5: Push with Retry Logic

Reliable Django image push with error handling.

#!/bin/bash
MAX_RETRIES=3
IMAGE="anihpj/web:prod"

for i in $(seq 1 $MAX_RETRIES); do
  echo "Push attempt $i..."
  if docker image push $IMAGE; then
    echo "✓ Push successful"
    exit 0
  fi
  sleep 5
done

echo "✗ Push failed after $MAX_RETRIES attempts"
exit 1
Push attempt 1...
✓ Push successful
✓ Django production image published

docker image rm

Official Docs: docker image rm | Docker CLI: docker image rm

Remove one or more images.

Full Description

The docker image rm command removes Docker images from the local system. For your anihpj Django project, this frees disk space by deleting old image versions, failed builds, or unnecessary base images no longer needed for User authentication, JobPost management, and other services development or deployment.

Syntax

docker image rm [OPTIONS] IMAGE [IMAGE...]

# Remove single image
docker image rm anihpj/web:old

# Remove multiple images
docker image rm anihpj/web:v0.9.0 anihpj/worker:old

# Force remove
docker image rm -f anihpj/web:latest

# Remove by ID
docker image rm 5k4j3i2h1g0f

Parameters Description

  • IMAGE: Name, tag, or ID of Django image to remove.
  • -f, --force: Force removal even if image has containers.
  • --no-prune: Don't delete untagged parent images.

Attributes

  • Space Management: Free disk space by removing old Django images. Maintain clean environment.
  • Multi-Remove: Delete multiple Django images in one command. Batch cleanup operations.
  • Safety Checks: Prevents removal if containers exist. Protects running Django services.
  • Force Option: Override safety checks when needed. Emergency Django image cleanup.
  • Parent Pruning: Removes dangling parent layers automatically. Complete Django cleanup.

Methods

Remove by Tag

Syntax: docker image rm [IMAGE]:[TAG]

Parameters: IMAGE, TAG (strings)

Delete specific Django image version.

Remove by ID

Syntax: docker image rm [IMAGE_ID]

Parameters: IMAGE_ID (string)

Remove Django image using unique identifier.

Force Remove

Syntax: docker image rm -f [IMAGE]

Parameters: IMAGE (string)

Force delete Django image ignoring container dependencies.

Return Value

Returns untagged and deleted image identifiers.

5 Practical Django Examples

Example 1: Remove Old Version

Delete outdated anihpj web image.

docker image rm anihpj/web:v0.9.0
Untagged: anihpj/web:v0.9.0
Deleted: sha256:3f2e1d0c9b8a...
Deleted: sha256:1a2b3c4d5e6f...
✓ Old Django version removed
Example 2: Batch Remove Old Images

Clean up multiple outdated Django images.

docker image rm anihpj/web:v0.8.0 anihpj/web:v0.9.0 anihpj/worker:old
Untagged: anihpj/web:v0.8.0
Untagged: anihpj/web:v0.9.0
Untagged: anihpj/worker:old
Deleted: 3 images
✓ 890MB reclaimed
Example 3: Remove by Pattern

Delete all Django dev images.

docker image ls --filter "reference=anihpj/*:dev*" -q | xargs docker image rm
Untagged: anihpj/web:dev
Untagged: anihpj/web:dev-v2
Untagged: anihpj/worker:dev
✓ Development Django images removed
Example 4: Force Remove Active Image

Remove Django image despite running containers.

# Check container
docker container ls | grep anihpj/web:test

# Force remove
docker image rm -f anihpj/web:test
anihpj-test-container   anihpj/web:test

Untagged: anihpj/web:test
Deleted: sha256:5k4j3i2h1g0f...
⚠️  Warning: Container still running with this image
Example 5: Remove Dangling Images

Clean up untagged Django build artifacts.

docker image ls -f "dangling=true" -q | xargs docker image rm
Deleted: sha256:9e8d7c6b5a4f...
Deleted: sha256:7c6b5a4d3e2f...
Deleted: sha256:5a4b3c2d1e0f...

✓ 775MB dangling Django layers removed

docker image save

Official Docs: docker image save | Docker CLI: docker image save

Save one or more images to a tar archive.

Full Description

The docker image save command exports Docker images to tar archives, preserving all layers, tags, and metadata. For your anihpj Django project, this creates backups of built images, enables offline transfers to production servers, and facilitates air-gapped deployments of User authentication, JobPost management, and other services without registry access.

Syntax

docker image save [OPTIONS] IMAGE [IMAGE...]

# Save to file
docker image save -o django-backup.tar anihpj/web:latest

# Save to stdout
docker image save anihpj/web:latest > django-backup.tar

# Save multiple images
docker image save -o stack.tar anihpj/web:latest anihpj/worker:latest

Parameters Description

  • IMAGE: Name or ID of Django image(s) to save.
  • -o, --output: Write to file instead of stdout.

Attributes

  • Complete Backup: Saves entire Django image with all layers and metadata. Full image preservation.
  • Offline Transfer: Move Django images without internet or registry. Air-gapped deployments.
  • Multi-Image Archive: Save multiple Django images in single tar. Stack distribution.
  • Stdout Support: Stream directly to commands or network. Efficient Django transfers.
  • Lossless Export: Preserves tags, history, configuration. Exact Django image replica.

Methods

Save to File

Syntax: docker image save -o [FILE].tar [IMAGE]

Parameters: FILE (path), IMAGE (string)

Export Django image to tar archive file.

Save to Stdout

Syntax: docker image save [IMAGE] > [FILE].tar

Parameters: IMAGE (string), FILE (path)

Redirect Django image to tar file via stdout.

Save Multiple Images

Syntax: docker image save -o [FILE].tar [IMAGE1] [IMAGE2]

Parameters: FILE (path), IMAGE1, IMAGE2 (strings)

Export multiple Django images to single archive.

Return Value

Creates tar archive containing image layers and configuration.

5 Practical Django Examples

Example 1: Backup Production Image

Create backup archive of anihpj production web image.

docker image save -o anihpj-web-prod-backup.tar anihpj/web:prod
✓ Saved anihpj/web:prod (447MB)
✓ Archive created: anihpj-web-prod-backup.tar
Example 2: Transfer to Production Server

Export Django image and transfer via SCP.

# Save image
docker image save -o django-web.tar anihpj/web:latest

# Transfer to production
scp django-web.tar prod-server:/tmp/

# On production server
ssh prod-server 'docker image load -i /tmp/django-web.tar'
✓ Image saved locally
django-web.tar: 100% 447MB

Loaded image: anihpj/web:latest
✓ Django deployed to production
Example 3: Save Complete Stack

Backup all Django application images together.

docker image save -o anihpj-stack.tar \
  anihpj/web:latest \
  anihpj/worker:latest \
  anihpj/nginx:latest \
  postgres:15 \
  redis:7-alpine
✓ Saved 5 images (1.2GB total)
✓ Complete Django stack archived
Example 4: Stream Save via Pipe

Save and transfer Django image in one operation.

docker image save anihpj/web:latest | \
  ssh prod-server 'docker image load'
Loaded image: anihpj/web:latest
✓ Django image streamed directly to production
Example 5: Compressed Backup

Create compressed Django image backup.

# Save and compress
docker image save anihpj/web:latest | gzip > anihpj-web-backup.tar.gz

# Check size reduction
ls -lh anihpj-web-backup.tar.gz
-rw-r--r-- 1 user user 185M Jan 14 10:30 anihpj-web-backup.tar.gz

✓ Compressed from 447MB to 185MB (59% reduction)

docker image tag

Official Docs: docker image tag | Docker CLI: docker image tag

Create a tag TARGET_IMAGE that refers to SOURCE_IMAGE.

Full Description

The docker image tag command creates new tags for existing images, enabling version management and environment labeling. For your anihpj Django project, this organizes images by version numbers, marks releases for different environments (dev, staging, prod), and prepares images for registry publishing of User authentication, JobPost management, and other services.

Syntax

docker image tag SOURCE_IMAGE[:TAG] TARGET_IMAGE[:TAG]

# Tag with version
docker image tag anihpj/web:latest anihpj/web:v1.0.0

# Tag for environment
docker image tag anihpj/web:latest anihpj/web:prod

# Tag for registry
docker image tag anihpj/web:latest registry.anihpj.com/web:latest

Parameters Description

  • SOURCE_IMAGE[:TAG]: Existing Django image to tag.
  • TARGET_IMAGE[:TAG]: New name and tag for Django image.

Attributes

  • Version Management: Tag Django images with semantic versions. Track releases systematically.
  • Environment Labels: Mark images for dev, staging, prod. Identify Django deployment targets.
  • No Data Duplication: Tags reference same image. Multiple Django labels without space overhead.
  • Registry Preparation: Tag with registry prefix for pushing. Prepare Django images for distribution.
  • Alias Creation: Create alternative names for same Django image. Flexible image referencing.

Methods

Version Tagging

Syntax: docker image tag [IMAGE]:latest [IMAGE]:[VERSION]

Parameters: IMAGE, VERSION (strings)

Assign semantic version to Django image.

Environment Tagging

Syntax: docker image tag [IMAGE]:latest [IMAGE]:[ENV]

Parameters: IMAGE, ENV (strings)

Label Django image for specific deployment environment.

Registry Tagging

Syntax: docker image tag [IMAGE] [REGISTRY]/[IMAGE]

Parameters: IMAGE, REGISTRY (strings)

Prepare Django image for private registry push.

Return Value

Creates new tag reference to existing image (no output on success).

5 Practical Django Examples

Example 1: Version Release

Tag anihpj web image with release version.

# Tag current build
docker image tag anihpj/web:latest anihpj/web:v1.0.0

# Verify tags
docker image ls anihpj/web
REPOSITORY     TAG       IMAGE ID       SIZE
anihpj/web     latest    5k4j3i2h1g0f   447MB
anihpj/web     v1.0.0    5k4j3i2h1g0f   447MB

✓ Same image, multiple tags (no extra space)
Example 2: Environment Tagging

Label Django images for different environments.

# Tag for production
docker image tag anihpj/web:v1.0.0 anihpj/web:prod

# Tag for staging
docker image tag anihpj/web:v1.0.0 anihpj/web:staging

# Tag for development
docker image tag anihpj/web:v1.0.0 anihpj/web:dev
✓ Environment tags created:
  - anihpj/web:prod
  - anihpj/web:staging
  - anihpj/web:dev
✓ All reference same Django image
Example 3: Prepare for Registry Push

Tag Django image for private registry.

# Tag for private registry
docker image tag anihpj/web:latest registry.anihpj.com/anihpj/web:latest
docker image tag anihpj/web:v1.0.0 registry.anihpj.com/anihpj/web:v1.0.0

# Push to registry
docker image push registry.anihpj.com/anihpj/web:latest
docker image push registry.anihpj.com/anihpj/web:v1.0.0
✓ Tagged for registry.anihpj.com
✓ Pushed both versions to private registry
Example 4: Multi-Version Tagging

Create multiple version tags for Django release.

# Tag with full semver
docker image tag anihpj/web:latest anihpj/web:1.2.3

# Tag with minor version
docker image tag anihpj/web:latest anihpj/web:1.2

# Tag with major version
docker image tag anihpj/web:latest anihpj/web:1

# List all tags
docker image ls anihpj/web
REPOSITORY     TAG       IMAGE ID
anihpj/web     latest    5k4j3i2h1g0f
anihpj/web     1.2.3     5k4j3i2h1g0f
anihpj/web     1.2       5k4j3i2h1g0f
anihpj/web     1         5k4j3i2h1g0f

✓ Flexible versioning for Django deployments
Example 5: CI/CD Auto-Tagging

Automated Django image tagging in build pipeline.

#!/bin/bash
IMAGE="anihpj/web"
COMMIT_SHA=$(git rev-parse --short HEAD)
BRANCH=$(git branch --show-current)

# Build image
docker build -t $IMAGE:$COMMIT_SHA .

# Tag with branch name
docker image tag $IMAGE:$COMMIT_SHA $IMAGE:$BRANCH

# Tag as latest if main branch
if [ "$BRANCH" = "main" ]; then
  docker image tag $IMAGE:$COMMIT_SHA $IMAGE:latest
fi
✓ Built: anihpj/web:a1b2c3d
✓ Tagged: anihpj/web:main
✓ Tagged: anihpj/web:latest

✓ Django image ready for deployment

Network Commands

Docker network commands manage container networking, allowing containers to communicate with each other and external systems. Networks provide isolation, service discovery, and load balancing capabilities for Django applications.

docker network

Official Docs: docker network | Docker CLI: docker network

Manage Docker networks.

Full Description

The docker network command provides a suite of subcommands for managing Docker networks, which enable communication between containers and with external systems. For your anihpj Django project, networks allow the web container to communicate with PostgreSQL databases, Redis cache, Celery workers, and Nginx reverse proxies while maintaining isolation and security for User authentication, JobPost management, Skills verification, Author profiles, and Location services.

Syntax

docker network COMMAND

# Available subcommands
docker network connect     # Connect container to network
docker network create      # Create new network
docker network disconnect  # Disconnect container from network
docker network inspect     # Display network details
docker network ls          # List networks
docker network prune       # Remove unused networks
docker network rm          # Remove one or more networks

Parameters Description

  • COMMAND: Subcommand for network operations.

Attributes

  • Container Communication: Enable Django containers to communicate securely. Connect web, database, cache, and worker services.
  • Network Isolation: Separate development, staging, and production networks. Isolate Django environments.
  • Service Discovery: Automatic DNS resolution by container name. Django services find each other easily.
  • Multiple Drivers: Support bridge, host, overlay, and custom networks. Flexible Django networking options.
  • Network Management: Create, inspect, connect, and remove networks. Complete Django network lifecycle.

Methods

Create and Connect

Syntax: docker network create [NAME] && docker network connect [NAME] [CONTAINER]

Parameters: NAME, CONTAINER (strings)

Create Django network and connect containers for communication.

List and Inspect

Syntax: docker network ls && docker network inspect [NAME]

Parameters: NAME (string)

View available networks and examine Django network configuration.

Disconnect and Remove

Syntax: docker network disconnect [NAME] [CONTAINER] && docker network rm [NAME]

Parameters: NAME, CONTAINER (strings)

Remove Django containers from network and clean up network.

Return Value

Returns usage information and available network subcommands.

5 Practical Django Examples

Example 1: Complete Network Workflow

Create network and connect Django application stack.

# Create application network
docker network create anihpj-network

# List networks
docker network ls

# Connect containers
docker network connect anihpj-network anihpj-web
docker network connect anihpj-network anihpj-postgres
5k4j3i2h1g0f9e8d7c6b5a4b3c2d1e0f
NETWORK ID     NAME              DRIVER    SCOPE
5k4j3i2h1g0f   anihpj-network   bridge    local
bridge         bridge            bridge    local
host           host              host      local

✓ Containers connected to anihpj-network
Example 2: Multi-Service Network

Connect Django web, database, cache, and worker services.

# Create network
docker network create anihpj-app-network

# Start services on network
docker run -d --name postgres --network anihpj-app-network postgres:15
docker run -d --name redis --network anihpj-app-network redis:alpine
docker run -d --name anihpj-web --network anihpj-app-network anihpj/web:latest
docker run -d --name anihpj-worker --network anihpj-app-network anihpj/worker:latest
✓ Network created: anihpj-app-network
✓ PostgreSQL available at: postgres:5432
✓ Redis available at: redis:6379
✓ Web service available at: anihpj-web:8000
✓ Worker service connected
✓ All Django services communicating
Example 3: Inspect Network Configuration

View Django network details and connected containers.

docker network inspect anihpj-network
[
    {
        "Name": "anihpj-network",
        "Driver": "bridge",
        "Containers": {
            "web123": {
                "Name": "anihpj-web",
                "IPv4Address": "172.20.0.2/16"
            },
            "db456": {
                "Name": "anihpj-postgres",
                "IPv4Address": "172.20.0.3/16"
            }
        }
    }
]
Example 4: Network Isolation

Separate Django environments with dedicated networks.

# Development network
docker network create anihpj-dev

# Staging network
docker network create anihpj-staging

# Production network
docker network create anihpj-prod

docker network ls --filter name=anihpj
NETWORK ID     NAME              DRIVER
9a8b7c6d5e4f   anihpj-dev       bridge
3f2e1d0c9b8a   anihpj-staging   bridge
1g0f9e8d7c6b   anihpj-prod      bridge

✓ Environment isolation configured
Example 5: Network Cleanup

Remove unused Django networks.

# Disconnect containers
docker network disconnect anihpj-network anihpj-web

# Remove specific network
docker network rm anihpj-old-network

# Prune unused networks
docker network prune
✓ Container disconnected
anihpj-old-network

WARNING! This will remove all networks not used by containers.
Are you sure? [y/N] y
Deleted Networks:
anihpj-test
anihpj-temp

docker network connect

Official Docs: docker network connect | Docker CLI: docker network connect

Connect a container to a network.

Full Description

The docker network connect command connects a running or stopped container to a Docker network, enabling it to communicate with other containers on that network. For your anihpj Django project, this allows dynamically adding the web container to application networks for database access, connecting Celery workers to message brokers, or joining containers to monitoring networks for User authentication, JobPost management, and Skills verification services.

Syntax

docker network connect [OPTIONS] NETWORK CONTAINER

# Basic connection
docker network connect anihpj-network anihpj-web

# Connect with IP address
docker network connect --ip 172.20.0.10 anihpj-network anihpj-web

# Connect with alias
docker network connect --alias web-service anihpj-network anihpj-web

# Connect with link
docker network connect --link postgres:db anihpj-network anihpj-web

Parameters Description

  • NETWORK: Name or ID of network to connect Django container to.
  • CONTAINER: Name or ID of Django container to connect.
  • --ip: IPv4 address for Django container on this network.
  • --ip6: IPv6 address for Django container on this network.
  • --alias: Add network-scoped alias for Django container DNS resolution.
  • --link: Add legacy link to another container (deprecated).

Attributes

  • Dynamic Connection: Connect running Django containers without restart. Add services to networks on-the-fly.
  • Multi-Network Support: Django containers can join multiple networks. Connect to both app and monitoring networks.
  • IP Assignment: Specify static IPs for Django services. Predictable container addressing.
  • DNS Aliases: Add multiple names for Django services. Flexible service discovery options.
  • Instant Communication: Containers immediately communicate after connection. No Django service interruption.

Methods

Basic Connection

Syntax: docker network connect [NETWORK] [CONTAINER]

Parameters: NETWORK, CONTAINER (strings)

Connect Django container to network with automatic IP assignment.

Static IP Connection

Syntax: docker network connect --ip [IP] [NETWORK] [CONTAINER]

Parameters: IP, NETWORK, CONTAINER (strings)

Connect Django container with specific IP address for predictable networking.

Alias Connection

Syntax: docker network connect --alias [ALIAS] [NETWORK] [CONTAINER]

Parameters: ALIAS, NETWORK, CONTAINER (strings)

Connect Django container with DNS alias for service discovery.

Return Value

Returns nothing on success. Container is immediately connected to network.

5 Practical Django Examples

Example 1: Connect Django Web to Database

Add anihpj web container to database network.

# Start database on network
docker network create db-network
docker run -d --name postgres --network db-network postgres:15

# Start web without network
docker run -d --name anihpj-web anihpj/web:latest

# Connect web to database network
docker network connect db-network anihpj-web

# Test connection
docker exec anihpj-web ping -c 2 postgres
✓ Database network created
✓ PostgreSQL started
✓ Web container started
✓ Web connected to db-network

PING postgres (172.20.0.2): 56 data bytes
64 bytes from 172.20.0.2: seq=0 ttl=64 time=0.123 ms
64 bytes from 172.20.0.2: seq=1 ttl=64 time=0.089 ms
✓ Django can reach database
Example 2: Multi-Network Django Container

Connect Django web to both app and monitoring networks.

# Create networks
docker network create anihpj-app
docker network create anihpj-monitoring

# Start web on app network
docker run -d --name anihpj-web --network anihpj-app anihpj/web:latest

# Add to monitoring network
docker network connect anihpj-monitoring anihpj-web

# Verify multiple networks
docker inspect anihpj-web --format='{{range $net, $config := .NetworkSettings.Networks}}{{$net}} {{end}}'
✓ App network created
✓ Monitoring network created
✓ Web started on app network
✓ Web added to monitoring network

anihpj-app anihpj-monitoring
✓ Django web on 2 networks
Example 3: Connect with Static IP

Assign predictable IP to Django web container.

# Create network with subnet
docker network create --subnet=172.25.0.0/16 anihpj-static

# Connect with static IP
docker network connect --ip 172.25.0.100 anihpj-static anihpj-web

# Verify IP assignment
docker inspect anihpj-web --format='{{range .NetworkSettings.Networks}}{{.IPAddress}}{{end}}'
✓ Network created with subnet
✓ Web connected with static IP

172.25.0.100
✓ Django web at predictable IP
Example 4: Connect with DNS Alias

Add multiple DNS names for Django service discovery.

# Connect with aliases
docker network connect --alias web --alias api --alias django anihpj-network anihpj-web

# Test from another container
docker run --rm --network anihpj-network alpine sh -c "nslookup web && nslookup api"
✓ Web connected with 3 aliases

Name:      web
Address 1: 172.20.0.5 anihpj-web

Name:      api
Address 1: 172.20.0.5 anihpj-web

✓ Django web accessible via web, api, django
Example 5: Hot-Add to Production Network

Add running Django container to network without downtime.

# Web is running
docker ps --filter name=anihpj-web

# Create production network
docker network create anihpj-prod

# Connect without stopping
docker network connect anihpj-prod anihpj-web

# Verify no downtime
docker exec anihpj-web curl -s http://localhost:8000/health/
CONTAINER ID   NAME          STATUS
abc123def456   anihpj-web   Up 2 hours

✓ Production network created
✓ Web connected without restart

{"status": "healthy", "uptime": 7200}
✓ Zero downtime network addition

docker network create

Official Docs: docker network create | Docker CLI: docker network create

Create a network.

Full Description

The docker network create command creates a new Docker network for container communication. For your anihpj Django project, this establishes isolated networks for different environments (dev, staging, prod), separates frontend from backend services, or creates dedicated networks for database clusters, cache layers, and worker pools supporting User authentication, JobPost management, Skills verification, Author profiles, and Location services.

Syntax

docker network create [OPTIONS] NETWORK

# Basic bridge network
docker network create anihpj-network

# Custom subnet
docker network create --subnet=172.25.0.0/16 anihpj-network

# Overlay network for swarm
docker network create --driver overlay anihpj-swarm-network

# Network with gateway
docker network create --subnet=172.25.0.0/16 --gateway=172.25.0.1 anihpj-network

Parameters Description

  • NETWORK: Name for the new Django network.
  • -d, --driver: Network driver (bridge, overlay, host, macvlan). Default is bridge for Django applications.
  • --subnet: Subnet in CIDR format. Define IP range for Django containers.
  • --gateway: IPv4 or IPv6 gateway for Django network subnet.
  • --ip-range: Allocate container IPs from sub-range for Django services.
  • --label: Set metadata on Django network for organization.

Attributes

  • Network Isolation: Create isolated networks for Django environments. Separate dev, staging, production traffic.
  • Custom Addressing: Define IP ranges and gateways. Control Django network topology.
  • Driver Support: Choose bridge, overlay, or custom drivers. Flexible Django deployment options.
  • Service Discovery: Automatic DNS resolution within network. Django containers find each other by name.
  • Network Security: Isolated communication channels. Django services secure by default.

Methods

Basic Network

Syntax: docker network create [NAME]

Parameters: NAME (string)

Create simple bridge network for Django containers with automatic configuration.

Custom Subnet Network

Syntax: docker network create --subnet=[CIDR] [NAME]

Parameters: CIDR, NAME (strings)

Create Django network with specific IP address range.

Labeled Network

Syntax: docker network create --label=[KEY=VALUE] [NAME]

Parameters: KEY, VALUE, NAME (strings)

Create Django network with metadata labels for organization.

Return Value

Returns network ID on successful creation.

5 Practical Django Examples

Example 1: Create Application Network

Basic network for anihpj Django stack.

docker network create anihpj-app
5k4j3i2h1g0f9e8d7c6b5a4b3c2d1e0f
✓ Network anihpj-app created
Example 2: Environment-Specific Networks

Create isolated networks for Django environments.

# Development network
docker network create --label env=development anihpj-dev

# Staging network with custom subnet
docker network create --subnet=172.25.0.0/16 --label env=staging anihpj-staging

# Production network
docker network create --subnet=172.26.0.0/16 --gateway=172.26.0.1 --label env=production anihpj-prod

docker network ls --filter label=env
NETWORK ID     NAME            DRIVER    SCOPE
3f2e1d0c9b8a   anihpj-dev     bridge    local
