7c6b5a4d3e2f   anihpj-staging bridge    local
9e8d7c6b5a4f   anihpj-prod    bridge    local

✓ Environment isolation configured
Example 3: Multi-Tier Network Architecture

Separate Django frontend, backend, and database networks.

# Frontend network for web/nginx
docker network create --label tier=frontend anihpj-frontend

# Backend network for Django/API
docker network create --label tier=backend anihpj-backend

# Database network for PostgreSQL/Redis
docker network create --label tier=database anihpj-database

docker network ls --filter label=tier
NETWORK ID     NAME               DRIVER
1a2b3c4d5e6f   anihpj-frontend   bridge
5k4j3i2h1g0f   anihpj-backend    bridge
9m8n7o6p5q4r   anihpj-database   bridge

✓ Three-tier Django architecture ready
Example 4: Custom IP Range Network

Create Django network with specific addressing.

# Network with custom subnet and IP range
docker network create \
  --subnet=172.28.0.0/16 \
  --gateway=172.28.0.1 \
  --ip-range=172.28.5.0/24 \
  anihpj-custom

# Inspect configuration
docker network inspect anihpj-custom --format='{{range .IPAM.Config}}{{.Subnet}} {{.Gateway}}{{end}}'
✓ Custom network created

172.28.0.0/16 172.28.0.1
Gateway: 172.28.0.1
Container IPs: 172.28.5.0-172.28.5.255
✓ Predictable Django IP allocation
Example 5: Network with DNS Configuration

Create Django network with custom DNS settings.

# Create network with metadata
docker network create \
  --label project=anihpj \
  --label team=backend \
  --label version=v1.0 \
  --opt com.docker.network.bridge.name=anihpj-br0 \
  anihpj-dns-network

# Verify labels
docker network inspect anihpj-dns-network --format='{{.Labels}}'
✓ Network created with labels

map[project:anihpj team:backend version:v1.0]
✓ Django network organized with metadata

docker network disconnect

Official Docs: docker network disconnect | Docker CLI: docker network disconnect

Disconnect a container from a network.

Full Description

The docker network disconnect command removes a container from a Docker network, breaking its connection with other containers on that network. For your anihpj Django project, this isolates containers during maintenance, removes services from networks before deletion, or disconnects staging containers from production networks for User authentication, JobPost management, Skills verification, Author profiles, and Location services.

Syntax

docker network disconnect [OPTIONS] NETWORK CONTAINER

# Basic disconnect
docker network disconnect anihpj-network anihpj-web

# Force disconnect
docker network disconnect -f anihpj-network anihpj-web

# Disconnect from multiple networks
docker network disconnect anihpj-network anihpj-web
docker network disconnect monitoring-network anihpj-web

Parameters Description

  • NETWORK: Name or ID of network to disconnect Django container from.
  • CONTAINER: Name or ID of Django container to disconnect.
  • -f, --force: Force disconnect even if container is running critical services.

Attributes

  • Dynamic Isolation: Remove Django containers from networks without stopping them. Isolate running services.
  • Maintenance Mode: Disconnect Django services for network changes. Safe maintenance operations.
  • Security Control: Remove container access to networks. Enforce Django security policies.
  • Clean Removal: Disconnect before network deletion. Proper Django cleanup workflow.
  • Multi-Network Support: Remove Django container from specific networks. Selective isolation.

Methods

Standard Disconnect

Syntax: docker network disconnect [NETWORK] [CONTAINER]

Parameters: NETWORK, CONTAINER (strings)

Remove Django container from network gracefully.

Force Disconnect

Syntax: docker network disconnect -f [NETWORK] [CONTAINER]

Parameters: NETWORK, CONTAINER (strings)

Force remove Django container from network immediately.

Batch Disconnect

Syntax: docker network disconnect [NET1] [CONTAINER] && docker network disconnect [NET2] [CONTAINER]

Parameters: NET1, NET2, CONTAINER (strings)

Remove Django container from multiple networks sequentially.

Return Value

Returns nothing on success. Container is immediately disconnected from network.

5 Practical Django Examples

Example 1: Disconnect for Maintenance

Isolate anihpj web container for maintenance.

# Check current networks
docker inspect anihpj-web --format='{{range $net, $config := .NetworkSettings.Networks}}{{$net}} {{end}}'

# Disconnect from production network
docker network disconnect anihpj-prod anihpj-web

# Verify disconnection
docker inspect anihpj-web --format='{{range $net, $config := .NetworkSettings.Networks}}{{$net}} {{end}}'
anihpj-prod bridge

✓ Disconnected from anihpj-prod

bridge

✓ Django web isolated for maintenance
Example 2: Remove from Monitoring Network

Disconnect Django service from monitoring while keeping app network.

# Web is on multiple networks
docker inspect anihpj-web --format='{{json .NetworkSettings.Networks}}' | jq 'keys'

# Remove from monitoring only
docker network disconnect monitoring-network anihpj-web

# Verify still on app network
docker exec anihpj-web ping -c 1 postgres
["anihpj-app", "monitoring-network"]

✓ Disconnected from monitoring-network

PING postgres (172.20.0.3): 56 data bytes
64 bytes from 172.20.0.3: seq=0 ttl=64 time=0.098 ms
✓ Django still connected to database
Example 3: Force Disconnect Stuck Container

Force remove Django container from network.

# Try normal disconnect
docker network disconnect anihpj-network anihpj-web || echo "Failed"

# Force disconnect
docker network disconnect -f anihpj-network anihpj-web
Error: container anihpj-web has active endpoints
Failed

✓ Force disconnect successful
✓ Django web removed from network
Example 4: Prepare Network for Deletion

Disconnect all containers before removing network.

# List containers on network
docker network inspect anihpj-old --format='{{range $id, $config := .Containers}}{{$config.Name}} {{end}}'

# Disconnect all containers
docker network disconnect anihpj-old anihpj-web
docker network disconnect anihpj-old anihpj-worker

# Remove network
docker network rm anihpj-old
anihpj-web anihpj-worker

✓ anihpj-web disconnected
✓ anihpj-worker disconnected

anihpj-old
✓ Network removed cleanly
Example 5: Environment Switch

Move Django container from staging to production network.

# Disconnect from staging
docker network disconnect anihpj-staging anihpj-web

# Connect to production
docker network connect anihpj-prod anihpj-web

# Verify new network
docker inspect anihpj-web --format='{{range $net, $config := .NetworkSettings.Networks}}{{$net}}: {{$config.IPAddress}} {{end}}'
✓ Disconnected from staging
✓ Connected to production

anihpj-prod: 172.26.0.10

✓ Django web promoted to production network

docker network inspect

Official Docs: docker network inspect | Docker CLI: docker network inspect

Display detailed information on one or more networks.

Full Description

The docker network inspect command returns detailed JSON information about Docker networks including configuration, connected containers, IP ranges, and driver options. For your anihpj Django project, this reveals network topology, diagnoses connectivity issues, verifies container IPs, and validates network configuration for User authentication, JobPost management, Skills verification, Author profiles, and Location services.

Syntax

docker network inspect [OPTIONS] NETWORK [NETWORK...]

# Inspect single network
docker network inspect anihpj-network

# Inspect with format
docker network inspect --format='{{range .Containers}}{{.Name}} {{end}}' anihpj-network

# Inspect multiple networks
docker network inspect anihpj-dev anihpj-prod

Parameters Description

  • NETWORK: Name or ID of Django network(s) to inspect.
  • -f, --format: Format output using Go template. Extract specific Django network fields.
  • -v, --verbose: Verbose output for detailed network diagnostics.

Attributes

  • Complete Details: Full JSON representation of Django network. All configuration visible.
  • Container Mapping: See all Django containers on network with IPs. Network topology view.
  • Subnet Information: View IPAM configuration and IP ranges. Understand Django addressing.
  • Template Formatting: Extract specific fields with Go templates. Query Django network programmatically.
  • Multi-Network Support: Inspect multiple Django networks at once. Compare configurations.

Methods

Full JSON Inspection

Syntax: docker network inspect [NETWORK]

Parameters: NETWORK (string)

Display complete Django network configuration as JSON.

Extract Container IPs

Syntax: docker network inspect --format='{{range .Containers}}{{.IPv4Address}}{{end}}' [NETWORK]

Parameters: NETWORK (string)

List all Django container IP addresses on network.

View Subnet Configuration

Syntax: docker network inspect --format='{{range .IPAM.Config}}{{.Subnet}}{{end}}' [NETWORK]

Parameters: NETWORK (string)

Extract Django network subnet information.

Return Value

Returns JSON array with detailed network information.

5 Practical Django Examples

Example 1: View Network Topology

Inspect anihpj application network configuration.

docker network inspect anihpj-app | head -n 40
[
    {
        "Name": "anihpj-app",
        "Id": "5k4j3i2h1g0f...",
        "Driver": "bridge",
        "Scope": "local",
        "IPAM": {
            "Config": [
                {
                    "Subnet": "172.20.0.0/16",
                    "Gateway": "172.20.0.1"
                }
            ]
        },
        "Containers": {
            "abc123": {
                "Name": "anihpj-web",
                "IPv4Address": "172.20.0.2/16"
            },
            "def456": {
                "Name": "postgres",
                "IPv4Address": "172.20.0.3/16"
            },
            "ghi789": {
                "Name": "redis",
                "IPv4Address": "172.20.0.4/16"
            }
        }
    }
]
Example 2: List Connected Containers

View all Django services on network.

docker network inspect anihpj-app --format='{{range $id, $config := .Containers}}{{$config.Name}}: {{$config.IPv4Address}}
{{end}}'
anihpj-web: 172.20.0.2/16
postgres: 172.20.0.3/16
redis: 172.20.0.4/16
anihpj-worker: 172.20.0.5/16

✓ 4 Django services on network
Example 3: Diagnose Connectivity Issues

Verify Django container network configuration.

# Check if container is on network
docker network inspect anihpj-app --format='{{range .Containers}}{{.Name}} {{end}}' | grep -q anihpj-web && echo "Connected" || echo "Not found"

# Get container IP
docker network inspect anihpj-app --format='{{range $id, $config := .Containers}}{{if eq $config.Name "anihpj-web"}}{{$config.IPv4Address}}{{end}}{{end}}'
Connected

172.20.0.2/16

✓ Django web properly configured on network
Example 4: Compare Environment Networks

View subnet differences between Django environments.

echo "=== Development ==="
docker network inspect anihpj-dev --format='Subnet: {{range .IPAM.Config}}{{.Subnet}}{{end}}'

echo "=== Staging ==="
docker network inspect anihpj-staging --format='Subnet: {{range .IPAM.Config}}{{.Subnet}}{{end}}'

echo "=== Production ==="
docker network inspect anihpj-prod --format='Subnet: {{range .IPAM.Config}}{{.Subnet}}{{end}}'
=== Development ===
Subnet: 172.20.0.0/16

=== Staging ===
Subnet: 172.25.0.0/16

=== Production ===
Subnet: 172.26.0.0/16

✓ Environment network isolation verified
Example 5: Extract Network Metadata

View Django network labels and configuration options.

# View labels
docker network inspect anihpj-prod --format='Labels: {{json .Labels}}'

# View driver options
docker network inspect anihpj-prod --format='Driver: {{.Driver}} Options: {{json .Options}}'

# View gateway
docker network inspect anihpj-prod --format='Gateway: {{range .IPAM.Config}}{{.Gateway}}{{end}}'
Labels: {"env":"production","project":"anihpj","version":"v1.0"}

Driver: bridge Options: {"com.docker.network.bridge.name":"anihpj-br0"}

Gateway: 172.26.0.1

✓ Django production network fully documented

docker network ls

Official Docs: docker network ls | Docker CLI: docker network ls

List networks.

Full Description

The docker network ls command lists all Docker networks on the system, showing network names, IDs, drivers, and scopes. For your anihpj Django project, this helps identify available networks, verify network creation, filter networks by labels or drivers, and manage network resources for User authentication, JobPost management, Skills verification, Author profiles, and Location services across different environments.

Syntax

docker network ls [OPTIONS]

# List all networks
docker network ls

# Filter by name
docker network ls --filter name=anihpj

# Filter by label
docker network ls --filter label=env=production

# Custom format
docker network ls --format "table {{.Name}}\t{{.Driver}}\t{{.Scope}}"

Parameters Description

  • -f, --filter: Filter Django networks by conditions (name, driver, label, scope).
  • --format: Format output using Go template for custom Django network display.
  • -q, --quiet: Only display network IDs for scripting.
  • --no-trunc: Don't truncate output (show full network IDs).

Attributes

  • Network Inventory: Complete list of Docker networks. Track all Django networking resources.
  • Filter Capabilities: Find specific Django networks by criteria. Locate networks by environment or project.
  • Driver Information: See network types (bridge, overlay, host). Understand Django network architecture.
  • Custom Formatting: Tailored output for scripts. Extract Django network data programmatically.
  • Quick Reference: See network IDs, names, and drivers. Verify Django network setup.

Methods

Standard List

Syntax: docker network ls

Parameters: None

Display all Docker networks with standard columns.

Filter by Pattern

Syntax: docker network ls --filter name=[PATTERN]

Parameters: PATTERN (string)

Show Django networks matching name pattern.

Custom Format Output

Syntax: docker network ls --format "{{.Name}}: {{.Driver}}"

Parameters: None

Display Django networks in custom format for scripting.

Return Value

Returns table with network ID, name, driver, and scope.

5 Practical Django Examples

Example 1: List All Networks

View all Docker networks including Django networks.

docker network ls
NETWORK ID     NAME              DRIVER    SCOPE
5k4j3i2h1g0f   anihpj-app       bridge    local
3f2e1d0c9b8a   anihpj-dev       bridge    local
7c6b5a4d3e2f   anihpj-prod      bridge    local
bridge         bridge            bridge    local
host           host              host      local
none           none              null      local

✓ 6 networks (3 Django, 3 default)
Example 2: Filter Django Networks

List only anihpj project networks.

docker network ls --filter name=anihpj
NETWORK ID     NAME            DRIVER    SCOPE
5k4j3i2h1g0f   anihpj-app     bridge    local
3f2e1d0c9b8a   anihpj-dev     bridge    local
7c6b5a4d3e2f   anihpj-prod    bridge    local
9e8d7c6b5a4f   anihpj-staging bridge    local

✓ 4 Django networks found
Example 3: Filter by Environment Label

List Django production networks only.

docker network ls --filter label=env=production
NETWORK ID     NAME          DRIVER    SCOPE
7c6b5a4d3e2f   anihpj-prod  bridge    local

✓ Production Django networks identified
Example 4: Custom Format Output

Display Django networks with custom columns.

docker network ls --filter name=anihpj --format "table {{.Name}}\t{{.Driver}}\t{{.Scope}}\t{{.ID}}"
NAME             DRIVER    SCOPE    ID
anihpj-app      bridge    local    5k4j3i2h1g0f
anihpj-dev      bridge    local    3f2e1d0c9b8a
anihpj-prod     bridge    local    7c6b5a4d3e2f
anihpj-staging  bridge    local    9e8d7c6b5a4f
Example 5: Network IDs for Scripting

Get Django network IDs for automation.

# Get network IDs
docker network ls --filter name=anihpj -q

# Use in script
for net_id in $(docker network ls --filter name=anihpj -q); do
  echo "Network: $(docker network inspect $net_id --format='{{.Name}}')"
  echo "Containers: $(docker network inspect $net_id --format='{{len .Containers}}')"
done
5k4j3i2h1g0f
3f2e1d0c9b8a
7c6b5a4d3e2f
9e8d7c6b5a4f

Network: anihpj-app
Containers: 4
Network: anihpj-dev
Containers: 2
Network: anihpj-prod
Containers: 3
Network: anihpj-staging
Containers: 0

docker network prune

Official Docs: docker network prune | Docker CLI: docker network prune

Remove all unused networks.

Full Description

The docker network prune command removes all Docker networks that are not currently in use by any containers. For your anihpj Django project, this cleans up orphaned networks from testing, removes old development networks, and frees resources after tearing down temporary Django environments for User authentication, JobPost management, Skills verification, Author profiles, and Location services development.

Syntax

docker network prune [OPTIONS]

# Remove unused networks
docker network prune

# Force prune without confirmation
docker network prune -f

# Filter by label
docker network prune --filter "label=env=development"

# Filter by age
docker network prune --filter "until=24h"

Parameters Description

  • -f, --force: Do not prompt for confirmation before pruning Django networks.
  • --filter: Filter networks to prune (label, until). Target specific Django network cleanup.

Attributes

  • Resource Cleanup: Remove unused Django networks automatically. Maintain clean environment.
  • Safe Operation: Only removes networks with no containers. Active Django services unaffected.
  • Selective Pruning: Filter by labels or age. Target specific Django network cleanup.
  • Confirmation Prompt: Shows networks to remove before pruning. Review Django cleanup impact.
  • Batch Removal: Remove multiple unused Django networks at once. Efficient cleanup.

Methods

Standard Prune

Syntax: docker network prune

Parameters: None

Remove all unused Docker networks with confirmation.

Force Prune

Syntax: docker network prune -f

Parameters: None

Remove unused Django networks without prompt for automation.

Filter by Label

Syntax: docker network prune --filter "label=[KEY=VALUE]"

Parameters: KEY, VALUE (strings)

Remove Django networks matching specific label criteria.

Return Value

Returns list of deleted network names.

5 Practical Django Examples

Example 1: Clean Unused Networks

Remove orphaned Django test networks.

docker network prune
WARNING! This will remove all networks not used by at least one container.
Are you sure you want to continue? [y/N] y
Deleted Networks:
anihpj-test
anihpj-temp
anihpj-old-dev

✓ 3 unused Django networks removed
Example 2: Force Cleanup in CI/CD

Automated Django network cleanup without prompt.

#!/bin/bash
echo "Cleaning up test networks..."
docker network prune -f
Cleaning up test networks...
Deleted Networks:
anihpj-ci-test-123
anihpj-ci-test-124
anihpj-ci-test-125

✓ CI networks cleaned automatically
Example 3: Prune Development Networks

Remove only Django development networks by label.

docker network prune --filter "label=env=development" -f
Deleted Networks:
anihpj-dev-old
anihpj-dev-test
anihpj-dev-backup

✓ Development Django networks pruned
✓ Production networks preserved
Example 4: Remove Old Networks

Clean Django networks older than 7 days.

# Prune networks created more than 168 hours ago
docker network prune --filter "until=168h"
WARNING! This will remove all networks not used by at least one container.
Are you sure? [y/N] y

Deleted Networks:
anihpj-old-staging
anihpj-archive-network

✓ Old Django networks removed (7+ days)
Example 5: Pre-Deployment Cleanup

Clean environment before Django deployment.

#!/bin/bash
echo "=== Pre-Deployment Cleanup ==="

# Check current networks
echo "Before cleanup:"
docker network ls --filter name=anihpj | wc -l

# Prune unused
docker network prune -f

# Check after cleanup
echo "After cleanup:"
docker network ls --filter name=anihpj | wc -l

echo "✓ Environment ready for deployment"
=== Pre-Deployment Cleanup ===
Before cleanup:
8

Deleted Networks:
anihpj-test1
anihpj-test2
anihpj-old-dev

After cleanup:
5

✓ Environment ready for deployment

docker network rm

Official Docs: docker network rm | Docker CLI: docker network rm

Remove one or more networks.

Full Description

The docker network rm command removes one or more Docker networks by name or ID. For your anihpj Django project, this deletes specific networks when tearing down environments, removes obsolete development networks, or cleans up after testing Django application configurations for User authentication, JobPost management, Skills verification, Author profiles, and Location services.

Syntax

docker network rm NETWORK [NETWORK...]

# Remove single network
docker network rm anihpj-test

# Remove multiple networks
docker network rm anihpj-dev anihpj-staging anihpj-test

# Remove by ID
docker network rm 5k4j3i2h1g0f

# Remove with error handling
docker network rm anihpj-network || echo "Network not found or in use"

Parameters Description

  • NETWORK: Name or ID of Django network(s) to remove.

Attributes

  • Selective Removal: Remove specific Django networks by name. Targeted cleanup.
  • Multi-Network Support: Delete multiple Django networks in one command. Batch operations.
  • Safety Checks: Prevents removal if containers connected. Protects active Django services.
  • ID or Name: Remove Django networks using name or unique identifier. Flexible syntax.
  • Immediate Deletion: Networks removed instantly when no containers attached. Quick Django cleanup.

Methods

Remove by Name

Syntax: docker network rm [NETWORK]

Parameters: NETWORK (string)

Delete specific Django network by name.

Remove by ID

Syntax: docker network rm [NETWORK_ID]

Parameters: NETWORK_ID (string)

Remove Django network using unique identifier.

Batch Remove

Syntax: docker network rm [NET1] [NET2] [NET3]

Parameters: NET1, NET2, NET3 (strings)

Delete multiple Django networks simultaneously.

Return Value

Returns network name or ID for each deleted network.

5 Practical Django Examples

Example 1: Remove Test Network

Delete Django test network after testing.

docker network rm anihpj-test
anihpj-test
✓ Test network removed
Example 2: Teardown Environment Networks

Remove multiple Django environment networks.

# Remove dev and staging networks
docker network rm anihpj-dev anihpj-staging anihpj-test
anihpj-dev
anihpj-staging
anihpj-test

✓ 3 Django networks removed
Example 3: Remove Network with Error Handling

Safe Django network removal with validation.

# Try to remove network
if docker network rm anihpj-old 2>/dev/null; then
  echo "✓ Network removed successfully"
else
  echo "⚠ Network not found or has active containers"
  docker network inspect anihpj-old --format='{{len .Containers}} containers connected'
fi
Error: network anihpj-old has active endpoints
⚠ Network not found or has active containers
3 containers connected

✓ Error handled gracefully
Example 4: Remove Old Networks by Pattern

Delete all Django test networks.

# Get test network IDs
test_networks=$(docker network ls --filter name=anihpj-test -q)

# Remove them
if [ -n "$test_networks" ]; then
  docker network rm $test_networks
  echo "✓ Test networks cleaned up"
else
  echo "No test networks found"
fi
anihpj-test-1
anihpj-test-2
anihpj-test-old

✓ Test networks cleaned up
Example 5: Complete Environment Teardown

Remove Django staging environment completely.

#!/bin/bash
echo "Tearing down staging environment..."

# Stop containers
docker container stop $(docker container ls -q --filter network=anihpj-staging)

# Remove containers
docker container rm $(docker container ls -aq --filter network=anihpj-staging)

# Remove network
docker network rm anihpj-staging

echo "✓ Staging environment removed"
Tearing down staging environment...
✓ Containers stopped
✓ Containers removed

anihpj-staging

✓ Staging environment removed
✓ Django staging completely torn down

Volume Commands

Docker volume commands manage persistent data storage for containers. Volumes preserve data across container restarts and enable data sharing between containers, making them essential for Django databases, media files, and application state.

docker volume

Official Docs: docker volume | Docker CLI: docker volume

Manage Docker volumes.

Full Description

The docker volume command provides a suite of subcommands for managing Docker volumes, which are the preferred mechanism for persisting data generated and used by Docker containers. For your anihpj Django project, volumes store PostgreSQL databases, uploaded media files, static assets, Redis persistence, and application logs, ensuring data survives container restarts for User authentication, JobPost management, Skills verification, Author profiles, and Location services.

Syntax

docker volume COMMAND

# Available subcommands
docker volume create   # Create new volume
docker volume inspect  # Display volume details
docker volume ls       # List volumes
docker volume prune    # Remove unused volumes
docker volume rm       # Remove one or more volumes
docker volume update   # Update volume configuration

Parameters Description

  • COMMAND: Subcommand for volume operations.

Attributes

  • Data Persistence: Store Django data beyond container lifecycle. Database and media files survive restarts.
  • Data Sharing: Multiple Django containers access same volume. Share uploads between web and worker.
  • Performance Optimization: Better I/O performance than bind mounts. Optimized for Django database operations.
  • Backup Support: Easy backup and restore workflows. Protect Django production data.
  • Driver Flexibility: Support local and network drivers. Deploy Django across infrastructure.

Methods

Create and Inspect

Syntax: docker volume create [NAME] && docker volume inspect [NAME]

Parameters: NAME (string)

Create Django volume and view detailed configuration.

List and Prune

Syntax: docker volume ls && docker volume prune

Parameters: None

View all volumes and remove unused Django volumes.

Update and Remove

Syntax: docker volume update [NAME] && docker volume rm [NAME]

Parameters: NAME (string)

Modify Django volume configuration and delete volumes.

Return Value

Returns usage information and available volume subcommands.

5 Practical Django Examples

Example 1: Complete Volume Workflow

Create, use, and manage Django application volumes.

# Create volumes
docker volume create anihpj-postgres-data
docker volume create anihpj-media

# List volumes
docker volume ls

# Use in containers
docker run -d --name postgres -v anihpj-postgres-data:/var/lib/postgresql/data postgres:15
docker run -d --name anihpj-web -v anihpj-media:/app/media anihpj/web:latest
anihpj-postgres-data
anihpj-media

VOLUME NAME              DRIVER
anihpj-postgres-data    local
anihpj-media            local

✓ Django volumes created and mounted
Example 2: Multi-Service Volume Architecture

Set up complete Django stack with persistent storage.

# Create all volumes
docker volume create anihpj-db-data
docker volume create anihpj-redis-data
docker volume create anihpj-media-files
docker volume create anihpj-static-files
docker volume create anihpj-logs

docker volume ls --filter name=anihpj
VOLUME NAME              DRIVER    SIZE
anihpj-db-data          local     1.2GB
anihpj-redis-data       local     128MB
anihpj-media-files      local     850MB
anihpj-static-files     local     45MB
anihpj-logs             local     230MB

✓ Complete Django storage infrastructure
Example 3: Inspect Volume Configuration

View Django volume details and mount points.

docker volume inspect anihpj-postgres-data
[
    {
        "Name": "anihpj-postgres-data",
        "Driver": "local",
        "Mountpoint": "/var/lib/docker/volumes/anihpj-postgres-data/_data",
        "Labels": {
            "project": "anihpj",
            "service": "database"
        }
    }
]
Example 4: Volume Backup and Restore

Backup Django database volume.

# Backup volume
docker run --rm -v anihpj-postgres-data:/data -v $(pwd):/backup alpine tar czf /backup/db-backup.tar.gz -C /data .

# Restore volume
docker run --rm -v anihpj-postgres-data:/data -v $(pwd):/backup alpine tar xzf /backup/db-backup.tar.gz -C /data
✓ Backup created: db-backup.tar.gz (1.2GB)
✓ Django database backed up

✓ Database restored from backup
✓ PostgreSQL data available
Example 5: Volume Cleanup

Remove unused Django volumes to free space.

# List dangling volumes
docker volume ls -f dangling=true

# Prune unused volumes
docker volume prune

# Remove specific volume
docker volume rm anihpj-old-media
VOLUME NAME           DRIVER
anihpj-old-media     local
anihpj-test-vol      local

WARNING! This will remove all volumes not used by containers.
Deleted Volumes:
anihpj-test-vol

anihpj-old-media
✓ 2 Django volumes removed

docker volume create

Official Docs: docker volume create | Docker CLI: docker volume create

Create a volume.

Full Description

The docker volume create command creates a new Docker volume for persistent data storage. For your anihpj Django project, this establishes storage for PostgreSQL databases, uploaded media files from JobPost listings, static assets, Redis cache persistence, and application logs, ensuring data persists across container restarts for User authentication, JobPost management, Skills verification, Author profiles, and Location services.

Syntax

docker volume create [OPTIONS] [VOLUME]

# Basic volume
docker volume create anihpj-data

# Volume with labels
docker volume create --label env=production anihpj-db

# Volume with driver options
docker volume create --driver local --opt type=nfs anihpj-shared

# Named volume
docker volume create --name anihpj-media-files

Parameters Description

  • VOLUME: Name for the new Django volume. Auto-generated if omitted.
  • -d, --driver: Volume driver (local, nfs, etc). Default is local for Django development.
  • --label: Set metadata on Django volume for organization and filtering.
  • --name: Specify volume name explicitly for Django services.
  • --opt: Driver-specific options for Django storage configuration.

Attributes

  • Persistent Storage: Create dedicated storage for Django data. Survives container deletion.
  • Named Volumes: Human-readable names for Django volumes. Easy identification and management.
  • Label Support: Tag volumes with metadata. Organize Django volumes by environment or service.
  • Driver Options: Configure storage behavior. Optimize Django database and media performance.
  • Automatic Creation: Volumes created on first use. Simplified Django deployment workflows.

Methods

Basic Volume Creation

Syntax: docker volume create [NAME]

Parameters: NAME (string)

Create simple named volume for Django data storage.

Labeled Volume Creation

Syntax: docker volume create --label [KEY=VALUE] [NAME]

Parameters: KEY, VALUE, NAME (strings)

Create Django volume with organizational metadata labels.

Driver-Specific Creation

Syntax: docker volume create --driver [DRIVER] --opt [OPTIONS] [NAME]

Parameters: DRIVER, OPTIONS, NAME (strings)

Create Django volume with custom storage driver and configuration.

Return Value

Returns volume name on successful creation.

5 Practical Django Examples

Example 1: Create Database Volume

Set up persistent storage for PostgreSQL database.

docker volume create anihpj-postgres-data
anihpj-postgres-data
✓ Database volume created
Example 2: Create Labeled Volumes for Environments

Organize Django volumes by environment and service.

# Development database
docker volume create --label env=development --label service=database anihpj-dev-db

# Production media storage
docker volume create --label env=production --label service=media anihpj-prod-media

# Staging logs
docker volume create --label env=staging --label service=logs anihpj-staging-logs

docker volume ls --filter label=env=production
anihpj-dev-db
anihpj-prod-media
anihpj-staging-logs

VOLUME NAME           DRIVER
anihpj-prod-media    local

✓ Environment-specific Django volumes created
Example 3: Create Complete Django Stack Volumes

Set up all required volumes for anihpj project.

# Database volume
docker volume create --label project=anihpj anihpj-postgres-data

# Redis cache volume
docker volume create --label project=anihpj anihpj-redis-data

# Media files volume
docker volume create --label project=anihpj anihpj-media

# Static files volume
docker volume create --label project=anihpj anihpj-static

# Application logs
docker volume create --label project=anihpj anihpj-logs

docker volume ls --filter label=project=anihpj
VOLUME NAME              DRIVER
anihpj-postgres-data    local
anihpj-redis-data       local
anihpj-media            local
anihpj-static           local
anihpj-logs             local

✓ Complete Django storage infrastructure ready
Example 4: Create NFS Volume for Shared Storage

Set up shared Django media storage across multiple servers.

docker volume create \
  --driver local \
  --opt type=nfs \
  --opt o=addr=192.168.1.100,rw \
  --opt device=:/mnt/nfs/anihpj-media \
  anihpj-shared-media
anihpj-shared-media
✓ NFS volume created for distributed Django deployment
Example 5: Create Volume with Size Limit

Limit Django media volume size for development.

# Create volume with size constraint
docker volume create \
  --driver local \
  --opt type=tmpfs \
  --opt device=tmpfs \
  --opt o=size=2g \
  anihpj-dev-media

docker volume inspect anihpj-dev-media --format='{{.Options}}'
anihpj-dev-media

map[device:tmpfs o:size=2g type:tmpfs]
✓ 2GB limited volume for Django development

docker volume inspect

Official Docs: docker volume inspect | Docker CLI: docker volume inspect

Display detailed information on one or more volumes.

Full Description

The docker volume inspect command returns detailed JSON information about Docker volumes including driver, mount point, labels, and configuration options. For your anihpj Django project, this reveals volume locations, verifies storage configuration, checks labels, and diagnoses volume issues for PostgreSQL data, media uploads, static files, and logs supporting User authentication, JobPost management, Skills verification, Author profiles, and Location services.

Syntax

docker volume inspect [OPTIONS] VOLUME [VOLUME...]

# Inspect single volume
docker volume inspect anihpj-postgres-data

# Inspect with format
docker volume inspect --format='{{.Mountpoint}}' anihpj-media

# Inspect multiple volumes
docker volume inspect anihpj-db anihpj-media anihpj-logs

Parameters Description

  • VOLUME: Name of Django volume(s) to inspect.
  • -f, --format: Format output using Go template. Extract specific Django volume fields.

Attributes

  • Complete Details: Full JSON representation of Django volume. All configuration visible.
  • Mount Point Access: View physical location of Django data on host. Direct file system access.
  • Label Information: See all metadata labels on Django volumes. Organizational details.
  • Template Formatting: Extract specific fields with Go templates. Query Django volumes programmatically.
  • Multi-Volume Support: Inspect multiple Django volumes at once. Compare configurations.

Methods

Full JSON Inspection

Syntax: docker volume inspect [VOLUME]

Parameters: VOLUME (string)

Display complete Django volume configuration as JSON.

Extract Mount Point

Syntax: docker volume inspect --format='{{.Mountpoint}}' [VOLUME]

Parameters: VOLUME (string)

Get physical path to Django volume data on host.

View Labels

Syntax: docker volume inspect --format='{{json .Labels}}' [VOLUME]

Parameters: VOLUME (string)

Extract Django volume metadata labels.

Return Value

Returns JSON array with detailed volume information.

5 Practical Django Examples

Example 1: Inspect Database Volume

View PostgreSQL volume configuration for anihpj.

docker volume inspect anihpj-postgres-data
[
    {
        "CreatedAt": "2026-01-15T10:30:00Z",
        "Driver": "local",
        "Labels": {
            "project": "anihpj",
            "service": "database",
            "env": "production"
        },
        "Mountpoint": "/var/lib/docker/volumes/anihpj-postgres-data/_data",
        "Name": "anihpj-postgres-data",
        "Options": {},
        "Scope": "local"
    }
]
Example 2: Get Volume Mount Point

Find physical location of Django media files.

docker volume inspect --format='{{.Mountpoint}}' anihpj-media
/var/lib/docker/volumes/anihpj-media/_data

✓ Django media files stored here
Example 3: Check Volume Labels

View Django volume metadata and organization tags.

docker volume inspect --format='{{json .Labels}}' anihpj-postgres-data | jq
{
  "project": "anihpj",
  "service": "database",
  "env": "production",
  "backup": "daily"
}

✓ Volume properly labeled for Django production
Example 4: Compare Multiple Volumes

Inspect all Django storage volumes at once.

docker volume inspect anihpj-postgres-data anihpj-media anihpj-logs --format='Name: {{.Name}} | Driver: {{.Driver}} | Size: {{.Options}}'
Name: anihpj-postgres-data | Driver: local | Size: map[]
Name: anihpj-media | Driver: local | Size: map[]
Name: anihpj-logs | Driver: local | Size: map[]

✓ All Django volumes using local driver
Example 5: Volume Size and Usage Check

Get Django volume details and check disk usage.

# Get mount point
MOUNT=$(docker volume inspect --format='{{.Mountpoint}}' anihpj-postgres-data)

# Check size
du -sh $MOUNT

# Check usage
df -h $MOUNT
1.2G    /var/lib/docker/volumes/anihpj-postgres-data/_data

Filesystem      Size  Used Avail Use% Mounted on
/dev/sda1       50G   15G   35G  30% /

✓ PostgreSQL using 1.2GB of Django storage

docker volume ls

Official Docs: docker volume ls | Docker CLI: docker volume ls

List volumes.

Full Description

The docker volume ls command lists all Docker volumes on the system, showing volume names, drivers, and mount points. For your anihpj Django project, this helps identify available storage, verify volume creation, filter volumes by labels or dangling status, and manage storage resources for PostgreSQL data, media files, static assets, Redis cache, and logs across User authentication, JobPost management, Skills verification, Author profiles, and Location services.

Syntax

docker volume ls [OPTIONS]

# List all volumes
docker volume ls

# Filter by name
docker volume ls --filter name=anihpj

# Filter by label
docker volume ls --filter label=env=production

# Filter dangling volumes
docker volume ls --filter dangling=true

# Custom format
docker volume ls --format "table {{.Name}}\t{{.Driver}}\t{{.Mountpoint}}"

Parameters Description

  • -f, --filter: Filter Django volumes by conditions (name, driver, label, dangling).
  • --format: Format output using Go template for custom Django volume display.
  • -q, --quiet: Only display volume names for scripting.

Attributes

  • Volume Inventory: Complete list of Docker volumes. Track all Django storage resources.
  • Filter Capabilities: Find specific Django volumes by criteria. Locate volumes by project or environment.
  • Dangling Detection: Identify unused volumes for cleanup. Find orphaned Django storage.
  • Custom Formatting: Tailored output for scripts. Extract Django volume data programmatically.
  • Quick Reference: See volume names and drivers. Verify Django storage setup.

Methods

Standard List

Syntax: docker volume ls

Parameters: None

Display all Docker volumes with standard columns.

Filter by Pattern

Syntax: docker volume ls --filter name=[PATTERN]

Parameters: PATTERN (string)

Show Django volumes matching name pattern.

Custom Format Output

Syntax: docker volume ls --format "{{.Name}}: {{.Driver}}"

Parameters: None

Display Django volumes in custom format for scripting.

Return Value

Returns table with volume driver and name.

5 Practical Django Examples

Example 1: List All Volumes

View all Docker volumes including Django volumes.

docker volume ls
DRIVER    VOLUME NAME
local     anihpj-postgres-data
local     anihpj-media
local     anihpj-static
local     anihpj-logs
local     anihpj-redis-data

✓ 5 Django volumes found
Example 2: Filter Django Project Volumes

List only anihpj-related volumes.

docker volume ls --filter name=anihpj
DRIVER    VOLUME NAME
local     anihpj-postgres-data
local     anihpj-media
local     anihpj-static
local     anihpj-logs
local     anihpj-redis-data
local     anihpj-dev-db
local     anihpj-staging-media

✓ 7 Django volumes identified
Example 3: Find Dangling Volumes

Identify unused Django volumes for cleanup.

docker volume ls --filter dangling=true
DRIVER    VOLUME NAME
local     anihpj-old-test
local     anihpj-temp-backup
local     3f2e1d0c9b8a

✓ 3 dangling volumes (not attached to containers)
✓ Safe to remove for Django cleanup
Example 4: Filter by Environment Label

List Django production volumes only.

docker volume ls --filter label=env=production
DRIVER    VOLUME NAME
local     anihpj-prod-db
local     anihpj-prod-media
local     anihpj-prod-logs

✓ Production Django volumes identified
Example 5: Custom Format for Scripts

Get Django volume names for automation.

# Get volume names only
docker volume ls --filter name=anihpj -q

# Custom format with details
docker volume ls --filter name=anihpj --format "table {{.Name}}\t{{.Driver}}"

# Use in backup script
for vol in $(docker volume ls --filter name=anihpj -q); do
  echo "Backing up: $vol"
done
anihpj-postgres-data
anihpj-media
anihpj-static

NAME                     DRIVER
anihpj-postgres-data    local
anihpj-media            local
anihpj-static           local

Backing up: anihpj-postgres-data
Backing up: anihpj-media
Backing up: anihpj-static

docker volume prune

Official Docs: docker volume prune | Docker CLI: docker volume prune

Remove all unused local volumes.

Full Description

The docker volume prune command removes all dangling volumes—volumes not referenced by any container—to reclaim disk space. For your anihpj Django project, this cleans up orphaned test volumes, removes temporary storage, frees disk space, and maintains volume hygiene by eliminating unused PostgreSQL test databases, old media file storage, abandoned log volumes, and development volumes from User testing, JobPost drafts, Skills experiments, Author profile testing, and Location service trials.

Syntax

docker volume prune [OPTIONS]

# Remove all dangling volumes
docker volume prune

# Force without prompt
docker volume prune -f

# Filter by label
docker volume prune --filter label=env=test

# Prune all volumes (including named ones)
docker volume prune -a

Parameters Description

  • -f, --force: Skip confirmation prompt for Django volume cleanup.
  • -a, --all: Remove all unused volumes, not just dangling. Be cautious with Django data.
  • --filter: Remove only Django volumes matching labels (e.g., env=test).

Attributes

  • Space Reclamation: Free disk space by removing unused Django volumes. Clean development environment.
  • Safety Confirmation: Prompts before deletion unless forced. Protect Django production data.
  • Label Filtering: Target specific Django volumes by metadata. Clean test but preserve production.
  • Dangling Detection: Removes only unattached volumes. Django volumes in use remain safe.
  • Batch Cleanup: Remove multiple unused volumes at once. Efficient Django maintenance.

Methods

Interactive Prune

Syntax: docker volume prune

Parameters: None

Remove dangling Django volumes with confirmation prompt.

Force Prune

Syntax: docker volume prune -f

Parameters: None

Remove dangling Django volumes without confirmation.

Filtered Prune

Syntax: docker volume prune --filter label=[KEY]=[VALUE]

Parameters: KEY, VALUE (strings)

Remove specific Django volumes by label criteria.

Return Value

Returns list of deleted volumes and space reclaimed.

5 Practical Django Examples

Example 1: Clean Dangling Volumes

Remove unused Django test volumes interactively.

docker volume prune
WARNING! This will remove all local volumes not used by at least one container.
Are you sure you want to continue? [y/N] y

Deleted Volumes:
anihpj-old-test
anihpj-temp-backup
3f2e1d0c9b8a

Total reclaimed space: 2.4GB

✓ Django test volumes cleaned
Example 2: Force Cleanup Without Prompt

Remove dangling volumes for Django CI/CD pipeline.

docker volume prune -f
Deleted Volumes:
anihpj-ci-test-db
anihpj-ci-media
anihpj-ci-temp

Total reclaimed space: 1.8GB

✓ CI volumes automatically cleaned
Example 3: Prune Test Environment Only

Clean Django test volumes while preserving production.

docker volume prune --filter label=env=test -f
Deleted Volumes:
anihpj-test-db
anihpj-test-media
anihpj-test-logs

Total reclaimed space: 3.2GB

✓ Test Django volumes removed
✓ Production volumes preserved
Example 4: Development Cleanup Script

Automated Django cleanup for development workflow.

#!/bin/bash
# Django cleanup script

echo "🧹 Cleaning Django development environment..."

# Stop all containers
docker-compose -f docker-compose.dev.yml down

# Clean dangling volumes
docker volume prune -f

# Clean test volumes
docker volume prune --filter label=env=test -f

echo "✓ Django cleanup complete"
🧹 Cleaning Django development environment...

Stopping anihpj_web_dev_1 ... done
Stopping anihpj_db_dev_1  ... done
Removing anihpj_web_dev_1 ... done
Removing anihpj_db_dev_1  ... done

Deleted Volumes:
anihpj-dev-temp
anihpj-dev-cache

Total reclaimed space: 850MB

Deleted Volumes:
anihpj-test-db-2
anihpj-test-media-3

Total reclaimed space: 1.1GB

✓ Django cleanup complete
Example 5: Check Before Pruning

Preview Django volumes to be removed.

# Check dangling volumes
docker volume ls --filter dangling=true

# Review before cleanup
docker volume ls --filter dangling=true -q

# Prune after review
docker volume prune -f
DRIVER    VOLUME NAME
local     anihpj-temp-logs
local     anihpj-dev-backup
local     anihpj-old-media

anihpj-temp-logs
anihpj-dev-backup
anihpj-old-media

Deleted Volumes:
anihpj-temp-logs
anihpj-dev-backup
anihpj-old-media

Total reclaimed space: 1.5GB

docker volume rm

Official Docs: docker volume rm | Docker CLI: docker volume rm

Remove one or more volumes.

Full Description

The docker volume rm command removes one or more named volumes from Docker storage, permanently deleting their data. For your anihpj Django project, this deletes specific volumes by name, removes old development databases, clears outdated media storage, eliminates test environments, and frees targeted storage for PostgreSQL databases, JobPost media files, static assets, Redis cache, and logs from User, JobPost, Skills, Author, and Location services. Use with caution as data is irretrievable.

Syntax

docker volume rm [OPTIONS] VOLUME [VOLUME...]

# Remove single volume
docker volume rm anihpj-test-db

# Remove multiple volumes
docker volume rm anihpj-dev-db anihpj-dev-media anihpj-dev-logs

# Force remove (if possible)
docker volume rm -f anihpj-old-volume

Parameters Description

  • VOLUME: Name(s) of Django volume(s) to remove. Multiple volumes can be specified.
  • -f, --force: Force removal even if volume is in use (requires Docker 1.14+).

Attributes

  • Targeted Deletion: Remove specific Django volumes by name. Precise cleanup control.
  • Multi-Volume Support: Delete multiple Django volumes in single command. Efficient batch removal.
  • Permanent Removal: Data is irretrievably deleted. Backup Django data before removal.
  • In-Use Protection: Cannot delete volumes attached to containers. Prevents accidental Django data loss.
  • Error Feedback: Clear messages if removal fails. Django troubleshooting guidance.

Methods

Single Volume Removal

Syntax: docker volume rm [VOLUME]

Parameters: VOLUME (string)

Delete one Django volume by name.

Multiple Volume Removal

Syntax: docker volume rm [VOLUME1] [VOLUME2]...

Parameters: Multiple VOLUME names (strings)

Delete multiple Django volumes simultaneously.

Batch Removal with Filter

Syntax: docker volume rm $(docker volume ls -q --filter name=[PATTERN])

Parameters: PATTERN (string)

Remove all Django volumes matching pattern.

Return Value

Returns names of deleted volumes or error if in use.

5 Practical Django Examples

Example 1: Remove Single Test Volume

Delete Django test database after testing.

docker volume rm anihpj-test-db
anihpj-test-db

✓ Django test database volume removed
Example 2: Remove Development Environment

Clean entire Django development storage setup.

docker volume rm anihpj-dev-db anihpj-dev-media anihpj-dev-logs anihpj-dev-redis
anihpj-dev-db
anihpj-dev-media
anihpj-dev-logs
anihpj-dev-redis

✓ Django development volumes removed
Example 3: Handle In-Use Volume Error

Proper sequence to remove active Django volume.

# Try to remove (fails if in use)
docker volume rm anihpj-postgres-data

# Stop containers using it
docker-compose down

# Now remove successfully
docker volume rm anihpj-postgres-data
Error response from daemon: remove anihpj-postgres-data: volume is in use - [abc123def456]

Stopping anihpj_web_1 ... done
Stopping anihpj_db_1  ... done
Removing anihpj_web_1 ... done
Removing anihpj_db_1  ... done

anihpj-postgres-data

✓ Django database volume removed after stopping containers
Example 4: Remove All Test Volumes

Batch delete Django test volumes using filters.

# List test volumes
docker volume ls --filter name=anihpj-test

# Remove all test volumes
docker volume rm $(docker volume ls -q --filter name=anihpj-test)
DRIVER    VOLUME NAME
local     anihpj-test-db
local     anihpj-test-media
local     anihpj-test-logs
local     anihpj-test-cache

anihpj-test-db
anihpj-test-media
anihpj-test-logs
anihpj-test-cache

✓ 4 Django test volumes removed
Example 5: Safe Volume Cleanup Script

Django volume removal with backup verification.

#!/bin/bash
# Safe Django volume removal

VOLUME="anihpj-old-db"
BACKUP_DIR="/backups"

# Check if volume exists
if docker volume inspect $VOLUME &>/dev/null; then
  echo "Volume exists: $VOLUME"
  
  # Verify backup exists
  if [ -f "$BACKUP_DIR/$VOLUME.tar.gz" ]; then
    echo "✓ Backup verified"
    
    # Remove volume
    docker volume rm $VOLUME
    echo "✓ Volume removed"
  else
    echo "✗ No backup found. Aborting."
  fi
else
  echo "Volume not found: $VOLUME"
fi
Volume exists: anihpj-old-db
✓ Backup verified
anihpj-old-db
✓ Volume removed

✓ Django volume safely deleted after backup verification

docker volume update

Official Docs: docker volume update | Docker CLI: docker volume update

Update a volume (Swarm only).

Full Description

The docker volume update command updates the configuration of a volume in Docker Swarm mode, primarily for modifying availability and labeling. For your anihpj Django project running in Swarm clusters, this updates volume availability across nodes, modifies labels for organization, changes volume constraints, and manages distributed storage for PostgreSQL databases, media file uploads, static assets, Redis cache, and logs across User authentication, JobPost management, Skills verification, Author profiles, and Location services in multi-node deployments.

Syntax

docker volume update [OPTIONS] VOLUME

# Update availability
docker volume update --availability=drain anihpj-postgres-data

# Update with label
docker volume update --label-add env=production anihpj-media

# Activate volume
docker volume update --availability=active anihpj-logs

Parameters Description

  • VOLUME: Name of Django Swarm volume to update.
  • --availability: Set volume availability (active, pause, drain). Control Django volume placement.
  • --label-add: Add metadata label to Django volume for organization.
  • --label-rm: Remove metadata label from Django volume.

Attributes

  • Swarm Only: Works exclusively in Docker Swarm mode. For distributed Django deployments.
  • Availability Control: Manage Django volume placement on nodes. Drain for maintenance.
  • Label Management: Update Django volume metadata without recreation. Dynamic organization.
  • Live Updates: Modify volume without stopping Django services. Zero-downtime changes.
  • Node Constraints: Control where Django volumes are available in cluster.

Methods

Set Availability

Syntax: docker volume update --availability=[STATE] [VOLUME]

Parameters: STATE (active, pause, drain), VOLUME (string)

Control Django volume availability across Swarm nodes.

Add Label

Syntax: docker volume update --label-add [KEY]=[VALUE] [VOLUME]

Parameters: KEY, VALUE, VOLUME (strings)

Add metadata label to Django Swarm volume.

Remove Label

Syntax: docker volume update --label-rm [KEY] [VOLUME]

Parameters: KEY, VOLUME (strings)

Remove metadata label from Django volume.

Return Value

Returns volume name on successful update.

5 Practical Django Examples

Example 1: Drain Volume for Maintenance

Prepare Django database volume for node maintenance.

docker volume update --availability=drain anihpj-postgres-data
anihpj-postgres-data

✓ Django database volume drained
✓ No new tasks will use this volume
✓ Safe to perform node maintenance
Example 2: Add Environment Label

Tag Django media volume for production environment.

docker volume update --label-add env=production --label-add backup=daily anihpj-media
anihpj-media

✓ Labels added to Django media volume
✓ env=production
✓ backup=daily
Example 3: Reactivate After Maintenance

Restore Django volume availability after node work.

# Check current status
docker volume inspect anihpj-postgres-data --format='{{.Availability}}'

# Reactivate
docker volume update --availability=active anihpj-postgres-data

# Verify
docker volume inspect anihpj-postgres-data --format='{{.Availability}}'
drain

anihpj-postgres-data

active

✓ Django database volume reactivated
✓ Available for new tasks
Example 4: Update Multiple Volume Labels

Organize Django Swarm volumes with metadata.

# Add project and service labels
docker volume update \
  --label-add project=anihpj \
  --label-add service=database \
  --label-add tier=critical \
  anihpj-postgres-data

docker volume update \
  --label-add project=anihpj \
  --label-add service=storage \
  --label-add tier=important \
  anihpj-media
anihpj-postgres-data
✓ Labels: project=anihpj, service=database, tier=critical

anihpj-media
✓ Labels: project=anihpj, service=storage, tier=important

✓ Django Swarm volumes properly labeled
Example 5: Rolling Volume Maintenance Script

Automate Django volume maintenance in Swarm.

#!/bin/bash
# Rolling Django volume maintenance

VOLUMES=("anihpj-postgres-data" "anihpj-media" "anihpj-logs")

for vol in "${VOLUMES[@]}"; do
  echo "Processing $vol..."
  
  # Drain volume
  docker volume update --availability=drain $vol
  echo "✓ Drained $vol"
  
  # Perform maintenance (backup, verify, etc.)
  docker run --rm -v $vol:/data alpine sh -c "du -sh /data"
  
  # Reactivate
  docker volume update --availability=active $vol
  echo "✓ Reactivated $vol"
  
  echo "---"
done

echo "✓ All Django volumes maintained"
Processing anihpj-postgres-data...
anihpj-postgres-data
✓ Drained anihpj-postgres-data
1.2G    /data
anihpj-postgres-data
✓ Reactivated anihpj-postgres-data
---
Processing anihpj-media...
anihpj-media
✓ Drained anihpj-media
3.4G    /data
anihpj-media
✓ Reactivated anihpj-media
---
Processing anihpj-logs...
anihpj-logs
✓ Drained anihpj-logs
256M    /data
anihpj-logs
✓ Reactivated anihpj-logs
---
✓ All Django volumes maintained

System Commands

Docker system commands provide system-wide information and management capabilities. These commands help monitor Docker resources, view events, gather diagnostic information, and clean up unused resources for maintaining healthy Django deployment environments.

docker system

Official Docs: docker system | Docker CLI: docker system

Manage Docker system.

Full Description

The docker system command provides a suite of subcommands for managing and monitoring the Docker system as a whole. For your anihpj Django project, these commands help track disk usage, monitor real-time events, gather system diagnostics, and perform maintenance operations to ensure optimal performance of containers running User authentication, JobPost management, Skills verification, Author profiles, and Location services.

When managing your anihpj Django system, the command family includes df for disk usage analysis, events for real-time monitoring, info for system diagnostics, and prune for cleanup operations. These subcommands provide comprehensive visibility into your Docker infrastructure, enabling proactive monitoring of Skills model testing containers, User authentication services, Author profile APIs, Location processing workers, and JobPost queue management systems with detailed resource tracking and event streaming capabilities.

Syntax

docker system COMMAND

# Available subcommands
docker system df       # Show Docker disk usage
docker system events   # Get real-time events from server
docker system info     # Display system-wide information
docker system prune    # Remove unused data

Parameters Description

  • COMMAND: Subcommand for system operations (df, events, info, prune).

Attributes

  • System Monitoring: Track Docker resource usage and health. Monitor Django deployment infrastructure.
  • Event Streaming: Real-time event notifications for containers, images, volumes. Debug Django application issues.
  • Diagnostic Information: Complete system configuration and status. Troubleshoot Django environment problems.
  • Resource Cleanup: Remove unused Docker objects to free space. Maintain clean Django development environment.
  • Centralized Management: Single command family for system operations. Simplified Django infrastructure administration.

Methods

Disk Usage Analysis

Syntax: docker system df

Parameters: None

Display disk space used by Docker images, containers, and volumes for Django.

Event Monitoring

Syntax: docker system events

Parameters: None

Stream real-time Docker events for Django container operations.

System Cleanup

Syntax: docker system prune

Parameters: None

Remove all unused Docker objects to reclaim space for Django.

Return Value

Returns usage information and available system subcommands.

  • Success (0): Command executed successfully with subcommand list
  • Error (1): Invalid subcommand or Docker daemon connection failure
  • Error (125): Docker daemon error or invalid command usage

5 Practical Django Examples

Example 1: Check System Status

View Docker system health for anihpj deployment.

# Check disk usage
docker system df

# View system info
docker system info | grep -i "containers\|images\|volumes"
TYPE            TOTAL     ACTIVE    SIZE      RECLAIMABLE
Images          12        5         4.2GB     2.1GB (50%)
Containers      8         3         1.5GB     800MB (53%)
Local Volumes   7         5         2.8GB     450MB (16%)
Build Cache     0         0         0B        0B

Containers: 8
  Running: 3
  Paused: 0
  Stopped: 5
Images: 12
Server Version: 24.0.7
Example 2: Monitor Django Container Events

Watch real-time events for anihpj containers.

# Monitor all events
docker system events --filter type=container --filter container=anihpj

# With timestamp
docker system events --since '10m' --filter type=container
2026-01-15T10:30:15.123456789Z container start abc123 (image=anihpj/web:latest, name=anihpj_web_1)
2026-01-15T10:30:16.234567890Z container health_status: healthy abc123 (image=anihpj/web:latest)
2026-01-15T10:31:22.345678901Z container exec_create def456 (image=anihpj/db:latest, name=anihpj_db_1)
2026-01-15T10:32:45.456789012Z container stop ghi789 (image=anihpj/worker:latest, name=anihpj_worker_1)

✓ Django container events streaming
Example 3: System Diagnostic Report

Generate comprehensive Django deployment diagnostics.

#!/bin/bash
# Django system diagnostic script

echo "=== ANIHPJ Django System Diagnostics ==="
echo ""
echo "Disk Usage:"
docker system df

echo ""
echo "System Info:"
docker system info --format '{{json .}}' | jq '{Containers, Images, MemTotal, NCPU}'

echo ""
echo "Recent Events (last 5):"
docker system events --since '5m' --until '0s' | tail -5
=== ANIHPJ Django System Diagnostics ===

Disk Usage:
TYPE            TOTAL     ACTIVE    SIZE      RECLAIMABLE
Images          12        5         4.2GB     2.1GB (50%)
Containers      8         3         1.5GB     800MB (53%)
Local Volumes   7         5         2.8GB     450MB (16%)

System Info:
{
  "Containers": 8,
  "Images": 12,
  "MemTotal": 8589934592,
  "NCPU": 4
}

Recent Events (last 5):
2026-01-15T10:30:15Z container start abc123
2026-01-15T10:31:22Z image pull anihpj/web:latest
2026-01-15T10:32:45Z volume create anihpj-media
2026-01-15T10:33:10Z network create anihpj-network
2026-01-15T10:34:28Z container health_status: healthy
Example 4: Automated Cleanup Workflow

Regular maintenance for Django infrastructure.

# Check reclaimable space
docker system df

# Cleanup unused resources
docker system prune -f

# Verify space reclaimed
docker system df
TYPE            TOTAL     ACTIVE    SIZE      RECLAIMABLE
Images          12        5         4.2GB     2.1GB (50%)
Containers      8         3         1.5GB     800MB (53%)
Local Volumes   7         5         2.8GB     450MB (16%)

Deleted Containers:
abc123def456
ghi789jkl012

Deleted Images:
anihpj/old-build:latest
anihpj/test:dev

Total reclaimed space: 3.4GB

TYPE            TOTAL     ACTIVE    SIZE      RECLAIMABLE
Images          8         5         2.8GB     150MB (5%)
Containers      3         3         700MB     0B (0%)
Local Volumes   5         5         2.3GB     0B (0%)
Example 5: Production Health Check

Monitor Django production environment health.

# Get system overview
docker system info | grep -E "Containers|Running|Paused|Stopped|Images|Server Version"

# Check for errors in last hour
docker system events --since '1h' --filter type=container --filter event=die
Containers: 8
  Running: 6
  Paused: 0
  Stopped: 2
Images: 12
Server Version: 24.0.7

2026-01-15T09:45:23Z container die abc123 (exitCode=1, image=anihpj/worker:latest)

✓ 6 Django containers running
⚠ 1 container died with exit code 1 in last hour
✓ Need to investigate anihpj worker container

docker system df

Official Docs: docker system df | Docker CLI: docker system df

Show Docker disk usage.

Full Description

The docker system df command displays the amount of disk space used by Docker images, containers, volumes, and build cache. For your anihpj Django project, this helps monitor storage consumption, identify space-hogging resources, plan cleanup operations, and optimize disk usage for PostgreSQL databases, media uploads, static files, Redis cache, and logs supporting User, JobPost, Skills, Author, and Location services.

When monitoring your anihpj Django storage, the command provides detailed breakdown of total vs active resources, reclaimable space percentages, and verbose listings of individual objects. This enables proactive management of Skills model test data volumes, User authentication database storage, Author profile media uploads, Location geospatial cache files, and JobPost queue processing logs with granular visibility into storage consumption patterns and cleanup opportunities.

Syntax

docker system df [OPTIONS]

# Basic disk usage
docker system df

# Verbose output with details
docker system df -v

# Format output
docker system df --format "table {{.Type}}\t{{.TotalCount}}\t{{.Size}}"

Parameters Description

  • -v, --verbose: Show detailed information for each object. Lists all Django images, containers, and volumes individually.
  • --format: Format output using Go template. Customize Django storage report display.

Attributes

  • Storage Overview: Comprehensive disk usage summary. Track Django resource consumption at a glance.
  • Reclaimable Space: Identify unused resources that can be removed. Optimize Django storage efficiency.
  • Type Breakdown: Separate stats for images, containers, volumes, cache. Understand Django storage distribution.
  • Active vs Total: Distinguish between in-use and available resources. Plan Django cleanup strategy.
  • Verbose Details: List individual objects with sizes. Pinpoint specific Django space consumers.

Methods

Basic Usage Summary

Syntax: docker system df

Parameters: None

Display high-level disk usage summary for Django resources.

Detailed Breakdown

Syntax: docker system df -v

Parameters: None

Show verbose listing of all Django images, containers, and volumes with sizes.

Custom Format

Syntax: docker system df --format "{{.Type}}: {{.Size}}"

Parameters: None

Display Django storage data in custom format for reporting.

Return Value

Returns table with disk usage statistics by type.

  • Success (0): Disk usage information displayed successfully
  • Error (1): Docker daemon connection failure or permission denied
  • Error (125): Invalid format option or Docker daemon error

5 Practical Django Examples

Example 1: Quick Storage Overview

Check Django infrastructure disk usage.

docker system df
TYPE            TOTAL     ACTIVE    SIZE      RECLAIMABLE
Images          12        5         4.2GB     2.1GB (50%)
Containers      8         3         1.5GB     800MB (53%)
Local Volumes   7         5         2.8GB     450MB (16%)
Build Cache     15        0         3.5GB     3.5GB (100%)

✓ Total Docker usage: 11.9GB
✓ Reclaimable space: 6.85GB (58%)
Example 2: Detailed Volume Analysis

List all Django volumes with sizes.

docker system df -v --format "table {{.Type}}\t{{.Name}}\t{{.Size}}"
TYPE       NAME                      SIZE
volume     anihpj-postgres-data     1.2GB
volume     anihpj-media             850MB
volume     anihpj-static            45MB
volume     anihpj-logs              230MB
volume     anihpj-redis-data        128MB
volume     anihpj-old-backup        450MB
volume     anihpj-test-data         0B

✓ PostgreSQL using most space (1.2GB)
⚠ Old backup volume can be removed (450MB)
Example 3: Image Storage Analysis

Identify large Django images for optimization.

docker system df -v | grep -A 20 "Images space usage"
Images space usage:

REPOSITORY              TAG          SIZE      SHARED SIZE
anihpj/web             latest       850MB     400MB
anihpj/worker          latest       820MB     400MB
anihpj/nginx           latest       180MB     120MB
postgres               15           380MB     0B
redis                  7            120MB     0B
anihpj/old-build       v1.0         1.2GB     300MB
                        950MB     0B

✓ Active images: 5 (2.35GB)
⚠ Unused images: 2 (2.15GB - 50% reclaimable)
Example 4: Build Cache Analysis

Check Django build cache consumption.

docker system df

# Clear if cache too large
if [ $(docker system df --format '{{.BuildCache}}' | grep -o '[0-9.]*GB' | cut -d'G' -f1) -gt 2 ]; then
  echo "Clearing build cache..."
  docker builder prune -f
fi
TYPE            TOTAL     ACTIVE    SIZE      RECLAIMABLE
Images          12        5         4.2GB     2.1GB (50%)
Containers      8         3         1.5GB     800MB (53%)
Local Volumes   7         5         2.8GB     450MB (16%)
Build Cache     15        0         3.5GB     3.5GB (100%)

Build cache exceeds 2GB threshold
Clearing build cache...
Deleted build cache objects
Total reclaimed space: 3.5GB

✓ Build cache cleared for Django
Example 5: Storage Monitoring Script

Automated Django storage health check.

#!/bin/bash
# Django storage monitoring

echo "=== Django Docker Storage Report ==="
echo "Generated: $(date)"
echo ""

# Get usage data
docker system df

echo ""
echo "=== Alerts ==="

# Check reclaimable percentage
RECLAIMABLE=$(docker system df | grep Images | awk '{print $5}' | tr -d '()%')
if [ "$RECLAIMABLE" -gt 70 ]; then
  echo "⚠ WARNING: ${RECLAIMABLE}% of images are reclaimable"
  echo "   Consider running: docker image prune -a"
fi

# Check container usage
STOPPED=$(docker ps -a -q -f status=exited | wc -l)
if [ "$STOPPED" -gt 5 ]; then
  echo "⚠ WARNING: $STOPPED stopped containers"
  echo "   Consider running: docker container prune"
fi

echo ""
echo "✓ Storage monitoring complete"
=== Django Docker Storage Report ===
Generated: Wed Jan 15 10:45:30 UTC 2026

TYPE            TOTAL     ACTIVE    SIZE      RECLAIMABLE
Images          12        5         4.2GB     2.1GB (50%)
Containers      8         3         1.5GB     800MB (53%)
Local Volumes   7         5         2.8GB     450MB (16%)
Build Cache     15        0         3.5GB     3.5GB (100%)

=== Alerts ===
⚠ WARNING: 7 stopped containers
   Consider running: docker container prune

✓ Storage monitoring complete

docker system events

Official Docs: docker system events | Docker CLI: docker system events

Get real-time events from the server.

Full Description

The docker system events command streams real-time events from the Docker daemon, including container lifecycle events, image operations, volume changes, network updates, and daemon activities. For your anihpj Django project, this provides live monitoring of container starts/stops, health checks, deployment updates, resource modifications, and troubleshooting insights for User authentication services, JobPost operations, Skills management, Author profiles, and Location services.

When monitoring your anihpj Django events, the command supports flexible filtering by type, container, image, volume, network, event, and label, along with time-based queries for historical analysis. This enables incident investigation of Skills model container failures, User authentication health status changes, Author profile deployment tracking, Location service network updates, and JobPost worker task execution with structured JSON output for automated monitoring and alerting systems.

Syntax

docker system events [OPTIONS]

# Stream all events
docker system events

# Filter by type
docker system events --filter type=container

# Time range
docker system events --since '1h' --until '30m'

# Filter by container
docker system events --filter container=anihpj_web

# Multiple filters
docker system events --filter type=container --filter event=start

# Format output
docker system events --format '{{.Time}} {{.Type}} {{.Action}}'

Parameters Description

  • --filter: Filter events by type, container, image, volume, network, event, or label. Target specific Django resources.
  • --since: Show events since timestamp or relative time. Review Django activity history.
  • --until: Show events until timestamp or relative time. Analyze Django events in time window.
  • --format: Format output using Go template. Customize Django event display.

Attributes

  • Real-time Streaming: Live event feed from Docker daemon. Monitor Django operations as they happen.
  • Comprehensive Coverage: All Docker object types and events. Complete Django infrastructure visibility.
  • Flexible Filtering: Target specific resources or event types. Focus on Django components of interest.
  • Time-based Queries: Historical event retrieval with time ranges. Investigate Django past incidents.
  • Structured Output: JSON-formatted events with metadata. Parse Django events programmatically.

Methods

Stream All Events

Syntax: docker system events

Parameters: None

Monitor all Docker events in real-time for Django infrastructure.

Filtered Event Stream

Syntax: docker system events --filter [KEY]=[VALUE]

Parameters: KEY, VALUE (strings)

Watch specific Django container, image, or volume events.

Historical Events

Syntax: docker system events --since [TIME] --until [TIME]

Parameters: TIME (timestamp or relative)

Retrieve Django events from specific time period.

Return Value

Returns stream of events in JSON format with timestamps.

  • Success (0): Event stream started successfully, outputs continuous events
  • Error (1): Invalid filter criteria or Docker daemon connection failure
  • Error (125): Invalid time format or Docker daemon error

5 Practical Django Examples

Example 1: Monitor Container Lifecycle

Watch Django container starts and stops in real-time.

docker system events --filter type=container --filter event=start --filter event=stop --filter event=die
2026-01-15T10:30:15.123456Z container start abc123 (image=anihpj/web:latest, name=anihpj_web_1)
2026-01-15T10:30:16.234567Z container start def456 (image=postgres:15, name=anihpj_db_1)
2026-01-15T10:32:45.345678Z container stop ghi789 (image=anihpj/worker:latest, name=anihpj_worker_1)
2026-01-15T10:35:22.456789Z container die jkl012 (exitCode=137, image=anihpj/celery:latest, name=anihpj_celery_1)

✓ Monitoring Django container lifecycle
⚠ Worker stopped, Celery died with code 137 (OOM killed)
Example 2: Health Check Monitoring

Track Django container health status changes.

docker system events --filter type=container --filter event=health_status
2026-01-15T10:30:20.123456Z container health_status: healthy abc123 (image=anihpj/web:latest, name=anihpj_web_1)
2026-01-15T10:31:15.234567Z container health_status: healthy def456 (image=postgres:15, name=anihpj_db_1)
2026-01-15T10:33:45.345678Z container health_status: unhealthy ghi789 (image=anihpj/api:latest, name=anihpj_api_1)
2026-01-15T10:34:00.456789Z container health_status: healthy ghi789 (image=anihpj/api:latest, name=anihpj_api_1)

✓ Django web and database healthy
⚠ API service had temporary health issue (resolved)
Example 3: Deployment Event Tracking

Monitor Django deployment process with image pulls and container updates.

docker system events --filter type=image --filter event=pull --since '5m'
2026-01-15T10:25:10.123456Z image pull anihpj/web:v2.0.0 (name=anihpj/web:v2.0.0)
2026-01-15T10:25:45.234567Z image pull anihpj/worker:v2.0.0 (name=anihpj/worker:v2.0.0)
2026-01-15T10:26:12.345678Z image pull anihpj/nginx:latest (name=anihpj/nginx:latest)

✓ New Django images pulled for v2.0.0 deployment
✓ Web, worker, and nginx updated
Example 4: Volume Activity Monitor

Track Django volume operations and data persistence events.

docker system events --filter type=volume --filter event=create --filter event=mount --filter event=unmount
2026-01-15T10:30:05.123456Z volume create anihpj-media-v2 (driver=local)
2026-01-15T10:30:15.234567Z volume mount anihpj-postgres-data (container=abc123, destination=/var/lib/postgresql/data)
2026-01-15T10:30:16.345678Z volume mount anihpj-media (container=abc123, destination=/app/media)
2026-01-15T10:35:45.456789Z volume unmount anihpj-logs (container=ghi789)

✓ Django media volume created
✓ Database and media volumes mounted to web container
✓ Logs unmounted from stopped worker
Example 5: Incident Investigation

Analyze Django events during specific incident timeframe.

#!/bin/bash
# Django incident investigation

INCIDENT_START="2026-01-15T10:30:00"
INCIDENT_END="2026-01-15T10:35:00"

echo "=== Django Incident Analysis ==="
echo "Timeframe: $INCIDENT_START to $INCIDENT_END"
echo ""

# Container events
echo "Container Events:"
docker system events --since "$INCIDENT_START" --until "$INCIDENT_END" \
  --filter type=container --format '{{.Time}} {{.Action}} {{.Actor.Attributes.name}}'

echo ""
# Network events
echo "Network Events:"
docker system events --since "$INCIDENT_START" --until "$INCIDENT_END" \
  --filter type=network --format '{{.Time}} {{.Action}} {{.Actor.Attributes.name}}'

echo ""
# Errors
echo "Container Failures:"
docker system events --since "$INCIDENT_START" --until "$INCIDENT_END" \
  --filter type=container --filter event=die
=== Django Incident Analysis ===
Timeframe: 2026-01-15T10:30:00 to 2026-01-15T10:35:00

Container Events:
2026-01-15T10:30:15Z start anihpj_web_1
2026-01-15T10:30:16Z start anihpj_db_1
2026-01-15T10:32:30Z restart anihpj_worker_1
2026-01-15T10:33:45Z stop anihpj_celery_1
2026-01-15T10:34:22Z start anihpj_celery_1

Network Events:
2026-01-15T10:30:14Z connect anihpj-network
2026-01-15T10:33:46Z disconnect anihpj-network

Container Failures:
2026-01-15T10:33:45Z container die (exitCode=1, image=anihpj/celery:latest)

✓ Timeline reconstructed
⚠ Celery worker crashed at 10:33:45
✓ Automatic restart occurred at 10:34:22

docker system info

Official Docs: docker system info | Docker CLI: docker system info

Display system-wide information.

Full Description

The docker system info command displays comprehensive information about the Docker system including daemon configuration, storage drivers, runtime details, plugin status, resource limits, and operating system information. For your anihpj Django project, this provides critical diagnostics for troubleshooting deployment issues, verifying system configuration, checking resource availability, and ensuring proper setup for User authentication, JobPost management, Skills verification, Author profiles, and Location services.

When diagnosing your anihpj Django environment, the command provides detailed overview of CPU count, total memory, storage driver configuration, container statistics, image counts, runtime status, and plugin availability. This enables verification of Skills model testing infrastructure capacity, User authentication database resource allocation, Author profile media storage configuration, Location service processing limits, and JobPost queue worker deployment specifications with exportable JSON format for automated environment validation and compliance checking.

Syntax

docker system info [OPTIONS]

# Full system information
docker system info

# Format specific fields
docker system info --format '{{.ServerVersion}}'

# JSON output
docker system info --format '{{json .}}'

# Specific info grep
docker system info | grep -i "containers\|images\|memory"

Parameters Description

  • -f, --format: Format output using Go template. Extract specific Django system metrics.

Attributes

  • Comprehensive Overview: Complete Docker system configuration and status. Full Django deployment environment details.
  • Resource Information: CPU, memory, storage driver details. Assess Django infrastructure capacity.
  • Runtime Configuration: Daemon settings, plugin status, networking. Verify Django environment setup.
  • Container Statistics: Running, paused, stopped container counts. Monitor Django application state.
  • Storage Metrics: Image and volume counts, storage driver. Track Django data persistence configuration.

Methods

Full Information Display

Syntax: docker system info

Parameters: None

Show complete Docker system information for Django environment.

Extract Specific Field

Syntax: docker system info --format '{{.Field}}'

Parameters: Field (string)

Get specific Django system metric programmatically.

JSON Export

Syntax: docker system info --format '{{json .}}'

Parameters: None

Export Django system configuration as JSON for processing.

Return Value

Returns detailed system information in text or JSON format.

  • Success (0): System information displayed successfully
  • Error (1): Docker daemon connection failure or permission denied
  • Error (125): Invalid format option or Docker daemon error

5 Practical Django Examples

Example 1: Quick System Overview

Check Django deployment system status.

docker system info | head -30
Client: Docker Engine - Community
 Version:    24.0.7
 Context:    default
 Debug Mode: false

Server:
 Containers: 8
  Running: 6
  Paused: 0
  Stopped: 2
 Images: 12
 Server Version: 24.0.7
 Storage Driver: overlay2
  Backing Filesystem: extfs
 Logging Driver: json-file
 Cgroup Driver: systemd
 Plugins:
  Volume: local
  Network: bridge host ipvlan macvlan null overlay
 Swarm: inactive
 Runtimes: runc
 Default Runtime: runc
 Kernel Version: 5.15.0-89-generic
 Operating System: Ubuntu 22.04.3 LTS
 OSType: linux
 Architecture: x86_64
 CPUs: 4
 Total Memory: 8GiB
 Name: django-prod-server
 Docker Root Dir: /var/lib/docker

✓ 6 Django containers running on Ubuntu 22.04
Example 2: Resource Capacity Check

Verify Django server resources and limits.

docker system info --format 'CPUs: {{.NCPU}}\nMemory: {{.MemTotal}}\nStorage Driver: {{.Driver}}'
CPUs: 4
Memory: 8589934592
Storage Driver: overlay2

✓ 4 CPU cores available
✓ 8GB RAM total
✓ Using overlay2 storage driver (optimal for Django)
Example 3: Container Statistics

Get Django container deployment statistics.

docker system info | grep -A 5 "Containers:"
Containers: 8
  Running: 6
