  Paused: 0
  Stopped: 2
Images: 12
Server Version: 24.0.7

✓ Django production: 6 active containers
⚠ 2 stopped containers (can be cleaned)
Example 4: Storage Configuration

Verify Django volume and storage setup.

docker system info | grep -E "Storage Driver|Backing Filesystem|Docker Root"
Storage Driver: overlay2
  Backing Filesystem: extfs
  Supports d_type: true
Docker Root Dir: /var/lib/docker

✓ overlay2 driver active (best performance)
✓ Extended filesystem support enabled
✓ Django data stored in /var/lib/docker
Example 5: Environment Diagnostic Report

Generate comprehensive Django environment report.

#!/bin/bash
# Django environment diagnostic

echo "=== ANIHPJ Django Environment Report ==="
echo "Generated: $(date)"
echo ""

# System info
INFO=$(docker system info 2>/dev/null)

echo "Docker Version: $(echo "$INFO" | grep "Server Version" | awk '{print $3}')"
echo "OS: $(echo "$INFO" | grep "Operating System" | cut -d: -f2 | xargs)"
echo "Kernel: $(echo "$INFO" | grep "Kernel Version" | cut -d: -f2 | xargs)"
echo "Architecture: $(echo "$INFO" | grep "Architecture" | awk '{print $2}')"
echo ""

echo "Resources:"
echo "  CPUs: $(echo "$INFO" | grep "CPUs:" | awk '{print $2}')"
echo "  Memory: $(echo "$INFO" | grep "Total Memory" | cut -d: -f2 | xargs)"
echo "  Storage: $(echo "$INFO" | grep "Storage Driver" | awk '{print $3}')"
echo ""

echo "Containers:"
echo "  Total: $(echo "$INFO" | grep -A 3 "Containers:" | grep -v "Containers:" | grep -E "Running|Paused|Stopped" | awk '{sum+=$2} END {print sum}')"
echo "  Running: $(echo "$INFO" | grep "Running:" | awk '{print $2}')"
echo "  Stopped: $(echo "$INFO" | grep "Stopped:" | awk '{print $2}')"
echo ""

echo "Images: $(echo "$INFO" | grep "^Images:" | awk '{print $2}')"
echo ""

# Check for warnings
STOPPED=$(echo "$INFO" | grep "Stopped:" | awk '{print $2}')
if [ "$STOPPED" -gt 0 ]; then
  echo "⚠ $STOPPED stopped containers detected"
fi

echo ""
echo "✓ Environment diagnostic complete"
=== ANIHPJ Django Environment Report ===
Generated: Wed Jan 15 10:45:30 UTC 2026

Docker Version: 24.0.7
OS: Ubuntu 22.04.3 LTS
Kernel: 5.15.0-89-generic
Architecture: x86_64

Resources:
  CPUs: 4
  Memory: 8GiB
  Storage: overlay2

Containers:
  Total: 8
  Running: 6
  Stopped: 2

Images: 12

⚠ 2 stopped containers detected

✓ Environment diagnostic complete

docker system prune

Official Docs: docker system prune | Docker CLI: docker system prune

Remove unused data.

Full Description

The docker system prune command removes all unused Docker objects including stopped containers, dangling images, unused networks, and optionally volumes and build cache to reclaim disk space. For your anihpj Django project, this performs comprehensive cleanup of development artifacts, test containers, old builds, orphaned volumes, and network configurations no longer needed by User authentication, JobPost management, Skills verification, Author profiles, and Location services, maintaining a clean and efficient Docker environment.

When cleaning your anihpj Django environment, the command provides safety confirmation prompts, detailed deletion reports, space reclamation metrics, and filtering options by label or age. This enables targeted cleanup of Skills model test containers, User authentication debugging snapshots, Author profile staging images, Location processing cache volumes, and JobPost worker build artifacts with selective pruning strategies for development, staging, and production environments while protecting active resources and critical data volumes.

Syntax

docker system prune [OPTIONS]

# Basic prune (containers, networks, images)
docker system prune

# Force without confirmation
docker system prune -f

# Include volumes
docker system prune --volumes

# Include all unused images
docker system prune -a

# Complete cleanup (everything)
docker system prune -a --volumes -f

# Filter by label
docker system prune --filter label=env=test

# Filter by time
docker system prune --filter until=24h

Parameters Description

  • -f, --force: Skip confirmation prompt. Automate Django cleanup in scripts.
  • -a, --all: Remove all unused images, not just dangling. Deep clean Django images.
  • --volumes: Prune unused volumes too. Clean Django database and media volumes.
  • --filter: Filter objects to prune (label, until). Target specific Django resources.

Attributes

  • Comprehensive Cleanup: Removes multiple object types in one command. Efficient Django maintenance.
  • Safety Confirmation: Prompts before deletion unless forced. Protect Django production data.
  • Space Reclamation: Reports exact disk space freed. Track Django cleanup benefits.
  • Selective Pruning: Filter by labels or age. Clean Django test environments only.
  • Volume Protection: Volumes excluded by default. Safeguard Django persistent data.

Methods

Standard Prune

Syntax: docker system prune

Parameters: None

Remove stopped containers, unused networks, dangling images for Django.

Complete Cleanup

Syntax: docker system prune -a --volumes

Parameters: None

Remove all unused Django resources including volumes.

Filtered Prune

Syntax: docker system prune --filter [KEY]=[VALUE]

Parameters: KEY, VALUE (strings)

Clean specific Django resources by label or age.

Return Value

Returns list of deleted objects and total space reclaimed.

  • Success (0): Cleanup completed successfully with space reclaimed report
  • Error (1): Invalid filter criteria or Docker daemon connection failure
  • Error (125): Docker daemon error or insufficient permissions

5 Practical Django Examples

Example 1: Basic System Cleanup

Clean Django development environment safely.

docker system prune
WARNING! This will remove:
  - all stopped containers
  - all networks not used by at least one container
  - all dangling images
  - all dangling build cache

Are you sure you want to continue? [y/N] y

Deleted Containers:
abc123def456
ghi789jkl012
mno345pqr678

Deleted Networks:
anihpj-old-network
test-network

Deleted Images:
sha256:1a2b3c4d5e6f...
sha256:7g8h9i0j1k2l...

Deleted build cache objects:
3m4n5o6p7q8r
9s0t1u2v3w4x

Total reclaimed space: 4.2GB

✓ Django environment cleaned
Example 2: Aggressive Cleanup

Remove all unused Django resources including images and volumes.

# Check space before
docker system df

# Complete cleanup
docker system prune -a --volumes -f

# Check space after
docker system df
TYPE            TOTAL     ACTIVE    SIZE      RECLAIMABLE
Images          18        5         6.8GB     4.2GB (62%)
Containers      12        3         2.1GB     1.5GB (71%)
Local Volumes   10        5         3.5GB     800MB (23%)
Build Cache     25        0         5.2GB     5.2GB (100%)

Deleted Containers: 9
Deleted Networks: 3
Deleted Images: 13
Deleted Volumes: 5
Deleted build cache: 25

Total reclaimed space: 11.7GB

TYPE            TOTAL     ACTIVE    SIZE      RECLAIMABLE
Images          5         5         2.6GB     0B (0%)
Containers      3         3         600MB     0B (0%)
Local Volumes   5         5         2.7GB     0B (0%)
Build Cache     0         0         0B        0B

✓ Reclaimed 11.7GB for Django
Example 3: Clean Test Environment

Remove Django test resources by label.

docker system prune --filter label=env=test -f
Deleted Containers:
anihpj-test-web
anihpj-test-db
anihpj-test-worker

Deleted Networks:
anihpj-test-network

Deleted Images:
anihpj/web:test
anihpj/worker:test

Total reclaimed space: 3.8GB

✓ Django test environment cleaned
✓ Production resources preserved
Example 4: Time-based Cleanup

Remove Django resources older than specified time.

# Clean resources older than 7 days
docker system prune --filter until=168h -f

# Alternative: 24 hours
docker system prune --filter until=24h -f
Deleted Containers:
anihpj-old-migration (stopped 8 days ago)
anihpj-old-backup (stopped 10 days ago)

Deleted Images:
anihpj/web:v1.0.0 (created 9 days ago)
anihpj/worker:old (created 15 days ago)

Deleted Networks:
anihpj-legacy-network (created 30 days ago)

Total reclaimed space: 2.4GB

✓ Removed Django resources older than 7 days
✓ Recent resources preserved
Example 5: Automated Maintenance Script

Regular Django cleanup with reporting.

#!/bin/bash
# Django weekly maintenance script

echo "=== ANIHPJ Django Weekly Maintenance ==="
echo "Started: $(date)"
echo ""

# Check space before
echo "Storage Before Cleanup:"
docker system df
echo ""

# Perform cleanup
echo "Running system prune..."
RESULT=$(docker system prune -f 2>&1)
echo "$RESULT"
echo ""

# Extract reclaimed space
RECLAIMED=$(echo "$RESULT" | grep "Total reclaimed" | awk '{print $3, $4}')
echo "✓ Space reclaimed: $RECLAIMED"
echo ""

# Check space after
echo "Storage After Cleanup:"
docker system df
echo ""

# Check for warnings
RECLAIMABLE=$(docker system df | grep Images | awk '{print $5}' | tr -d '()%')
if [ "$RECLAIMABLE" -gt 50 ]; then
  echo "⚠ WARNING: Still ${RECLAIMABLE}% images reclaimable"
  echo "   Consider running: docker system prune -a"
fi

# Log completion
echo ""
echo "✓ Django maintenance complete"
echo "Completed: $(date)"

# Send notification (optional)
# curl -X POST https://hooks.slack.com/... -d "Django cleanup: $RECLAIMED freed"
=== ANIHPJ Django Weekly Maintenance ===
Started: Wed Jan 15 03:00:00 UTC 2026

Storage Before Cleanup:
TYPE            TOTAL     ACTIVE    SIZE      RECLAIMABLE
Images          15        5         5.8GB     3.2GB (55%)
Containers      10        3         1.8GB     1.2GB (67%)
Local Volumes   8         5         2.9GB     400MB (14%)
Build Cache     20        0         4.5GB     4.5GB (100%)

Running system prune...
Deleted Containers: 7
Deleted Networks: 2
Deleted Images: 8
Deleted build cache: 20

Total reclaimed space: 9.3GB

✓ Space reclaimed: 9.3GB

Storage After Cleanup:
TYPE            TOTAL     ACTIVE    SIZE      RECLAIMABLE
Images          7         5         2.6GB     800MB (31%)
Containers      3         3         600MB     0B (0%)
Local Volumes   6         5         2.5GB     100MB (4%)
Build Cache     0         0         0B        0B

✓ Django maintenance complete
Completed: Wed Jan 15 03:02:15 UTC 2026

docker version

Official Docs: docker version | Docker CLI: docker version

Show Docker version information.

Full Description

The docker version command displays comprehensive version information for both the Docker client and server (daemon). For your anihpj Django project, this command helps verify Docker installation, check client-server compatibility, troubleshoot version-related issues, and document deployment environments running User authentication services, JobPost management APIs, Skills verification containers, Author profile processors, and Location geospatial workers.

When managing your anihpj Django deployment, the command provides detailed version information including client version, server version, API version, Go version, Git commit, build time, OS/architecture, and experimental features status. This enables verification of Skills model container compatibility, User authentication service requirements, Author profile API dependencies, Location processing engine versions, and JobPost queue management system prerequisites with comprehensive version tracking and compatibility validation.

Syntax

docker version [OPTIONS]

# Common usage
docker version              # Show all version info
docker version --format json  # JSON output
docker version -f '{{.Server.Version}}'  # Specific field

Parameters Description

  • --format, -f: Format output using Go template. Output specific version fields.
  • --help: Print usage information. Display command help.

Attributes

  • Version Verification: Check Docker client and server versions. Ensure Django deployment compatibility.
  • API Compatibility: Verify client-server API version alignment. Prevent Django container compatibility issues.
  • Build Information: Display Git commit and build time details. Track Django environment versions.
  • Platform Details: Show OS, architecture, and Go runtime version. Validate Django deployment platform.
  • Format Flexibility: Output in human-readable or JSON format. Integrate with Django deployment automation.

Methods

Basic Version Check

Syntax: docker version

Parameters: None

Display complete Docker version information for anihpj Django environment.

JSON Format Output

Syntax: docker version --format json

Parameters: --format json

Output version information in JSON format for Django automation scripts.

Specific Field Extraction

Syntax: docker version -f '{{.Server.Version}}'

Parameters: -f with Go template

Extract specific version field for Django deployment verification.

Return Value

Returns Docker version information for client and server components.

  • Success (0): Version information displayed successfully
  • Error (1): Cannot connect to Docker daemon or invalid format
  • Error (125): Docker daemon error or command syntax error

5 Practical Django Examples

Example 1: Pre-Deployment Version Check

Verify Docker version before deploying anihpj Django containers.

# Check complete version info
docker version

# Verify minimum version requirement
echo "Checking Docker version for anihpj deployment..."
SERVER_VERSION=$(docker version -f '{{.Server.Version}}')
echo "Server version: $SERVER_VERSION"
Client:
 Version:           24.0.7
 API version:       1.43
 Go version:        go1.21.3
 Git commit:        afdd53b
 Built:             Thu Oct 26 09:08:44 2023
 OS/Arch:           linux/amd64
 Context:           default

Server: Docker Engine - Community
 Engine:
  Version:          24.0.7
  API version:      1.43 (minimum version 1.12)
  Go version:       go1.21.3
  Git commit:       311b9ff
  Built:            Thu Oct 26 09:08:44 2023
  OS/Arch:          linux/amd64
  Experimental:     false

Checking Docker version for anihpj deployment...
Server version: 24.0.7
✓ Docker version compatible with anihpj Django
Example 2: CI/CD Version Validation

Validate Docker version in GitHub Actions workflow for anihpj.

# CI/CD version check script
cat > check-docker.sh <<'EOF'
#!/bin/bash
MIN_VERSION="20.10"
CURRENT=$(docker version -f '{{.Server.Version}}' | cut -d. -f1,2)

echo "anihpj Django Deployment - Docker Version Check"
echo "Current: $CURRENT | Required: $MIN_VERSION+"

if [ "$(printf '%s\n' "$MIN_VERSION" "$CURRENT" | sort -V | head -n1)" = "$MIN_VERSION" ]; then
    echo "✓ Docker version meets requirements"
    exit 0
else
    echo "✗ Docker version too old"
    exit 1
fi
EOF

chmod +x check-docker.sh
./check-docker.sh
anihpj Django Deployment - Docker Version Check
Current: 24.0 | Required: 20.10+
✓ Docker version meets requirements

Docker version: 24.0.7 validated for:
  - User authentication service
  - JobPost management API
  - Skills verification containers
  - Author profile processors
  - Location geospatial workers
Example 3: JSON Format for Automation

Extract version data in JSON for anihpj deployment automation.

# Get JSON output
docker version --format json | python3 -m json.tool

# Extract specific fields for anihpj deployment
cat > extract-version.py <<'EOF'
import json, subprocess

result = subprocess.run(['docker', 'version', '--format', 'json'], 
                       capture_output=True, text=True)
data = json.loads(result.stdout)

print("anihpj Django Deployment Environment")
print(f"Client Version: {data['Client']['Version']}")
print(f"Server Version: {data['Server']['Version']}")
print(f"API Version: {data['Server']['ApiVersion']}")
print(f"Platform: {data['Server']['Os']}/{data['Server']['Arch']}")
EOF

python3 extract-version.py
{
  "Client": {
    "Version": "24.0.7",
    "ApiVersion": "1.43",
    "GoVersion": "go1.21.3",
    "Os": "linux",
    "Arch": "amd64"
  },
  "Server": {
    "Version": "24.0.7",
    "ApiVersion": "1.43",
    "GoVersion": "go1.21.3",
    "Os": "linux",
    "Arch": "amd64"
  }
}

anihpj Django Deployment Environment
Client Version: 24.0.7
Server Version: 24.0.7
API Version: 1.43
Platform: linux/amd64
Example 4: API Version Compatibility

Check client-server API compatibility for anihpj Django.

# Check API versions
echo "anihpj Django - API Version Compatibility Check"
CLIENT_API=$(docker version -f '{{.Client.APIVersion}}')
SERVER_API=$(docker version -f '{{.Server.APIVersion}}')

echo "Client API: $CLIENT_API"
echo "Server API: $SERVER_API"

if [ "$CLIENT_API" == "$SERVER_API" ]; then
    echo "✓ API versions match - full compatibility"
else
    echo "⚠ API versions differ - check compatibility matrix"
fi

# Show minimum API version supported
docker version -f 'Minimum API: {{.Server.MinAPIVersion}}'
anihpj Django - API Version Compatibility Check
Client API: 1.43
Server API: 1.43
✓ API versions match - full compatibility

Minimum API: 1.12

Compatible with all anihpj Django services:
  ✓ User authentication containers
  ✓ JobPost management APIs
  ✓ Skills verification services
  ✓ Author profile handlers
  ✓ Location geospatial workers
Example 5: Deployment Documentation

Document Docker version for anihpj deployment records.

# Create deployment documentation
cat > deployment-info.txt <<EOF
anihpj Django Production Deployment
Deployment Date: $(date)
Environment: Production
---
Docker Version Information:
EOF

docker version >> deployment-info.txt

echo "" >> deployment-info.txt
echo "Deployed Services:" >> deployment-info.txt
echo "- User Authentication (anihpj-auth)" >> deployment-info.txt
echo "- JobPost Management (anihpj-jobs)" >> deployment-info.txt
echo "- Skills Verification (anihpj-skills)" >> deployment-info.txt
echo "- Author Profiles (anihpj-authors)" >> deployment-info.txt
echo "- Location Services (anihpj-locations)" >> deployment-info.txt

cat deployment-info.txt
anihpj Django Production Deployment
Deployment Date: Wed Jan 15 14:30:00 UTC 2026
Environment: Production
---
Docker Version Information:
Client: Docker Engine - Community
 Version:           24.0.7
 API version:       1.43

Server: Docker Engine - Community
 Engine:
  Version:          24.0.7
  API version:      1.43 (minimum version 1.12)

Deployed Services:
- User Authentication (anihpj-auth)
- JobPost Management (anihpj-jobs)
- Skills Verification (anihpj-skills)
- Author Profiles (anihpj-authors)
- Location Services (anihpj-locations)

✓ Deployment documentation saved to deployment-info.txt

docker info

Official Docs: docker info | Docker CLI: docker info

Display system-wide Docker information.

Full Description

The docker info command provides comprehensive system-wide information about the Docker installation, including containers, images, storage drivers, plugins, networking, and resource limits. For your anihpj Django project, this command helps diagnose system issues, verify configurations, monitor resource usage, and validate deployment environments supporting User authentication services, JobPost APIs, Skills verification systems, Author profile handlers, and Location processing workers.

When troubleshooting your anihpj Django deployment, the command displays detailed system metrics including running/paused/stopped container counts, image statistics, storage driver configuration, network plugins, security options, kernel version, operating system details, CPU/memory resources, and registry configuration. This enables comprehensive diagnostics of Skills model deployment health, User authentication infrastructure status, Author profile system resources, Location processing capabilities, and JobPost management environment validation with complete system visibility.

Syntax

docker info [OPTIONS]

# Common usage
docker info                    # Show all system info
docker info --format json      # JSON output
docker info -f '{{.Containers}}' # Specific field

Parameters Description

  • --format, -f: Format output using Go template. Extract specific information fields.
  • --help: Print usage information. Display command help.

Attributes

  • System Overview: Complete Docker system status and configuration. Monitor Django deployment infrastructure.
  • Resource Metrics: Container, image, and volume statistics. Track Django resource utilization.
  • Storage Information: Storage driver and disk usage details. Optimize Django data persistence.
  • Network Configuration: Network plugins and bridge setup. Validate Django service connectivity.
  • Security Settings: Security options and registry configuration. Ensure Django deployment security.

Methods

Full System Information

Syntax: docker info

Parameters: None

Display complete Docker system information for anihpj Django environment.

JSON Format Output

Syntax: docker info --format json

Parameters: --format json

Output system information in JSON for Django monitoring automation.

Specific Field Query

Syntax: docker info -f '{{.NCPU}}'

Parameters: -f with Go template

Extract specific system metric for Django capacity planning.

Return Value

Returns comprehensive Docker system information and configuration.

  • Success (0): System information displayed successfully
  • Error (1): Cannot connect to Docker daemon or invalid format
  • Error (125): Docker daemon error or command syntax error

5 Practical Django Examples

Example 1: System Health Check

Verify Docker system health for anihpj Django deployment.

# Check complete system info
docker info

# Extract key metrics for anihpj
echo "=== anihpj Django System Health ==="
echo "Containers Running: $(docker info -f '{{.ContainersRunning}}')"
echo "Total Images: $(docker info -f '{{.Images}}')"
echo "CPUs Available: $(docker info -f '{{.NCPU}}')"
echo "Total Memory: $(docker info -f '{{.MemTotal}}')" | numfmt --to=iec
Containers: 15
 Running: 8
 Paused: 0
 Stopped: 7
Images: 24
Server Version: 24.0.7
Storage Driver: overlay2
Logging Driver: json-file
Plugins:
 Volume: local
 Network: bridge host ipvlan macvlan null overlay
CPUs: 8
Total Memory: 15.63GiB
Name: docker-host
Docker Root Dir: /var/lib/docker
Registry: https://index.docker.io/v1/

=== anihpj Django System Health ===
Containers Running: 8
Total Images: 24
CPUs Available: 8
Total Memory: 16G

✓ System healthy for anihpj Django services
Example 2: Resource Capacity Planning

Check available resources for scaling anihpj Django services.

# Create capacity report
cat > capacity-check.sh <<'EOF'
#!/bin/bash
echo "anihpj Django Capacity Planning Report"
echo "========================================"

CPUS=$(docker info -f '{{.NCPU}}')
MEM=$(docker info -f '{{.MemTotal}}')
RUNNING=$(docker info -f '{{.ContainersRunning}}')
TOTAL=$(docker info -f '{{.Containers}}')

echo "CPU Cores: $CPUS"
echo "Memory: $(echo $MEM | numfmt --to=iec)"
echo "Containers: $RUNNING running / $TOTAL total"
echo ""

# Calculate capacity
CAPACITY=$((CPUS * 2))
AVAILABLE=$((CAPACITY - RUNNING))

echo "Estimated Capacity: $CAPACITY containers"
echo "Current Usage: $RUNNING containers"
echo "Available Slots: $AVAILABLE containers"

if [ $AVAILABLE -gt 5 ]; then
    echo "✓ Sufficient capacity for anihpj scaling"
else
    echo "⚠ Limited capacity - consider resource upgrade"
fi
EOF

chmod +x capacity-check.sh
./capacity-check.sh
anihpj Django Capacity Planning Report
========================================
CPU Cores: 8
Memory: 16G
Containers: 8 running / 15 total

Estimated Capacity: 16 containers
Current Usage: 8 containers
Available Slots: 8 containers
✓ Sufficient capacity for anihpj scaling

Ready for additional services:
  - User authentication replicas
  - JobPost management workers
  - Skills verification instances
  - Author profile processors
  - Location geospatial services
Example 3: Storage Driver Validation

Verify storage configuration for anihpj Django volumes.

# Check storage configuration
echo "anihpj Django Storage Configuration"
echo "Storage Driver: $(docker info -f '{{.Driver}}')"
echo "Docker Root: $(docker info -f '{{.DockerRootDir}}')"

# Check backing filesystem
docker info | grep "Backing Filesystem"

# Verify overlay2 support (recommended for Django)
DRIVER=$(docker info -f '{{.Driver}}')
if [ "$DRIVER" == "overlay2" ]; then
    echo "✓ Using recommended overlay2 driver"
    docker info | grep -A 10 "Storage Driver"
else
    echo "⚠ Not using overlay2 - performance may be impacted"
fi
anihpj Django Storage Configuration
Storage Driver: overlay2
Docker Root: /var/lib/docker
 Backing Filesystem: extfs

✓ Using recommended overlay2 driver
Storage Driver: overlay2
 Backing Filesystem: extfs
 Supports d_type: true
 Using metacopy: false
 Native Overlay Diff: true
 userxattr: false

Configuration optimal for:
  ✓ User authentication database volumes
  ✓ JobPost management media storage
  ✓ Skills verification cache volumes
  ✓ Author profile uploads
  ✓ Location geospatial data
Example 4: Registry Configuration Check

Verify Docker registry settings for anihpj Django images.

# Check registry configuration
docker info | grep -A 5 "Registry"

# Check insecure registries
echo "Registry Configuration for anihpj:"
REGISTRY=$(docker info -f '{{.IndexServerAddress}}')
echo "Default Registry: $REGISTRY"

# Check registry mirrors
docker info --format json | python3 -c "
import json, sys
data = json.load(sys.stdin)
print('Registry Mirrors:', data.get('RegistryConfig', {}).get('Mirrors', 'None'))
print('Insecure Registries:', data.get('RegistryConfig', {}).get('InsecureRegistryCIDRs', []))
"

echo "✓ Registry configuration valid for anihpj image pulls"
Registry: https://index.docker.io/v1/
Labels:
 provider=generic
Experimental: false
Insecure Registries:
 127.0.0.0/8

Registry Configuration for anihpj:
Default Registry: https://index.docker.io/v1/

Registry Mirrors: None
Insecure Registries: ['127.0.0.0/8']

✓ Registry configuration valid for anihpj image pulls

Configured for pulling:
  - python:3.11-slim base images
  - postgres:16-alpine database
  - redis:7-alpine cache
  - nginx:alpine reverse proxy
  - Custom anihpj Django images
Example 5: Monitoring Dashboard Data

Extract metrics for anihpj Django monitoring dashboard.

# Create monitoring data export
cat > export-metrics.py <<'EOF'
import json
import subprocess
from datetime import datetime

# Get docker info in JSON
result = subprocess.run(['docker', 'info', '--format', 'json'],
                       capture_output=True, text=True)
data = json.loads(result.stdout)

# Create monitoring payload
metrics = {
    "timestamp": datetime.now().isoformat(),
    "project": "anihpj-django",
    "environment": "production",
    "docker": {
        "version": data.get("ServerVersion"),
        "containers": {
            "running": data.get("ContainersRunning"),
            "paused": data.get("ContainersPaused"),
            "stopped": data.get("ContainersStopped"),
            "total": data.get("Containers")
        },
        "images": data.get("Images"),
        "resources": {
            "cpus": data.get("NCPU"),
            "memory_gb": round(data.get("MemTotal") / 1024**3, 2)
        },
        "storage": {
            "driver": data.get("Driver"),
            "root_dir": data.get("DockerRootDir")
        }
    },
    "services": [
        "User-Authentication",
        "JobPost-Management",
        "Skills-Verification",
        "Author-Profiles",
        "Location-Services"
    ]
}

print(json.dumps(metrics, indent=2))
EOF

python3 export-metrics.py
{
  "timestamp": "2026-01-15T14:45:00.123456",
  "project": "anihpj-django",
  "environment": "production",
  "docker": {
    "version": "24.0.7",
    "containers": {
      "running": 8,
      "paused": 0,
      "stopped": 7,
      "total": 15
    },
    "images": 24,
    "resources": {
      "cpus": 8,
      "memory_gb": 15.63
    },
    "storage": {
      "driver": "overlay2",
      "root_dir": "/var/lib/docker"
    }
  },
  "services": [
    "User-Authentication",
    "JobPost-Management",
    "Skills-Verification",
    "Author-Profiles",
    "Location-Services"
  ]
}

✓ Metrics exported for anihpj monitoring dashboard

docker init

Official Docs: docker init | Docker CLI: docker init

Initialize Docker assets in a project.

Full Description

The docker init command initializes a project with Docker assets by detecting the project type and generating appropriate Dockerfile, .dockerignore, and compose.yaml files. For your anihpj Django project, this command helps bootstrap Docker configuration, create production-ready Dockerfiles, generate compose files, and establish containerization best practices for User authentication services, JobPost management APIs, Skills verification systems, Author profile handlers, and Location processing workers.

When initializing Docker support for your anihpj Django application, the command analyzes project structure, detects Django framework, creates optimized Dockerfile with multi-stage builds, generates .dockerignore for efficient context, and produces compose.yaml with service definitions. This enables rapid containerization of Skills model services, User authentication APIs, Author profile systems, Location geospatial processors, and JobPost queue workers with production-ready Docker configurations and development environment setup.

Syntax

docker init [OPTIONS]

# Common usage
docker init              # Interactive project initialization
docker init --template python  # Specify template type

Parameters Description

  • --template: Specify template type (python, go, node, etc.). Skip auto-detection.
  • --help: Print usage information. Display command help.

Attributes

  • Project Detection: Automatically detect project type and framework. Smart Django project recognition.
  • File Generation: Create Dockerfile, .dockerignore, and compose.yaml. Complete Django containerization setup.
  • Best Practices: Generate production-ready configurations. Optimized Django deployment patterns.
  • Interactive Mode: Guided setup with customization options. Tailored for Django project needs.
  • Multi-Service Support: Generate compose files with multiple services. Django with database and cache.

Methods

Interactive Initialization

Syntax: docker init

Parameters: None

Interactively initialize Docker assets for anihpj Django project.

Template-Based Init

Syntax: docker init --template python

Parameters: --template python

Initialize with Python template for Django application.

Non-Interactive Init

Syntax: docker init --template python --port 8000

Parameters: --template, --port

Initialize without prompts using predefined Django settings.

Return Value

Returns initialization status and generates Docker configuration files.

  • Success (0): Docker assets created successfully
  • Error (1): Cannot detect project type or file write error
  • Error (125): Invalid template or command syntax error

5 Practical Django Examples

Example 1: Initialize New Django Project

Bootstrap Docker configuration for new anihpj Django project.

# Create new Django project structure
mkdir anihpj-new && cd anihpj-new
mkdir -p anihpj jobpost
touch manage.py requirements.txt

# Add Django to requirements
cat > requirements.txt <<EOF
Django==4.2.7
psycopg2-binary==2.9.9
djangorestframework==3.14.0
gunicorn==21.2.0
EOF

# Initialize Docker assets
echo "Initializing Docker for anihpj Django project..."
docker init

# Answer prompts:
# - Application type: Python
# - Python version: 3.11
# - Port: 8000
# - Entry point: gunicorn anihpj.wsgi:application
Welcome to Docker Init! 🐳

This utility will guide you through creating Docker assets for your project.

? What application platform does your project use? Python
? What version of Python do you want to use? 3.11
? What port does your server listen on? 8000
? What is the command to run your app? gunicorn anihpj.wsgi:application --bind 0.0.0.0:8000

✓ Created Dockerfile
✓ Created .dockerignore
✓ Created compose.yaml
✓ Created README.Docker.md

Your Docker files are ready! 

Next steps:
  docker compose up --build

✓ anihpj Django project initialized with Docker support
Example 2: Template-Based Initialization

Use Python template for anihpj Django without interactive prompts.

# Navigate to existing Django project
cd /path/to/anihpj

# Initialize with Python template
docker init --template python

# View generated Dockerfile
echo "=== Generated Dockerfile for anihpj ==="
head -20 Dockerfile

# View compose.yaml
echo "=== Generated compose.yaml ==="
cat compose.yaml
✓ Detected Python project
✓ Creating Docker assets for anihpj Django...

Generated files:
  - Dockerfile (multi-stage build)
  - .dockerignore (optimized context)
  - compose.yaml (with PostgreSQL and Redis)
  - README.Docker.md (usage instructions)

=== Generated Dockerfile for anihpj ===
# syntax=docker/dockerfile:1
FROM python:3.11-slim as base
ENV PYTHONDONTWRITEBYTECODE=1
ENV PYTHONUNBUFFERED=1
WORKDIR /app

FROM base as builder
COPY requirements.txt .
RUN pip install --no-cache-dir -r requirements.txt

FROM base as final
COPY --from=builder /usr/local/lib/python3.11/site-packages /usr/local/lib/python3.11/site-packages
COPY . .

=== Generated compose.yaml ===
services:
  web:
    build: .
    ports:
      - "8000:8000"
    environment:
      - DATABASE_URL=postgresql://user:pass@db:5432/anihpj
    depends_on:
      - db
  db:
    image: postgres:16-alpine
Example 3: Customize Generated Files

Modify docker init output for anihpj Django production needs.

# Initialize Docker assets
cd anihpj
docker init --template python

# Customize Dockerfile for Django
cat >> Dockerfile <<'EOF'

# Collect Django static files
RUN python manage.py collectstatic --noinput

# Run migrations on startup (development only)
CMD python manage.py migrate && gunicorn anihpj.wsgi:application --bind 0.0.0.0:8000
EOF

# Enhance compose.yaml with Redis and volumes
cat >> compose.yaml <<'EOF'
  redis:
    image: redis:7-alpine
    ports:
      - "6379:6379"
  
volumes:
  postgres_data:
  redis_data:
EOF

# Update .dockerignore for Django
cat >> .dockerignore <<'EOF'
# Django specific
*.pyc
__pycache__/
db.sqlite3
.env
staticfiles/
mediafiles/
*.log
EOF

echo "✓ Docker files customized for anihpj Django production"
Customizing Docker configuration...

Enhanced Dockerfile features:
  ✓ Static file collection
  ✓ Database migrations
  ✓ Gunicorn WSGI server
  ✓ Multi-stage build optimization

Enhanced compose.yaml services:
  ✓ PostgreSQL database
  ✓ Redis cache
  ✓ Named volumes
  ✓ Network isolation

Enhanced .dockerignore:
  ✓ Python cache files
  ✓ Django SQLite database
  ✓ Environment variables
  ✓ Static/media directories
  ✓ Log files

✓ anihpj Django Docker configuration production-ready
Example 4: Multi-Service Django Application

Initialize Docker for anihpj with separate service containers.

# Initialize base configuration
cd anihpj
docker init --template python

# Create comprehensive compose file
cat > compose.yaml <<'EOF'
services:
  # User Authentication Service
  auth:
    build: .
    command: gunicorn anihpj.wsgi:application --bind 0.0.0.0:8001
    ports:
      - "8001:8001"
    environment:
      - SERVICE_NAME=auth
      - DATABASE_URL=postgresql://user:pass@db:5432/anihpj
    depends_on:
      - db
      - redis
  
  # JobPost Management API
  jobs:
    build: .
    command: gunicorn anihpj.wsgi:application --bind 0.0.0.0:8002
    ports:
      - "8002:8002"
    environment:
      - SERVICE_NAME=jobs
      - DATABASE_URL=postgresql://user:pass@db:5432/anihpj
    depends_on:
      - db
      - redis
  
  # Skills Verification Service
  skills:
    build: .
    command: python manage.py process_skills
    environment:
      - SERVICE_NAME=skills
    depends_on:
      - db
      - redis
  
  # Database
  db:
    image: postgres:16-alpine
    volumes:
      - postgres_data:/var/lib/postgresql/data
    environment:
      - POSTGRES_DB=anihpj
      - POSTGRES_USER=user
      - POSTGRES_PASSWORD=pass
  
  # Cache
  redis:
    image: redis:7-alpine
    volumes:
      - redis_data:/data

volumes:
  postgres_data:
  redis_data:
EOF

echo "✓ Multi-service compose created for anihpj"
docker compose config | grep -A 1 "services:"
✓ Multi-service compose created for anihpj

services:
  auth:
    build: .
    ports: ["8001:8001"]
  jobs:
    build: .
    ports: ["8002:8002"]
  skills:
    build: .
  db:
    image: postgres:16-alpine
  redis:
    image: redis:7-alpine

Architecture Overview:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────┐
│ anihpj Django Multi-Service Platform   │
├─────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ ✓ User Authentication (Port 8001)      │
│ ✓ JobPost Management (Port 8002)       │
│ ✓ Skills Verification (Background)     │
│ ✓ PostgreSQL Database (Port 5432)      │
│ ✓ Redis Cache (Port 6379)              │
└─────────────────────────────────────────┘

✓ Docker init complete - ready for deployment
Example 5: Development vs Production Init

Initialize Docker for different anihpj Django environments.

# Initialize base configuration
docker init --template python

# Create development override
cat > compose.dev.yaml <<'EOF'
services:
  web:
    build:
      context: .
      target: development
    volumes:
      - .:/app
      - /app/__pycache__
    environment:
      - DEBUG=True
      - DJANGO_SETTINGS_MODULE=anihpj.settings.dev
    command: python manage.py runserver 0.0.0.0:8000
  
  db:
    ports:
      - "5432:5432"  # Expose for debugging
EOF

# Create production configuration
cat > compose.prod.yaml <<'EOF'
services:
  web:
    build:
      context: .
      target: production
    environment:
      - DEBUG=False
      - DJANGO_SETTINGS_MODULE=anihpj.settings.prod
      - SECRET_KEY=${SECRET_KEY}
    restart: always
    deploy:
      replicas: 3
      resources:
        limits:
          cpus: '0.5'
          memory: 512M
EOF

# Test configurations
echo "Testing development setup..."
docker compose -f compose.yaml -f compose.dev.yaml config > /dev/null && echo "✓ Dev config valid"

echo "Testing production setup..."
docker compose -f compose.yaml -f compose.prod.yaml config > /dev/null && echo "✓ Prod config valid"
Generated environment configurations:

Development (compose.dev.yaml):
  ✓ Hot-reload enabled
  ✓ Debug mode active
  ✓ Database ports exposed
  ✓ Volume mounts for live editing
  ✓ Django development server

Production (compose.prod.yaml):
  ✓ Debug disabled
  ✓ Secret key from environment
  ✓ Auto-restart enabled
  ✓ 3 replicas for high availability
  ✓ Resource limits configured
  ✓ Gunicorn production server

Testing development setup...
✓ Dev config valid

Testing production setup...
✓ Prod config valid

Usage:
  Development: docker compose -f compose.yaml -f compose.dev.yaml up
  Production:  docker compose -f compose.yaml -f compose.prod.yaml up

✓ anihpj Django environments initialized

Desktop Commands

Docker Desktop commands provide management and control capabilities for Docker Desktop application. These commands help manage Desktop services, configure features, control lifecycle, and monitor Docker Desktop status for maintaining optimal Django development environments on Windows, macOS, and Linux workstations.

docker debug

Official Docs: docker debug | Docker CLI: docker debug

Interactive debugging environment for containers.

Full Description

The docker debug command provides an interactive debugging shell into running or stopped containers without modifying the container image. For your anihpj Django project, this enables troubleshooting production issues, inspecting container filesystems, examining process states, and debugging Django application problems in User authentication services, JobPost management workers, Skills verification APIs, Author profile handlers, and Location processing containers.

When debugging your anihpj Django containers, the command creates a privileged debugging session with access to container processes, filesystems, network interfaces, and environment variables. This enables real-time inspection of Skills model database connections, User authentication token validation, Author profile media file access, Location geospatial queries, and JobPost queue processing states with full debugging tools and utilities without requiring container restarts or image rebuilds.

Syntax

# Debug running container
docker debug {container}

# Debug with specific shell
docker debug --shell /bin/bash {container}

# Debug with custom image
docker debug --image {debug-image} {container}

# Debug Django web container
docker debug anihpj_web

# Debug with bash shell
docker debug --shell /bin/bash anihpj_db

Parameters Description

  • container: Name or ID of the container to debug. Target Django application container for troubleshooting.
  • --shell: Shell to use for debugging session (default: /bin/sh). Specify /bin/bash for Django containers with bash.
  • --image: Custom debug image with additional tools. Use images with Django debugging utilities.

Attributes

  • Interactive Shell: Full shell access to container environment. Debug Django application in real-time.
  • Process Inspection: View and analyze running processes. Monitor Django worker states and database connections.
  • Filesystem Access: Browse container filesystem without modification. Inspect Django logs, config files, and media uploads.
  • Network Debugging: Test network connectivity and DNS resolution. Troubleshoot Django API connections and external services.
  • Non-invasive: Debugging session doesn't modify container. Safe Django production troubleshooting.

Methods

Debug Running Container

Syntax: docker debug {container}

Parameters: container (string) - Django container name or ID

Start interactive debugging session in running Django container with default shell.

Debug with Specific Shell

Syntax: docker debug --shell {shell} {container}

Parameters: shell (string) - Shell path, container (string) - Django container

Debug Django container using specified shell for enhanced command availability.

Debug with Custom Tools

Syntax: docker debug --image {image} {container}

Parameters: image (string) - Debug image with tools, container (string) - Django container

Debug Django container using custom image with additional debugging utilities.

Return Value

Returns interactive shell session in container with debugging capabilities.

  • Success (0): Debug session started successfully with shell access
  • Error (1): Container not found or Docker Desktop not running
  • Error (125): Invalid parameters or insufficient permissions

5 Practical Django Examples

Example 1: Debug Django Web Container

Troubleshoot Skills model API issues in production container.

# Start debug session
docker debug anihpj_web

# Inside debug shell - check Django logs
cat /app/logs/django.log | tail -50

# Check Skills model database connections
ps aux | grep python

# Test database connectivity
python manage.py dbshell
Starting debug session for container: anihpj_web
Debug shell: /bin/sh

# Django application logs
[2026-01-15 10:30:45] ERROR Skills API: Database connection timeout
[2026-01-15 10:31:12] INFO Skills query: SELECT * FROM jobpost_skills
[2026-01-15 10:32:05] ERROR SkillsViewSet: PostgreSQL connection lost

# Python processes
root  125  0.5  2.1  python manage.py runserver
root  147  0.2  1.8  /usr/bin/python /app/manage.py

✓ Debug session active
⚠ Database connection issues detected
Example 2: Debug User Authentication Service

Inspect User model authentication failures.

# Debug with bash shell
docker debug --shell /bin/bash anihpj_auth

# Check environment variables
env | grep DJANGO

# Inspect User model migrations
ls -la /app/jobpost/migrations/

# Test authentication token generation
python manage.py shell
Debug session: anihpj_auth (bash)

# Environment variables
DJANGO_SETTINGS_MODULE=anihpj.settings
DJANGO_SECRET_KEY=***hidden***
DJANGO_DEBUG=False
DJANGO_ALLOWED_HOSTS=*

# Migration files
-rw-r--r-- 1 root root  2341 Jan 10 __init__.py
-rw-r--r-- 1 root root  5678 Jan 12 0001_initial.py
-rw-r--r-- 1 root root  3456 Jan 14 0002_user_auth_fields.py

✓ Migrations present
✓ Environment configured correctly
Example 3: Debug JobPost Worker Crashes

Investigate JobPost queue processing failures.

# Debug crashed worker container
docker debug anihpj_worker

# Check for core dumps
ls -la /tmp/core*

# Examine worker logs
tail -100 /var/log/celery/worker.log

# Check memory usage
free -h
df -h
Debugging: anihpj_worker

# No core dumps found
ls: cannot access '/tmp/core*': No such file or directory

# Worker logs
[2026-01-15 10:25:00] INFO JobPost worker started
[2026-01-15 10:26:15] ERROR Memory limit exceeded
[2026-01-15 10:26:16] CRITICAL Worker terminated: OOM

# Memory status
              total        used        free
Mem:          2.0Gi       1.9Gi       100Mi
Swap:         1.0Gi       1.0Gi         0Bi

# Disk usage
Filesystem      Size  Used Avail Use%
/dev/sda1        50G   48G   2.0G  96%

⚠ Worker killed by OOM
⚠ Disk nearly full
Example 4: Debug Network Connectivity

Test Django container network connections to external services.

# Debug network issues
docker debug anihpj_web

# Test PostgreSQL connectivity
ping anihpj_db
nc -zv anihpj_db 5432

# Test Redis connection
nc -zv anihpj_redis 6379

# Check DNS resolution
nslookup anihpj_db
cat /etc/resolv.conf
Debug session: anihpj_web

# PostgreSQL connectivity
PING anihpj_db (172.18.0.3): 56 data bytes
64 bytes from 172.18.0.3: icmp_seq=0 ttl=64 time=0.123 ms

Connection to anihpj_db 5432 port [tcp/postgresql] succeeded!

# Redis connectivity
Connection to anihpj_redis 6379 port [tcp/redis] succeeded!

# DNS resolution
Server:    127.0.0.11
Address:   127.0.0.11:53

Name:      anihpj_db
Address:   172.18.0.3

✓ Network connectivity verified
✓ All services reachable
Example 5: Debug Author Profile Media Files

Inspect Author model media upload permissions and storage.

# Debug media storage
docker debug anihpj_web

# Check media directory permissions
ls -la /app/media/authors/

# Check disk space for uploads
du -sh /app/media/*

# Verify file ownership
stat /app/media/authors/profile_123.jpg

# Test write permissions
touch /app/media/test_upload.txt
Debugging: anihpj_web

# Author media files
drwxr-xr-x  5 www-data www-data  4096 Jan 15 10:00 .
drwxr-xr-x  8 root     root      4096 Jan 10 12:00 ..
-rw-r--r--  1 www-data www-data 45678 Jan 12 profile_123.jpg
-rw-r--r--  1 www-data www-data 67890 Jan 14 profile_456.jpg

# Storage usage
2.3G    /app/media/authors
850M    /app/media/jobposts
450M    /app/media/skills

# File details
  File: /app/media/authors/profile_123.jpg
  Size: 45678     Blocks: 90   IO Block: 4096
Access: 0644/-rw-r--r--  Uid: (  33/www-data)   Gid: (  33/www-data)

✓ Permissions correct
✓ Write test successful

docker desktop

Official Docs: docker desktop | Docker CLI: docker desktop

Manage Docker Desktop application and services.

Full Description

The docker desktop command provides a management interface for Docker Desktop application including lifecycle control, feature configuration, engine management, Kubernetes operations, and status monitoring. For your anihpj Django project, these commands help manage your local development environment, control Docker Desktop services, configure development features, and ensure optimal performance for User authentication, JobPost management, Skills verification, Author profiles, and Location services.

When managing your anihpj Django Desktop environment, the command family includes start/stop/restart for lifecycle control, enable/disable for feature management, engine commands for version switching, kubernetes commands for cluster management, logs for diagnostics, status for health checks, update for version management, and version for information display. This enables comprehensive control of Skills model development environments, User authentication testing setups, Author profile staging configurations, Location service debugging platforms, and JobPost worker development workflows.

Syntax

docker desktop COMMAND

# Available subcommands
docker desktop disable         # Disable features
docker desktop enable          # Enable features
docker desktop engine          # Manage engine versions
docker desktop kubernetes      # Manage Kubernetes
docker desktop logs            # View Desktop logs
docker desktop restart         # Restart Desktop
docker desktop start           # Start Desktop
docker desktop status          # Check Desktop status
docker desktop stop            # Stop Desktop
docker desktop update          # Update Desktop
docker desktop version         # Show Desktop version

Parameters Description

  • COMMAND: Subcommand for Desktop operations (start, stop, restart, status, logs, version, update, enable, disable, engine, kubernetes).

Attributes

  • Lifecycle Management: Start, stop, restart Docker Desktop. Control Django development environment state.
  • Feature Control: Enable or disable Desktop features. Configure Django development capabilities.
  • Engine Management: Switch between Docker engine versions. Test Django compatibility across versions.
  • Kubernetes Integration: Manage local Kubernetes cluster. Test Django deployments on Kubernetes.
  • Status Monitoring: Check Desktop health and readiness. Ensure Django development environment availability.

Methods

Lifecycle Control

Syntax: docker desktop {start|stop|restart}

Parameters: None

Control Docker Desktop application state for Django development environment.

Status Check

Syntax: docker desktop status

Parameters: None

Verify Docker Desktop is running and ready for Django operations.

Feature Management

Syntax: docker desktop {enable|disable} {feature}

Parameters: feature (string) - Feature name to enable or disable

Configure Docker Desktop features for Django development needs.

Return Value

Returns command execution status and subcommand information.

  • Success (0): Desktop command executed successfully
  • Error (1): Invalid subcommand or Docker Desktop not installed
  • Error (125): Docker Desktop unavailable or permission denied

5 Practical Django Examples

Example 1: Start Django Development Environment

Start Docker Desktop for anihpj project development.

# Check Docker Desktop status
docker desktop status

# Start Docker Desktop
docker desktop start

# Wait for readiness
docker desktop status

# Verify Django containers can be created
docker ps
# Status before start
Docker Desktop is not running

# Starting Desktop
Starting Docker Desktop...

# Status after start
Docker Desktop is running
Engine: Docker Engine 24.0.7
Status: Ready

# Container status
CONTAINER ID   IMAGE     COMMAND   CREATED   STATUS    PORTS     NAMES

✓ Docker Desktop ready
✓ Ready for Django development
Example 2: Restart Desktop After Configuration

Restart Desktop to apply Django environment changes.

# Restart Docker Desktop
docker desktop restart

# Monitor restart progress
docker desktop status
Restarting Docker Desktop...
Stopping services...
Starting services...

Docker Desktop is running
Engine: Docker Engine 24.0.7
Status: Ready

✓ Desktop restarted successfully
✓ All Django services available
Example 3: Check Desktop Version

Verify Docker Desktop version for Django compatibility.

# Show Desktop version
docker desktop version

# Show engine version
docker version --format '{{.Server.Version}}'
Docker Desktop version: 4.26.1
Engine version: 24.0.7
Compose version: 2.23.0
Kubernetes: v1.29.1 (enabled)

✓ Desktop version: 4.26.1
✓ Compatible with Django development
Example 4: View Desktop Logs

Troubleshoot Docker Desktop issues affecting Django.

# View recent logs
docker desktop logs --tail 50

# Follow logs in real-time
docker desktop logs --follow
[2026-01-15 10:30:00] INFO Engine started successfully
[2026-01-15 10:30:15] INFO Kubernetes cluster ready
[2026-01-15 10:30:30] INFO Resource usage: CPU 15%, Memory 2.1GB
[2026-01-15 10:31:00] INFO File sharing enabled
[2026-01-15 10:31:15] INFO Network proxy configured
[2026-01-15 10:32:00] INFO All services operational

✓ Desktop healthy
✓ Ready for Django operations
Example 5: Stop Desktop When Not Developing

Stop Desktop to free system resources.

# Stop all Django containers first
docker-compose -f docker-compose.yml down

# Stop Docker Desktop
docker desktop stop

# Verify stopped
docker desktop status
# Stopping Django services
Stopping anihpj_web ... done
Stopping anihpj_db ... done
Stopping anihpj_redis ... done
Removing containers...

# Stopping Desktop
Stopping Docker Desktop...
All services stopped

# Status check
Docker Desktop is not running

✓ Desktop stopped successfully
✓ System resources freed

docker desktop disable

Official Docs: docker desktop disable | Docker CLI: docker desktop disable

Disable Docker Desktop features and extensions.

Full Description

The docker desktop disable command disables specific Docker Desktop features, extensions, and capabilities without stopping the entire application. For your anihpj Django project, this allows selective feature management, resource optimization, and development environment customization by disabling unused features while maintaining core Django container operations for User authentication, JobPost management, Skills verification, Author profiles, and Location services.

When disabling features in your anihpj Django Desktop environment, the command affects specific capabilities like model-runner for AI workloads, experimental features, or resource-intensive extensions. This enables optimization of Skills model development performance, User authentication testing efficiency, Author profile debugging resources, Location service memory usage, and JobPost worker processing capacity by removing unnecessary features and freeing system resources for core Django operations.

Syntax

docker desktop disable FEATURE

# Disable model runner
docker desktop disable model-runner

# Disable experimental features
docker desktop disable experimental

Parameters Description

  • FEATURE: Name of the feature to disable (model-runner, experimental). Specify Django-relevant feature for optimization.

Attributes

  • Selective Control: Disable specific features without stopping Desktop. Maintain Django development while optimizing resources.
  • Resource Optimization: Free CPU and memory by disabling unused features. Improve Django container performance.
  • Configuration Management: Customize Desktop capabilities per project needs. Tailor environment for Django workflows.
  • Non-destructive: Features can be re-enabled anytime. Safe Django environment experimentation.
  • Immediate Effect: Changes apply without Desktop restart. Quick Django environment adjustments.

Methods

Disable Single Feature

Syntax: docker desktop disable {feature}

Parameters: feature (string) - Feature name to disable

Disable specific Docker Desktop feature to optimize Django development environment.

Check Feature Status

Syntax: docker desktop status

Parameters: None

Verify which features are enabled or disabled for Django environment.

Re-enable Feature

Syntax: docker desktop enable {feature}

Parameters: feature (string) - Feature name to enable

Re-enable previously disabled feature when needed for Django operations.

Return Value

Returns confirmation of feature disable operation.

  • Success (0): Feature disabled successfully
  • Error (1): Unknown feature name or already disabled
  • Error (125): Docker Desktop not running or insufficient permissions

5 Practical Django Examples

Example 1: Disable AI Model Runner

Free resources by disabling model-runner for Django development.

# Disable model runner
docker desktop disable model-runner

# Verify status
docker desktop status
Disabling model-runner...
Model runner disabled successfully

Docker Desktop Status:
Engine: Running
Kubernetes: Enabled
Model Runner: Disabled

✓ Model runner disabled
✓ Resources freed for Django
Example 2: Optimize for Django Development

Disable unused features to improve Skills model performance.

# Check current features
docker desktop status

# Disable model runner
docker desktop disable model-runner

# Monitor resource improvement
docker stats --no-stream
Current Features:
- Engine: Enabled
- Kubernetes: Enabled
- Model Runner: Enabled (consuming 1.2GB RAM)

Disabling model-runner...
Feature disabled

CONTAINER       CPU %    MEM USAGE / LIMIT
anihpj_web      2.5%     256MB / 2GB
anihpj_db       1.2%     512MB / 2GB
anihpj_worker   3.1%     384MB / 2GB

✓ 1.2GB RAM freed
✓ Django containers running smoothly
Example 3: Disable During Testing

Disable features for User authentication test suite performance.

# Before tests - disable extras
docker desktop disable model-runner

# Run Django tests
docker-compose exec web python manage.py test jobpost.tests

# Re-enable after tests
docker desktop enable model-runner
Model runner disabled

Running tests...
Creating test database...
..................................................
----------------------------------------------------------------------
Ran 50 tests in 12.3s

OK (skipped=2)
Destroying test database...

Model runner re-enabled

✓ Tests completed faster
✓ Environment restored
Example 4: Troubleshoot Resource Issues

Disable features to isolate JobPost worker memory problems.

# System under memory pressure
free -h

# Disable non-essential features
docker desktop disable model-runner

# Check improvement
free -h
# Before disable
              total        used        free
Mem:           8Gi        7.8Gi       200Mi
Swap:          2Gi        1.5Gi       500Mi

Disabling model-runner...
Feature disabled

# After disable
              total        used        free
Mem:           8Gi        6.6Gi       1.4Gi
Swap:          2Gi        500Mi       1.5Gi

✓ 1.2GB memory freed
✓ Django workers stable
Example 5: Production-like Environment

Disable development features for staging environment testing.

# Configure for production-like setup
docker desktop disable model-runner

# Verify lean configuration
docker desktop status

# Deploy Django staging
docker-compose -f docker-compose.staging.yml up -d
Disabling model-runner...
Feature disabled successfully

Desktop Status:
Engine: Running (production mode)
Kubernetes: Disabled
Model Runner: Disabled
Resource Usage: Minimal

Creating network "anihpj-staging"...
Creating anihpj_staging_db...
Creating anihpj_staging_redis...
Creating anihpj_staging_web...

✓ Production-like environment
✓ All staging services running

docker desktop disable model-runner

Official Docs: docker desktop disable model-runner | Docker CLI: docker desktop disable model-runner

Disable Docker Desktop AI model runner feature.

Full Description

The docker desktop disable model-runner command specifically disables the AI model runner feature in Docker Desktop, freeing significant system resources (CPU, memory, GPU) for other operations. For your anihpj Django project, this optimizes development environment performance by reclaiming resources for Django containers running User authentication services, JobPost queue processing, Skills model database operations, Author profile API endpoints, and Location-based query handlers.

When disabling model-runner for your anihpj Django environment, the command stops AI/ML workload capabilities, releases GPU memory, reduces CPU usage, and frees RAM previously allocated to model inference services. This enables better performance for Skills model testing with large datasets, User authentication load testing, Author profile image processing, Location geospatial calculations, and JobPost bulk operations by dedicating system resources exclusively to Django application needs rather than AI features.

Syntax

# Disable model runner
docker desktop disable model-runner

# Verify disabled
docker desktop status | grep -i "model"

Parameters Description

  • No parameters required: Command is self-contained. Disables model-runner feature immediately.

Attributes

  • Resource Liberation: Frees 1-2GB RAM and GPU resources. Allocate to Django containers instead.
  • Performance Boost: Reduces CPU overhead from model services. Speed up Django operations.
  • Immediate Effect: Takes effect without Desktop restart. Quick Django optimization.
  • Reversible: Can be re-enabled anytime with enable command. Flexible Django development.
  • No Data Loss: Disabling preserves all configurations. Safe Django environment change.

Methods

Disable Model Runner

Syntax: docker desktop disable model-runner

Parameters: None

Disable AI model runner to optimize Django development resources.

Verify Disabled Status

Syntax: docker desktop status

Parameters: None

Confirm model-runner is disabled and resources freed for Django.

Re-enable If Needed

Syntax: docker desktop enable model-runner

Parameters: None

Re-enable model runner if AI features needed for Django ML tasks.

Return Value

Returns confirmation of model-runner disable operation.

  • Success (0): Model runner disabled successfully with resource metrics
  • Error (1): Model runner already disabled or not available
  • Error (125): Docker Desktop not running or feature not supported

5 Practical Django Examples

Example 1: Free RAM for Django Database

Disable model-runner to allocate more memory to PostgreSQL.

# Check current memory usage
docker stats --no-stream anihpj_db

# Disable model runner
docker desktop disable model-runner

# Increase database memory limit
docker-compose down
# Edit docker-compose.yml: db mem_limit: 4g
docker-compose up -d

# Verify improved performance
docker stats --no-stream anihpj_db
# Before disable
CONTAINER    CPU %    MEM USAGE / LIMIT     MEM %
anihpj_db    45%      1.8GB / 2GB           90%

Disabling model-runner...
Model runner disabled (freed 1.5GB)

# After with increased limit
CONTAINER    CPU %    MEM USAGE / LIMIT     MEM %
anihpj_db    25%      2.2GB / 4GB           55%

✓ Database performance improved
✓ Query times reduced by 40%
Example 2: Optimize for Skills Model Testing

Disable model-runner during intensive Skills API load tests.

# Prepare for load testing
docker desktop disable model-runner

# Run Skills model load test
ab -n 10000 -c 100 http://localhost:8000/api/skills/

# Monitor resources
docker stats
Model runner disabled

Apache Bench Results:
Requests per second:    850.23 [#/sec]
Time per request:       117.6 [ms]
Failed requests:        0
Total transferred:      8.5 MB

CONTAINER       CPU %    MEM %
anihpj_web      85%      65%
anihpj_db       55%      70%
anihpj_redis    15%      25%

✓ 850 req/sec (up from 620)
✓ Zero failed requests
Example 3: Fix JobPost Worker OOM

Resolve JobPost worker out-of-memory by disabling model-runner.

# Worker keeps crashing
docker logs anihpj_worker --tail 20

# Check system memory
free -h

# Disable model runner
docker desktop disable model-runner

# Restart worker
docker-compose restart worker

# Monitor stability
docker logs -f anihpj_worker
# Worker logs before
[ERROR] Memory limit exceeded
[CRITICAL] Worker killed by OOM
[INFO] Attempting restart...

# System memory (before)
Mem:  8Gi  7.9Gi  100Mi

Model runner disabled (freed 1.5GB)

# System memory (after)
Mem:  8Gi  6.4Gi  1.6Gi

# Worker logs after restart
[INFO] JobPost worker started
[INFO] Processing queue: 150 jobs
[INFO] Memory usage: 512MB / 2GB (25%)
[INFO] All jobs completed successfully

✓ Worker stable
✓ No more OOM kills
Example 4: Speed Up CI/CD Pipeline

Disable model-runner in CI environment for faster Django tests.

#!/bin/bash
# CI pipeline script

# Optimize Docker Desktop
docker desktop disable model-runner

# Build Django images faster
time docker-compose build

# Run test suite
docker-compose run --rm web python manage.py test

# Deploy if tests pass
docker-compose up -d
Model runner disabled

Building images...
web: Build time: 2m 15s (was 3m 45s)
worker: Build time: 1m 50s (was 2m 40s)

Running tests...
..................................................
Ran 50 tests in 8.2s (was 12.5s)
OK

Deploying...
All services started

✓ Build time: 30% faster
✓ Test time: 34% faster
✓ Total pipeline: 4m faster
Example 5: Laptop Battery Conservation

Disable model-runner for mobile Django development.

# Check battery status
pmset -g batt

# Disable resource-intensive features
docker desktop disable model-runner

# Monitor power consumption
sudo powermetrics --samplers cpu_power -n 1

# Develop Django on battery
docker-compose up anihpj_web anihpj_db
# Battery before
100% charged; 3h 15m remaining

Model runner disabled

# Power consumption
CPU Power: 8.5W (was 15.2W)
GPU Power: 2.1W (was 8.5W)
Total: 10.6W (was 23.7W)

# Battery after
100% charged; 5h 45m remaining

Starting Django services...
anihpj_web running
anihpj_db running

✓ Battery life: +2h 30m
✓ 55% less power draw
✓ Django development on battery viable

docker desktop enable

Official Docs: docker desktop enable | Docker CLI: docker desktop enable

Enable Docker Desktop features and extensions.

Full Description

The docker desktop enable command activates specific Docker Desktop features, extensions, and capabilities that were previously disabled. For your anihpj Django project, this allows enabling additional functionality, AI workloads, experimental features, and development tools needed for specific Django operations in User authentication, JobPost management, Skills verification, Author profiles, and Location services.

When enabling features in your anihpj Django Desktop environment, the command activates capabilities like model-runner for AI/ML workflows, experimental features for testing, or additional extensions for enhanced development. This supports Skills model machine learning integration, User authentication biometric features, Author profile AI recommendations, Location service geospatial enhancements, and JobPost matching algorithms by providing necessary AI infrastructure and experimental capabilities for advanced Django features.

Syntax

docker desktop enable FEATURE

# Enable model runner
docker desktop enable model-runner

# Enable experimental features
docker desktop enable experimental

Parameters Description

  • FEATURE: Name of the feature to enable (model-runner, experimental). Specify Django-relevant feature for activation.

Attributes

  • Feature Activation: Enable specific Desktop capabilities. Add Django development functionality.
  • AI/ML Support: Enable model-runner for ML workloads. Support Django AI features.
  • Experimental Access: Enable beta features for testing. Try new Django capabilities early.
  • Configuration Flexibility: Enable features as needed per workflow. Customize Django environment dynamically.
  • Immediate Activation: Features available right away. Quick Django capability enhancement.

Methods

Enable Single Feature

Syntax: docker desktop enable {feature}

Parameters: feature (string) - Feature name to enable

Activate specific Docker Desktop feature for Django development needs.

Verify Feature Enabled

Syntax: docker desktop status

Parameters: None

Confirm feature is enabled and operational for Django environment.

Disable Feature Later

Syntax: docker desktop disable {feature}

Parameters: feature (string) - Feature name to disable

Deactivate feature when no longer needed for Django operations.

Return Value

Returns confirmation of feature enable operation.

  • Success (0): Feature enabled successfully
  • Error (1): Unknown feature name or already enabled
  • Error (125): Docker Desktop not running or insufficient permissions

5 Practical Django Examples

Example 1: Enable AI Model Runner for Skills

Enable model-runner for Skills model ML recommendations.

# Enable model runner
docker desktop enable model-runner

# Verify enabled
docker desktop status

# Deploy ML model for Skills matching
docker run -d --name skills-ml-model \
  -v $(pwd)/models:/models \
  -p 5000:5000 \
  anihpj/skills-recommender:latest
Enabling model-runner...
Model runner enabled successfully
Allocating GPU resources...

Docker Desktop Status:
Engine: Running
Kubernetes: Enabled
Model Runner: Enabled (GPU: Tesla T4)

Deploying Skills ML model...
Model loaded: skills_recommender_v2.pkl
Inference API: http://localhost:5000/predict

✓ Model runner active
✓ Skills ML recommendations ready
Example 2: Enable for JobPost Matching

Enable AI features for JobPost candidate matching.

# Enable model runner for AI matching
docker desktop enable model-runner

# Start JobPost matching service
docker-compose -f docker-compose.ml.yml up -d

# Test matching API
curl -X POST http://localhost:8000/api/jobpost/match/ \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"skills": ["Python", "Django", "PostgreSQL"]}'
Model runner enabled

Starting ML services...
jobpost-matcher: Started
skills-encoder: Started
candidate-ranker: Started

JobPost Matching Results:
{
  "matches": [
    {"job_id": 123, "score": 0.95, "title": "Senior Django Developer"},
    {"job_id": 456, "score": 0.87, "title": "Python Backend Engineer"},
    {"job_id": 789, "score": 0.82, "title": "Full Stack Developer"}
  ],
  "processing_time": "45ms"
}

✓ AI matching operational
✓ 3 job matches found
Example 3: Enable Experimental Features

Test new Docker features for Django development.

# Enable experimental features
docker desktop enable experimental

# Check available experimental features
docker info | grep -i experimental

# Use new build features for Django
docker buildx create --use --name django-builder
docker buildx build --platform linux/amd64,linux/arm64 \
  -t anihpj/web:multiarch .
Experimental features enabled

Experimental: true
BuildKit: enabled
Multi-platform builds: available

Creating builder instance...
django-builder created and set as current

Building multi-platform Django image...
[linux/amd64] Building... ████████████████ 100%
[linux/arm64] Building... ████████████████ 100%

Successfully built anihpj/web:multiarch

✓ Experimental features active
✓ Multi-platform build successful
Example 4: Re-enable After Optimization

Re-enable model-runner after performance testing.

# Performance tests completed
# Re-enable AI features

docker desktop enable model-runner

# Restore full Django ML stack
docker-compose -f docker-compose.full.yml up -d

# Verify all services
docker-compose ps
Model runner re-enabled

Starting full Django stack...
anihpj_web: Running
anihpj_db: Running
anihpj_redis: Running
anihpj_worker: Running
skills_ml_model: Running
jobpost_matcher: Running
author_recommender: Running

Service Status:
       Name              State    Ports
anihpj_web           Up      0.0.0.0:8000->8000/tcp
anihpj_db            Up      5432/tcp
skills_ml_model      Up      0.0.0.0:5000->5000/tcp
jobpost_matcher      Up      0.0.0.0:5001->5001/tcp

✓ Full stack operational
✓ All ML services available
Example 5: Enable for Author Profile AI

Enable model-runner for Author profile recommendations.

# Enable AI capabilities
docker desktop enable model-runner

# Deploy Author recommendation model
docker run -d --name author-recommender \
  --gpus all \
  -v $(pwd)/models/author:/models \
  -e MODEL_PATH=/models/recommender.h5 \
  anihpj/author-ai:latest

# Test recommendations
docker exec author-recommender python test_recommend.py
Model runner enabled
GPU allocation: NVIDIA GTX 1080 Ti

Deploying Author AI model...
Loading model: recommender.h5 (125MB)
Initializing inference engine...
Warming up GPU...

Testing Author recommendations:
Input: User preferences, reading history
Output: Top 10 recommended authors

Recommendations generated:
  1. John Doe (confidence: 0.94)
  2. Jane Smith (confidence: 0.89)
  3. Bob Johnson (confidence: 0.85)
  ...

Inference time: 12ms per request

✓ Author AI operational
✓ GPU-accelerated inference ready

docker desktop enable model-runner

Official Docs: docker desktop enable model-runner | Docker CLI: docker desktop enable model-runner

Enable Docker Desktop AI model runner feature.

Full Description

The docker desktop enable model-runner command activates the AI model runner feature in Docker Desktop, providing GPU-accelerated machine learning inference capabilities. For your anihpj Django project, this enables AI/ML features including Skills model recommendation engines, User authentication biometric verification, JobPost intelligent matching, Author profile content recommendations, and Location-based predictive analytics within containerized Django applications.

When enabling model-runner for your anihpj Django environment, the command allocates GPU resources, initializes inference engines, configures ML frameworks, and establishes model serving infrastructure. This supports Skills similarity scoring with neural networks, User behavior prediction with deep learning, Author content classification with NLP models, Location demand forecasting with time series analysis, and JobPost candidate matching with transformer models by providing production-ready AI infrastructure integrated with Django backend services.

Syntax

# Enable model runner
docker desktop enable model-runner

# Verify enabled with GPU info
docker desktop status
nvidia-smi  # If GPU available

Parameters Description

  • No parameters required: Command is self-contained. Enables model-runner with automatic GPU detection.

Attributes

  • GPU Acceleration: Automatic NVIDIA GPU detection and allocation. Fast Django ML inference.
  • ML Framework Support: TensorFlow, PyTorch, ONNX compatibility. Flexible Django AI integration.
  • Model Serving: Built-in inference API endpoints. Easy Django ML deployment.
  • Resource Management: Intelligent GPU memory allocation. Efficient Django AI workloads.
  • Production Ready: Scalable inference with load balancing. Enterprise Django ML capability.

Methods

Enable Model Runner

Syntax: docker desktop enable model-runner

Parameters: None

Activate AI model runner for Django ML workloads with GPU support.

Verify GPU Available

Syntax: docker run --rm --gpus all nvidia/cuda:11.8.0-base nvidia-smi

Parameters: None

Test GPU accessibility from Django containers with model-runner enabled.

Deploy ML Model

Syntax: docker run --gpus all -v /models:/models ml-image

Parameters: Model volume mount and GPU specification

Run Django ML models with GPU acceleration via model-runner.

Return Value

Returns confirmation of model-runner enable with GPU information.

  • Success (0): Model runner enabled with GPU details
  • Error (1): Model runner already enabled or GPU unavailable
  • Error (125): Docker Desktop not running or GPU drivers missing

5 Practical Django Examples

Example 1: Enable for Skills ML Pipeline

Enable model-runner for Skills recommendation system.

# Enable model runner
docker desktop enable model-runner

# Verify GPU available
docker run --rm --gpus all nvidia/cuda:11.8.0-base nvidia-smi

# Deploy Skills ML model
docker run -d --name skills-ml \
  --gpus all \
  -v $(pwd)/models/skills:/models \
  -e MODEL_FILE=skills_bert_v2.onnx \
  -p 5000:5000 \
  anihpj/skills-inference:latest
Model runner enabled successfully
GPU: NVIDIA RTX 3090 (24GB)
CUDA: 11.8
cuDNN: 8.6

GPU Status:
+-----------------------------------------------------------------------------+
| NVIDIA-SMI 525.60       Driver Version: 525.60       CUDA Version: 11.8   |
|-------------------------------+----------------------+----------------------+
| GPU  Name        Persistence-M| Bus-Id        Disp.A | Volatile Uncorr. ECC |
|   0  NVIDIA RTX 3090    Off  | 00000000:01:00.0 Off |                  N/A |
+-------------------------------+----------------------+----------------------+

Deploying Skills ML model...
Loading ONNX model: skills_bert_v2.onnx (512MB)
Initializing GPU inference...
Model warmup: 10 iterations

Skills ML API ready:
POST http://localhost:5000/predict
Throughput: 450 inferences/sec
Latency: 2.2ms avg

✓ Model runner active
✓ GPU-accelerated Skills recommendations
Example 2: JobPost Semantic Matching

Enable AI for JobPost-candidate semantic matching.

# Enable model runner
docker desktop enable model-runner

# Start JobPost matching stack
docker-compose -f docker-compose.ml.yml up -d

# Test semantic matching
curl -X POST http://localhost:8000/api/jobpost/semantic-match/ \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{
    "job_description": "Senior Django developer with 5+ years experience",
    "candidate_resume": "Full-stack developer, 6 years Python/Django..."
  }'
Model runner enabled (GPU: Tesla T4)

Starting ML services...
jobpost-encoder: Loading sentence-transformers/all-MiniLM-L6-v2
candidate-encoder: Model loaded (GPU memory: 1.2GB)
similarity-scorer: Ready

Semantic Matching Result:
{
  "similarity_score": 0.87,
  "match_quality": "Excellent",
  "key_matches": [
    {"skill": "Django", "score": 0.95},
    {"skill": "Python", "score": 0.92},
    {"skill": "Experience", "score": 0.85}
  ],
  "recommendation": "Strong candidate match",
  "inference_time_ms": 15
}

✓ Semantic matching operational
✓ 87% candidate-job match
Example 3: Author Content Classification

Enable NLP models for Author profile content analysis.

# Enable model runner
docker desktop enable model-runner

# Deploy Author NLP model
docker run -d --name author-nlp \
  --gpus all \
  -v $(pwd)/models/nlp:/models \
  -e TRANSFORMERS_CACHE=/models/cache \
  -p 5001:5001 \
  anihpj/author-nlp:bert-base

# Analyze Author content
docker exec author-nlp python analyze_content.py \
  --input "/app/data/author_profiles.json" \
  --output "/app/results/classifications.json"
Model runner enabled

Deploying Author NLP model...
Loading: bert-base-uncased (440MB)
GPU memory allocated: 2.1GB
Batch size: 32

Analyzing 500 Author profiles...
████████████████████████████████ 100% (500/500)

Classification Results:
- Technical Writing: 187 authors (37.4%)
- Creative Fiction: 156 authors (31.2%)
- Business Content: 89 authors (17.8%)
- Academic Research: 68 authors (13.6%)

Processing stats:
Total time: 45 seconds
Throughput: 11.1 profiles/sec
GPU utilization: 78% avg

Results saved: classifications.json

✓ NLP classification complete
✓ 500 authors categorized
Example 4: Location Demand Forecasting

Enable time series ML for Location-based job demand prediction.

# Enable model runner
docker desktop enable model-runner

# Deploy forecasting model
docker run -d --name location-forecast \
  --gpus all \
  -v $(pwd)/models/forecast:/models \
  -v $(pwd)/data:/data \
  anihpj/location-lstm:latest

# Generate predictions
docker exec location-forecast python forecast.py \
  --location "San Francisco" \
  --horizon 30 \
  --output /data/forecasts/sf_30day.json
Model runner enabled (GPU: RTX 2080)

Loading LSTM forecasting model...
Model: location_demand_lstm_v3.h5 (89MB)
Historical data: 730 days
Feature engineering complete

Forecasting job demand for San Francisco (30 days):
████████████████████████ 100%

Predictions:
Week 1: 1,245 jobs (±85) - Trend: ↑ 5.2%
Week 2: 1,310 jobs (±92) - Trend: ↑ 5.2%
Week 3: 1,378 jobs (±95) - Trend: ↑ 5.2%
Week 4: 1,450 jobs (±98) - Trend: ↑ 5.2%

High-demand skills forecast:
1. Python (↑ 8.3%)
2. Django (↑ 7.1%)
3. PostgreSQL (↑ 6.5%)

Model confidence: 87.3%
Forecast saved: sf_30day.json

✓ 30-day forecast generated
✓ High confidence predictions
Example 5: Real-time Skills Similarity

Enable model-runner for real-time Skills similarity API.

# Enable model runner
docker desktop enable model-runner

# Deploy Skills similarity service
docker-compose -f docker-compose.skills-ml.yml up -d

# Test real-time similarity
for skill in "Python" "Django" "Flask" "FastAPI"; do
  curl -s "http://localhost:5000/similarity?skill=$skill" | jq
done

Starting Skills ML service...
Loading GloVe embeddings (300d)...

Index built: 5,000 skills

Endpoint: http://localhost:5000/similarity
Model: Word2Vec + Cosine similarity
Throughput: 2,000 queries/sec
