{
  "similar_skills": [
    {"skill": "Django", "score": 0.92},
    {"skill": "Flask", "score": 0.88},
    {"skill": "FastAPI", "score": 0.85},
    {"skill": "Pandas", "score": 0.82},
    {"skill": "NumPy", "score": 0.79}
  ],
  "query_time_ms": 1.2
}

Query: Django
{
  "similar_skills": [
    {"skill": "Python", "score": 0.92},
    {"skill": "Flask", "score": 0.87},
    {"skill": "PostgreSQL", "score": 0.84},
    {"skill": "REST API", "score": 0.81}
  ]
}

✓ Real-time similarity operational
✓ Sub-2ms response times

docker desktop engine

Official Docs: docker desktop engine | Docker CLI: docker desktop engine

Manage Docker engine versions in Desktop.

Full Description

The docker desktop engine command provides management capabilities for Docker engine versions within Docker Desktop, allowing listing available engines and switching between versions. For your anihpj Django project, this enables testing Django applications across different Docker engine versions, ensuring compatibility, debugging engine-specific issues, and validating deployments before production updates for User authentication, JobPost management, Skills verification, Author profiles, and Location services.

When managing engine versions for your anihpj Django environment, the command family includes ls for listing available engine versions and use for switching to specific versions. This supports Skills model container compatibility testing, User authentication security patch validation, Author profile image format compatibility checks, Location service network feature testing, and JobPost worker engine performance comparisons by providing controlled engine version management for comprehensive Django deployment validation across Docker engine releases.

Syntax

docker desktop engine COMMAND

# Available subcommands
docker desktop engine ls     # List available engines
docker desktop engine use    # Switch to specific engine

Parameters Description

  • COMMAND: Subcommand for engine operations (ls, use). Manage Docker engine versions.

Attributes

  • Version Management: List and switch between Docker engine versions. Test Django across engines.
  • Compatibility Testing: Validate Django apps on different engines. Ensure production readiness.
  • Quick Switching: Change engines without Desktop reinstall. Fast Django compatibility checks.
  • Version Information: View engine versions and features. Plan Django deployment upgrades.
  • Non-destructive: Switching preserves containers and images. Safe Django testing environment.

Methods

List Available Engines

Syntax: docker desktop engine ls

Parameters: None

Display all available Docker engine versions for Django testing.

Switch Engine Version

Syntax: docker desktop engine use {version}

Parameters: version (string) - Engine version to activate

Change to specific Docker engine version for Django compatibility testing.

Check Current Engine

Syntax: docker version --format '{{.Server.Version}}'

Parameters: None

Verify currently active Docker engine version for Django environment.

Return Value

Returns engine management command results and version information.

  • Success (0): Engine command executed with version details
  • Error (1): Invalid subcommand or engine version unavailable
  • Error (125): Docker Desktop not running or permission denied

5 Practical Django Examples

Example 1: Check Available Engines

List Docker engine versions for Django compatibility testing.

# List available engines
docker desktop engine ls

# Show current engine
docker version --format '{{.Server.Version}}'
Available Docker Engines:
  24.0.7 (current)
  24.0.6
  23.0.6
  23.0.5

Current engine: 24.0.7
API version: 1.43
Build: afdd53b

✓ 4 engine versions available
✓ Running latest stable: 24.0.7
Example 2: Test Django on Different Engines

Validate anihpj Django app across engine versions.

# Test on current engine (24.0.7)
docker-compose up -d
docker-compose exec web python manage.py test

# Switch to older engine
docker desktop engine use 23.0.6

# Test again on older engine
docker-compose up -d
docker-compose exec web python manage.py test
# Engine 24.0.7 test results
Creating test database...
..................................................
Ran 50 tests in 12.3s
OK

Switching to engine 23.0.6...
Engine switched successfully
Desktop restarted

# Engine 23.0.6 test results
Creating test database...
..................................................
Ran 50 tests in 12.8s
OK

✓ Django compatible with both engines
✓ All tests pass on 23.0.6 and 24.0.7
Example 3: Validate New Engine Before Upgrade

Test Skills model on new Docker engine before production update.

# Check current production engine
docker version --format '{{.Server.Version}}'

# List available newer engines
docker desktop engine ls

# Switch to newer engine for testing
docker desktop engine use 24.0.7

# Run Skills model integration tests
docker-compose run --rm web python manage.py test jobpost.tests.test_skills
Current production engine: 23.0.6

Available engines:
  24.0.7
  24.0.6
  23.0.6 (current)
  23.0.5

Switching to 24.0.7 for validation...
Engine switched: 24.0.7

Running Skills model tests...
test_skills_creation ... ok
test_skills_validation ... ok
test_skills_api_endpoints ... ok
test_skills_search ... ok
test_skills_recommendations ... ok

----------------------------------------------------------------------
Ran 15 tests in 8.2s
OK

✓ Skills model works on 24.0.7
✓ Safe to upgrade production
Example 4: Debug Engine-Specific Issue

Isolate JobPost worker issue to specific engine version.

# Issue reported on engine 24.0.6
docker desktop engine use 24.0.6

# Reproduce issue
docker-compose up -d worker
docker logs -f anihpj_worker

# Switch to 24.0.7 to verify fix
docker desktop engine use 24.0.7
docker-compose up -d worker
docker logs -f anihpj_worker
Testing on engine 24.0.6...

Worker logs (24.0.6):
[ERROR] Network timeout on job queue
[ERROR] Redis connection lost
[ERROR] Worker crashed

Issue reproduced on 24.0.6

Switching to engine 24.0.7...
Engine switched

Worker logs (24.0.7):
[INFO] JobPost worker started
[INFO] Connected to Redis
[INFO] Processing queue: 50 jobs
[INFO] All jobs completed

✓ Issue isolated to engine 24.0.6
✓ Fixed in engine 24.0.7
✓ Recommendation: Upgrade to 24.0.7
Example 5: Rollback After Problem

Switch back to previous engine if Django issues occur.

# New engine causing issues
docker version

# List engines
docker desktop engine ls

# Rollback to previous stable
docker desktop engine use 23.0.6

# Verify Django works
docker-compose up -d
curl http://localhost:8000/health/
Current engine: 24.0.7 (experiencing issues)

Available engines:
  24.0.7 (current)
  24.0.6
  23.0.6
  23.0.5

Rolling back to 23.0.6...
Engine switched successfully
Desktop restarted

Starting Django services...
anihpj_web: Running
anihpj_db: Running
anihpj_redis: Running

Health check:
{
  "status": "healthy",
  "engine": "23.0.6",
  "services": {
    "web": "up",
    "db": "up",
    "redis": "up"
  }
}

✓ Rollback successful
✓ Django stable on 23.0.6

docker desktop engine ls

Official Docs: docker desktop engine ls | Docker CLI: docker desktop engine ls

List available Docker engine versions.

Full Description

The docker desktop engine ls command lists all Docker engine versions available in Docker Desktop, showing version numbers, release dates, and indicating the currently active engine. For your anihpj Django project, this helps identify available engine versions for compatibility testing, security patch verification, performance comparison, and upgrade planning for User authentication services, JobPost processing workers, Skills verification APIs, Author profile handlers, and Location query engines.

When listing engines for your anihpj Django environment, the command displays version information including major/minor/patch numbers, API versions, build identifiers, and active status indicators. This supports Skills model engine compatibility matrices, User authentication security compliance checking, Author profile feature availability verification, Location service network capability assessment, and JobPost worker performance benchmarking by providing comprehensive engine version information for informed Django deployment decisions across development, staging, and production environments.

Syntax

# List all available engines
docker desktop engine ls

# With formatted output
docker desktop engine ls --format table

Parameters Description

  • --format: Output format (table, json). Customize engine list display for Django CI/CD.

Attributes

  • Version Listing: Display all available engine versions. Plan Django compatibility testing.
  • Current Indicator: Shows which engine is active. Verify Django deployment engine.
  • Version Details: API version and build information. Assess Django feature availability.
  • Release Information: Engine release dates and status. Track Django security updates.
  • Quick Reference: Fast version lookup without switching. Efficient Django planning.

Methods

List All Engines

Syntax: docker desktop engine ls

Parameters: None

Display complete list of available Docker engines for Django testing.

Formatted Output

Syntax: docker desktop engine ls --format json

Parameters: None

Get engine list in JSON format for Django automation scripts.

Check Current Active

Syntax: docker desktop engine ls | grep current

Parameters: None

Quickly identify active engine version for Django environment.

Return Value

Returns table of available engine versions with status indicators.

  • Success (0): Engine list displayed successfully
  • Error (1): Unable to retrieve engine list
  • Error (125): Docker Desktop not running or service unavailable

5 Practical Django Examples

Example 1: View Available Engines

List all Docker engines for Django testing strategy.

# List available engines
docker desktop engine ls
VERSION    API     BUILD     STATUS
24.0.7     1.43    afdd53b   current
24.0.6     1.43    ed223bc   available
24.0.5     1.43    ced0996   available
23.0.6     1.42    ef23cbc   available
23.0.5     1.42    bc4487a   available
23.0.4     1.42    f31b7a8   available

✓ 6 engine versions available
✓ Currently on: 24.0.7
✓ API compatibility: 1.42-1.43
Example 2: Check for Security Updates

Identify newer engines with Django security patches.

# List engines
docker desktop engine ls

# Check current version
CURRENT=$(docker version --format '{{.Server.Version}}')
echo "Current: $CURRENT"

# Identify newer versions
docker desktop engine ls | awk -v curr="$CURRENT" \
  'NR>1 && $1>curr {print "Newer: " $1}'
VERSION    API     BUILD     STATUS
24.0.7     1.43    afdd53b   available
24.0.6     1.43    ed223bc   current
24.0.5     1.43    ced0996   available
23.0.6     1.42    ef23cbc   available

Current: 24.0.6

Newer: 24.0.7

Security advisory:
- 24.0.7 includes CVE-2024-1234 fix
- Affects container networking
- Impacts Django API communication

✓ Update available: 24.0.7
⚠ Security patch recommended
Example 3: CI/CD Engine Matrix

Test Django on all available engines automatically.

#!/bin/bash
# Test Django on all engines

# Get engine list
ENGINES=$(docker desktop engine ls | awk 'NR>1 {print $1}')

# Test each engine
for engine in $ENGINES; do
  echo "Testing on engine: $engine"
  
  docker desktop engine use $engine
  docker-compose up -d
  docker-compose exec -T web python manage.py test
  
  if [ $? -eq 0 ]; then
    echo "✓ $engine: PASS"
  else
    echo "✗ $engine: FAIL"
  fi
  
  docker-compose down
done
Available engines:
24.0.7
24.0.6
24.0.5
23.0.6

Testing on engine: 24.0.7
Running Django tests...
..................................................
Ran 50 tests in 12.3s
✓ 24.0.7: PASS

Testing on engine: 24.0.6
Running Django tests...
..................................................
Ran 50 tests in 12.5s
✓ 24.0.6: PASS

Testing on engine: 24.0.5
Running Django tests...
..................................................
Ran 50 tests in 12.8s
✓ 24.0.5: PASS

Testing on engine: 23.0.6
Running Django tests...
..................................................
Ran 50 tests in 13.1s
✓ 23.0.6: PASS

Test Summary:
✓ All 4 engines compatible
✓ Django works on 23.0.6 through 24.0.7
Example 4: JSON Output for Automation

Parse engine list for Django deployment automation.

# Get engines in JSON
docker desktop engine ls --format json > engines.json

# Parse with jq
cat engines.json | jq '.[] | select(.status == "current")'

# Find latest available
cat engines.json | jq 'sort_by(.version) | reverse | .[0]'
Engine list saved: engines.json

Current engine:
{
  "version": "24.0.6",
  "api_version": "1.43",
  "build": "ed223bc",
  "status": "current",
  "release_date": "2024-01-10"
}

Latest available:
{
  "version": "24.0.7",
  "api_version": "1.43",
  "build": "afdd53b",
  "status": "available",
  "release_date": "2024-01-14",
  "security_fixes": ["CVE-2024-1234"]
}

✓ JSON parsed successfully
✓ Automation data ready
Example 5: Document Django Environment

Record engine versions for Django deployment documentation.

# Generate engine report
cat << 'EOF' > engine_report.sh
#!/bin/bash
echo "=== Django Environment Engine Report ==="
echo "Generated: $(date)"
echo ""
echo "Available Engines:"
docker desktop engine ls
echo ""
echo "Current Engine Details:"
docker version --format 'Version: {{.Server.Version}}'
docker version --format 'API: {{.Server.APIVersion}}'
docker version --format 'OS/Arch: {{.Server.Os}}/{{.Server.Arch}}'
EOF

chmod +x engine_report.sh
./engine_report.sh
=== Django Environment Engine Report ===
Generated: Wed Jan 15 10:30:00 UTC 2026

Available Engines:
VERSION    API     BUILD     STATUS
24.0.7     1.43    afdd53b   available
24.0.6     1.43    ed223bc   current
24.0.5     1.43    ced0996   available
23.0.6     1.42    ef23cbc   available

Current Engine Details:
Version: 24.0.6
API: 1.43
OS/Arch: linux/amd64

Django Compatibility:
✓ Supports Docker Compose 2.23+
✓ BuildKit enabled
✓ Multi-platform builds available
✓ Recommended for Django production

Report saved: engine_report.txt

docker desktop engine use

Official Docs: docker desktop engine use | Docker CLI: docker desktop engine use

Switch to a specific Docker engine version.

Full Description

The docker desktop engine use command switches the active Docker engine to a specified version, allowing testing and validation across different engine releases. For your anihpj Django project, this enables compatibility testing before production upgrades, reproducing engine-specific bugs, validating security patches, and ensuring Django applications work correctly across engine versions for User authentication, JobPost management, Skills verification, Author profiles, and Location services.

When switching engines for your anihpj Django environment, the command stops the current engine, activates the specified version, restarts Docker Desktop services, and preserves all containers, images, and volumes. This supports Skills model compatibility validation across engines, User authentication security patch testing, Author profile image build verification, Location service network feature checks, and JobPost worker performance comparisons by providing seamless engine version switching without data loss or complex reconfiguration.

Syntax

# Switch to specific engine version
docker desktop engine use {version}

# Example: Switch to 24.0.7
docker desktop engine use 24.0.7

# Example: Switch to 23.0.6
docker desktop engine use 23.0.6

Parameters Description

  • version: Docker engine version to activate (e.g., 24.0.7, 23.0.6). Must be available in engine list.

Attributes

  • Version Switching: Change active Docker engine version. Test Django across releases.
  • Data Preservation: Maintains containers, images, volumes. Safe Django testing.
  • Desktop Restart: Automatically restarts Desktop with new engine. Seamless Django transition.
  • Rollback Capability: Switch back to previous version anytime. Flexible Django testing.
  • Compatibility Validation: Verify Django works on target engine. Ensure production readiness.

Methods

Switch to Specific Version

Syntax: docker desktop engine use {version}

Parameters: version (string) - Target engine version

Activate specified Docker engine version for Django testing.

Verify Switch Success

Syntax: docker version --format '{{.Server.Version}}'

Parameters: None

Confirm engine switch completed successfully for Django environment.

Rollback if Needed

Syntax: docker desktop engine use {previous-version}

Parameters: previous-version (string) - Previous engine version

Revert to previous engine if Django issues occur.

Return Value

Returns confirmation of engine switch with restart notification.

  • Success (0): Engine switched successfully, Desktop restarted
  • Error (1): Invalid engine version or version not available
  • Error (125): Docker Desktop not running or switch operation failed

5 Practical Django Examples

Example 1: Upgrade to Latest Engine

Switch to latest Docker engine for Django development.

# Check current engine
docker version --format '{{.Server.Version}}'

# Switch to latest
docker desktop engine use 24.0.7

# Verify switch
docker version --format '{{.Server.Version}}'

# Test Django
docker-compose up -d
curl http://localhost:8000/health/
Current engine: 24.0.6

Switching to engine 24.0.7...
Stopping Docker Desktop...
Switching engine...
Starting Docker Desktop with 24.0.7...

Engine switch successful
Desktop restarted

New engine: 24.0.7

Starting Django services...
anihpj_web: Running
anihpj_db: Running

Health check:
{"status": "healthy", "engine": "24.0.7"}

✓ Upgraded to 24.0.7
✓ Django operational
Example 2: Reproduce Bug on Older Engine

Switch to specific engine to reproduce reported issue.

# Bug reported on 24.0.5
docker desktop engine use 24.0.5

# Wait for restart
sleep 10

# Reproduce issue
docker-compose up -d worker
docker logs anihpj_worker | grep ERROR
Switching to engine 24.0.5 for bug reproduction...
Engine switch in progress...
Desktop restarted with 24.0.5

Starting JobPost worker...
Worker container created

Worker logs:
[ERROR] Network timeout connecting to Redis
[ERROR] Failed to acquire job lock
[ERROR] Worker crashed: Redis connection refused

Bug reproduced on 24.0.5
Issue confirmed:
- Redis networking problem
- Specific to engine 24.0.5
- Fixed in 24.0.6+

✓ Bug reproduction successful
✓ Root cause identified
Example 3: Security Patch Validation

Test Django security patch on new engine version.

# Current engine has CVE
docker version --format 'Engine: {{.Server.Version}}'

# Switch to patched engine
docker desktop engine use 24.0.7

# Run security tests
docker-compose run --rm web python manage.py test \
  jobpost.tests.test_security

# Scan for vulnerabilities
docker scan anihpj/web:latest
Engine: 24.0.6 (CVE-2024-1234 present)

Switching to patched engine 24.0.7...
Engine switched successfully

Running Django security tests...
test_sql_injection_protection ... ok
test_xss_prevention ... ok
test_csrf_token_validation ... ok
test_authentication_bypass ... ok
test_file_upload_security ... ok

----------------------------------------------------------------------
Ran 15 security tests in 5.2s
OK

Scanning image for vulnerabilities...
✓ No HIGH or CRITICAL vulnerabilities
✓ CVE-2024-1234: FIXED in 24.0.7

Security validation: PASSED
✓ Safe to deploy on 24.0.7
Example 4: Performance Comparison

Compare Django performance across engine versions.

#!/bin/bash
# Performance test script

engines=("24.0.7" "24.0.6" "23.0.6")

for engine in "${engines[@]}"; do
  echo "Testing engine: $engine"
  
  # Switch engine
  docker desktop engine use $engine
  sleep 15
  
  # Start Django
  docker-compose up -d
  sleep 10
  
  # Run load test
  ab -n 1000 -c 50 http://localhost:8000/api/skills/ \
    | grep "Requests per second" \
    | awk -v eng="$engine" '{print eng ": " $4 " req/s"}'
  
  docker-compose down
done
Performance Comparison Results:

Testing engine: 24.0.7
Switching to 24.0.7...
Starting Django services...
Running load test...
24.0.7: 845.23 req/s

Testing engine: 24.0.6
Switching to 24.0.6...
Starting Django services...
Running load test...
24.0.6: 832.15 req/s

Testing engine: 23.0.6
Switching to 23.0.6...
Starting Django services...
Running load test...
23.0.6: 798.47 req/s

Performance Summary:
✓ 24.0.7: Best performance (+5.8% vs 23.0.6)
✓ 24.0.6: Good performance (+4.2% vs 23.0.6)
✓ 23.0.6: Baseline

Recommendation: Upgrade to 24.0.7
Example 5: Rollback After Failed Upgrade

Revert to stable engine if new version causes issues.

# Upgraded to 24.0.7, encountering issues
docker version

# Check Django errors
docker-compose logs web | tail -20

# Rollback to previous stable
docker desktop engine use 24.0.6

# Restart Django
docker-compose restart

# Verify stability
docker-compose ps
Current engine: 24.0.7

Django errors detected:
[ERROR] Database connection pool exhausted
[ERROR] Redis timeout errors
[ERROR] Worker tasks failing

Rolling back to 24.0.6...
Switching engine...
Desktop restarted with 24.0.6

Restarting Django services...
anihpj_web: Restarted
anihpj_db: Restarted
anihpj_redis: Restarted
anihpj_worker: Restarted

Service Status:
NAME              STATE     HEALTH
anihpj_web        Up        healthy
anihpj_db         Up        healthy
anihpj_redis      Up        healthy
anihpj_worker     Up        healthy

✓ Rollback successful
✓ All services stable on 24.0.6
✓ Will investigate 24.0.7 issues

docker desktop kubernetes

Official Docs: docker desktop kubernetes | Docker CLI: docker desktop kubernetes

Manage Kubernetes integration in Docker Desktop.

Full Description

The docker desktop kubernetes command manages Kubernetes integration within Docker Desktop, providing local Kubernetes cluster capabilities for container orchestration testing. For your anihpj Django project, this enables Kubernetes deployment testing, microservices orchestration, scaling validation, and production-readiness checks for User authentication services, JobPost processing workers, Skills verification APIs, Author profile handlers, and Location query engines in Kubernetes environments.

When managing Kubernetes for your anihpj Django environment, the command family includes images for managing Kubernetes system images and configuration for cluster settings. This supports Skills model pod deployment testing, User authentication service mesh configuration, Author profile horizontal pod autoscaling, Location service load balancing validation, and JobPost worker StatefulSet management by providing local Kubernetes environment that mirrors production orchestration scenarios for comprehensive Django cloud-native deployment preparation.

Syntax

docker desktop kubernetes COMMAND

# Available subcommands
docker desktop kubernetes images    # Manage Kubernetes images

Parameters Description

  • COMMAND: Subcommand for Kubernetes operations (images). Manage Kubernetes cluster components.

Attributes

  • Local Kubernetes: Single-node cluster in Docker Desktop. Test Django on Kubernetes locally.
  • Production Parity: Matches production Kubernetes behavior. Validate Django deployments.
  • Resource Management: Control cluster resource allocation. Optimize Django pod limits.
  • Image Operations: Manage Kubernetes system images. Maintain Django cluster components.
  • Integration Testing: Test Django microservices orchestration. Ensure production readiness.

Methods

Manage K8s Images

Syntax: docker desktop kubernetes images

Parameters: None

View and manage Kubernetes system images for Django cluster.

Enable/Disable K8s

Syntax: Via Docker Desktop Settings UI

Parameters: None

Toggle Kubernetes cluster for Django development needs.

Check K8s Status

Syntax: kubectl cluster-info

Parameters: None

Verify Kubernetes cluster health for Django deployments.

Return Value

Returns Kubernetes command results and cluster information.

  • Success (0): Kubernetes command executed successfully
  • Error (1): Kubernetes not enabled or command failed
  • Error (125): Docker Desktop not running or Kubernetes unavailable

5 Practical Django Examples

Example 1: Deploy Django on Kubernetes

Test anihpj Django app on local Kubernetes cluster.

# Verify Kubernetes enabled
kubectl cluster-info

# Create Django deployment
kubectl create deployment anihpj-web \
  --image=anihpj/web:latest \
  --replicas=3

# Expose service
kubectl expose deployment anihpj-web \
  --type=LoadBalancer \
  --port=8000

# Check pods
kubectl get pods
Kubernetes master is running at https://kubernetes.docker.internal:6443

Creating Django deployment...
deployment.apps/anihpj-web created

Exposing service...
service/anihpj-web exposed

Pod Status:
NAME                          READY   STATUS    AGE
anihpj-web-7d9f8c4b5-8x2k9   1/1     Running   15s
anihpj-web-7d9f8c4b5-m5n7p   1/1     Running   15s
anihpj-web-7d9f8c4b5-q3r4s   1/1     Running   15s

Service:
NAME         TYPE           CLUSTER-IP      EXTERNAL-IP   PORT(S)
anihpj-web   LoadBalancer   10.96.155.243   localhost     8000:30123/TCP

✓ Django deployed on Kubernetes
✓ 3 replicas running
✓ Service accessible: http://localhost:8000
Example 2: Skills Model Horizontal Scaling

Test Skills API autoscaling on Kubernetes.

# Deploy Skills API
kubectl create deployment skills-api \
  --image=anihpj/skills-api:latest

# Configure autoscaling
kubectl autoscale deployment skills-api \
  --cpu-percent=50 \
  --min=2 \
  --max=10

# Generate load
kubectl run -it --rm load-generator \
  --image=busybox --restart=Never \
  -- /bin/sh -c "while true; do wget -q -O- http://skills-api:8000/api/skills/; done"

# Watch scaling
kubectl get hpa -w
Skills API deployment created

Horizontal Pod Autoscaler configured:
- Min pods: 2
- Max pods: 10
- Target CPU: 50%

Generating load...

HPA Status (real-time):
NAME         REFERENCE             TARGETS    MINPODS   MAXPODS   REPLICAS
skills-api   Deployment/skills-api 15%/50%    2         10        2
skills-api   Deployment/skills-api 68%/50%    2         10        4
skills-api   Deployment/skills-api 52%/50%    2         10        6
skills-api   Deployment/skills-api 48%/50%    2         10        6

Load test complete
Final state:
- Replicas: 6 pods
- CPU: 48% (under threshold)
- Response time: 45ms avg

✓ Autoscaling functional
✓ Skills API scales automatically
Example 3: JobPost StatefulSet Deployment

Deploy JobPost worker with persistent storage.

# Create StatefulSet manifest
cat << 'EOF' > jobpost-worker.yaml
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: StatefulSet
metadata:
  name: jobpost-worker
spec:
  serviceName: jobpost-worker
  replicas: 3
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: jobpost-worker
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: jobpost-worker
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: worker
        image: anihpj/worker:latest
        volumeMounts:
        - name: worker-data
          mountPath: /data
  volumeClaimTemplates:
  - metadata:
      name: worker-data
    spec:
      accessModes: [ "ReadWriteOnce" ]
      resources:
        requests:
          storage: 5Gi
EOF

# Deploy StatefulSet
kubectl apply -f jobpost-worker.yaml

# Check status
kubectl get statefulset
kubectl get pvc
StatefulSet manifest created

Deploying JobPost workers...
statefulset.apps/jobpost-worker created

StatefulSet Status:
NAME             READY   AGE
jobpost-worker   3/3     2m15s

Pods:
NAME               READY   STATUS    VOLUMES
jobpost-worker-0   1/1     Running   worker-data-jobpost-worker-0
jobpost-worker-1   1/1     Running   worker-data-jobpost-worker-1
jobpost-worker-2   1/1     Running   worker-data-jobpost-worker-2

Persistent Volume Claims:
NAME                              STATUS   CAPACITY
worker-data-jobpost-worker-0      Bound    5Gi
worker-data-jobpost-worker-1      Bound    5Gi
worker-data-jobpost-worker-2      Bound    5Gi

✓ StatefulSet deployed
✓ Each worker has persistent storage
✓ Job queue data preserved
Example 4: Django Microservices Architecture

Deploy full Django microservices on Kubernetes.

kubectl apply -f k8s/

# - Location Service

kubectl get pods -l app=anihpj

# Check services
kubectl get svc

Example 5: Test Kubernetes Images

Manage Kubernetes system images for Django cluster.

# List Kubernetes images
docker desktop kubernetes images

# Check image versions
docker images | grep k8s

kubectl get nodes
kubectl get componentstatuses

docker desktop kubernetes images

Official Docs: docker desktop kubernetes images | Docker CLI: docker desktop kubernetes images

Manage Kubernetes system images in Docker Desktop.

Full Description

The docker desktop kubernetes images command manages Kubernetes system images required for the local Kubernetes cluster in Docker Desktop, including kube-apiserver, kube-controller-manager, kube-scheduler, etcd, and coredns. For your anihpj Django project, this ensures Kubernetes cluster health for testing Django deployments, managing system image updates for User authentication microservices, maintaining JobPost worker orchestration infrastructure, validating Skills API Kubernetes compatibility, and supporting Author profile and Location service container orchestration environments.

When managing Kubernetes images for your anihpj Django environment, the command displays Kubernetes system images with versions, sizes, and IDs for cluster maintenance and troubleshooting. This supports Skills model pod deployment infrastructure validation, User authentication service mesh component verification, Author profile horizontal autoscaling system checks, Location service load balancer image management, and JobPost worker StatefulSet infrastructure maintenance by providing visibility into Kubernetes cluster images that power Django container orchestration for production-ready cloud-native deployments.

Syntax

docker desktop kubernetes images

# No additional options available

Parameters Description

  • No Parameters: Command has no parameters. Lists all Kubernetes system images.

Attributes

  • System Image Visibility: Shows all K8s component images. Verify Django cluster infrastructure.
  • Version Information: Displays image versions and tags. Track Django K8s compatibility.
  • Cluster Health: Validates required images present. Ensure Django deployments work.
  • Storage Management: Shows image sizes for capacity planning. Optimize Django K8s resources.
  • Troubleshooting Support: Identifies missing or outdated images. Fix Django K8s issues.

Methods

List All K8s Images

Syntax: docker desktop kubernetes images

Parameters: None

Display all Kubernetes system images for Django cluster.

Verify Cluster Images

Syntax: docker desktop kubernetes images | grep kube

Parameters: None

Filter Kubernetes control plane images for Django infrastructure.

Check Image Versions

Syntax: docker desktop kubernetes images | awk '{print $1":"$2}'

Parameters: None

Extract image:tag pairs for Django K8s version tracking.

Return Value

Returns list of Kubernetes system images with details.

  • Success (0): Image list displayed successfully
  • Error (1): Kubernetes not enabled or images unavailable
  • Error (125): Docker Desktop not running

5 Practical Django Examples

Example 1: Verify K8s Cluster Images

Check all Kubernetes images before Django deployment.

# List Kubernetes images
docker desktop kubernetes images

# Verify core components
docker desktop kubernetes images | grep -E "apiserver|controller|scheduler"

# Check DNS component
docker desktop kubernetes images | grep coredns
Kubernetes System Images:

REPOSITORY                           TAG       IMAGE ID       SIZE
k8s.gcr.io/kube-apiserver            v1.29.1   a4c8e8e5f9d1   127MB
k8s.gcr.io/kube-controller-manager   v1.29.1   b5d9f0f6a2e2   119MB
k8s.gcr.io/kube-scheduler            v1.29.1   c6e0a1b7c3f3   58.5MB
k8s.gcr.io/kube-proxy                v1.29.1   d7f1b2c8d4g4   73.2MB
k8s.gcr.io/pause                     3.9       e8g2c3d9e5h5   514KB
k8s.gcr.io/etcd                      3.5.10    f9h3d4e0f6i6   181MB
k8s.gcr.io/coredns                   v1.11.1   g0i4e5f1g7j7   51.9MB

Core Components:
✓ kube-apiserver v1.29.1 present
✓ kube-controller-manager v1.29.1 present
✓ kube-scheduler v1.29.1 present

DNS Component:
✓ coredns v1.11.1 present

Cluster Status: All images present
✓ Ready for Django deployments
Example 2: Track K8s Version Updates

Monitor Kubernetes version for Django compatibility.

# Extract K8s version
k8s_version=$(docker desktop kubernetes images | \
  grep kube-apiserver | \
  awk '{print $2}')

echo "Kubernetes Version: $k8s_version"

# Check Django compatibility
echo "Checking Django compatibility with $k8s_version"

# Verify minimum version requirements
echo "Minimum required: v1.28.0"
echo "Current version: $k8s_version"
Kubernetes Version: v1.29.1

Checking Django compatibility with v1.29.1

Django anihpj requirements:
- Minimum K8s: v1.28.0
- Current K8s: v1.29.1

Compatibility Check:
✓ Version meets minimum requirements
✓ All Django features supported
✓ StatefulSet API: Available
✓ HorizontalPodAutoscaler: Available
✓ Service mesh ready: Yes

Deployment Status: COMPATIBLE

Django services supported:
✓ User authentication microservice
✓ JobPost worker orchestration
✓ Skills API scaling
✓ Author profile deployments
✓ Location service load balancing
Example 3: Troubleshoot Missing Images

Diagnose Django K8s deployment failures due to images.

# Django deployment failing, check images
kubectl get pods

# List K8s images
docker desktop kubernetes images

# Check if all required images present
required_images=(
  "kube-apiserver"
  "kube-controller-manager"
  "kube-scheduler"
  "etcd"
  "coredns"
)

for img in "${required_images[@]}"; do
  if docker desktop kubernetes images | grep -q "$img"; then
    echo "✓ $img found"
  else
    echo "✗ $img MISSING"
  fi
done
Django Pod Status:
NAME                         READY   STATUS            RESTARTS
anihpj-web-5d6f7g8h-9i0j1   0/1     ImagePullBackOff  3

Investigating Kubernetes cluster...

Checking required images:
✓ kube-apiserver found
✓ kube-controller-manager found
✓ kube-scheduler found
✓ etcd found
✗ coredns MISSING

Problem Identified:
- coredns image missing
- DNS resolution failing
- Django services can't resolve names

Solution:
1. Restart Docker Desktop
2. Let K8s re-download images
3. Verify images present

After restart:
docker desktop kubernetes images | grep coredns
coredns v1.11.1 present

✓ DNS image restored
✓ Django pods starting successfully
Example 4: Calculate K8s Image Storage

Determine storage used by Kubernetes infrastructure.

# List images with sizes
docker desktop kubernetes images

# Calculate total K8s image size
total_size=$(docker desktop kubernetes images | \
  awk 'NR>1 {sum+=$NF} END {print sum}')

echo "Total K8s Image Size: $total_size MB"

# Check available disk space
df -h /var/lib/docker

# Calculate space for Django images
echo "Space remaining for Django images"
Kubernetes Image Storage Analysis:

Image Sizes:
kube-apiserver:           127.0 MB
kube-controller-manager:  119.0 MB
kube-scheduler:           58.5 MB
kube-proxy:               73.2 MB
pause:                    0.5 MB
etcd:                     181.0 MB
coredns:                  51.9 MB

Total K8s Image Size: 611.1 MB

Disk Space:
Filesystem      Size   Used   Avail   Use%
/dev/sda1       100G   35G    65G     35%

Space Analysis:
✓ K8s images: 611 MB
✓ Django images: 2.3 GB
✓ Total used: 2.9 GB
✓ Available: 65 GB

Storage Status: Sufficient
✓ Plenty of space for Django development
✓ Can deploy 20+ Django services
✓ Room for image version testing
Example 5: Document Django K8s Environment

Generate Kubernetes infrastructure report for Django project.

#!/bin/bash
# Generate K8s environment report

cat << 'EOF' > k8s-environment-report.md
# Django anihpj Kubernetes Environment

## Cluster Information
EOF

# Add K8s version
echo "**Kubernetes Version:** $(docker desktop kubernetes images | \
  grep kube-apiserver | awk '{print $2}')" >> k8s-environment-report.md

# Add component versions
echo -e "\n## System Components\n" >> k8s-environment-report.md
docker desktop kubernetes images | \
  awk 'NR>1 {print "- " $1 ": " $2}' >> k8s-environment-report.md

# Add storage info
echo -e "\n## Storage\n" >> k8s-environment-report.md
total=$(docker desktop kubernetes images | \
  awk 'NR>1 {sum+=$NF} END {print sum}')
echo "Total K8s image storage: ${total} MB" >> k8s-environment-report.md

cat k8s-environment-report.md
Generating Kubernetes environment report...

# Django anihpj Kubernetes Environment

## Cluster Information
**Kubernetes Version:** v1.29.1

## System Components
- k8s.gcr.io/kube-apiserver: v1.29.1
- k8s.gcr.io/kube-controller-manager: v1.29.1
- k8s.gcr.io/kube-scheduler: v1.29.1
- k8s.gcr.io/kube-proxy: v1.29.1
- k8s.gcr.io/pause: 3.9
- k8s.gcr.io/etcd: 3.5.10
- k8s.gcr.io/coredns: v1.11.1

## Storage
Total K8s image storage: 611 MB

## Django Services
- User Authentication: Ready for deployment
- JobPost Workers: StatefulSet compatible
- Skills API: HPA ready
- Author Profiles: Service mesh ready
- Location Services: Load balancer ready

## Status
✓ All components present
✓ Cluster healthy
✓ Ready for Django production testing

Report saved: k8s-environment-report.md

docker desktop logs

Official Docs: docker desktop logs | Docker CLI: docker desktop logs

View Docker Desktop application logs.

Full Description

The docker desktop logs command displays Docker Desktop application logs, including engine events, daemon operations, networking changes, volume management, and Desktop service activities. For your anihpj Django project, this enables troubleshooting Docker Desktop issues affecting User authentication services, diagnosing JobPost worker connection problems, investigating Skills API networking failures, debugging Author profile volume mount errors, and resolving Location service engine communication issues by providing comprehensive Desktop application logging.

When reviewing Desktop logs for your anihpj Django environment, the command shows engine startup events, service lifecycle operations, network configuration changes, volume operations, and Desktop feature activities with timestamps. This supports Skills model container creation debugging, User authentication network policy troubleshooting, Author profile image pull failure diagnosis, Location service DNS resolution issue investigation, and JobPost worker resource allocation problem identification by providing detailed Desktop operational logs that reveal Django infrastructure issues at the Docker Desktop level.

Syntax

docker desktop logs [OPTIONS]

# Common usage patterns
docker desktop logs                     # Show recent logs
docker desktop logs --follow            # Stream logs in real-time
docker desktop logs --since 1h          # Logs from last hour
docker desktop logs --tail 100          # Last 100 log lines

Parameters Description

  • --follow, -f: Follow log output in real-time. Monitor Django Desktop events continuously.
  • --since: Show logs since timestamp (e.g., 2024-01-15T10:00:00) or relative (e.g., 10m, 1h). Filter Django recent events.
  • --tail: Number of lines to show from end of logs. Quick Django issue investigation.

Attributes

  • Desktop Events: Shows Docker Desktop operations and lifecycle. Debug Django infrastructure issues.
  • Engine Logging: Displays Docker engine events and errors. Troubleshoot Django container problems.
  • Network Operations: Logs networking activities and configurations. Fix Django connectivity issues.
  • Real-time Monitoring: Follow logs as they're generated. Watch Django Desktop events live.
  • Time Filtering: Filter logs by time range or recency. Focus on Django specific incidents.

Methods

View Recent Logs

Syntax: docker desktop logs --tail 100

Parameters: --tail (integer) - Number of recent lines

Display recent Desktop logs for Django troubleshooting.

Follow Logs Real-time

Syntax: docker desktop logs --follow

Parameters: --follow (flag) - Stream logs continuously

Monitor Desktop events in real-time for Django debugging.

Filter by Time Range

Syntax: docker desktop logs --since 1h

Parameters: --since (string) - Time duration or timestamp

View logs from specific timeframe for Django incident investigation.

Return Value

Returns Docker Desktop log entries with timestamps and details.

  • Success (0): Logs displayed successfully
  • Error (1): Unable to retrieve logs
  • Error (125): Docker Desktop not running

5 Practical Django Examples

Example 1: Troubleshoot Django Startup Failure

Investigate why Django containers won't start.

# Django services failing to start
docker-compose up -d
# Error: Cannot connect to Docker daemon

# Check Desktop logs
docker desktop logs --tail 50 | grep -i error

# Look for engine issues
docker desktop logs --since 5m
Django Startup Error:
Cannot connect to the Docker daemon at unix:///var/run/docker.sock

Checking Desktop logs...

[2024-01-15 10:23:15] ERROR: Engine failed to start
[2024-01-15 10:23:15] ERROR: Port 2375 already in use
[2024-01-15 10:23:16] INFO: Attempting to kill process using port 2375
[2024-01-15 10:23:17] ERROR: Failed to release port
[2024-01-15 10:23:18] WARN: Engine startup aborted

Recent events (last 5 minutes):
[10:20:00] INFO: Docker Desktop starting
[10:20:05] INFO: Loading configuration
[10:23:15] ERROR: Engine startup failed
[10:23:18] ERROR: Port conflict detected

Problem Identified:
✗ Port 2375 already in use
✗ Engine can't start
✗ Django services blocked

Solution: Kill conflicting process
sudo lsof -i :2375
# Kill process, restart Desktop

✓ Port freed, engine started
✓ Django services operational
Example 2: Debug Skills API Network Issues

Investigate Skills service networking problems.

# Skills API not reachable
curl http://localhost:8000/api/skills/
# Connection refused

# Monitor network events
docker desktop logs --follow | grep -i network &

# Restart Skills service
docker-compose restart skills-api

# Check logs for network activity
wait
docker desktop logs --since 30s | grep skills
Skills API connection refused

Following Desktop network logs...

[10:35:42] INFO: Network bridge created: bridge
[10:35:43] INFO: Container network connecting: anihpj_skills-api
[10:35:43] ERROR: Network assignment failed
[10:35:43] ERROR: IP address pool exhausted
[10:35:44] WARN: Container skills-api network timeout

Network Events:
- Bridge network IP pool: 172.17.0.0/16
- Allocated IPs: 254/254
- No available addresses

Skills API restart attempt:
[10:35:50] INFO: Container restart requested
[10:35:51] ERROR: Cannot assign IP to skills-api
[10:35:52] ERROR: Network operation failed

Problem Identified:
✗ IP address pool exhausted
✗ Too many containers created
✗ Skills API can't get IP

Solution: Prune unused containers
docker container prune -f
docker desktop logs | grep "IP address assigned"

✓ IP pool freed
✓ Skills API network connected
✓ Service reachable: 200 OK
Example 3: Investigate JobPost Volume Mount Errors

Debug persistent volume mounting issues for JobPost data.

# JobPost worker can't access data volume
docker-compose logs worker
# Error: Permission denied on /data

# Check Desktop volume operations
docker desktop logs --tail 200 | grep -i volume

# Look for mount failures
docker desktop logs | grep "jobpost-data"
JobPost Worker Error:
[worker] PermissionError: /data/jobs.db
[worker] Failed to open database file

Desktop Volume Logs:

[10:40:12] INFO: Volume mount requested: jobpost-data
[10:40:13] INFO: Source path: /var/lib/docker/volumes/jobpost-data
[10:40:13] WARN: Volume permission mismatch
[10:40:14] ERROR: Mount operation failed
[10:40:14] ERROR: UID/GID mismatch detected
[10:40:15] INFO: Container UID: 1000, Volume UID: 0

Volume Mount Details:
- Volume: jobpost-data
- Mount point: /data
- Container user: django (UID 1000)
- Volume owner: root (UID 0)
- Permissions: 0755 (root read/write)

Problem Identified:
✗ Volume owned by root
✗ Worker runs as UID 1000
✗ Permission denied on write

Solution:
docker volume rm jobpost-data
docker volume create jobpost-data
docker run --rm -v jobpost-data:/data alpine \
  chown -R 1000:1000 /data

✓ Volume permissions fixed
✓ Worker can access data
✓ Jobs processing resumed
Example 4: Monitor Django Image Pull Progress

Watch Desktop logs during Django image updates.

# Pull new Django image
docker pull anihpj/web:latest &

# Follow Desktop logs
docker desktop logs --follow | grep -E "pull|download|extract"

# Watch progress
# Press Ctrl+C when complete
Pulling Django image...

Desktop Image Pull Logs:

[10:45:00] INFO: Image pull started: anihpj/web:latest
[10:45:01] INFO: Pulling from anihpj/web
[10:45:02] INFO: Downloading layer: sha256:abc123... (25%)
[10:45:05] INFO: Downloading layer: sha256:abc123... (50%)
[10:45:08] INFO: Downloading layer: sha256:abc123... (75%)
[10:45:10] INFO: Downloading layer: sha256:abc123... (100%)
[10:45:11] INFO: Extracting layer: sha256:abc123...
[10:45:15] INFO: Extracting layer: sha256:def456...
[10:45:18] INFO: Extracting layer: sha256:ghi789...
[10:45:20] INFO: Image pull complete: anihpj/web:latest

Pull Statistics:
- Total layers: 8
- Downloaded: 342 MB
- Time: 20 seconds
- Average speed: 17.1 MB/s

Image Details:
- Repository: anihpj/web
- Tag: latest
- Digest: sha256:abc123def456ghi789...
- Size: 1.2 GB

✓ Django image updated
✓ Ready to deploy new version
Example 5: Debug Desktop Restart Issues

Investigate Desktop restart problems affecting Django.

# Desktop restart takes too long
docker desktop restart

# Monitor restart process
docker desktop logs --follow --since now | grep -i restart

# Check service startup
docker desktop logs | tail -100
Desktop Restart Initiated...

Restart Logs:

[10:50:00] INFO: Restart requested
[10:50:01] INFO: Stopping Docker engine
[10:50:02] INFO: Engine stopped gracefully
[10:50:03] INFO: Stopping Desktop services
[10:50:05] INFO: All services stopped
[10:50:06] INFO: Starting Docker engine
[10:50:08] WARN: Engine startup slow
[10:50:10] WARN: Waiting for daemon to initialize
[10:50:15] ERROR: Timeout waiting for engine
[10:50:16] INFO: Retrying engine startup
[10:50:18] INFO: Engine started successfully
[10:50:20] INFO: Starting Desktop services
[10:50:22] INFO: Kubernetes initialization skipped (disabled)
[10:50:24] INFO: Desktop restart complete

Restart Timeline:
- Stop engine: 2s
- Stop services: 3s
- Start engine: 12s (slow - timeout retry)
- Start services: 4s
- Total time: 24s (expected: 10s)

Issue Identified:
⚠ Engine startup timeout
⚠ Daemon initialization delayed
⚠ Possibly disk I/O bottleneck

Django Impact:
✓ All services restored after restart
✓ Containers restarted automatically
✓ anihpj_web: Running
✓ anihpj_db: Running
✓ anihpj_worker: Running

Recommendation: Check disk performance

docker desktop restart

Official Docs: docker desktop restart | Docker CLI: docker desktop restart

Restart Docker Desktop application.

Full Description

The docker desktop restart command restarts the Docker Desktop application, stopping and then starting the Docker engine, daemon, and all Desktop services. For your anihpj Django project, this resolves engine performance degradation affecting User authentication response times, applies configuration changes for JobPost worker optimizations, clears daemon cache issues impacting Skills API queries, resets networking problems affecting Author profile media delivery, and refreshes resource allocations for Location service database connections by providing complete Desktop environment restart.

When restarting Desktop for your anihpj Django environment, the command stops all running containers, terminates the Docker engine gracefully, clears in-memory cache, reloads configurations, restarts the engine and daemon, and restores container auto-restart policies. This supports Skills model deployment after settings changes, User authentication service recovery from memory leaks, Author profile image caching reset after updates, Location service network configuration application, and JobPost worker queue reconnection after Redis configuration changes by providing clean Django development environment restart without data loss.

Syntax

docker desktop restart

# No additional options available

Parameters Description

  • No Parameters: Command has no parameters. Restarts Docker Desktop completely.

Attributes

  • Full Restart: Stops and starts Docker Desktop completely. Fresh Django environment.
  • Container Preservation: Maintains container configurations. Django services auto-restart.
  • Configuration Application: Applies pending config changes. Activate Django settings.
  • Cache Clearing: Clears daemon cache and memory. Fix Django performance issues.
  • Network Reset: Refreshes networking stack. Resolve Django connectivity problems.

Methods

Restart Desktop

Syntax: docker desktop restart

Parameters: None

Perform complete Docker Desktop restart for Django environment.

Verify Restart Complete

Syntax: docker desktop status

Parameters: None

Confirm Desktop restarted successfully for Django operations.

Check Service Health

Syntax: docker ps --format '{{.Names}}: {{.Status}}'

Parameters: None

Verify Django containers restarted correctly after Desktop restart.

Return Value

Returns restart confirmation and completion status.

  • Success (0): Desktop restarted successfully
  • Error (1): Restart operation failed
  • Error (125): Docker Desktop not running or unable to stop

5 Practical Django Examples

Example 1: Apply Configuration Changes

Restart Desktop to activate new Django resource limits.

# Updated Desktop settings: 4GB RAM → 8GB RAM
# Changes require restart

# Restart Desktop
docker desktop restart

# Wait for startup
sleep 15

# Verify new limits
docker info | grep -i memory

# Test Django with new resources
docker-compose up -d
docker stats --no-stream
Configuration changed: RAM limit 8GB

Restarting Docker Desktop...
Stopping Docker engine...
Docker engine stopped
Starting Docker engine...
Docker engine started

Docker Desktop restarted successfully

System Information:
Total Memory: 8.00 GiB (increased from 4.00 GiB)
Memory Limit: 8.00 GiB

Starting Django services...
anihpj_web: Starting
anihpj_db: Starting
anihpj_redis: Starting
anihpj_worker: Starting

Resource Usage:
NAME              MEM USAGE / LIMIT   MEM %
anihpj_web        512MB / 8GB         6.4%
anihpj_db         1.2GB / 8GB         15%
anihpj_redis      256MB / 8GB         3.2%
anihpj_worker     768MB / 8GB         9.6%

✓ Configuration applied
✓ More memory available
✓ Django performance improved
Example 2: Fix Networking Issues

Restart Desktop to resolve Django service connectivity.

# Skills API can't reach database
curl http://localhost:8000/api/skills/
# Error: Database connection failed

# Check container network
docker exec anihpj_web ping anihpj_db
# Network unreachable

# Restart Desktop to reset networking
docker desktop restart

# Wait for services
sleep 20

# Test connectivity
docker exec anihpj_web ping -c 3 anihpj_db

Example 3: Clear Cache After Build Changes

Restart Desktop to clear build cache for Django image.

# Rebuilt Django image but old cache persists
docker build -t anihpj/web:latest .

# Image seems to use old cached layers
docker run --rm anihpj/web:latest python -c "print(__version__)"
# Shows old version

# Restart Desktop to clear cache
docker desktop restart

# Wait for restart
sleep 15

# Rebuild without cache
docker build --no-cache -t anihpj/web:latest .

# Verify new version
docker run --rm anihpj/web:latest \
  python -c "import django; print(django.VERSION)"

Restart Desktop when Docker daemon memory usage is high.

# Docker daemon using excessive memory
ps aux | grep -i docker

# Desktop performance degraded

docker-compose ps
docker stats --no-stream anihpj_web

# Restart Desktop to free memory
docker desktop restart

# Monitor memory after restart
sleep 20
ps aux | grep -i docker

# Just upgraded Docker Desktop

docker desktop start

Official Docs: docker desktop start | Docker CLI: docker desktop start

Start Docker Desktop application.

Full Description

The docker desktop start command starts the Docker Desktop application when it's stopped, initializing the Docker engine, daemon, and all Desktop services. For your anihpj Django project, this enables starting development environment for User authentication testing, launching JobPost worker infrastructure for queue processing, activating Skills API services for verification testing, preparing Author profile media handling services, and initializing Location query database connections by starting complete Django containerized development environment from stopped state.

When starting Desktop for your anihpj Django environment, the command initializes the Docker engine, starts the daemon process, creates default networks, prepares volume infrastructure, enables configured features (Kubernetes, AI model runner), and triggers auto-start containers. This supports Skills model development workflow initialization, User authentication service readiness for testing, Author profile container orchestration startup, Location service database preparation, and JobPost worker queue connection establishment by providing automated Django infrastructure startup that brings entire development environment online with single command.

Syntax

docker desktop start

# No additional options available

Parameters Description

  • No Parameters: Command has no parameters. Starts Docker Desktop from stopped state.

Attributes

  • Environment Initialization: Starts complete Docker environment. Launch Django development setup.
  • Service Startup: Initializes engine, daemon, networking. Prepare Django infrastructure.
  • Auto-start Containers: Starts containers with restart policies. Resume Django services automatically.
  • Feature Activation: Enables configured Desktop features. Activate Django development tools.
  • Network Creation: Establishes default networks. Connect Django services.

Methods

Start Desktop

Syntax: docker desktop start

Parameters: None

Launch Docker Desktop for Django development.

Verify Startup Complete

Syntax: docker desktop status

Parameters: None

Confirm Desktop started successfully for Django operations.

Start Django Services

Syntax: docker-compose up -d

Parameters: None

Launch Django containers after Desktop startup.

Return Value

Returns startup confirmation and readiness status.

  • Success (0): Docker Desktop started successfully
  • Error (1): Desktop already running or startup failed
  • Error (125): Unable to start Desktop (system resource issues)

5 Practical Django Examples

Example 1: Start Development Environment

Begin Django development session by starting Desktop.

# Check if Desktop is running
docker desktop status
# Status: stopped

# Start Desktop
docker desktop start

# Wait for startup
sleep 15

# Verify Desktop running
docker desktop status

# Start Django services
cd /path/to/anihpj
docker-compose up -d
Checking Docker Desktop status...
Status: stopped

Starting Docker Desktop...
Initializing Docker engine...
Starting daemon...
Creating default networks...
Docker Desktop started successfully

Verifying status...
Status: running
Engine: 24.0.7
API version: 1.43

Starting Django development environment...
Creating network anihpj_default
Creating volume anihpj_postgres_data
Creating volume anihpj_redis_data

Starting services...
anihpj_db: Started
anihpj_redis: Started
anihpj_web: Started
anihpj_worker: Started

Development Environment Ready:
✓ Docker Desktop: Running
✓ Database: PostgreSQL 15.0
✓ Cache: Redis 7.2
✓ Web: http://localhost:8000
✓ Worker: Processing jobs

Ready for Django development!
Example 2: Resume After System Reboot

Start Desktop and restore Django services after reboot.

#!/bin/bash
# Startup script after system reboot

echo "Starting Docker Desktop..."
docker desktop start

# Wait for Desktop ready
while ! docker desktop status &>/dev/null; do
  echo "Waiting for Desktop..."
  sleep 5
done

echo "Desktop ready. Starting Django services..."

# Containers with restart=always will auto-start
docker ps --filter "name=anihpj"

# Check service health
docker-compose ps

Start Desktop and wait for Kubernetes cluster ready.

# Start Desktop (K8s enabled in settings)
docker desktop start

# Wait for Desktop
echo "Waiting for Docker Desktop..."
sleep 20

echo "Waiting for Kubernetes..."
kubectl cluster-info

# Deploy Django on K8s
kubectl apply -f k8s/django-deployment.yaml

Example 4: Automated Morning Startup

Create script to start Desktop and Django automatically.

#!/bin/bash
# save as: start-django-dev.sh

echo "=== Django Development Startup ==="

# Start Desktop if not running
if ! docker desktop status &>/dev/null; then
  echo "Starting Docker Desktop..."
  docker desktop start
  sleep 20
else
  echo "Docker Desktop already running"
fi

# Navigate to project
cd ~/projects/anihpj

# Pull latest images
echo "Updating Django images..."
docker-compose pull

# Start services
echo "Starting Django services..."
docker-compose up -d

# Wait for health checks
echo "Waiting for services..."
sleep 10

# Show status
docker-compose ps

# Open in browser
echo "Opening Django admin..."
open http://localhost:8000/admin/

echo "✓ Django development environment ready!"

Example 5: Start and Run Migrations

Start Desktop and initialize Django database.

# Start Desktop
docker desktop start

# Wait for ready
sleep 20

# Start Django services
docker-compose up -d db

# Wait for database
echo "Waiting for PostgreSQL..."
sleep 10

# Run migrations
docker-compose run --rm web \
  python manage.py migrate

# Create superuser
docker-compose run --rm web \
  python manage.py createsuperuser --noinput \
  --username=admin --email=admin@anihpj.com

# Start all services
docker-compose up -d

docker desktop status

Official Docs: docker desktop status | Docker CLI: docker desktop status

Check Docker Desktop application status.

Full Description

The docker desktop status command displays the current operational status of Docker Desktop, including running state, engine version, API version, and service health. For your anihpj Django project, this verifies Docker environment readiness before User authentication testing, confirms JobPost worker infrastructure availability for deployment, validates Skills API platform health before load testing, checks Author profile services readiness for media uploads, and ensures Location query engine accessibility for database operations by providing quick Django development environment health check.

When checking Desktop status for your anihpj Django environment, the command reports whether Desktop is running or stopped, displays engine and API versions for compatibility verification, shows Kubernetes status if enabled, and indicates feature availability. This supports Skills model deployment readiness checks, User authentication service platform validation before CI/CD runs, Author profile container orchestration availability confirmation, Location service infrastructure health verification, and JobPost worker environment readiness assessment by providing instant Django infrastructure status visibility for troubleshooting and pre-deployment validation.

Syntax

docker desktop status

# No additional options available

Parameters Description

  • No Parameters: Command has no parameters. Returns current Desktop status.

Attributes

  • Running State: Shows if Desktop is running or stopped. Verify Django environment available.
  • Version Information: Displays engine and API versions. Check Django compatibility.
  • Feature Status: Shows enabled features (Kubernetes, etc.). Validate Django orchestration.
  • Quick Check: Fast status command for automation. CI/CD readiness verification.
  • Health Indicator: Overall Desktop health status. Ensure Django infrastructure operational.

Methods

Check Desktop Status

Syntax: docker desktop status

Parameters: None

Get current Docker Desktop status for Django environment.

Script Status Check

Syntax: docker desktop status &>/dev/null && echo "ready"

Parameters: None

Silent status check for Django automation scripts.

Wait for Ready

Syntax: while ! docker desktop status; do sleep 5; done

Parameters: None

Poll status until Desktop ready for Django operations.

Return Value

Returns Desktop status information and running state.

  • Success (0): Desktop running and operational
  • Error (1): Desktop not running or not installed
  • Error (125): Unable to determine status

5 Practical Django Examples

Example 1: Pre-deployment Status Check

Verify Docker Desktop ready before Django deployment.

# Check Desktop status
docker desktop status

# If running, deploy Django
if docker desktop status &>/dev/null; then
  echo "Desktop ready. Deploying Django..."
  docker-compose up -d
else
  echo "Desktop not running. Starting..."
  docker desktop start
  sleep 20
  docker-compose up -d
fi
Checking Docker Desktop status...

Status: running
Docker Desktop: 4.27.0
Engine: 24.0.7
API version: 1.43
OS/Arch: linux/amd64

Desktop ready. Deploying Django...

Creating network anihpj_default
Starting services...
anihpj_db: Started
anihpj_redis: Started
anihpj_web: Started
anihpj_worker: Started

Django Deployment Complete:
✓ Docker Desktop: Running
✓ Engine version: 24.0.7
✓ Database: Up and healthy
✓ Redis: Up and healthy
✓ Web: Up and healthy
✓ Worker: Up and running

Services accessible:
- Web: http://localhost:8000
- Admin: http://localhost:8000/admin/
- API: http://localhost:8000/api/
Example 2: CI/CD Pipeline Health Check

Verify Docker environment before running Django tests.

#!/bin/bash
# CI/CD pipeline script

echo "=== Django Test Pipeline ==="

# Check Desktop status
if ! docker desktop status &>/dev/null; then
  echo "ERROR: Docker Desktop not running"
  exit 1
fi

# Get version info
engine_version=$(docker desktop status | grep "Engine:" | awk '{print $2}')
echo "Engine: $engine_version"

# Verify minimum version
min_version="24.0.0"
if [[ "$engine_version" < "$min_version" ]]; then
  echo "ERROR: Engine version too old"
  exit 1
fi

echo "✓ Docker environment validated"

# Run Django tests
docker-compose run --rm web python manage.py test
=== Django Test Pipeline ===

- Minimum required: 24.0.0
- Current version: 24.0.7

test_location_query (jobpost.tests.TestLocation)... ok

✓ All tests passed
Example 3: Monitor Desktop During Load Test

Track Desktop status during Django stress testing.

#!/bin/bash
# Load test monitoring script

# Start load test in background
echo "Starting Skills API load test..."
ab -n 10000 -c 100 http://localhost:8000/api/skills/ > load_test.log &
LOAD_PID=$!

# Monitor Desktop status
while kill -0 $LOAD_PID 2>/dev/null; do
  clear
  echo "=== Load Test In Progress ==="
  docker desktop status
  echo ""
  echo "Container Stats:"
  docker stats --no-stream anihpj_web anihpj_db
  sleep 5
done

echo "Load test complete"
cat load_test.log | grep "Requests per second"
Starting Skills API load test...

=== Load Test In Progress ===

API version: 1.43

Container Stats:
NAME          CPU %   MEM USAGE / LIMIT     MEM %

anihpj_db     22.1%   800MB / 8GB           10.0%

[5 seconds later]

Engine: 24.0.7
API version: 1.43

Container Stats:
NAME          CPU %   MEM USAGE / LIMIT     MEM %
anihpj_web    78.5%   1.8GB / 8GB           22.5%
anihpj_db     35.7%   1.1GB / 8GB           13.8%

[Load test continues...]

Load test complete

Results:

Time per request: 121.5 ms (mean)
Failed requests: 0

Desktop Status: Stable throughout
✓ No engine issues
✓ No memory problems
✓ Django handled load successfully
Example 4: Startup Wait Script

Wait for Desktop ready before Django operations.

#!/bin/bash
# Wait for Desktop ready

echo "Waiting for Docker Desktop..."

MAX_WAIT=60
ELAPSED=0

while ! docker desktop status &>/dev/null; do
  if [ $ELAPSED -ge $MAX_WAIT ]; then
    echo "ERROR: Desktop startup timeout"
    exit 1
  fi
  
  echo "Waiting... ($ELAPSED/${MAX_WAIT}s)"
  sleep 5
  ELAPSED=$((ELAPSED + 5))
done

echo "✓ Docker Desktop ready"
docker desktop status

# Continue with Django startup
echo "Starting Django services..."
docker-compose up -d

Waiting... (10/60s)
Waiting... (15/60s)

Example 5: Health Check Dashboard

Create Django environment health dashboard.

#!/bin/bash
# Django environment dashboard

while true; do
  clear
  echo "╔══════════════════════════════════════════╗"
  echo "║   Django anihpj Environment Status      ║"
  echo "╚══════════════════════════════════════════╝"
  echo ""
  
  # Desktop status
  echo "Docker Desktop:"
  if docker desktop status &>/dev/null; then
    docker desktop status | head -5
  else
    echo "  ✗ Not running"
  fi
  
  echo ""
  echo "Django Services:"
  docker-compose ps --format "table {{.Name}}\t{{.Status}}" 2>/dev/null || echo "  Services not started"
  
  echo ""
  echo "Resource Usage:"
  docker stats --no-stream --format "table {{.Name}}\t{{.CPUPerc}}\t{{.MemUsage}}" 2>/dev/null || echo "  No containers running"
  
  echo ""
  echo "Press Ctrl+C to exit"
  sleep 10
done

║   Django anihpj Environment Status      ║
╚══════════════════════════════════════════╝

docker desktop stop

Official Docs: docker desktop stop | Docker CLI: docker desktop stop

Stop Docker Desktop application.

Full Description

The docker desktop stop command stops the Docker Desktop application, gracefully shutting down the Docker engine, daemon, and all Desktop services while preserving container configurations and data. For your anihpj Django project, this enables resource conservation when not developing User authentication features, freeing system memory after completing JobPost worker testing, reducing CPU usage when Skills API verification is finished, releasing disk I/O after Author profile operations, and shutting down Location service infrastructure when database work is complete by stopping entire Django development environment safely.

When stopping Desktop for your anihpj Django environment, the command stops all running containers gracefully, terminates the Docker daemon process, closes engine connections, releases network resources, preserves volume data and configurations, and maintains container restart policies for next startup. This supports Skills model development workflow completion without data loss, User authentication testing session cleanup, Author profile batch processing shutdown, Location service scheduled maintenance preparation, and JobPost worker graceful queue disconnection by providing safe Django infrastructure shutdown that preserves all work while freeing system resources for other applications.

Syntax

docker desktop stop

# No additional options available

Parameters Description

  • No Parameters: Command has no parameters. Stops Docker Desktop gracefully.

Attributes

  • Graceful Shutdown: Stops containers and services safely. No Django data loss.
  • Resource Release: Frees memory, CPU, disk I/O. Improve system performance.
  • Data Preservation: Maintains volumes, images, configs. Django state preserved.
  • Container Stopping: Sends SIGTERM then SIGKILL to containers. Clean Django shutdown.
  • Network Cleanup: Releases network resources and ports. Free Django ports.

Methods

Stop Desktop

Syntax: docker desktop stop

Parameters: None

Gracefully stop Docker Desktop and Django environment.

Verify Stopped

Syntax: docker desktop status

Parameters: None

Confirm Desktop stopped successfully.

Stop with Container Check

Syntax: docker-compose down && docker desktop stop

Parameters: None

Stop Django containers explicitly before stopping Desktop.

Return Value

Returns stop confirmation and completion status.

  • Success (0): Desktop stopped successfully
  • Error (1): Desktop already stopped or stop failed
  • Error (125): Unable to stop Desktop (forced shutdown may be required)

5 Practical Django Examples

Example 1: End Development Session

Stop Docker Desktop when finished Django development.

# Save Django work
docker-compose exec web python manage.py dumpdata > backup.json

# Stop Django services
docker-compose down

# Stop Desktop to free resources
docker desktop stop

# Verify stopped
docker desktop status
Backing up Django data...
Dumped 1,247 objects to backup.json
✓ Data backed up

Stopping Django services...
Stopping anihpj_worker...done
Stopping anihpj_web...done
Stopping anihpj_redis...done
Stopping anihpj_db...done

Removing containers...
anihpj_worker removed
anihpj_web removed
anihpj_redis removed
anihpj_db removed

Network anihpj_default removed

Stopping Docker Desktop...
Stopping Docker engine...
Stopping daemon...
Releasing resources...
Docker Desktop stopped

Verifying status...
Status: stopped

System Resources Released:
- Memory freed: 4.2 GB
- CPU usage: 15% → 2%
- Disk I/O: Active → Idle

✓ Development session ended
✓ All Django data preserved
✓ System resources freed
Example 2: Scheduled Shutdown Script

Automatically stop Desktop after Django batch processing.

#!/bin/bash
# Run Django batch job then shutdown

echo "Starting JobPost batch processing..."

# Run worker task
docker-compose run --rm worker \
  python manage.py process_jobs --batch

# Check if successful
if [ $? -eq 0 ]; then
  echo "✓ Batch processing complete"
  
  # Stop services
  docker-compose down
  
  # Stop Desktop
  docker desktop stop
  
  echo "✓ System shutdown complete"
else
  echo "✗ Batch processing failed"
  echo "Desktop left running for debugging"
fi

Example 3: Maintenance Mode Preparation

Stop Django environment for system maintenance.

# Notify users (if production-like)
echo "Entering maintenance mode..."

# Backup database
docker-compose exec db pg_dump -U postgres anihpj > backup.sql

# Stop containers gracefully
docker-compose stop

# Wait for containers to stop
sleep 10

# Stop Desktop
docker desktop stop

# Verify everything stopped
docker desktop status
ps aux | grep docker

Example 4: Battery Conservation

Stop Desktop when on battery power to conserve energy.

#!/bin/bash
# Battery-aware Docker management

# Check if on battery
if pmset -g batt | grep -q "Battery Power"; then
  echo "On battery power - stopping Docker Desktop"
  
  # Check if work in progress
  running=$(docker ps -q | wc -l)
  
  if [ $running -gt 0 ]; then
    echo "Warning: $running containers running"
    read -p "Stop anyway? (y/n) " -n 1 -r
    echo
    if [[ ! $REPLY =~ ^[Yy]$ ]]; then
      exit 0
    fi
  fi
  
  # Stop Django services
  docker-compose down
  
  # Stop Desktop
  docker desktop stop
  
  echo "✓ Desktop stopped for battery conservation"
else
  echo "On AC power - Desktop can continue running"
fi

Example 5: Clean Development Workflow

Stop Desktop with cleanup and verification.

#!/bin/bash
# Clean Django development shutdown

echo "=== Clean Django Shutdown ==="

# Stop and remove containers
echo "Stopping Django services..."
docker-compose down

# Optional: Clean up old images
echo "Cleaning unused images..."
docker image prune -f

# Optional: Clean build cache
echo "Cleaning build cache..."
docker builder prune -f

# Show space freed
echo "Calculating space freed..."
docker system df

# Stop Desktop
echo "Stopping Docker Desktop..."
docker desktop stop

# Final verification
sleep 5
if docker desktop status &>/dev/null; then
  echo "✗ Desktop still running"
else
  echo "✓ Clean shutdown complete"
fi

docker desktop update

Official Docs: docker desktop update | Docker CLI: docker desktop update

Check for and install Docker Desktop updates.

Full Description

The docker desktop update command checks for available Docker Desktop updates and optionally installs them, ensuring access to latest features, security patches, and bug fixes. For your anihpj Django project, this enables accessing new engine features for User authentication optimizations, applying security patches for JobPost worker protection, obtaining bug fixes for Skills API stability improvements, updating orchestration features for Author profile scaling, and implementing performance enhancements for Location service database operations by maintaining current Docker Desktop installation with latest improvements.

When updating Desktop for your anihpj Django environment, the command checks for new releases, downloads update packages, performs backup of current settings, installs the new version, migrates configurations, and restarts Desktop with updated components. This supports Skills model deployment with latest Docker features, User authentication testing with patched security vulnerabilities, Author profile operations with improved networking, Location service compatibility with new Kubernetes versions, and JobPost worker performance with optimized engine by providing seamless Django development environment updates that enhance capabilities while preserving configurations and data.

Syntax

docker desktop update [OPTIONS]

# Common usage patterns
docker desktop update                    # Check for updates
docker desktop update --install          # Install available updates
docker desktop update --check            # Only check, don't install

Parameters Description

  • --install: Automatically install available updates. Apply Django environment updates.
  • --check: Only check for updates without installing. View available Django infrastructure updates.
  • --quiet, -q: Suppress output except errors. Silent Django update checks.

Attributes

  • Update Detection: Checks for new Desktop releases. Stay current with Django infrastructure.
  • Automatic Installation: Downloads and installs updates. Simplify Django environment maintenance.
  • Configuration Migration: Preserves settings across updates. Maintain Django customizations.
  • Security Patching: Applies critical security fixes. Protect Django infrastructure.
  • Feature Access: Enables new Docker capabilities. Enhance Django development.

Methods

Check for Updates

Syntax: docker desktop update --check

Parameters: --check (flag) - Check only

Identify available Desktop updates for Django environment.

Install Updates

Syntax: docker desktop update --install

Parameters: --install (flag) - Install automatically

Download and install Desktop updates for Django infrastructure.

Verify Update

Syntax: docker desktop version

Parameters: None

Confirm Desktop version after update for Django compatibility.

Return Value

Returns update availability and installation status.

  • Success (0): Update check or installation successful
  • Error (1): No updates available or installation failed
  • Error (125): Unable to check for updates or network error

5 Practical Django Examples

Example 1: Check for Security Updates

Verify if security patches available for Django infrastructure.

# Check current version
docker desktop version

# Check for updates
docker desktop update --check

# Review release notes if available
echo "Review: https://docs.docker.com/desktop/release-notes/"
Current Docker Desktop Version:
Docker Desktop: 4.26.1
Engine: 24.0.6
Compose: v2.23.0

Checking for updates...
Connecting to update server...

Update Available:
Docker Desktop 4.27.0

Release Highlights:
- Security fix: CVE-2024-1234 (High)
- Engine updated to 24.0.7
- Compose updated to v2.24.0
- Performance improvements for networking
- Bug fixes for volume mounting

Security Advisories:
⚠ CVE-2024-1234: Container escape vulnerability (FIXED in 4.27.0)
⚠ CVE-2024-5678: Privilege escalation (FIXED in 4.27.0)

Django Impact:
✓ Security patches critical for production
✓ Networking improvements benefit Skills API
✓ Volume fixes help JobPost data persistence
✓ Engine update supports User authentication

Recommendation: Install update immediately

Release notes: https://docs.docker.com/desktop/release-notes/
Example 2: Scheduled Update Installation

Automate Desktop updates for Django environment.

#!/bin/bash
# Weekly update script

echo "=== Docker Desktop Update Check ==="

# Check for updates
if docker desktop update --check | grep -q "Update Available"; then
  echo "Update available"
  
  # Backup Django data
  docker-compose exec db pg_dump -U postgres anihpj > backup.sql
  
  # Stop Django services
  docker-compose down
  
  # Install update
  docker desktop update --install
  
  # Wait for restart
  sleep 30
  
  # Verify new version
  docker desktop version
  
  # Restart Django
  docker-compose up -d
  
  echo "✓ Update complete"
else
  echo "Already up to date"
fi

Example 3: Pre-production Update Validation

Test Desktop update before applying to production.

# Check update details
docker desktop update --check > update_info.txt
cat update_info.txt

# Install update on dev environment
docker desktop update --install

# Wait for restart
sleep 30

# Run Django test suite
docker-compose run --rm web python manage.py test

# Check service compatibility
docker-compose up -d
curl http://localhost:8000/health/

# Load test
ab -n 1000 -c 50 http://localhost:8000/api/skills/

Example 4: Feature-based Update

Update to access new features for Django development.

# Check for features in new version
docker desktop update --check

# Read release notes
echo "New features:"
echo "- Improved Kubernetes integration"
echo "- Enhanced AI model runner"
echo "- Better volume performance"

# Install for new features
docker desktop update --install

# Test new Kubernetes features
kubectl version
kubectl get nodes

# Test improved volumes
docker volume create --driver local \
  --opt type=none \
  --opt device=/data/django \
  --opt o=bind \
  django-fast-volume

Example 5: Automated CI/CD Update Check

Check for critical updates in CI/CD pipeline.

#!/bin/bash
# CI/CD environment validation

echo "Validating Docker Desktop version..."

# Check current version
current=$(docker desktop version | grep "Desktop:" | awk '{print $3}')
echo "Current version: $current"

# Check for updates
docker desktop update --check -q > /tmp/update_check.txt

# Check if security updates available
if grep -q "Security" /tmp/update_check.txt; then
  echo "⚠ Security updates available!"
  echo "Current environment vulnerable"
  
  # In CI/CD, might want to fail the build
  cat /tmp/update_check.txt
  
  echo ""
  echo "Action required:"
  echo "1. Update Docker Desktop"
  echo "2. Rerun pipeline"
  
  exit 1
else
  echo "✓ Environment up to date"
  echo "✓ No security vulnerabilities"
fi
Validating Docker Desktop version...

Current version: 4.26.1

Checking for updates...

⚠ Security updates available!
Current environment vulnerable

Update Details:
Docker Desktop 4.27.0 available

Security Fixes:
- CVE-2024-1234: Container escape (HIGH)
- CVE-2024-5678: Privilege escalation (MEDIUM)

Impact on Django:
⚠ Containers may be vulnerable to escape
⚠ Production deployment at risk
⚠ User data potentially exposed

Action required:
1. Update Docker Desktop
2. Rerun pipeline

Reason: Security vulnerabilities in Docker Desktop
Required action: Update to 4.27.0 or later

✓ No security vulnerabilities
