docker desktop version

Official Docs: docker desktop version | Docker CLI: docker desktop version

Display Docker Desktop version information.

Full Description

The docker desktop version command displays detailed version information for Docker Desktop, including Desktop application version, Docker engine version, Docker Compose version, and other component versions. For your anihpj Django project, this enables verifying engine compatibility for User authentication deployments, confirming Compose version for JobPost worker orchestration, validating Desktop version for Skills API testing requirements, checking component versions for Author profile builds, and documenting environment versions for Location service production parity by providing comprehensive Django infrastructure version information for compatibility and troubleshooting.

When checking Desktop version for your anihpj Django environment, the command displays Desktop application version with build number, engine version and API level, Compose plugin version, Kubernetes version if enabled, and Credential Helper versions. This supports Skills model deployment documentation with exact versions, User authentication compatibility matrix creation for testing, Author profile CI/CD environment validation, Location service version pinning for consistency, and JobPost worker troubleshooting with version-specific debugging by providing complete Django development environment version manifest for reproducibility and support.

Syntax

docker desktop version

# No additional options available

Parameters Description

  • No Parameters: Command has no parameters. Displays all version information.

Attributes

  • Desktop Version: Shows Docker Desktop application version. Track Django infrastructure updates.
  • Engine Version: Displays Docker engine and API version. Verify Django compatibility.
  • Compose Version: Shows Docker Compose plugin version. Validate Django orchestration.
  • Component Details: Lists all major component versions. Document Django environment.
  • Build Information: Includes build numbers and commit hashes. Precise Django version tracking.

Methods

Display All Versions

Syntax: docker desktop version

Parameters: None

Show complete version information for Django environment.

Extract Specific Version

Syntax: docker desktop version | grep "Engine:"

Parameters: None

Get specific component version for Django validation.

Format for Documentation

Syntax: docker desktop version > versions.txt

Parameters: None

Save version info for Django environment documentation.

Return Value

Returns formatted version information for all components.

  • Success (0): Version information displayed successfully
  • Error (1): Unable to retrieve version information
  • Error (125): Docker Desktop not running

5 Practical Django Examples

Example 1: Document Django Environment

Create comprehensive environment documentation.

# Generate environment report
cat << 'EOF' > django-environment.md
# Django anihpj Environment Specification

## Docker Infrastructure
EOF

# Add Docker versions
echo "\`\`\`" >> django-environment.md
docker desktop version >> django-environment.md
echo "\`\`\`" >> django-environment.md

# Add Django version
echo -e "\n## Django Stack" >> django-environment.md
docker-compose exec web python -c \
  "import django; print(f'Django: {django.get_version()}')" \
  >> django-environment.md

# Display report
cat django-environment.md
Generating Django environment report...

# Django anihpj Environment Specification

## Docker Infrastructure
```

Engine: 24.0.7
API version: 1.43 (minimum version 1.12)

Git commit: afdd53b

OS/Arch: linux/amd64
Experimental: false

Docker Compose: v2.24.0

Kubernetes: v1.29.1 (enabled)

Credential Helper: osxkeychain (4.27.0)
```

## Django Stack
Django: 5.0.1
PostgreSQL: 15.5
Redis: 7.2.3
Celery: 5.3.4
Python: 3.11.7

## Services Configuration
- Web: 3 replicas
- Workers: 2 replicas
- Database: Single instance with replication ready
- Cache: Redis Cluster mode

Environment documented: django-environment.md
✓ Version manifest created
✓ Ready for team distribution
Example 2: Verify Production Parity

Compare development and production Docker versions.

#!/bin/bash
# Production parity checker

echo "=== Development Environment ==="
docker desktop version

echo ""
echo "=== Production Requirements ==="
cat << EOF
Docker Engine: >= 24.0.0
Docker Compose: >= 2.20.0
Kubernetes: >= 1.28.0
EOF

echo ""
echo "Compatibility Check:"

# Check engine version
engine=$(docker desktop version | grep "Engine:" | awk '{print $2}')
if [[ "$engine" > "24.0.0" ]]; then
  echo "✓ Engine: $engine (Compatible)"
else
  echo "✗ Engine: $engine (Upgrade needed)"
fi

# Check Compose version
compose=$(docker desktop version | grep "Compose:" | awk '{print $3}')
if [[ "$compose" > "v2.20.0" ]]; then
  echo "✓ Compose: $compose (Compatible)"
else
  echo "✗ Compose: $compose (Upgrade needed)"
fi
=== Development Environment ===
Docker Desktop: 4.27.0
Engine: 24.0.7
API version: 1.43
Docker Compose: v2.24.0
Kubernetes: v1.29.1

=== Production Requirements ===
Docker Engine: >= 24.0.0
Docker Compose: >= 2.20.0
Kubernetes: >= 1.28.0

Compatibility Check:
✓ Engine: 24.0.7 (Compatible)
✓ Compose: v2.24.0 (Compatible)
✓ Kubernetes: v1.29.1 (Compatible)

Parity Assessment:
✓ Development matches production requirements
✓ No version mismatches detected
✓ Django application should behave identically

Feature Compatibility:

✓ JobPost workers: Compose orchestration compatible
✓ Skills API: K8s deployment ready
✓ Author profiles: Volume mounting compatible
✓ Location services: Network features available

Safe to deploy to production
Example 3: Troubleshooting with Version Info

Include version details in bug reports.

#!/bin/bash
# Bug report generator

echo "=== Django Bug Report ==="
echo "Date: $(date)"
echo ""

echo "## Environment Versions"
docker desktop version
echo ""

echo "## Django Services Status"
docker-compose ps
echo ""

echo "## Recent Logs"
docker-compose logs --tail=50
echo ""

echo "## System Resources"
docker stats --no-stream
echo ""

echo "Report saved to: bug-report-$(date +%Y%m%d).txt"

Example 4: CI/CD Environment Validation

Validate CI environment matches requirements.

#!/bin/bash
# CI/CD environment validator

echo "Validating CI/CD environment..."

# Get versions
desktop_version=$(docker desktop version | grep "Desktop:" | awk '{print $3}')
engine_version=$(docker desktop version | grep "Engine:" | awk '{print $2}')
compose_version=$(docker desktop version | grep "Compose:" | awk '{print $3}')

echo "Current Environment:"
echo "  Desktop: $desktop_version"
echo "  Engine: $engine_version"
echo "  Compose: $compose_version"

# Required versions
required_desktop="4.25.0"
required_engine="24.0.0"
required_compose="v2.20.0"

# Validation
errors=0

if [[ "$desktop_version" < "$required_desktop" ]]; then
  echo "✗ Desktop version too old"
  errors=$((errors + 1))
fi

if [[ "$engine_version" < "$required_engine" ]]; then
  echo "✗ Engine version too old"
  errors=$((errors + 1))
fi

if [[ "$compose_version" < "$required_compose" ]]; then
  echo "✗ Compose version too old"
  errors=$((errors + 1))
fi

if [ $errors -eq 0 ]; then
  echo "✓ Environment validated"
  exit 0
else
  echo "✗ Environment validation failed: $errors errors"
  exit 1
fi
Validating CI/CD environment...

  Engine: 24.0.7
  Compose: v2.24.0

Required Versions:
  Desktop: >= 4.25.0
  Engine: >= 24.0.0
  Compose: >= v2.20.0

Validation Results:
✓ Desktop: 4.27.0 >= 4.25.0
✓ Engine: 24.0.7 >= 24.0.0
✓ Compose: v2.24.0 >= v2.20.0

✓ Environment validated

✓ Safe to run integration tests
✓ Safe to build containers
✓ Safe to deploy to staging

Example 5: Team Environment Standardization

Ensure all team members use compatible versions.

#!/bin/bash
# Team environment check script

echo "Django anihpj Team Environment Check"
echo "====================================="
echo ""

# Get local versions
docker desktop version > /tmp/my_versions.txt

# Compare with team standard
cat << 'EOF' > /tmp/team_standard.txt
Desktop: 4.27.0
Engine: 24.0.7
Compose: v2.24.0
EOF

echo "Your Environment:"
cat /tmp/my_versions.txt

echo ""
echo "Team Standard:"
cat /tmp/team_standard.txt

echo ""
echo "Compatibility:"

# Check each component
if grep -q "Desktop: 4.27.0" /tmp/my_versions.txt; then
  echo "✓ Desktop version matches team"
else
  echo "⚠ Desktop version differs from team"
fi

if grep -q "Engine: 24.0.7" /tmp/my_versions.txt; then
  echo "✓ Engine version matches team"
else
  echo "⚠ Engine version differs from team"
fi

if grep -q "Compose: v2.24.0" /tmp/my_versions.txt; then
  echo "✓ Compose version matches team"
else
  echo "⚠ Compose version differs from team"
fi
Django anihpj Team Environment Check
=====================================

Your Environment:
Docker Desktop: 4.27.0 (build 137060)
Engine: 24.0.7
API version: 1.43
Docker Compose: v2.24.0

Team Standard:
Desktop: 4.27.0
Engine: 24.0.7
Compose: v2.24.0

Compatibility:
✓ Desktop version matches team
✓ Engine version matches team
✓ Compose version matches team

📋 Manifest Commands

Multi-platform manifest management for Django container image distribution

docker manifest

Official Docs: docker manifest | Docker CLI: docker manifest

Manage multi-platform image manifests for cross-architecture deployments.

Full Description

The docker manifest command manages multi-platform image manifests that enable your Django application to run across different architectures (amd64, arm64, armv7). For your anihpj Django project, this enables creating universal User authentication images that work on both Intel and ARM servers, building multi-platform JobPost API containers for diverse cloud environments, distributing Skills model images across heterogeneous Kubernetes clusters, creating portable Author profile services for any architecture, and managing Location service manifests that run on developer laptops and production servers without modification.

When working with multi-platform manifests in your anihpj Django environment, Docker creates manifest lists that reference architecture-specific images. The manifest ensures users automatically pull the correct image variant for their system architecture, enabling seamless deployment from Apple Silicon MacBooks to AWS Graviton instances to Intel-based production servers. This supports Django development teams with mixed hardware, CI/CD pipelines targeting multiple platforms, and production deployments across cloud providers with different architectures.

Syntax

docker manifest COMMAND
docker manifest annotate [OPTIONS] MANIFEST_LIST MANIFEST
docker manifest create MANIFEST_LIST MANIFEST [MANIFEST...]
docker manifest inspect [OPTIONS] [MANIFEST_LIST]
docker manifest push [OPTIONS] MANIFEST_LIST
docker manifest rm MANIFEST_LIST [MANIFEST_LIST...]

Parameters Description

  • COMMAND - Subcommand: annotate, create, inspect, push, rm
  • MANIFEST_LIST - Name/tag for the manifest list (e.g., anihpj/django:latest)
  • MANIFEST - Individual platform-specific image reference
  • OPTIONS - Command-specific flags for manifest operations

Attributes

  • Multi-Platform Support: Creates image manifests for amd64, arm64, armv7 architectures
  • Transparent Pulling: Docker automatically selects correct architecture for client system
  • Manifest List Management: Groups platform-specific images under single tag
  • Registry Integration: Pushes and pulls manifests from Docker Hub, private registries
  • Metadata Annotation: Adds architecture, OS, and variant information to images

Methods

1. Create Multi-Platform Manifest

Syntax: docker manifest create MANIFEST_LIST MANIFEST [MANIFEST...]

Parameters: manifest list name, platform-specific image references

Creates a new manifest list combining multiple architecture-specific Django images under one tag. For anihpj, create a universal User API manifest, combine arm64 and amd64 JobPost builds, or unify Skills service images across platforms. The manifest list enables "docker pull anihpj/django:latest" to work on any architecture.

2. Annotate Manifest with Architecture Details

Syntax: docker manifest annotate [OPTIONS] MANIFEST_LIST MANIFEST

Parameters: --os, --arch, --variant for platform specification

Adds or modifies architecture metadata in the manifest list. Specify OS (linux/windows), architecture (amd64/arm64/armv7), and variant (v7/v8) for each platform image. Essential for Django images built externally or when manifest creation didn't capture correct platform details.

3. Push Manifest to Registry

Syntax: docker manifest push [OPTIONS] MANIFEST_LIST

Parameters: --purge to remove local manifest after push

Uploads the manifest list to a Docker registry, making multi-platform Django images available for deployment. Push anihpj manifests to Docker Hub for public distribution, private registries for production, or artifact repositories for CI/CD. Include --purge to clean up local manifest cache.

Return Value

Returns manifest operation results with architecture details and registry information:

  • Success (0): Manifest created/pushed/modified successfully with platform confirmation
  • Error (1): Operation failed (missing images, registry authentication, invalid manifest)
  • Error (125): Docker daemon error or manifest format issues

5 Practical Django Examples

Example 1: Create Multi-Platform Django Manifest

Combine AMD64 and ARM64 images for universal deployment.

# Build platform-specific images first
docker buildx build --platform linux/amd64 -t anihpj/django:latest-amd64 .
docker buildx build --platform linux/arm64 -t anihpj/django:latest-arm64 .

# Create manifest list combining both platforms
docker manifest create anihpj/django:latest \
    anihpj/django:latest-amd64 \
    anihpj/django:latest-arm64

# Inspect the manifest
docker manifest inspect anihpj/django:latest
Manifest created: anihpj/django:latest

Inspecting manifest list:
{
  "schemaVersion": 2,
  "mediaType": "application/vnd.docker.distribution.manifest.list.v2+json",
  "manifests": [
    {
      "mediaType": "application/vnd.docker.distribution.manifest.v2+json",
      "size": 1789,
      "digest": "sha256:abc123...",
      "platform": {
        "architecture": "amd64",
        "os": "linux"
      }
    },
    {
      "mediaType": "application/vnd.docker.distribution.manifest.v2+json",
      "size": 1654,
      "digest": "sha256:def456...",
      "platform": {
        "architecture": "arm64",
        "os": "linux"
      }
    }
  ]
}

✓ Multi-platform manifest created
✓ Supports amd64 (Intel/AMD servers)
✓ Supports arm64 (Apple Silicon, AWS Graviton)
✓ Ready for universal deployment
Example 2: Annotate Manifest with OS/Architecture Details

Add detailed platform metadata to manifest entries.

# Create base manifest
docker manifest create anihpj/jobpost-api:v1.0 \
    anihpj/jobpost-api:v1.0-linux-amd64 \
    anihpj/jobpost-api:v1.0-linux-arm64

# Annotate AMD64 variant
docker manifest annotate anihpj/jobpost-api:v1.0 \
    anihpj/jobpost-api:v1.0-linux-amd64 \
    --os linux \
    --arch amd64

# Annotate ARM64 variant
docker manifest annotate anihpj/jobpost-api:v1.0 \
    anihpj/jobpost-api:v1.0-linux-arm64 \
    --os linux \
    --arch arm64 \
    --variant v8

# Verify annotations
docker manifest inspect anihpj/jobpost-api:v1.0
Created manifest list anihpj/jobpost-api:v1.0

Annotating amd64 image...
✓ OS: linux
✓ Architecture: amd64

Annotating arm64 image...
✓ OS: linux
✓ Architecture: arm64
✓ Variant: v8

Manifest Details:
{
  "manifests": [
    {
      "digest": "sha256:amd64digest...",
      "platform": {
        "architecture": "amd64",
        "os": "linux"
      }
    },
    {
      "digest": "sha256:arm64digest...",
      "platform": {
        "architecture": "arm64",
        "os": "linux",
        "variant": "v8"
      }
    }
  ]
}

✓ Annotations complete
✓ Platform metadata accurate
✓ Ready for deployment verification
Example 3: Push Multi-Platform Manifest to Registry

Deploy universal Django image to Docker Hub.

# Login to Docker Hub
echo "$DOCKER_PASSWORD" | docker login -u anihpjteam --password-stdin

# Push platform images first
docker push anihpj/django:latest-amd64
docker push anihpj/django:latest-arm64

# Create and push manifest
docker manifest create anihpj/django:latest \
    anihpj/django:latest-amd64 \
    anihpj/django:latest-arm64

docker manifest push --purge anihpj/django:latest

# Verify on different architecture
docker pull anihpj/django:latest
docker inspect anihpj/django:latest --format='{{.Architecture}}'
Login Succeeded

Pushing amd64 image...
latest-amd64: digest: sha256:abc123... size: 3456

Pushing arm64 image...
latest-arm64: digest: sha256:def456... size: 3398

Creating manifest list...
Created manifest list anihpj/django:latest

Pushing manifest to Docker Hub...
Pushed ref anihpj/django:latest with digest: sha256:manifest789...

✓ Manifest pushed successfully
✓ Local manifest purged
✓ Available on Docker Hub

Pulling on current system (arm64)...
latest: Pulling from anihpj/django
Selected architecture: arm64
Status: Downloaded newer image for anihpj/django:latest

Architecture verification:
arm64

✓ Docker automatically selected ARM64 variant
✓ No manual architecture specification needed
✓ Seamless multi-platform deployment working
Example 4: Inspect Manifest for Platform Availability

Verify supported platforms before deployment.

# Inspect public Django image
docker manifest inspect python:3.11-slim

# Inspect anihpj custom image
docker manifest inspect anihpj/skills-api:production

# Extract available platforms
docker manifest inspect anihpj/skills-api:production | \
    jq -r '.manifests[].platform | "\(.os)/\(.architecture)"'

# Check if specific platform exists
docker manifest inspect anihpj/skills-api:production | \
    jq '.manifests[] | select(.platform.architecture=="arm64")'
Inspecting python:3.11-slim manifest:
{
  "manifests": [
    {"platform": {"architecture": "amd64", "os": "linux"}},
    {"platform": {"architecture": "arm", "os": "linux", "variant": "v5"}},
    {"platform": {"architecture": "arm", "os": "linux", "variant": "v7"}},
    {"platform": {"architecture": "arm64", "os": "linux", "variant": "v8"}},
    {"platform": {"architecture": "386", "os": "linux"}},
    {"platform": {"architecture": "ppc64le", "os": "linux"}},
    {"platform": {"architecture": "s390x", "os": "linux"}}
  ]
}

Available Platforms for anihpj/skills-api:production:
linux/amd64
linux/arm64

ARM64 Platform Details:
{
  "mediaType": "application/vnd.docker.distribution.manifest.v2+json",
  "size": 1876,
  "digest": "sha256:arm64hash...",
  "platform": {
    "architecture": "arm64",
    "os": "linux",
    "variant": "v8"
  }
}

Platform Support Summary:
✓ amd64 (Intel/AMD servers) - Available
✓ arm64 (Apple Silicon, AWS Graviton) - Available
✗ armv7 (Raspberry Pi) - Not available

Deployment Recommendations:
✓ Safe for standard cloud deployments
✓ Compatible with modern development machines
✓ Works on AWS, GCP, Azure ARM instances
Example 5: Remove Manifest List

Clean up old or incorrectly created manifests.

# List local manifests
docker manifest inspect anihpj/django:v1.0-beta 2>/dev/null && echo "Exists" || echo "Not found"

# Remove specific manifest
docker manifest rm anihpj/django:v1.0-beta

# Remove multiple manifests
docker manifest rm \
    anihpj/django:v1.0-beta \
    anihpj/django:v1.0-rc1 \
    anihpj/django:v1.0-rc2

# Verify removal
docker manifest inspect anihpj/django:v1.0-beta
Checking manifest existence:
Exists

Removing manifest anihpj/django:v1.0-beta...
Deleted: anihpj/django:v1.0-beta

Removing multiple manifests...
Deleted: anihpj/django:v1.0-beta
Deleted: anihpj/django:v1.0-rc1
Deleted: anihpj/django:v1.0-rc2

✓ 3 manifests removed

Verification:
Error: No such manifest: anihpj/django:v1.0-beta

Cleanup Results:
✓ Old beta manifest removed
✓ Release candidate manifests cleaned
✓ Local manifest cache freed
✓ Only production v1.0 manifest remains

Note: This only removes local manifest references
Registry manifests require separate deletion via Docker Hub UI
or registry API for permanent removal.

docker manifest annotate

Official Docs: docker manifest annotate | Docker CLI: docker manifest annotate

Add or modify platform metadata in multi-architecture manifest lists.

Full Description

The docker manifest annotate command adds or modifies platform-specific metadata (OS, architecture, variant) for images within a manifest list. For your anihpj Django project, this enables correcting architecture details for User authentication images built externally, specifying OS variants for JobPost API containers targeting different Linux distributions, adding ARM variant information (v7/v8) for Skills model services, documenting platform requirements for Author profile images, and ensuring Location service manifests have accurate metadata for deployment validation.

When annotating manifests in your anihpj Django environment, Docker updates the manifest list metadata without rebuilding images. This supports correcting manifests created by CI/CD systems that didn't capture platform details, adding variant information for ARM images, specifying OS versions for Windows containers, documenting custom platform requirements, and preparing manifests for registries that require explicit platform declarations. Annotations ensure Docker clients select the correct image variant for their architecture.

Syntax

docker manifest annotate [OPTIONS] MANIFEST_LIST MANIFEST

# Common options:
docker manifest annotate --os linux --arch amd64 MANIFEST_LIST MANIFEST
docker manifest annotate --os linux --arch arm64 --variant v8 MANIFEST_LIST MANIFEST
docker manifest annotate --os-features "feature1,feature2" MANIFEST_LIST MANIFEST

Parameters Description

  • MANIFEST_LIST - Target manifest list name (e.g., anihpj/django:latest)
  • MANIFEST - Specific platform image to annotate within the list
  • --os - Operating system (linux, windows, darwin)
  • --arch - Architecture (amd64, arm64, arm, 386, ppc64le, s390x)
  • --variant - Architecture variant (v5, v6, v7, v8 for ARM)
  • --os-version - OS version string (e.g., 10.0.14393.2189 for Windows)
  • --os-features - Comma-separated OS features required by image

Attributes

  • Metadata Precision: Adds detailed platform information (OS, architecture, variant)
  • Post-Creation Editing: Modifies manifest without rebuilding images
  • Client Compatibility: Ensures Docker selects correct image for target platform
  • Variant Specification: Distinguishes ARM variants (v7 vs v8) for optimal compatibility
  • Feature Declaration: Documents OS features required by containerized application

Methods

1. Annotate Architecture and OS

Syntax: docker manifest annotate --os OS --arch ARCH MANIFEST_LIST MANIFEST

Parameters: OS (linux/windows), architecture (amd64/arm64)

Specifies the operating system and CPU architecture for a platform-specific image in the manifest list. Essential for Django images where build metadata is missing or incorrect. Annotate anihpj User API for linux/amd64, JobPost services for linux/arm64, Skills containers for specific platforms.

2. Add ARM Variant Information

Syntax: docker manifest annotate --arch arm64 --variant v8 MANIFEST_LIST MANIFEST

Parameters: architecture, variant (v5/v6/v7/v8)

Specifies ARM architecture variant to differentiate between ARMv7 (32-bit) and ARMv8 (64-bit). Critical for Django deployments on Raspberry Pi (armv7), Apple Silicon (arm64/v8), or AWS Graviton (arm64/v8). Ensures correct instruction set compatibility.

3. Declare OS Features

Syntax: docker manifest annotate --os-features "features" MANIFEST_LIST MANIFEST

Parameters: comma-separated feature list

Documents OS-level features required by the Django container (e.g., win32k for Windows GUI, seccomp for Linux security). Prevents deployment to incompatible hosts. Useful for anihpj images requiring specific kernel features or Windows capabilities.

Return Value

Returns annotation confirmation with updated manifest metadata:

  • Success (0): Manifest annotated successfully with platform details updated
  • Error (1): Annotation failed (manifest not found, invalid image reference, bad parameters)
  • Error (125): Docker daemon error during manifest modification

5 Practical Django Examples

Example 1: Annotate AMD64 and ARM64 Django Images

Add correct architecture metadata to multi-platform manifest.

# Create manifest list
docker manifest create anihpj/user-api:v2.0 \
    anihpj/user-api:v2.0-amd64 \
    anihpj/user-api:v2.0-arm64

# Annotate AMD64 variant
docker manifest annotate anihpj/user-api:v2.0 \
    anihpj/user-api:v2.0-amd64 \
    --os linux \
    --arch amd64

# Annotate ARM64 variant
docker manifest annotate anihpj/user-api:v2.0 \
    anihpj/user-api:v2.0-arm64 \
    --os linux \
    --arch arm64 \
    --variant v8

# Verify annotations
docker manifest inspect anihpj/user-api:v2.0 | \
    jq '.manifests[] | {arch: .platform.architecture, variant: .platform.variant}'
Created manifest list anihpj/user-api:v2.0 for 2 images

Annotating AMD64 image...
✓ anihpj/user-api:v2.0-amd64 annotated
  OS: linux
  Architecture: amd64

Annotating ARM64 image...
✓ anihpj/user-api:v2.0-arm64 annotated
  OS: linux
  Architecture: arm64
  Variant: v8

Platform Details:
{
  "arch": "amd64",
  "variant": null
}
{
  "arch": "arm64",
  "variant": "v8"
}

Annotation Summary:
✓ AMD64: Correctly identified for Intel/AMD servers
✓ ARM64: v8 variant specified for Apple Silicon/Graviton
✓ Docker will select appropriate image automatically
✓ Ready to push to registry
Example 2: Correct Missing Platform Metadata

Fix manifest created without architecture information.

# Check existing manifest (missing details)
docker manifest inspect anihpj/jobpost-api:latest | jq '.manifests[].platform'

# Fix AMD64 image metadata
docker manifest annotate anihpj/jobpost-api:latest \
    anihpj/jobpost-api:latest-x86 \
    --os linux \
    --arch amd64

# Fix ARM64 image metadata  
docker manifest annotate anihpj/jobpost-api:latest \
    anihpj/jobpost-api:latest-arm \
    --os linux \
    --arch arm64 \
    --variant v8

# Verify corrections
docker manifest inspect anihpj/jobpost-api:latest | jq '.manifests[].platform'
Before Annotation:
{
  "architecture": "unknown",
  "os": "linux"
}
{
  "architecture": "unknown",
  "os": "linux"
}

⚠ Architecture metadata missing!

Correcting AMD64 metadata...
✓ anihpj/jobpost-api:latest-x86 → linux/amd64

Correcting ARM64 metadata...
✓ anihpj/jobpost-api:latest-arm → linux/arm64/v8

After Annotation:
{
  "architecture": "amd64",
  "os": "linux"
}
{
  "architecture": "arm64",
  "os": "linux",
  "variant": "v8"
}

✓ Platform metadata corrected
✓ Docker clients can now properly select images
✓ Deployment errors prevented

Impact:
- Intel servers will pull amd64 variant
- ARM servers will pull arm64/v8 variant
- No more "exec format error" issues
Example 3: Specify ARM Variant for Raspberry Pi Deployment

Distinguish ARMv7 and ARMv8 for embedded Django deployments.

# Create manifest with multiple ARM variants
docker manifest create anihpj/skills-api:embedded \
    anihpj/skills-api:armv7 \
    anihpj/skills-api:armv8

# Annotate ARMv7 (32-bit, Raspberry Pi 2/3)
docker manifest annotate anihpj/skills-api:embedded \
    anihpj/skills-api:armv7 \
    --os linux \
    --arch arm \
    --variant v7

# Annotate ARMv8 (64-bit, Raspberry Pi 4/5)
docker manifest annotate anihpj/skills-api:embedded \
    anihpj/skills-api:armv8 \
    --os linux \
    --arch arm64 \
    --variant v8

# Test on Raspberry Pi
docker pull anihpj/skills-api:embedded
docker inspect anihpj/skills-api:embedded --format='{{.Architecture}} {{.Variant}}'
Created manifest for embedded deployments

Annotating ARMv7 variant...
✓ Target: Raspberry Pi 2, 3 (32-bit ARM)
✓ Architecture: arm
✓ Variant: v7

Annotating ARMv8 variant...
✓ Target: Raspberry Pi 4, 5 (64-bit ARM)
✓ Architecture: arm64
✓ Variant: v8

Platform Matrix:
┌──────────────┬──────────┬─────────┬─────────────────┐
│ Device       │ Arch     │ Variant │ Image Selected  │
├──────────────┼──────────┼─────────┼─────────────────┤
│ RPi 2/3      │ arm      │ v7      │ armv7           │
│ RPi 4/5      │ arm64    │ v8      │ armv8           │
│ Apple M1/M2  │ arm64    │ v8      │ armv8           │
│ AWS Graviton │ arm64    │ v8      │ armv8           │
└──────────────┴──────────┴─────────┴─────────────────┘

Testing on Raspberry Pi 4:
Pulling anihpj/skills-api:embedded...
Selected platform: linux/arm64/v8
Status: Downloaded newer image

Verification:
arm64 v8

✓ Correct variant selected automatically
✓ Django Skills API running on Raspberry Pi
✓ ARMv7/v8 distinction working properly
Example 4: Annotate Windows Container with OS Version

Specify Windows build version for container compatibility.

# Create manifest for Windows Django deployment
docker manifest create anihpj/django:windows \
    anihpj/django:ltsc2019 \
    anihpj/django:ltsc2022

# Annotate Windows Server 2019 image
docker manifest annotate anihpj/django:windows \
    anihpj/django:ltsc2019 \
    --os windows \
    --arch amd64 \
    --os-version "10.0.17763.3406"

# Annotate Windows Server 2022 image
docker manifest annotate anihpj/django:windows \
    anihpj/django:ltsc2022 \
    --os windows \
    --arch amd64 \
    --os-version "10.0.20348.1487"

# Inspect OS versions
docker manifest inspect anihpj/django:windows | \
    jq '.manifests[] | {os: .platform.os, version: .platform["os.version"]}'

✓ Architecture: amd64
✓ OS Version: 10.0.20348.1487 (Server 2022)

  "version": "10.0.17763.3406"
}

Windows Compatibility:
┌─────────────────────┬──────────────────┬─────────────────┐
│ Host OS             │ Build            │ Can Run         │
├─────────────────────┼──────────────────┼─────────────────┤
│ Windows Server 2019 │ 10.0.17763.*     │ LTSC2019 only   │
│ Windows Server 2022 │ 10.0.20348.*     │ LTSC2019/2022   │
│ Windows 11 Pro      │ 10.0.22621.*     │ Both (with VM)  │
└─────────────────────┴──────────────────┴─────────────────┘

✓ OS version metadata ensures compatibility
✓ Prevents container/host mismatch errors
✓ Docker selects compatible Windows variant
Example 5: Declare OS Features for Security Requirements

Annotate manifest with required Linux kernel features.

# Create manifest for security-hardened Django
docker manifest create anihpj/django:secure \
    anihpj/django:secure-amd64

# Annotate with required OS features
docker manifest annotate anihpj/django:secure \
    anihpj/django:secure-amd64 \
    --os linux \
    --arch amd64 \
    --os-features "seccomp,apparmor,selinux"

# Inspect features
docker manifest inspect anihpj/django:secure | \
    jq '.manifests[].platform["os.features"]'

# Attempt deployment
docker pull anihpj/django:secure
docker run --security-opt seccomp=default anihpj/django:secure
Created secure Django manifest

Annotating with security features...
✓ OS: linux
✓ Architecture: amd64
✓ Required Features:
  - seccomp (Secure Computing Mode)
  - apparmor (Application Armor)
  - selinux (Security-Enhanced Linux)

[
  "seccomp",
  "apparmor",
  "selinux"
]

✓ apparmor: enabled (profile loaded)
✓ selinux: enforcing mode

  Seccomp: Enabled (default profile)
  AppArmor: docker-default
  SELinux: system_u:system_r:container_t:s0

✓ All required OS features available
✓ Django running with enhanced security
✓ anihpj User authentication protected by kernel features

Feature Requirements Enforced:
- Syscall filtering via seccomp
- Mandatory Access Control via AppArmor
- SELinux context isolation active

docker manifest create

Official Docs: docker manifest create | Docker CLI: docker manifest create

Create a new multi-platform manifest list from existing images.

Full Description

The docker manifest create command creates a new manifest list that groups multiple platform-specific images under a single tag for multi-architecture deployments. For your anihpj Django project, this enables bundling User authentication images for Intel and ARM architectures, creating unified JobPost API manifests that work across developer laptops and cloud servers, combining Skills model images built for different platforms, grouping Author profile containers for heterogeneous deployment environments, and creating Location service manifests that support seamless deployment from MacBooks to AWS Graviton instances.

When creating manifests in your anihpj Django environment, Docker produces a manifest list referencing platform-specific image digests without duplicating image layers. The manifest serves as a pointer to architecture-appropriate images, enabling "docker pull anihpj/django:latest" to automatically fetch the correct variant for the user's system. This supports CI/CD pipelines building for multiple architectures, development teams with mixed hardware, and production deployments across diverse cloud infrastructure without separate image tags per platform.

Syntax

docker manifest create MANIFEST_LIST MANIFEST [MANIFEST...]

# Create with options:
docker manifest create --amend MANIFEST_LIST MANIFEST [MANIFEST...]
docker manifest create --insecure MANIFEST_LIST MANIFEST [MANIFEST...]

Parameters Description

  • MANIFEST_LIST - Name for the new manifest list (e.g., anihpj/django:v2.0)
  • MANIFEST - One or more platform-specific image references to include
  • --amend - Add images to an existing manifest list instead of creating new
  • --insecure - Allow communication with insecure (HTTP) registries

Attributes

  • Manifest List Creation: Combines multiple platform images under single tag
  • Digest References: Uses image digests rather than tags for consistency
  • Local Operation: Creates manifest locally before pushing to registry
  • Amendable: Can add or remove images from existing manifest lists
  • Automatic Platform Detection: Extracts OS/architecture from image metadata

Methods

1. Create New Multi-Platform Manifest

Syntax: docker manifest create MANIFEST_LIST IMAGE1 IMAGE2 ...

Parameters: manifest list name, platform-specific images

Creates a new manifest list from scratch combining multiple architecture-specific Django images. Include all platform variants you've built (amd64, arm64, armv7). For anihpj, combine Intel and ARM User API builds, unify JobPost service images, create universal Skills model manifests.

2. Amend Existing Manifest

Syntax: docker manifest create --amend MANIFEST_LIST IMAGE

Parameters: --amend flag, existing manifest, new images

Adds additional platform images to an existing manifest list without recreating from scratch. Useful when you've built additional platform variants (e.g., adding armv7 support to existing amd64/arm64 manifest) or correcting platform-specific images in the manifest.

3. Create from Insecure Registry

Syntax: docker manifest create --insecure MANIFEST_LIST IMAGE

Parameters: --insecure flag, images from HTTP registries

Creates manifest list using images from insecure (non-HTTPS) registries. Required for local development registries or internal CI systems without TLS certificates. Use cautiously—only for trusted internal networks and development environments.

Return Value

Returns manifest creation confirmation with included image references:

  • Success (0): Manifest list created successfully with platform images listed
  • Error (1): Creation failed (image not found, invalid reference, registry error)
  • Error (125): Docker daemon error during manifest creation

5 Practical Django Examples

Example 1: Create Basic Multi-Platform Manifest

Combine AMD64 and ARM64 Django images.

# Build platform-specific images
docker build --platform linux/amd64 -t anihpj/django:v1.0-amd64 .
docker build --platform linux/arm64 -t anihpj/django:v1.0-arm64 .

# Push individual platform images
docker push anihpj/django:v1.0-amd64
docker push anihpj/django:v1.0-arm64

# Create manifest list
docker manifest create anihpj/django:v1.0 \
    anihpj/django:v1.0-amd64 \
    anihpj/django:v1.0-arm64

# Verify creation
docker manifest inspect anihpj/django:v1.0
Building amd64 image...
[+] Building 45.2s (12/12) FINISHED
✓ Image built: anihpj/django:v1.0-amd64

Building arm64 image...
[+] Building 52.8s (12/12) FINISHED
✓ Image built: anihpj/django:v1.0-arm64

Pushing images...
v1.0-amd64: digest: sha256:amd64abc... size: 3456
v1.0-arm64: digest: sha256:arm64def... size: 3401

Creating manifest list anihpj/django:v1.0...
✓ Added anihpj/django:v1.0-amd64 (linux/amd64)
✓ Added anihpj/django:v1.0-arm64 (linux/arm64)

Manifest created successfully: anihpj/django:v1.0

{
  "manifests": [
    {
      "digest": "sha256:amd64abc...",
      "platform": {"architecture": "amd64", "os": "linux"}
    },
    {
      "digest": "sha256:arm64def...",
      "platform": {"architecture": "arm64", "os": "linux"}
    }
  ]
}

✓ Multi-platform manifest ready
✓ Supports amd64 and arm64
✓ Ready to push to registry
Example 2: Create Manifest with Three Architectures

Include AMD64, ARM64, and ARMv7 for maximum compatibility.

# Build for three platforms
docker buildx build --platform linux/amd64 \
    -t anihpj/jobpost-api:latest-amd64 --push .

docker buildx build --platform linux/arm64 \
    -t anihpj/jobpost-api:latest-arm64 --push .

docker buildx build --platform linux/arm/v7 \
    -t anihpj/jobpost-api:latest-armv7 --push .

# Create comprehensive manifest
docker manifest create anihpj/jobpost-api:latest \
    anihpj/jobpost-api:latest-amd64 \
    anihpj/jobpost-api:latest-arm64 \
    anihpj/jobpost-api:latest-armv7

# List platforms
docker manifest inspect anihpj/jobpost-api:latest | \
    jq -r '.manifests[] | "\(.platform.os)/\(.platform.architecture)\(.platform.variant // "")"'

✓ Built and pushed: anihpj/jobpost-api:latest-amd64

Building for linux/arm64...
✓ Built and pushed: anihpj/jobpost-api:latest-arm64

Building for linux/arm/v7...
✓ Built and pushed: anihpj/jobpost-api:latest-armv7

Creating manifest list...

linux/amd64
linux/arm64
linux/arm/v7

Deployment Compatibility:

│ Platform            │ Supported      │
├─────────────────────┼────────────────┤
│ Intel/AMD Servers   │ ✓ amd64        │
│ Apple Silicon       │ ✓ arm64        │
│ AWS Graviton        │ ✓ arm64        │
│ Raspberry Pi 2/3    │ ✓ armv7        │
│ Raspberry Pi 4/5    │ ✓ arm64        │
│ Standard Cloud VMs  │ ✓ amd64        │
└─────────────────────┴────────────────┘

✓ Universal JobPost API manifest
✓ Works on virtually all modern hardware
✓ Developers can use any machine
Example 3: Amend Manifest to Add New Platform

Add ARM variant to existing manifest without recreating.

# Check existing manifest
docker manifest inspect anihpj/skills-api:v2.0

# Build new ARMv7 variant
docker build --platform linux/arm/v7 -t anihpj/skills-api:v2.0-armv7 .
docker push anihpj/skills-api:v2.0-armv7

# Amend existing manifest (don't recreate)
docker manifest create --amend anihpj/skills-api:v2.0 \
    anihpj/skills-api:v2.0-armv7

# Verify amendment
docker manifest inspect anihpj/skills-api:v2.0 | \
    jq '.manifests | length'
Current manifest platforms:
- linux/amd64
- linux/arm64
Total: 2 platforms

✓ Kept anihpj/skills-api:v2.0-arm64
✓ Added anihpj/skills-api:v2.0-armv7

Amended manifest anihpj/skills-api:v2.0

- linux/arm64 (preserved)
- linux/arm/v7 (NEW)

✓ ARMv7 support added
✓ Existing platforms unchanged
✓ Raspberry Pi deployment now possible
✓ No need to recreate entire manifest
Example 4: Create Manifest from BuildKit Multi-Platform Build

Combine images from single buildx command.

# Build all platforms simultaneously with buildx
docker buildx build \
    --platform linux/amd64,linux/arm64,linux/arm/v7 \
    -t anihpj/author-api:multi \
    --push \
    .

# BuildKit automatically creates manifest
# Verify the auto-generated manifest
docker manifest inspect anihpj/author-api:multi

# Pull on different architectures
# On ARM64 Mac:
docker pull anihpj/author-api:multi
docker inspect anihpj/author-api:multi | grep Architecture

# On AMD64 Linux:
docker pull anihpj/author-api:multi
docker inspect anihpj/author-api:multi | grep Architecture

 => [linux/arm64 stage-1 2/5] RUN pip install -r requirements.txt

 => pushing manifest for anihpj/author-api:multi

Example 5: Create Manifest from Local Development Registry

Work with insecure local registry during development.

# Start local insecure registry
docker run -d -p 5000:5000 --name registry registry:2

# Build and push to local registry
docker build --platform linux/amd64 -t localhost:5000/anihpj/location:dev-amd64 .
docker push localhost:5000/anihpj/location:dev-amd64

docker build --platform linux/arm64 -t localhost:5000/anihpj/location:dev-arm64 .
docker push localhost:5000/anihpj/location:dev-arm64

# Create manifest from insecure registry
docker manifest create --insecure localhost:5000/anihpj/location:dev \
    localhost:5000/anihpj/location:dev-amd64 \
    localhost:5000/anihpj/location:dev-arm64

# Push manifest to local registry
docker manifest push --insecure localhost:5000/anihpj/location:dev

# Test pull from another developer's machine
docker pull --insecure-registry=localhost:5000 localhost:5000/anihpj/location:dev
Starting local development registry...
Registry running on localhost:5000

✓ Pushed to local registry

Building and pushing ARM64 variant...
dev-arm64: digest: sha256:localarm64... size: 3178
✓ Pushed to local registry

⚠ Using insecure registry localhost:5000
✓ Created manifest list localhost:5000/anihpj/location:dev

Pushing manifest to local registry...
Pushed manifest to localhost:5000/anihpj/location:dev

dev: Pulling from anihpj/location
Platform auto-selected: linux/amd64
Status: Downloaded newer image for localhost:5000/anihpj/location:dev

Local Development Benefits:
✓ Fast iteration without remote pushes
✓ Test multi-platform manifests locally
✓ No Docker Hub rate limits
✓ Works offline

Note: --insecure flag required for HTTP registries
For production, always use HTTPS registries with valid certificates

docker manifest inspect

Official Docs: docker manifest inspect | Docker CLI: docker manifest inspect

Display detailed information about image manifests and supported platforms.

Full Description

The docker manifest inspect command displays detailed manifest information including supported platforms, layer digests, image sizes, and architecture metadata for both single images and manifest lists. For your anihpj Django project, this enables verifying User authentication image platform support before deployment, checking JobPost API architecture availability across cloud providers, confirming Skills model image compatibility with target hardware, validating Author profile container platforms for mixed-architecture clusters, and inspecting Location service manifests to ensure ARM64 and AMD64 support for development and production environments.

When inspecting manifests in your anihpj Django environment, Docker returns JSON-formatted manifest data showing all platform variants, layer information, and metadata. This supports pre-deployment verification of architecture support, troubleshooting "exec format error" issues by confirming platform availability, documenting image architecture compatibility for deployment runbooks, comparing manifest versions to track platform additions, and validating CI/CD pipeline outputs to ensure all target platforms are built correctly.

Syntax

docker manifest inspect [OPTIONS] MANIFEST_LIST|IMAGE

# Common options:
docker manifest inspect --verbose MANIFEST_LIST
docker manifest inspect --insecure MANIFEST_LIST

Parameters Description

  • MANIFEST_LIST|IMAGE - Image or manifest list reference to inspect
  • --verbose, -v - Show additional image layer and configuration details
  • --insecure - Allow inspection from insecure (HTTP) registries

Attributes

  • Platform Discovery: Lists all supported OS/architecture combinations
  • JSON Output: Returns structured data suitable for parsing and automation
  • Digest Information: Shows SHA256 digests for manifest and layers
  • Size Reporting: Displays manifest size and layer sizes per platform
  • Remote Inspection: Can inspect images without pulling them locally

Methods

1. Inspect Manifest List Platforms

Syntax: docker manifest inspect MANIFEST_LIST

Parameters: manifest list reference (local or remote)

Displays all platform variants included in a manifest list with architecture, OS, and variant details. Use to verify anihpj Django images support required deployment targets, check third-party base images (like python:3.11) for platform coverage, confirm CI/CD built all expected architectures.

2. Inspect with Verbose Layer Details

Syntax: docker manifest inspect --verbose IMAGE

Parameters: --verbose flag, image reference

Shows detailed layer information including layer digests, sizes, and build metadata. Useful for comparing layer sharing between platform variants, debugging layer caching issues in multi-platform builds, verifying image composition and understanding image size by platform.

3. Inspect Without Authentication

Syntax: docker manifest inspect public/image:tag

Parameters: public image reference

Inspects public images from Docker Hub or other registries without authentication. Check platform support of base images before building, verify official Django/Python image architectures, inspect competitor or reference implementations for platform strategy.

Return Value

Returns JSON-formatted manifest data with platform and layer information:

  • Success (0): Manifest data retrieved and displayed successfully
  • Error (1): Inspection failed (image not found, network error, authentication required)
  • Error (125): Docker daemon error during manifest retrieval

5 Practical Django Examples

Example 1: Check Platform Support Before Deployment

Verify Django image supports target architecture.

# Inspect anihpj Django image
docker manifest inspect anihpj/django:production

# Extract platform list
docker manifest inspect anihpj/django:production | \
    jq -r '.manifests[] | "\(.platform.os)/\(.platform.architecture)\(if .platform.variant then "/"+.platform.variant else "" end)"'

# Check if ARM64 is supported
docker manifest inspect anihpj/django:production | \
    jq '.manifests[] | select(.platform.architecture=="arm64")' | \
    jq -e 'length > 0' && echo "ARM64 supported" || echo "ARM64 NOT supported"

# Check deployment readiness
echo "Deployment Platform: linux/arm64 (AWS Graviton)"
docker manifest inspect anihpj/django:production | \
    jq -e '.manifests[] | select(.platform.os=="linux" and .platform.architecture=="arm64")' \
    && echo "✓ Compatible" || echo "✗ Incompatible"
Inspecting anihpj/django:production manifest...

{
  "schemaVersion": 2,
  "mediaType": "application/vnd.docker.distribution.manifest.list.v2+json",
  "manifests": [

      "size": 1789,
      "digest": "sha256:amd64digest...",
      "platform": {
        "architecture": "amd64",
        "os": "linux"
      }
    },
    {
      "mediaType": "application/vnd.docker.distribution.manifest.v2+json",
      "size": 1654,
      "digest": "sha256:arm64digest...",
      "platform": {
        "architecture": "arm64",
        "os": "linux",
        "variant": "v8"
      }
    }
  ]
}

}
✓ Compatible

✓ Safe to deploy to AWS Graviton instances
✓ ARM64 variant available and ready
Example 2: Compare Platform Availability Across Versions

Track platform support changes between image versions.

# Compare v1.0 and v2.0 platforms
echo "Version 1.0 platforms:"
docker manifest inspect anihpj/jobpost-api:v1.0 | \
    jq -r '.manifests[] | .platform.architecture' | sort

echo ""
echo "Version 2.0 platforms:"
docker manifest inspect anihpj/jobpost-api:v2.0 | \
    jq -r '.manifests[] | .platform.architecture' | sort

# Find new platforms in v2.0
comm -13 \
    <(docker manifest inspect anihpj/jobpost-api:v1.0 | jq -r '.manifests[].platform.architecture' | sort) \
    <(docker manifest inspect anihpj/jobpost-api:v2.0 | jq -r '.manifests[].platform.architecture' | sort)

# Show platform details side-by-side
echo ""
echo "Platform Coverage Comparison:"
echo "v1.0:" $(docker manifest inspect anihpj/jobpost-api:v1.0 | jq '.manifests | length') "platforms"
echo "v2.0:" $(docker manifest inspect anihpj/jobpost-api:v2.0 | jq '.manifests | length') "platforms"
Version 1.0 platforms:
amd64
arm64

Version 2.0 platforms:
amd64
arm
arm64

New platforms added in v2.0:
arm

Platform Coverage Comparison:
v1.0: 2 platforms
v2.0: 3 platforms

Detailed Comparison:
┌─────────────┬────────┬────────┐
│ Platform    │ v1.0   │ v2.0   │
├─────────────┼────────┼────────┤
│ amd64       │ ✓      │ ✓      │
│ arm64       │ ✓      │ ✓      │
│ arm (v7)    │ ✗      │ ✓ NEW  │
└─────────────┴────────┴────────┘

Version 2.0 Improvements:
✓ Maintained amd64 support
✓ Maintained arm64 support
✓ Added ARMv7 support (Raspberry Pi)

Impact:
- v2.0 has wider hardware compatibility
- Raspberry Pi deployments now possible
- Embedded Django applications supported
- Backward compatible with v1.0 platforms
Example 3: Inspect Base Image Platform Support

Verify Python base image supports required architectures.

# Check official Python image platforms
docker manifest inspect python:3.11-slim

# Count available platforms
docker manifest inspect python:3.11-slim | \
    jq '.manifests | length'

# List all platforms
docker manifest inspect python:3.11-slim | \
    jq -r '.manifests[] | "\(.platform.os)/\(.platform.architecture)\(if .platform.variant then "/"+.platform.variant else "" end)"' | \
    sort

# Check specific platform availability
echo "Checking anihpj deployment targets:"
for platform in "linux/amd64" "linux/arm64" "linux/arm/v7"; do
    os=$(echo $platform | cut -d/ -f1)
    arch=$(echo $platform | cut -d/ -f2)
    variant=$(echo $platform | cut -d/ -f3)
    
    if docker manifest inspect python:3.11-slim | \
        jq -e ".manifests[] | select(.platform.os==\"$os\" and .platform.architecture==\"$arch\")" > /dev/null; then
        echo "✓ $platform supported"
    else
        echo "✗ $platform NOT supported"
    fi
done

✓ Safe to use as base for anihpj Django
✓ No platform limitations for deployment

Example 4: Inspect with Verbose Layer Details

Examine layer composition and sizes per platform.

# Inspect with verbose output
docker manifest inspect --verbose anihpj/skills-api:latest

# Extract layer sizes for each platform
docker manifest inspect --verbose anihpj/skills-api:latest | \
    jq '.[] | {platform: .Descriptor.platform, size: .LayersSize}'

# Compare layer count across platforms
echo "Layer Analysis:"
docker manifest inspect --verbose anihpj/skills-api:latest | \
    jq -r '.[] | "\(.Descriptor.platform.architecture): \(.SchemaV2Manifest.layers | length) layers"'

# Calculate total size per platform
docker manifest inspect --verbose anihpj/skills-api:latest | \
    jq '.[] | {arch: .Descriptor.platform.architecture, total_mb: (([.SchemaV2Manifest.layers[].size] | add) / 1024 / 1024 | floor)}'
Verbose manifest inspection...

Platform: linux/amd64
  Layers: 8
  Base layer: sha256:abc123... (27.5 MB)
  Django layer: sha256:def456... (45.2 MB)
  Requirements: sha256:ghi789... (125.8 MB)
  Application: sha256:jkl012... (12.3 MB)
  [... 4 more layers ...]
  Total Size: 267 MB

Platform: linux/arm64
  Layers: 8 (same structure)
  Base layer: sha256:mno345... (26.8 MB)
  Django layer: sha256:pqr678... (44.1 MB)
  Requirements: sha256:stu901... (118.9 MB)
  Application: sha256:vwx234... (12.3 MB)
  [... 4 more layers ...]
  Total Size: 251 MB

Layer Analysis:
amd64: 8 layers
arm64: 8 layers

Size Comparison:
{
  "arch": "amd64",
  "total_mb": 267
}
{
  "arch": "arm64",
  "total_mb": 251
}

Insights:
✓ Consistent layer count across platforms
✓ ARM64 image 6% smaller than AMD64
✓ Application layer identical (12.3 MB) - good
✓ Dependencies compiled differently per arch

Layer Sharing:
- Application code layers: Identical
- Python dependencies: Different (compiled binaries)
- Base OS layers: Platform-specific

Storage Optimization:
- Total if separate: 518 MB
- Shared layers: Application + source = ~50 MB
- Registry storage: ~468 MB unique + 50 MB shared
Example 5: Automated Deployment Verification Script

Pre-deployment check for platform compatibility.

#!/bin/bash
# Deployment readiness checker for anihpj Django

IMAGE="anihpj/django:production"
REQUIRED_PLATFORMS=("linux/amd64" "linux/arm64")

echo "=== anihpj Django Deployment Verification ==="
echo "Image: $IMAGE"
echo ""

# Check if image exists
if ! docker manifest inspect $IMAGE &>/dev/null; then
    echo "✗ ERROR: Image not found"
    exit 1
fi

echo "✓ Image found"
echo ""

# Extract available platforms
echo "Available platforms:"
AVAILABLE=$(docker manifest inspect $IMAGE | \
    jq -r '.manifests[] | "\(.platform.os)/\(.platform.architecture)"')
echo "$AVAILABLE"
echo ""

# Check required platforms
echo "Checking required platforms:"
ALL_FOUND=true
for platform in "${REQUIRED_PLATFORMS[@]}"; do
    if echo "$AVAILABLE" | grep -q "^$platform$"; then
        echo "✓ $platform - Available"
    else
        echo "✗ $platform - MISSING"
        ALL_FOUND=false
    fi
done
echo ""

# Get image size info
echo "Image sizes:"
docker manifest inspect --verbose $IMAGE | \
    jq -r '.[] | "\(.Descriptor.platform.architecture): \((([.SchemaV2Manifest.layers[].size] | add) / 1024 / 1024 | floor)) MB"'
echo ""

# Final verdict
if $ALL_FOUND; then
    echo "╔════════════════════════════════════════╗"
    echo "║  ✓ DEPLOYMENT READY                   ║"
    echo "║  All required platforms available      ║"
    echo "╚════════════════════════════════════════╝"
    exit 0
else
    echo "╔════════════════════════════════════════╗"
    echo "║  ✗ DEPLOYMENT BLOCKED                  ║"
    echo "║  Missing required platforms            ║"
    echo "╚════════════════════════════════════════╝"
    exit 1
fi
=== anihpj Django Deployment Verification ===
Image: anihpj/django:production

✓ Image found

Available platforms:
linux/amd64
linux/arm64

Checking required platforms:
✓ linux/amd64 - Available
✓ linux/arm64 - Available

Image sizes:
amd64: 312 MB
arm64: 298 MB

╔════════════════════════════════════════╗
║  ✓ DEPLOYMENT READY                   ║
║  All required platforms available      ║
╚════════════════════════════════════════╝

Deployment Targets Verified:
✓ AWS EC2 (amd64) - Compatible
✓ AWS Graviton (arm64) - Compatible
✓ GCP Compute (amd64) - Compatible
✓ Azure VMs (amd64) - Compatible
✓ Developer MacBooks (arm64) - Compatible

Proceeding with deployment...
Exit Code: 0

docker manifest push

Official Docs: docker manifest push | Docker CLI: docker manifest push

Upload multi-platform manifest list to a Docker registry.

Full Description

The docker manifest push command uploads a locally created manifest list to a Docker registry, making multi-platform images available for deployment and distribution. For your anihpj Django project, this enables publishing User authentication images to Docker Hub for public access, pushing JobPost API manifests to private registries for production deployment, distributing Skills model images across team members and CI/CD systems, uploading Author profile manifests to artifact repositories for versioned releases, and making Location service multi-platform images available for deployment across heterogeneous infrastructure.

When pushing manifests in your anihpj Django environment, Docker uploads the manifest list metadata to the registry, creating references to already-pushed platform-specific images without re-uploading image layers. The pushed manifest enables users to "docker pull anihpj/django:latest" from any architecture and automatically receive the correct platform variant. This supports production release workflows where platform images are built in CI/CD and manifests published after all platforms complete, team distribution of development images across mixed hardware, and public open-source releases supporting the widest range of user systems.

Syntax

docker manifest push [OPTIONS] MANIFEST_LIST

# Common options:
docker manifest push --purge MANIFEST_LIST
docker manifest push --insecure MANIFEST_LIST

Parameters Description

  • MANIFEST_LIST - Name of the manifest list to push to registry
  • --purge - Remove the local manifest list after successful push
  • --insecure - Allow push to insecure (HTTP) registries

Attributes

  • Registry Upload: Pushes manifest metadata to remote Docker registry
  • Reference Creation: Creates manifest list tags in registry namespace
  • Metadata Only: Uploads manifest JSON without re-pushing image layers
  • Atomic Operation: Manifest becomes available to all users simultaneously
  • Optional Cleanup: Can auto-remove local manifest after push with --purge

Methods

1. Push Manifest to Docker Hub

Syntax: docker manifest push namespace/image:tag

Parameters: Docker Hub image reference with manifest list

Uploads manifest list to Docker Hub public or private repository. Requires prior authentication with "docker login". Push anihpj Django manifests for open-source distribution, team images to organization repos, or production releases to private repositories. Manifest becomes immediately pullable worldwide.

2. Push with Local Cleanup

Syntax: docker manifest push --purge MANIFEST_LIST

Parameters: --purge flag to remove local manifest after push

Pushes manifest and removes local manifest cache in one operation. Useful in CI/CD pipelines to avoid manifest accumulation, cleanup after successful release, or ensure fresh manifest creation on next build. Prevents stale local manifests from interfering with future operations.

3. Push to Private Registry

Syntax: docker manifest push registry.company.com/image:tag

Parameters: private registry hostname and image reference

Uploads manifest to organization's private Docker registry or cloud artifact repository (ECR, ACR, GCR). Configure registry authentication first. Push production anihpj manifests to company registry, staging images to development registry, or versioned releases to artifact management system.

Return Value

Returns push confirmation with manifest digest and registry location:

  • Success (0): Manifest pushed successfully with SHA256 digest returned
  • Error (1): Push failed (authentication error, registry unreachable, manifest invalid)
  • Error (125): Docker daemon error during manifest upload

5 Practical Django Examples

Example 1: Push Multi-Platform Manifest to Docker Hub

Publish anihpj Django image for public distribution.

# Login to Docker Hub
echo "$DOCKER_PASSWORD" | docker login -u anihpjteam --password-stdin

# Verify manifest is ready
docker manifest inspect anihpj/django:v1.0

# Push platform-specific images first (if not already pushed)
docker push anihpj/django:v1.0-amd64
docker push anihpj/django:v1.0-arm64

# Push the manifest list
docker manifest push anihpj/django:v1.0

# Verify on Docker Hub
docker manifest inspect anihpj/django:v1.0

# Test pull from different architecture
docker pull anihpj/django:v1.0
Login Succeeded

  - linux/arm64

  "manifests": [
    {"platform": {"architecture": "amd64", "os": "linux"}},
    {"platform": {"architecture": "arm64", "os": "linux"}}

v1.0: Pulling from anihpj/django
Selected platform: linux/arm64

✓ Manifest live on Docker Hub
✓ Multi-platform pull working
✓ Users worldwide can pull appropriate variant

Example 2: Push with Purge to Clean Up Local Cache

Upload manifest and remove local copy automatically.

# Create manifest for JobPost API
docker manifest create anihpj/jobpost-api:v2.5 \
    anihpj/jobpost-api:v2.5-amd64 \
    anihpj/jobpost-api:v2.5-arm64 \
    anihpj/jobpost-api:v2.5-armv7

# Check local manifest exists
docker manifest inspect anihpj/jobpost-api:v2.5 > /dev/null && echo "Local manifest exists"

# Push with purge flag
docker manifest push --purge anihpj/jobpost-api:v2.5

# Verify local manifest was removed
docker manifest inspect anihpj/jobpost-api:v2.5 2>&1 | grep -q "no such manifest" && \
    echo "Local manifest purged" || echo "Manifest still exists"

# Can still pull from registry
docker pull anihpj/jobpost-api:v2.5
Creating manifest list for 3 platforms...
✓ Created anihpj/jobpost-api:v2.5

Local manifest check:
Local manifest exists

Pushing with --purge flag...
Pushing manifest list anihpj/jobpost-api:v2.5...
✓ Pushed to registry: sha256:jobpostmanifest...
✓ Purging local manifest cache...
✓ Local manifest removed

Local manifest verification:
Error: no such manifest: anihpj/jobpost-api:v2.5
Local manifest purged

Registry pull test:
v2.5: Pulling from anihpj/jobpost-api
Selected: linux/amd64
Status: Downloaded newer image

Benefits of --purge:
✓ Cleans up local Docker storage
✓ Prevents manifest conflicts on rebuild
✓ Forces fresh manifest creation next time
✓ Ideal for CI/CD pipelines
✓ Ensures registry is single source of truth

CI/CD Use Case:
1. Build platform images
2. Create manifest list
3. Push with --purge
4. Next build starts fresh without old manifests
Example 3: Push to Private AWS ECR Registry

Deploy production Django manifest to Amazon ECR.

# Login to AWS ECR
aws ecr get-login-password --region us-east-1 | \
    docker login --username AWS --password-stdin 123456789012.dkr.ecr.us-east-1.amazonaws.com

# Tag images for ECR
docker tag anihpj/skills-api:prod-amd64 \
    123456789012.dkr.ecr.us-east-1.amazonaws.com/anihpj/skills-api:prod-amd64

docker tag anihpj/skills-api:prod-arm64 \
    123456789012.dkr.ecr.us-east-1.amazonaws.com/anihpj/skills-api:prod-arm64

# Push platform images to ECR
docker push 123456789012.dkr.ecr.us-east-1.amazonaws.com/anihpj/skills-api:prod-amd64
docker push 123456789012.dkr.ecr.us-east-1.amazonaws.com/anihpj/skills-api:prod-arm64

# Create manifest for ECR
docker manifest create 123456789012.dkr.ecr.us-east-1.amazonaws.com/anihpj/skills-api:production \
    123456789012.dkr.ecr.us-east-1.amazonaws.com/anihpj/skills-api:prod-amd64 \
    123456789012.dkr.ecr.us-east-1.amazonaws.com/anihpj/skills-api:prod-arm64

# Push manifest to ECR
docker manifest push 123456789012.dkr.ecr.us-east-1.amazonaws.com/anihpj/skills-api:production

# Deploy from ECS/EKS
# Clusters will automatically pull correct architecture
Authenticating with AWS ECR...
Login Succeeded: 123456789012.dkr.ecr.us-east-1.amazonaws.com

Tagging images for ECR namespace...

✓ Tagged arm64 variant

prod-arm64: digest: sha256:ecrarm64... size: 3489

✓ Production manifest live in ECR

Deployment Scenarios:
┌─────────────────────┬──────────────────┬─────────────┐
│ Target              │ Instance Type    │ Pulls       │
├─────────────────────┼──────────────────┼─────────────┤
│ ECS on EC2 (Intel)  │ t3.large         │ amd64       │
│ ECS on Graviton     │ t4g.medium       │ arm64       │
│ EKS Mixed Cluster   │ m6g + m5 nodes   │ Both        │
│ Fargate (Intel)     │ Fargate          │ amd64       │
│ Fargate (Graviton)  │ Fargate ARM      │ arm64       │
└─────────────────────┴──────────────────┴─────────────┘

Production Benefits:
✓ Single image tag for all environments
✓ No architecture-specific deployment configs
✓ Graviton cost savings (20-40% cheaper)
✓ Seamless migration between instance types
✓ Developer/staging/prod consistency
Example 4: CI/CD Pipeline Manifest Push

Automated manifest creation and push in GitHub Actions.

# .github/workflows/release.yml excerpt
# This runs after multi-platform builds complete

# Build was done with:
# docker buildx build --platform linux/amd64,linux/arm64 --push ...

# Now create and push manifest
echo "Creating manifest for anihpj/author-api:${{ github.sha }}"

docker manifest create anihpj/author-api:${{ github.sha }} \
    anihpj/author-api:${{ github.sha }}-amd64 \
    anihpj/author-api:${{ github.sha }}-arm64

docker manifest push anihpj/author-api:${{ github.sha }}

# Also tag as 'latest' if main branch
if [ "${{ github.ref }}" == "refs/heads/main" ]; then
    docker manifest create anihpj/author-api:latest \
        anihpj/author-api:${{ github.sha }}-amd64 \
        anihpj/author-api:${{ github.sha }}-arm64
    
    docker manifest push --purge anihpj/author-api:latest
    
    echo "✓ Latest manifest updated"
fi

# Verify manifest
docker manifest inspect anihpj/author-api:${{ github.sha }}

echo "✓ Release complete: anihpj/author-api:${{ github.sha }}"

Step: Update 'latest' tag (main branch)

Step: Verify release

  - linux/arm64

│ Action             │ Status      │
├────────────────────┼─────────────┤
│ Build amd64        │ ✓ Success   │
│ Build arm64        │ ✓ Success   │
│ Create manifest    │ ✓ Success   │
│ Push commit tag    │ ✓ Success   │
│ Push latest tag    │ ✓ Success   │
│ Verify deployment  │ ✓ Success   │
└────────────────────┴─────────────┘

Deployment Tags Available:
- anihpj/author-api:abc123def (commit-specific)
- anihpj/author-api:latest (main branch)

Ready for deployment to production
Example 5: Push to Multiple Registries

Distribute Django manifest across Docker Hub and private registry.

#!/bin/bash
# Multi-registry manifest push script

IMAGE_NAME="location-api"
VERSION="v3.2"
PLATFORMS=("amd64" "arm64")

REGISTRIES=(
    "anihpj"  # Docker Hub
    "registry.company.com/anihpj"  # Private registry
)

echo "Pushing anihpj/$IMAGE_NAME:$VERSION to multiple registries..."

for registry in "${REGISTRIES[@]}"; do
    echo ""
    echo "=== Pushing to $registry ==="
    
    # Create manifest for this registry
    MANIFEST_REF="$registry/$IMAGE_NAME:$VERSION"
    IMAGE_REFS=""
    
    for platform in "${PLATFORMS[@]}"; do
        IMAGE_REFS="$IMAGE_REFS $registry/$IMAGE_NAME:$VERSION-$platform"
    done
    
    docker manifest create $MANIFEST_REF $IMAGE_REFS
    docker manifest push --purge $MANIFEST_REF
    
    echo "✓ Pushed to $registry"
done

echo ""
echo "Verifying across registries..."

for registry in "${REGISTRIES[@]}"; do
    docker manifest inspect $registry/$IMAGE_NAME:$VERSION | \
        jq -r '.manifests[].platform | "\(.os)/\(.architecture)"'
done

echo ""
echo "✓ Multi-registry push complete"
Pushing anihpj/location-api:v3.2 to multiple registries...

=== Pushing to anihpj (Docker Hub) ===
Creating manifest: anihpj/location-api:v3.2
  Including: anihpj/location-api:v3.2-amd64
  Including: anihpj/location-api:v3.2-arm64

Pushing to Docker Hub...
✓ Pushed: sha256:hubmanifest...
✓ Purged local cache

✓ Pushed to anihpj

=== Pushing to registry.company.com/anihpj (Private Registry) ===
Creating manifest: registry.company.com/anihpj/location-api:v3.2
  Including: registry.company.com/anihpj/location-api:v3.2-amd64
  Including: registry.company.com/anihpj/location-api:v3.2-arm64

Pushing to private registry...
✓ Pushed: sha256:privatmanifest...
✓ Purged local cache

✓ Pushed to registry.company.com/anihpj

Verifying across registries...

Docker Hub platforms:
linux/amd64
linux/arm64

Private Registry platforms:
linux/amd64
linux/arm64

✓ Multi-registry push complete

Distribution Summary:
┌────────────────────────────┬──────────┬────────────┐
│ Registry                   │ Public   │ Platforms  │
├────────────────────────────┼──────────┼────────────┤
│ Docker Hub (anihpj)        │ Yes      │ amd64+arm64│
│ registry.company.com       │ No       │ amd64+arm64│
└────────────────────────────┴──────────┴────────────┘

Use Cases:
- Docker Hub: Open source, public access, CI/CD
- Private: Production deployments, internal teams

Both registries have identical multi-platform support

docker manifest rm

Official Docs: docker manifest rm | Docker CLI: docker manifest rm

Remove one or more manifest lists from local storage.

Full Description

The docker manifest rm command deletes manifest lists from local Docker storage, freeing disk space and removing outdated or incorrectly created manifests. For your anihpj Django project, this enables cleaning up failed User authentication manifest attempts during development, removing old JobPost API manifest versions after successful releases, deleting experimental Skills model manifests that are no longer needed, clearing Author profile manifest drafts before production push, and managing Location service manifest cache to prevent storage accumulation in CI/CD environments.

When removing manifests in your anihpj Django environment, Docker only deletes the local manifest list metadata—platform-specific images and registry-stored manifests remain unaffected. This supports iterative manifest development where you create, inspect, modify, and recreate manifests, CI/CD cleanup of temporary manifests after successful pushes, local storage management to free disk space, and correcting manifest mistakes before pushing to registries. The command is safe and reversible—deleted local manifests can be recreated from platform images or re-inspected from registries.

Syntax

docker manifest rm MANIFEST_LIST [MANIFEST_LIST...]

# Remove single manifest:
docker manifest rm anihpj/django:dev

# Remove multiple manifests:
docker manifest rm manifest1 manifest2 manifest3

Parameters Description

  • MANIFEST_LIST - One or more manifest list names to remove from local storage

Attributes

  • Local Only: Removes manifest from local Docker storage, not from registries
  • Storage Cleanup: Frees disk space occupied by manifest list metadata
  • Safe Operation: Doesn't delete platform-specific images or registry manifests
  • Batch Deletion: Can remove multiple manifests in single command
  • Reversible: Deleted manifests can be recreated or re-pulled from registry

Methods

1. Remove Single Manifest

Syntax: docker manifest rm MANIFEST_LIST

Parameters: manifest list reference to delete

Deletes one specific manifest list from local storage. Use to remove incorrectly created anihpj Django manifests, clean up experimental manifest versions, delete development manifests after successful production push, or free space occupied by large manifest lists no longer needed.

2. Remove Multiple Manifests

Syntax: docker manifest rm MANIFEST1 MANIFEST2 ...

Parameters: space-separated list of manifests

Batch deletes multiple manifest lists in one command. Efficient for CI/CD cleanup after release cycles, removing entire version series (beta, rc1, rc2), clearing development manifest accumulation, or periodic maintenance of local Docker storage.

3. Clean Up Failed Manifest Attempts

Syntax: docker manifest rm $(docker manifest inspect manifest 2>&1 | grep -q "error" && echo manifest)

Parameters: conditional removal based on manifest state

Removes manifests that are corrupted or incomplete from failed creation attempts. Part of CI/CD error recovery, development troubleshooting, or maintenance scripts. Ensures clean slate for manifest recreation after addressing underlying issues.

Return Value

Returns deletion confirmation for removed manifest lists:

  • Success (0): Manifest list(s) deleted successfully from local storage
  • Error (1): Deletion failed (manifest not found locally, permission error)
  • Error (125): Docker daemon error during manifest removal

5 Practical Django Examples

Example 1: Remove Development Manifest After Production Push

Clean up local manifest after successful registry push.

# Create and test development manifest
docker manifest create anihpj/django:dev-test \
    anihpj/django:dev-amd64 \
    anihpj/django:dev-arm64

# Inspect and verify
docker manifest inspect anihpj/django:dev-test

# After successful push to production
docker manifest push anihpj/django:production

# Remove local development manifest
docker manifest rm anihpj/django:dev-test

# Verify removal
docker manifest inspect anihpj/django:dev-test 2>&1
Creating development manifest...
✓ Created anihpj/django:dev-test with 2 platforms

Inspecting manifest...
{
  "manifests": [
    {"platform": {"architecture": "amd64"}},
    {"platform": {"architecture": "arm64"}}
  ]
}

Testing complete. Pushing to production...
✓ Pushed anihpj/django:production

Removing local development manifest...
Deleted: anihpj/django:dev-test

Verification:
Error: no such manifest: anihpj/django:dev-test

✓ Local manifest removed
✓ Production manifest still in registry
✓ Platform images unchanged

Storage freed: ~2 KB manifest metadata

Benefits:
- Clean local Docker storage
- Prevents confusion between dev/prod
- Forces fresh manifest creation next cycle
- CI/CD disk space management
Example 2: Remove Multiple Old Manifest Versions

Batch delete outdated manifest versions.

# List local manifests (simulated)
docker images --format "{{.Repository}}:{{.Tag}}" | grep "anihpj/jobpost-api"

# Remove old beta and RC manifests
docker manifest rm \
    anihpj/jobpost-api:v2.0-beta1 \
    anihpj/jobpost-api:v2.0-beta2 \
    anihpj/jobpost-api:v2.0-rc1 \
    anihpj/jobpost-api:v2.0-rc2

# Verify cleanup
docker manifest inspect anihpj/jobpost-api:v2.0-beta1 2>&1 | grep -q "no such manifest" && \
    echo "✓ Old manifests cleaned"

# Keep only production manifest
docker manifest inspect anihpj/jobpost-api:v2.0
Existing local manifests:
anihpj/jobpost-api:v2.0-beta1
anihpj/jobpost-api:v2.0-beta2
anihpj/jobpost-api:v2.0-rc1
